This paper applies a new Generative Adversarial Network-based technique for detecting anomalies (GAN-AN), tailored to early defect identification in software development. Our technique, which detects defects as anomalies, is compared against three established methods: autoencoder, isolation forest, and one-class support vector machine (OCSVM). Our experimentation, utilizing five datasets from the NASA Promise repository, shows GAN-AN to be superior, with improvements of 7 % in precision-recall area under the curve (PR-AUC) and a significant 20 % in F1 score over previous techniques. Further evaluation using validation datasets reveals that while GAN-AN's overall performance was not uniformly superior, it notably outperformed isolation forest and OCSVM in recall on several datasets. This underscores GAN-AN's potential in specific contexts where recall is critical and highlights the importance of context-specific model selection. Our investigation also reveals that performance metrics vary among models, suggesting that the most effective model may vary depending on the dataset and specific detection goals.

title: A SAC-GHULSTM Algorithm for Consumer Robot Navigation in Complex Indoor Environments with Progressive Training
authors: Tianyi Jin, Yuemin Ding, Renzhi Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: To improve the robustness and efficiency of consumer mobile robot navigation in complex indoor environments, this paper presents a deep reinforcement learning method named SAC-GHULSTM. The method integrates a gated highway unit enhanced long short-term memory (GHULSTM) into the soft actor-critic (SAC) framework, combined with a progressive training strategy. Standard LSTM networks tend to exhibit limited effectiveness in preserving informative temporal features when processing long-sequence navigation data, which can lead to slow convergence and suboptimal stability performance. To address this issue, a GHULSTM network structure is proposed. By integrating GHU into each hidden layer of the LSTM, the hidden state can directly retain and transmit key historical sequence information while fusing with current inputs. This design enhances long-term information retention and improves the effectiveness of temporal dependency modeling in long-horizon navigation scenarios. Additionally, a curriculum-based progressive training mechanism is designed during the training phase. This approach ensures the algorithm quickly learns effective strategies in initial simple scenarios and gradually adapts to complex environments, thereby improving its generalization ability. Experimental results in scenarios with output noise demonstrate that the proposed algorithm significantly accelerates training convergence and enhances stable-phase performance. In particular, the robot’s average collision rate is substantially reduced, the target arrival rate is notably improved, and the GHULSTM-based strategy combined with progressive training achieves superior target arrival efficiency in identical test scenarios, enabling reliable target reaching.

title: Buffer Management for Timely Reconstruction: Lower Bounds and Near-Optimal Policies_supp1-3684353.pdf
authors: Sunjung Kang
publish: N/A
abstract: Remote tracking systems play a critical role in applications such as IoT sensing, monitoring, surveillance, and healthcare, where updates must be sufficiently fresh for real-time decisions while also informative enough to reconstruct past trajectories. We study this dual objective for a single-source system whose state follows a Wiener process and whose updates are generated at rate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> and transmitted over a non-preemptive <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$M/M/1/2$</tex-math></inline-formula> communication link, i.e., a single-server link with exponential service times of rate <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math></inline-formula> and a buffer that can store at most one waiting packet. Freshness is measured using the average peak Age of Information (AoI) and the time-average AoI, while historical fidelity is captured by a long-run reconstruction error (RE) derived from LMMSE interpolation. We first analyze a stationary randomized replacement (SRP) rule that, upon an arrival to a full system, keeps the fresh packet with probability <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and derive closed-form expressions for AoI and RE showing that AoI decreases monotonically with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> while RE varies non-monotonically with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>. In the heavy-traffic limit (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda \to \infty$</tex-math></inline-formula>), RE converges to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/(3\mu)$</tex-math></inline-formula> regardless of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula>, revealing a fundamental lower bound on reconstruction accuracy imposed by the service rate of the communication link. To further navigate the timeliness-fidelity trade-off, we introduce a threshold-based dropping policy that switches from keeping old to fresh packets after a tunable threshold <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gamma$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Closed-form analysis and numerical experiments reveal regimes where thresholding reduces RE relative to keep-fresh with only a modest age penalty, providing practical guidelines for selecting <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha$</tex-math></inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gamma$</tex-math></inline-formula> across different load conditions.

title: Translating Community-Specific Factors into Engineering Solutions: An HCD Framework for Rural Technology Adoption
authors: Aroun Clément Baudouin-van Os, S. Sabarinath, Udaya Bhaskar Reddy Ragula, Sasangan Ramanathan, Renjith Mohan, Maneesha Vinodini Ramesh
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Achieving sustainable livelihoods in marginalized communities remains a complex global challenge. Too often, technical interventions falter due to a disconnect between design and local context. This study examines a solar thermal steam distillation initiative in the tribal community of Valaramkunnu, Kerala, India, introduced to replace a traditional wood-fired livelihood lost to deforestation policies. While environmentally sound, the initial solar prototype suffered from low adoption, highlighting the gap between technological potential and community acceptance. Addressing this gap, this research defines and applies a Socio-Technical Translation Framework to structure a dual-phase methodology: first, an inductive thematic analysis of project documents and stakeholder interviews identified 76 community-specific adoption factors across eight dimensions, revealing that economic viability and system reliability were paramount over environmental purity. Second, by employing Human-Centered Design (HCD) principles within the framework's translation phase, the study converted these insights into 15 targeted system enhancements. The most significant outcome is the development of a pragmatic hybrid energy system integrating solar, electric, and gas sources that prioritizes the year-round operational reliability required for livelihood security. The successful technical validation of the hybrid architecture and safety systems through controlled endurance testing supports this HCD-integrated framework as a replicable model for integrating deep contextual analysis into the iterative design of rural technologies. It argues that for sustainable solutions to be resilient, they must balance environmental goals with the specific socio-economic capabilities of the user community.

title: A Framework for Measuring Trust as a Software Architecture Quality Attribute
authors: Ricardo Gacitua, Carla Taramasco Toro
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Trust is a critical yet underspecified concern in modern software-intensive systems, especially in regulated domains such as healthcare and finance. While widely discussed, trust remains weakly formalized at the software architecture level, leaving architects without systematic means to reason about or measure it. Existing trust-assessment approaches lack architectural focus, often addressing trust in an ad hoc manner that fails to capture its socio-technical, multidimensional, and lifecycle-dependent nature. This paper proposes a novel, comprehensive framework for conceptualising, measuring, and integrating trust as a first-class attribute of software architecture quality. The framework structures trust into six socio-technical dimensions and operationalises it using the Goal–Question-Metric (GQM) approach, yielding traceable, context-sensitive trust metrics linked to architectural decisions and lifecycle stages. The framework is empirically evaluated through in-depth case studies in healthcare information systems and AI-enabled credit scoring platforms, two trust-critical domains with stringent regulatory constraints. The results show that the framework supports systematic trust operationalisation, improves transparency, explainability, and regulatory alignment, and facilitates stakeholder-driven architectural reasoning across domains. This work positions trust as an explicit, measurable quality attribute and provides a structured, adaptable foundation for designing and evaluating trustworthy software architectures under evolving socio-technical conditions.

title: Graph Neural Network-Enhanced Multi-Objective Scheduling and Offloading for Hybrid Applications in Edge-Cloud Networks
authors: Yang Sun, Jitao Yi, Yuwei Bian, Meng Li, Pengbo Si, Fei Richard Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: The rapid proliferation of the Internet of Things (IoT) has driven the emergence of intelligent applications characterized by complex task dependencies and heterogeneous quality of service (QoS) requirements. Efficient coordination of edge-cloud resources to satisfy diverse QoS demands of hybrid applications remains a challenging problem. In this paper, we formulate the task scheduling and offloading problem in edge-cloud networks as a multi-constraint, multi-objective combinatorial optimization problem through a two-dimensional decision model that jointly determines task prioritization and server selection. To support adaptive decision-making in dynamic environments, the problem is further modeled as a multi-objective Markov decision process (MOMDP). Based on this formulation, we propose a graph neural network-enhanced multi-objective reinforcement learning (GEMORL) framework that integrates a graph neural network (GNN) with multi-objective reinforcement learning (MORL). The GNN hierarchically extracts task-level, application-level, and global-level representations from task-related and latency-related attributes of applications, providing expressive state embeddings for policy learning. A multi-objective policy gradient (MOPG) algorithm is employed to train an independent policy network for each objective, and a deterministic policy is derived via a scalarization method. Extensive simulations demonstrate that GEMORL achieves superior trade-offs among conflicting objectives and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baseline schemes across diverse network scenarios.

title: Towards AI-Native Ultra-Dense Networks: Cell Association via Multi-View Sensing
authors: Khoa Anh Ngo, Seungnyun Kim, Jihoon Moon, Byungju Lee, Jiao Wu, Byonghyo Shim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Millimeter wave (mmWave)/terahertz (THz) ultra-dense networks (UDNs) are anticipated to meet the technical performance requirements (TPRs) of immersive 6G applications. By deploying a large number of small base stations (SBSs), UDN can mitigate the severe attenuation in mmWave and THz frequency bands. To fully enjoy the benefits of mmWave/THz UDNs, ensuring line-of-sight (LoS) communication between SBSs and user equipments (UEs) is essential. However, identifying LoS conditions and determining proper cell associations require a contextual understanding of the surrounding environments, which conventional wireless measurement-based approaches may not readily provide. In this paper, we propose a novel cell association scheme for 6G mmWave/THz UDN based on vision sensing and computer vision (CV) technologies. The key idea of the proposed technique is to directly extract the positions and LoS conditions of UEs using multi-view RGB images. As the proposed technique operates without relying on wireless measurements, it eliminates the need for pilot transmissions and channel feedback, thereby reducing signaling overhead and latency significantly. Furthermore, by capturing UEs from multiple viewpoints, we can effectively detect them even in the presence of occlusions. From extensive numerical evaluations on realistic UDN scenarios, we show that the proposed scheme achieves 30% throughput gain over conventional cell association methods.

title: Nightlights for Older Adults: Enhancing Bedroom-to-Bathroom Transitions With Community Resident Feedback
authors: Yue Luo, Aubrey Contreras, Susana Valdivia-Rivas, Yuhao Chen, Boyi Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems
abstract: This study aimed to inform nightlight design by analyzing older adults’ subjective feedback and kinematic responses during evening activities. Twenty-seven older adults (mean age: 81 ± 6 years; 14 females) participated in the study. Their preferences for nightlight illuminance and hue were surveyed, and gait and posture adaptations under two illuminance levels were analyzed using spatiotemporal comparisons, principal component analysis (PCA) based analysis, and waveform reconstruction. The results revealed that older adults preferred low illuminance over high illuminance, with warm white and cool colors as the favored choices for evening lighting. Significant differences were observed in spatiotemporal gait variables, particularly stride length, and walking speed, between the two illuminance levels. Differences in illuminance conditions also led to overall and localized posture changes, as demonstrated by the reconstruction of joint angles through PCA-based posture analysis. Additionally, correlations between spatiotemporal gait variables and principal component vectors revealed that PCA provides complementary spatial and temporal information, emphasizing its value alongside traditional gait analysis. These findings emphasize the importance of integrating older adults’ subjective preferences and objective kinematic observations into nightlight design. Nightlight systems that consider illuminance levels, color tones, and biomechanical efficiency can enhance confidence, comfort, and mobility during nighttime activities, contributing to the residential well-being of older adults.

title: CaptionPS: Benchmarking and Analyzing Worker-Centric Detailed Image Captioning in Power Scenarios
authors: Zixiang Wang, Xin Chen, Jiajia Han, Junyu Cai, Shuangchen Wang, Junjie Huang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Image captioning has long been a persistent challenge in the field of vision-language research. With the rise of Large Language Models (LLMs), modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can produce effective captions in general-purpose scenarios. However, when confronted with complex power safety violation scenarios, they often omit critical violation details, greatly limiting their reliability in safety supervision. Therefore, we propose CaptionPS, an image-captioning dataset tailored for violation detection. CaptionPS is constructed through a systematic pipeline with multiple rounds of manual annotation. Each sample includes not only the classification of the violation scene and the description of the image content, but also the prediction of the safety of subsequent behaviors, ensuring the safety of workers. Based on CaptionPS, We evaluate a suite of VLMs. Experimental results show that open-source pretrained models generally exhibit low performance. However, after supervised fine-tuning, the overall performance of all models has been significantly improved. Among the evaluated models, InternVL3.5-8B achieves the highest performance on detailed captioning. Meanwhile, InternVL3.5-8B and Llama-3.2-11B achieve the best performance on scene classification and safety prediction tasks, respectively, with accuracy rates reaching 87.32% and 78.51%, which are 48.47% and 45.66% higher than those of the baseline models. The proposed CaptionPS helps MLLMs better learn violation characteristics and improve scene recognition in power scenarios.

title: Multi-Cycle Optimal Trajectory Control (OTC) with ZVS for LCC Converters Under Wide Range Load Transition
authors: Deshang Sha, Chao Gao, Xiangshuai Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Fast dynamic response is a critical attribute for LCC resonant converters. This paper proposes a multi-cycle optimal trajectory control (OTC) for LCC resonant converters under wide range load transition. The steady-state trajectories and characteristic equations are established. Then the muti-cycle control trajectories are designed to enable the resonant tank to reach the new steady state quickly. Meanwhile, zero-voltage-switching (ZVS) on for all switches are ensured during all OTC cycles. With real-time load detection, the target power can be quickly determined, and the required OTC cycles can be found in lookup tables. The effectiveness of the proposed control is verified on a 1-kW prototype.

title: Accelerated Transfer-Entropy-Weighted ADMM for Learning Causal Directed Acyclic Graphs
authors: Jinren Zhang, Jinde Cao, Leszek Rutkowski, Xinli Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Learning directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from observational time series is a fundamental task in causal inference, yet commonly used objectives based on instantaneous scoring rules can fail to capture lagged or nonlinear dependencies. To address this issue, we propose a transfer entropy-weighted (TE-weighted) optimization problem with an accelerated ADMM solver, called ATEW–ADMM. The framework embeds empirical TE as prior edge weights in an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\ell \_{1}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-regularized objective under a NOTEARS-style smooth acyclicity constraint. ATEW–ADMM combines a lazy static TE–ordered projection, over-relaxation, and adaptive penalty scheduling to reduce per-iteration cost, damp oscillations, and balance primal-dual progress. Under a Kurdyka-Łojasiewicz (KL) property and bounded penalty scheduling, we prove global convergence of ATEW–ADMM to a KKT point and establish an <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(1/\sqrt{K})$</tex-math></inline-formula> best-iterate bound on the combined primal–dual residual. We further show that the lazy projection step satisfies a one-step energy decrease inequality for the augmented Lagrangian. In addition, we derive sufficient conditions for exact support recovery under a transfer-entropy separation margin. Experiments on synthetic data across multiple network sizes demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves higher recall, lower false discovery rates, and smaller structural Hamming distances than continuous optimization baselines based on instantaneous scores. Ablation studies show that each of the three accelerations contributes markedly to the overall performance. Overall, ATEW–ADMM provides a scalable and theoretically grounded approach for causal DAG recovery in complex dynamic systems.

title: Remote Monitoring, Control, and Update Triggering Via LoRaWAN in an Edge-Based Vehicle Counting System
authors: Fabian Palacios-Pereira, Marcelo Palma, Mario Arzamendia, Derlis O. Gregor, Diego Palacios, Ariel Fleitas, Hernán Lezcano
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents an autonomous, low-power, and scalable vehicle counting and monitoring system, both in terms of network and functionality, that does not depend on a continuous internet connection for its operation. The proposal integrates LoRa communication for long-distance transmission of counting data with high energy efficiency, edge processing using an NVIDIA Jetson Nano running a YOLOv8n-based detection model, and remote feature update capability through the decentralized InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) using hash addressing. The system implements bidirectional data flow over LoRa technology, allowing both the sending of information (counts, measurements) and the receiving of control commands (remote reboots, hash updates). Field evaluations demonstrate the system's ability to detect and classify vehicles in real-time with low latency, maintain low power consumption, and update operational scripts with 100 % success in test scenarios. On average, 8.92 FPS were achieved during inference and a full update deployment time of less than 1.2 minutes, validating the effectiveness, resilience, and functional scalability of this architecture for vehicle monitoring in diverse environments.

title: Mesdasd: A Medical Expert System for Diagnosing Autoimmune System Diseases
authors: Mohammadreza Hajiarbabi
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Expert systems are valuable tools in various fields, including control, diagnosis, and medical applications. In recent decades, expert systems have been increasingly used in the medical field. Immune system diseases encompass a wide range of conditions, affecting approximately 8% of the U.S. population. These diseases target different parts of the body, including neurons, tissues, and skin. Some immune disorders share similar symptoms, while others resemble unrelated conditions, making diagnosis challenging even for specialized physicians. An expert system can assist not only the patients but also the physicians in the diagnostic process by leveraging artificial intelligence. For designing MESDASD (Medical Expert System for Diagnosing Autoimmune System Diseases), forward chaining method was used. For inference, certainty theory was applied. By using certainty theory the rules which are the symptoms of the diseases will have different certainty factors, that shows those with higher certainty factor are more important in the process of decision making. Fourteen tests, two for each diseases in moderate and severe case were used to evaluate the system. The system managed to pass all tests correctly with high margin. The results has demonstrated that MESDASD can accurately diagnose these diseases with a high level of certainty. MESDASD is the first expert system designed to diagnose various immune system diseases. In its initial version, the system primarily focuses on immune disorders affecting the skin and other closely related conditions. The system passed all the tests with high certainty.

title: Flexible Graphene Tactile Sensors with Spider Web Designs for Human-Computer Interaction
authors: Brandon Singleton, Teja V. S. N. Vuddagiri, Juan A. Zepeda Maldonado, Mose Williams, Sandip Das
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Laser Induced graphene (LIG) is an emerging technology that facilitates the production of multilayer graphene sheets and has found applications in various types of electronic sensors. Here, we present a set of three innovative spider web-shaped tactile sensors fabricated using LIG electrodes on flexible polyimide (PI) substrates. A 450 nm near-UV diode laser was used to directly form conductive spider web graphene electrodes and their connection traces on PI films. Raman spectroscopy and optical microscopy confirmed the formation of multilayer graphene under optimized laser irradiation conditions. By interfacing the graphene electrodes with an ATmega328P microcontroller and front-end electronics, functional capacitive touch sensors were realized. The spider web electrode geometry provided wide-area coverage while maintaining electrical conductivity and mechanical compliance under bending, making these flexible tactile sensors suitable for human-machine interaction (HMI) applications.

title: Accelerating Enterprise Application Development Through AI-Enhanced Low-Code Platforms: An Empirical Analysis of Oracle APEX with Generative AI Integration
authors: Mohankrishna Kothapalli, Dharanidhar Vuppu
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Low-code platforms promise faster application delivery, but rigorous empirical evidence remains scarce. We conducted exploratory multi-study experiments with 12 professional developers and 20 business analysts to evaluate Oracle APEX with Select AI integration. Our findings reveal substantial productivity gains: developers completed equivalent functionality significantly faster than with ReactJS (mean speedup <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$37 \times$</tex>, though this ratio reflects both platform abstraction and AI benefits), writing 95 % less code. When we tested Select AI's natural language query capabilities across 1,250 queries, GPT4 achieved 71 % accuracy while Cohere and Oracle's models reached 64 % and 61 % respectively. Security testing with 140 adversarial prompts showed Row-Level Security blocked 96% of attacks. Business analysts with no Structured Query Language (SQL) training completed 78 % of data access tasks successfully using natural language alone. These exploratory results suggest AI-enhanced low-code platforms offer significant potential for enterprise development, warranting larger-scale replication studies.

title: Human-Centered Explainable AI for Security Enhancement: A Deep Intrusion Detection Framework
authors: Md Muntasir Jahid Ayan, Md. Shahriar Rashid, Tazzina Afroze Hassan, Hossain Md. Mubashshir Jamil, Mahbubul Islam, Lisan Al Amin, Rupak Kumar Das, Farzana Akter, Faisal Quader
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The increasing complexity and frequency of cyberthreats demand intrusion detection systems (IDS) that are not only accurate but also interpretable. This paper presented a novel IDS framework that integrated Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to enhance transparency in deep learning models. The framework was evaluated experimentally using benchmark dataset NSL-KDD demonstrating superior performance compared to traditional IDS and black-box deep learning (DL) models. The proposed approach combined Convolutional Neural Network (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network for capturing temporal dependencies in traffic sequences. Our DL results showed that both CNN and LSTM reached 0.99 for accuracy whereas LSTM outperformed CNN at macro average precision, recall and F-1 score. For weighted average precision, recall and F-1 score, both model scored almost similar. To ensure interpretability, XAI model SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) was incorporated, enabling security analysts to understand and validate model decisions. Some notable influential features were srv_serror_rate, dst_host_srv_serror_rate, and serror_rate for both models as pointed out by SHAP. We also conducted a trust-focused expert survey based on IPIP6 and Big Five personality traits via an interactive user interface (UI) to evaluate the system's reliability and usability. This work highlighted the potential of combining performance and transparency in cybersecurity solutions and recommends future enhancements through adaptive learning for real-time threat detection.

title: Editor Preface
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This IEEE Chilecon Conference is the continuation of the Chilean Congress of Electrical Engineering, founded in 1975 by Dr. Guillermo González Rees, and was the first conference held at the University of Chile. For 37 years, it was held every two years in odd-numbered years, in agreement with the Chilean Automatic Control Association, which holds its conferences in even-numbered years. In 1987, a cooperative alliance was signed between ACCA and the IEEE Chilean Control Chapter to co-organize these conferences. In 2012, the IEEE Chilean Section assumed co-organization, changing the name to IEEE Chilecon.

title: Calculation of the Impact of Overvoltage Transients in Hydroelectric Plants and the Electric Power Industry
authors: Álvaro Soto A., Vladimir Esparza H., José Mahomar J., Daniel Quezada M., Jaime Rohten C.
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This article analyzes the impact of transient overvoltages on electrical power systems based on field measurements in Chile. Three representative cases were studied: a hydroelectric power plant, an electric vehicle charging station, and an industrial plant. The campaigns were carried out in accordance with IEEE 1159-2019 using a Fluke 434 with a valid calibration certificate, recording brief high-magnitude events with cumulative effects on insulation. At the Cipreses Power Plant, pulses of up to 60.91 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{kV}(441 \%$</tex> of Un) were detected, concurrent with 2.37 million partial discharges per second, evidence of advanced stator degradation. In DC chargers, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5-20 \text{kHz}$</tex> disturbances with 600 V and 200 A were identified during energization, causing erratic tripping due to low relay immunity. In the industrial plant, impulsive/oscillatory transients were observed which, when interacting with inductance and capacitance, amplified the surges and compromised the stability of the equipment. A synergistic mitigation scheme based on coordinated SPDs (Types 1-2-3), Point-on-Wave (PoW) switching, and detuned reactive compensation is proposed, aimed at reducing the magnitude of impulses, avoiding resonances, and preserving operational reliability. The results support the incorporation of transient analysis into predictive maintenance and critical asset planning, and provide guidelines for prioritizing mitigation investments with high technical and economic impact.

title: End-to-End Virtual Try-on Via Latent Diffusion Model with Multi-Condition Controlnet
authors: Dongchuang Zhao, Lili Ma, Jinguang Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Image-based virtual try-on is widely used in the e-commerce and fashion fields, aiming to synthesize images of people wearing designated clothing. The rise of diffusion models has promoted relevant research, but existing methods rely on specialized clothing warping modules, suffering from issues such as high system complexity, poor generality, and insufficient high-resolution texture fidelity. To address these problems, this paper proposes an end-to-end latent diffusion model framework that eliminates the need for explicit warping processes. The framework embeds clothing deformation in the latent space and leverages enhanced cross-attention blocks to learn semantic correspondences between clothing and the human body for patchlevel deformation. It also introduces ControlNet, fusing clothing images and DensePose features as multi-condition signals to improve the accuracy of structural alignment under complex poses and backgrounds. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances the generation quality of high-resolution virtual try-on and achieves both qualitative and quantitative breakthroughs on standard benchmarks.

title: Data Science and Privacy in Secure Predictive Analysis with Homomorphic Encryption and Partial Anonymization
authors: Alan Rodrigo Corini Guarachi, Juan Pablo Vásconez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Homomorphic encryption (HE) is an advanced cryptographic technique that allows algebraic operations to be performed directly on encrypted data without needing to decrypt it first. This property ensures data confidentiality throughout the entire computation process, making it a valuable tool in environments where privacy is critical. Partial anonymization of sensitive attributes that could facilitate re-identification of individuals mitigates risks associated with personal data analysis. By selectively removing or masking certain identifiers, data utility for analytical tasks is preserved without compromising subject identity. Combining homomorphic encryption with partial anonymization techniques enables a new paradigm of secure predictive analytics. It becomes possible to train machine learning models or perform inference directly on protected data without ever exposing it at any stage of the workflow.

title: From Prototype to Production: A Checklist for Production-Ready AI Focused on Data Quality, Monitoring, and Human Oversight
authors: Femi Oke, Anjum Razzaque, Abdullahi Ibiyeye
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Many AI projects face failure since the data is messy, since AI is not monitored properly, or possibly end-users are not involved enough. Building such a prototype appears straightforward, whereas a production-ready system is more challenging. For instance, Machine Learning (ML) projects fail to transition from promising prototypes to reliable products due to issues with data quality, lack of continuous monitoring, or insufficient human oversight. We present a clear checklist of the current research gap for developing “production-ready” AI systems across three critical areas: data quality, continuous monitoring, and human oversight. Our work offers best practices for each area, using clean, unbiased, and representative data, monitoring models for performance and data changes, and incorporating human-in-the-loop for safety and accountability. Such a checklist helps identify early risks, mitigate technical problems, and promote trust in AI. Case studies and references help us establish best practices to bridge the gap from prototype to production research.

title: DT-Empowered, Social-Aware Service Provisioning in Edge Computing
authors: Jing Li, Jianping Wang, Weifa Liang, Jie Wu, Quan Chen, Zichuan Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Networking
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is gathering paces in the new era of Industry 4.0, and the Digital Twin (DT) technology bridges the gap between the bursting amounts of data generated by IoT devices and the user requirements for real-time data processing. DT services maintain living digital models of physical objects, and a DT network enables comprehensive service provisioning with the global knowledge of a group of DTs. On the other hand, exposing serverless computing at network edges, the recent advances in Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) introduce new inspirations to the DT landscape that ensure fine-grained resource management and low network-wide delay of DT services. However, social relationships among IoT devices and DT data privacy impact DT orchestrations. In this paper, we first design a differential privacy-based federated learning framework to build a DT network for DT services in response to user requests in an MEC, thereby enhancing the Quality of Services (QoS). Built upon the proposed framework, we then formulate two novel social-aware DT placement problems: the static social-aware S_DT placement problem, and the dynamic social-aware S_DT placement problem, respectively. We also show the NP-hardness of the defined problems. Then, we formulate an Integer Linear Program (ILP) solution to the static social-aware S_DT placement problem when the problem size is small; otherwise we develop an approximation algorithm with a provable approximation ratio for it. Third, we study the dynamic social-aware S_DT placement problem when requests arrive one by one without the knowledge of future request arrivals over the time horizon, for which we devise an online algorithm with a provable competitive ratio. Finally, we conduct simulations to evaluate the performance of the proposed algorithms. Simulation results show that the proposed algorithms outperform their counterparts, improving the performance compared with their baselines by no less than 14.9%.

title: Data-Driven Insights & Predictive Modeling in Fitness Tracking
authors: Nabeel M Sheikh, Amit Kumar, Vansh Agarwal
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Heart rate variability (HRV) is increasingly used in wearable-based fitness tracking due to its sensitivity to stress, recovery, and cardiovascular function. This study examined whether body composition metrics (BMI and body fat percentage) collected manually and via wearables are consistent, and whether HRV features offer additional insight into individual health trends. A review of prior literature highlighted both the promise and challenges of integrating HRV into predictive modeling, especially regarding individual variability and external influences. The purpose of this study was to assess the reliability of wearable data and evaluate whether HRV varies systematically with body composition. Using datasets from Kaggle and Harvard Dataverse, we analyzed biometric, activity, and HRV metrics from 35 participants. Results showed that wearable-reported body composition data closely matched manual entries, supporting their use in health tracking. However, no strong associations were found between body composition and HRV features. In contrast, HRV varied significantly over time, while BMI remained stable, highlighting HRV's utility for personalized monitoring. These findings support the reliability of wearable sensors and reinforce the value of HRV as a dynamic health indicator for individualized wellness insights. These findings suggest that HRV may serve as a more responsive and individualized metric for real-time health feedback, offering practical applications for adaptive fitness programs and stress management interventions.

title: Monolithic 3-D Integration of IAZO/Si-based 2T0C DRAM with RRAM for Monte Carlo Dropconnect Neural Networks
authors: Lin Bao, Bowen Jiang, Jiaqi Zhao, Haisu Zhang, Yingtong Ji, Yingchen Ji, Jingwei Sun, Yujie Chen, Fan Zhang, Cuimei Wang, Ling Liang, Zheng Zhou, Zongwei Wang, Yimao Cai, Shanguo Huang
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: Edge intelligent systems demand high energy efficiency and robustness, driving interest in Bayesian method-based AI algorithms such as Monte Carlo dropconnect neural networks (MCDNNs) for uncertainty quantification. However, in-memory computing (IMC) for MCDNN faces a critical endurance-retention trade-off. In this paper, we propose a 2T0C-1R IMC unit leveraging heterogeneous volatile/non-volatile memory to resolve this. Fabricated via monolithic 3-D (M3D) integration, it combines an oxide semiconductor write transistor for DRAM retention and a silicon read transistor for RRAM operation. The 2T0C-1R unit achieves >1000 s volatile retention and >4-bit non-volatile storage while meeting endurance requirements of random dropconnect. Experimental MCDNN validation shows a 33% calibration performance improvement over traditional neural networks, highlighting the 2T0C-1R array’s potential for reliable, energy-efficient edge AI applications.

title: On the Systematic Design Framework for Intuitive Interfaces in Telerobotic Operations: A Case Study in Surgical Robotics_supp1-3684095.docx
authors: Seok Chang Ryu
publish: N/A
abstract: This paper proposes a design framework for intuitive interfaces in telerobotic operation, moving beyond outcome-based evaluation toward systematic interface design. A human-centered framework is introduced to guide interface strategy selection based on learning dynamics and to support implementation-level design decisions through analysis of human sensorimotor interaction. As a case study, the framework is instantiated through an embodiment-oriented index finger interface for controlling a distally bendable surgical instrument with unconventional kinematics. A telerobotic system was implemented, and the finger interface was compared with a familiarity-driven stylus interface in a dual-session human-subject study consisting of a training session and a retention session two weeks later. Results reveal a distinction between initial and later-stage interaction characteristics. While the stylus interface exhibited higher initial performance, the finger interface showed faster learning progression and achieved higher performance after learning stabilized. These findings suggest that embodiment-oriented interface design can support intuitive control by facilitating sensorimotor adaptation beyond immediate familiarity. Although evaluated in a surgical context, the proposed framework is applicable to the design of teleoperation interfaces for robotic systems with unconventional kinematics in broader domains.

title: Dual-timescale Joint Optimization for Dynamic Edge Service Deployment and Task Scheduling in Space-air-ground Integrated Networks
authors: Shuai Liu, Xiangxu Meng, Yun Zhong, Wenfeng Li, Kanglian Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: This paper investigates dual-timescale joint optimization for dynamic edge service deployment and task scheduling in space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGINs) with mobile edge computing (MEC). We decompose the original problem into a long-term (LT) service deployment subproblem and a short-term (ST) task scheduling subproblem to address the timescale disparity and coupling constraints. For the LT subproblem, an improved whale optimization algorithm (IWOA) is developed with a cosine-based nonlinear convergence factor, vertical-horizontal crossover, and elite opposition-based learning to enhance exploration and convergence. For the ST subproblem, we model task scheduling as a decentralized partially observable Markov decision process (Dec-POMDP) and adopt multi-agent proximal policy optimization (MAPPO) with KL regularization and clipping for stable policy updates. A hierarchical alternating framework (with a self-attention module for traffic-feature extraction) is designed to coordinate the two timescales iteratively. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with the baseline WOA-PPO algorithm, the proposed method increases service deployment benefits by 27.2% and reduces task scheduling costs by 38.5%.

title: EduCore: A Privacy-Aware LLM-Driven Education Framework with Intelligent Multi-Agent Support
authors: Debashis Das, Tenicka Norwood, Pushpita Chatterjee, Uttam Ghosh
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Educational institutions still depend on manual and disconnected systems for administration, teaching, assessment, and student support. This increases faculty workload, slows academic processes, and limits personalized learning. Most existing digital platforms only store content and do not actively assist teachers or students. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) make it possible to build intelligent systems that can support and automate routine academic tasks. Therefore, this paper presents the design and implementation of a privacy-preserving, LLM-driven agentic education framework that integrates smart administration, AI-assisted teaching, personalized learning, and workforce-oriented academic advising into a unified system. The framework uses multiple AI agents, each designed for a specific task, and runs on locally deployed LLMs. A retrieval-based approach is used to ensure responses are accurate, relevant, and secure. By keeping data within the institution, the system protects privacy and reduces operational costs. The proposed system also reduces faculty workload, improves efficiency in academic processes, increases student engagement, and provides better personalized support. Overall, this work shows that agent-based AI can modernize education systems practically and securely to support both educators and students without replacing human decision-making.

title: Engineering Management for AI-Driven Software Quality Assurance: Practices, Challenges, and Future Direction
authors: Srinivas Reddy Kosna, Subba Rao Katragadda
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into software systems has introduced unprecedented complexities and opportunities for quality assurance. This paper explores the evolving landscape of software quality engineering management in the age of AI. It examines the critical challenges and best practices for managing the quality of AI-driven software, from data acquisition to model deployment and maintenance. We present a comprehensive framework that synthesizes established software engineering principles with emerging machine learning (ML) development lifecycles. The paper analyzes key engineering management practices, including automated test generation, defect prediction, and continuous model validation, drawing on case studies and industry research. Furthermore, it discusses the future of AI in quality assurance, highlighting the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) and knowledge graphs to enable more autonomous and intelligent testing paradigms. This paper aims to provide a valuable resource for engineering managers, QA professionals, and researchers navigating the transition to AI-centric software development.

title: MFND: A Multimodal Fusion Framework for Noise-Robust Fault Detection in Microservice Architectures
authors: Haokun Fan, Cheng Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Microservices architecture has become the main paradigm of cloud computing because it is easy to scale and agility. However, its distributed property brings some problems to fault detection, such as complex service dependency and various monitoring data (logs, metrics, Traces). The existing methods often fail to cope with the noise of the raw data effectively, are unable to make full use of the cross-modal correlation of data, resulting in inaccurate fault diagnosis or misjudgment. To solve this problem, this paper puts forward MFND a new multimodal fault detection framework based on dynamic graph construction and robust feature learning of logs, metrics and traces. Framework describes service dependencies in the form of dynamic graphs and retains important features by filtering out noises, and uses Hybrid GNN for adaptively capturing the system state transition. Experiments on public data sets show that MFND can achieve the leading detection effect in the industry and provide the optimal deployment configuration through parameter analysis, and it is proved to be an effective and stable fault detection solution for dynamic microservice environment.

title: Automated Evaluation of Authentication Features of Web Pages from the Low Vision User Perspective
authors: Zeyu He, Suzanna Schmeelk, Jose Florez-Arango, Helen Petrie
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Ensuring secure and accessible authentication is crucial for protecting user data while maintaining inclusivity for individuals with disabilities. However, security mechanisms such as CAPTCHAs, password strength meters, and complex password policies often create barriers that hinder accessibility. This study conducted an automated evaluation of authentication features across 39 widely used websites, assessing CAPTCHA implementation, password strength indicators, and policy transparency using Selenium-based automation. The findings revealed that only 7.7% of analyzed websites implemented CAPTCHA, yet none provided an audio alternative, limiting accessibility for visually impaired users. Password-based authentication remained the dominant mechanism, with 71.8% of websites including password fields; however, only 28.2% utilized password strength meters, and among them, just 23.1% incorporated ARIA attributes necessary for screen reader compatibility. Additionally, password policies were frequently hidden, with only 12.8% of sites displaying complexity requirements before user input. The presence of anti-bot measures on certain websites further restricted automated accessibility evaluations, suggesting that authentication mechanisms dynamically adapt based on user interactions. These findings underscore the need for inclusive security design, ensuring that CAPTCHA alternatives, screen reader-compatible password meters, and transparent password policies are integrated into authentication systems. Future work should explore biometric authentication and adaptive multifactor authentication as potential solutions to enhance both security and accessibility. Expanding automated testing frameworks could also improve large-scale evaluations of authentication barriers, contributing to more inclusive and secure digital environments.

title: Enhanced MIT Coffee Can Radar with Integrated Infrared Imaging for Target Detection and Classification for Experiential Learning Platform
authors: Isaac Anokye, Hidalgo Mudonhi, Goodwill Kwenda, Delores Baker
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This paper presents an enhanced version of the MIT Coffee Can Radar platform that integrates a softwaredefined radio (SDR) and an infrared (IR) imaging sensor to create a low-cost, modular learning system for radar and signal processing education. The proposed system replaces traditional analog radar components with an ADALM-Pluto SDR and an IR camera, enabling basic sensor fusion for improved target detection. Preliminary experimental results demonstrate range and Doppler detection capabilities, as well as qualitative improvements in target confirmation using thermal imagery. Designed as a reusable educational module, the platform bridges theoretical instruction and hands-on experimentation, providing an accessible framework for undergraduate engineering education.

title: Cascaded H-Bridge-Based Battery Storage Systems: A Distributed Control Approach for SoC Equalization of Second-Life Batteries
authors: Carlos Smart, Andrés Mora, Claudio Burgos Mellado
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The objective of this paper is to validate a novel consensus-based distributed control scheme for a cascaded Hbridge (CHB) battery energy storage system integrating heterogeneous second-life batteries (SLBs). The inherent variability in capacity of SLBs is the critical challenge this work addresses, justifying the need for a specific control approach. The proposed modular architecture groups submodules (SMs) into clusters based on shared degradation and residual capacity characteristics. The distributed control combines a central controller (CC) for inter-cluster SoC balancing and grid power management, with local controllers (LCs) that employ a consensus-based algorithm to equalize the SoC within each cluster. This dual-level strategy is essential to mitigate the individual parameter mismatch of the SLBs. Detailed simulation studies demonstrate effective and coordinated SoC balancing at both inter-cluster and intra-cluster levels, confirming that the distributed control ensures stable performance, making the CHB-based SLB system a promising, cost-effective solution for grid support.

title: A Software-Defined Testbed for Quantifying Deauthentication Resilience in Modern Wi-Fi Networks
authors: Alex Carbajal, Asma Jodeiri Akbarfam
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Wi-Fi deauthentication attacks remain a practical denial-of-service (DoS) threat by exploiting unprotected management frames to disrupt client connectivity. In this work, we introduce a software-defined testbed to measure Wi-Fi resilience to deauthentication attacks. We experimentally evaluate five wireless security configurations: open networks, WPA1, WPA2 without Protected Management Frames (PMF), WPA2 with PMF, and WPA3. Using controlled experiments, we measure client disconnection rates, packet injection volume, and time-to-disruption under each configuration. Packet-level behavior is analyzed using standard wireless auditing tools. Open networks, WPA1, and WPA2 without PMF proved entirely vulnerable to deauthentication, while no successful attacks were observed for WPA2 with PMF or WPA3 under tested conditions. These findings confirm the effectiveness of management-frame protection and highlight the continued risk posed by legacy or misconfigured wireless deployments.

title: Low-complexity Adaptive Fourier Integral Method for Large Aperture Horizontal Linear Arrays at Low Frequency and Low SNR Environments
authors: Cong Wang, Xionghou Liu, Yuyuan Zhou, Yixin Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Weak signal detection poses a fundamental challenge in passive sonar measurement systems, particularly under low-frequency and low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a low-complexity adaptive Fourier integral method (LCA-FIM), which introduces an innovative signal processing framework designed to enhance the measurement capabilities of large-aperture horizontal linear arrays (HLAs). The core innovation is an adaptive window selection strategy: multiple pre-designed windows with different noise gains and sidelobe levels are applied to FIM, generating several groups of weighted spatial spectra. Subsequently, an adaptive selection rule extracts the minimum output at each azimuth. This strategy enables LCA-FIM to effectively optimize the trade-off between key performance metrics, achieving high noise gain, high angular resolution and low sidelobe levels simultaneously. Numerical simulations demonstrate that LCA-FIM shows a better weak signal detection performance under strong-interference and low-SNR conditions, when compared to conventional beamformer (CBF), FIM, weighted FIM (WFIM), and diagonally loaded minimum variance distortionless response beamformer. Experimental validation using real ocean data further confirms the effectiveness of the proposed LCA-FIM method in clutter-rich, noisy, low-frequency and low-SNR environments. While validated in a passive sonar context, the proposed LCA-FIM is a general-purpose measurement method applicable to a wide range of array-based sensing system requiring weak signal analysis.

title: Assessment of Driver Cognitive Load in Autonomous Driving Using Extended Reality Simulation
authors: Seth Hatchett, Connor Viana, Arman Sargolzaei, Saman Sargolzaei
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: In this study, an extended reality-based driving simulation combined with electroencephalography (EEG), heart rate variability (HRV), and eye tracking data was used to assess the impact of advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) on cognitive load, which is the proportion of a driver's total cognitive resources needed to complete the driving task. The EEG data, processed using both multitaper spectral estimation (MT) and wavelet transform (WT), showed that AI-assisted steering and fully autonomous driving reduced cognitive load, whereas AIassisted throttle and manual driving did not significantly affect cognitive load relative to the baseline. This was indicated by decreased alpha, theta, and beta for fully autonomous driving and AI-assisted steering trials. HRV data further supported this trend, with the root mean square of successive inter-beat interval differences (RMSSD) increasing during manual and AI-assisted steering trials, indicating reduced cognitive load during these conditions. Eye tracking data also supported the EEG trend, with an increased total absolute change in eye motion speed variance during AI-assisted throttle trials. This exploratory study provides preliminary evidence that different ADAS functions may differentially influence driver cognitive load and engagement.

title: Distributed Estimator-Based Adaptive Fuzzy Prescribed-Time Consensus Control for Nonlinear Multiagent Systems
authors: Jiguang Peng, Zhi Liu, C. L. Philip Chen, Yun Zhang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This article investigates the problem of the prescribed-time consensus control for nonlinear multiagent systems under performance constraints and actuator attacks. First, a distributed output-feedback-based prescribed-time estimator is designed for each follower. This enables the followers to precisely estimate the leader’s signal. Subsequently, to achieve the control objective with prescribed performance, a prescribed-time performance function is constructed. Integrated with an asymmetric barrier Lyapunov function approach, it guarantees that asymmetric time-varying performance constraints are satisfied within the preset time. Furthermore, fuzzy logic systems are adopted to approximate unknown nonlinear functions. In addition, to handle the issue of unknown control directions, Nussbaum gain functions are incorporated into the controller design. The proposed controller ensures the boundedness of all signals in the closed-loop system, while simultaneously guaranteeing that the tracking errors remain within the prescribed performance bounds. Finally, simulation results demonstrate the advantages and effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Prefrontal Hierarchical Control of Corticomuscular Coherence in Stroke
authors: Jasper I. Mark, Rachana Gangwani, Justin Riddle, Jessica M. Cassidy
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Stroke recovery involves reorganization of large-scale brain networks, yet most neurophysiological biomarkers assess either motor or cognitive function in isolation. To address this limitation, we introduce phase-modulated corticomuscular coherence (pmCMC), a novel metric that quantifies how prefrontal delta-phase activity modulates beta-band corticomuscular coherence between the primary motor cortex and peripheral muscle. This approach integrates cross-frequency and corticomuscular coupling, offering a dynamic index of cognitive-motor interaction. We applied pmCMC to a cohort of individuals with hemiparetic stroke who performed a precision grip task during high-density EEG and EMG recording at admission and discharge from an inpatient rehabilitation facility. Our findings reveal that pmCMC increases across rehabilitation and positively relates to corticostriatal but not corticospinal tract injury. Additionally, higher pmCMC at admission was positively associated with greater gains in motor coordination, functional independence, and cognitive status. These results position pmCMC as hybrid neurophysiological measure of cognitive-motor integration relevant to post-stroke recovery and encourages further investigation of its prognostic utility, causal mechanisms, and applicability across diverse motor tasks and clinical populations.

title: Modeling and Capacity Analysis of UAV-Assisted Three-tier Heterogeneous Wireless Networks
authors: Su Zhan, Xiaorong Zhu, Xiaohua Qiu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Sudden disasters frequently disrupt terrestrial cellular infrastructure, severely degrading network capacity in affected regions and necessitating the rapid restoration of uplink connectivity for rescue and monitoring operations. To address this challenge, we propose a systematic capacity analysis framework for a three-tier emergency wireless network comprising a device access layer, an unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) relay layer, and a backhaul layer. First, within the access layer, heterogeneous ground devices are modeled using a Poisson point process (PPP). We analyze the uplink transmissions under non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA), deriving the coverage probability and ergodic capacity while accounting for imperfect successive interference cancellation (SIC). Next, in the relay layer, multiple concurrent flows are scheduled via time-frequency resource allocation across end-to-end UAV links, incorporating practical communication constraints and multihop packet-loss effects. To balance throughput and fairness, we introduce a joint resource allocation scheme that maximizes a novel satisfaction function. In the backhaul layer, the capacity between UAVs and base stations (BSs) is characterized under space-division multiple access (SDMA) with directional beamforming, explicitly modeling the impact of antenna-orientation jitter at both the UAV and BS sides. Finally, the overall end-to-end system capacity is formulated as the bottleneck capacity among the three tiers. Analytical and simulation results validate the accuracy of the proposed theoretical model. Furthermore, comparative evaluations demonstrate that the proposed resource allocation scheme for the UAV relay layer significantly improves allocation fairness among concurrent flows relative to conventional baselines.

title: Adaptive Codebook Design and Beam Training for RIS-Aided Communication Systems With Hardware Constraints
authors: Jiahao Gao, Shuhao Zeng, Boya Di, Lin Chang, Lianlin Li, Wei Xiang Jiang, Lingyang Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) has emerged as a promising solution to enable ultra-massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) for 6G wireless communications. To direct the transmitted beam towards users with low pilot overhead, multi-resolution codebook-based beam training has been proposed for RIS-aided communication systems, which employs codewords with varying beamwidths to progressively narrow the search range and identify the optimal beam direction. However, the hardware constraints of the RIS such as the limited control capability, mutual coupling and manufacturing tolerances have not been fully considered, which may result in non-ideal beam patterns and misleading beam search directions, thereby degrading the overall beam training performance. To address this issue, in this paper, we revisit the multi-resolution codebook design and beam training scheme by taking the RIS hardware constraints into account. First, we propose a wide-beam generation method via multi-beam superposition, where the RIS reflection coefficients are optimized to achieve high beam gain with suppressed undesired fluctuations. Then, to mitigate the impact of the residual fluctuations in beam patterns, a posterior-based beam training scheme is proposed to adaptively correct erroneous beam search directions, thereby improving the beam training accuracy. To experimentally evaluate the proposed scheme, we implement a prototype RIS with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$32\times 32$ </tex-math></inline-formula> reflective elements and deploy a 26 GHz millimeter-wave RIS-aided wireless communication testbed. Numerical and experimental results indicate that our approach achieves low training overhead and a data rate close to the exhaustive search method.

title: A Water-Controlled Dielectric Resonator Antenna with Unilateral and Omnidirectional Pattern Reconfigurability
authors: Xuan-Ji Wu, Yu-Tao Liang, Zhe Chen, Tao Yuan
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This paper presents a reconfigurable rectangular dielectric resonator antenna (RDRA) whose radiation patterns can be switched by controlling the liquid water. By loading a high-permittivity material to modify the feeding source, specific resonant modes of the RDRA are excited, thereby achieving reconfigurable radiation patterns. Based on this concept, a 3D-printed microfluidic container is integrated at the bottom of the RDRA. By injecting water into different sections of the container, reconfigurable control of the radiation pattern is realized. To validate the proposed idea, a prototype is fabricated and measured, demonstrating excellent overall performance, including five distinct patterns with an overlapping bandwidth of 20% while maintaining a compact size. The demonstrated performance indicates that the proposed antenna is well-suited for integrated sensing and communication applications.

title: VTBSAE: Provably Secure Verifier-based Password Authenticated Key Exchange for IIoT
authors: Mingping Qi
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Password-authenticated key exchange (PAKE) has gained widespread adoption in prominent platforms such as iCloud to provide realistic security assurance. It offers robust security by enabling mutual authentication and strong session key establishment between two parties via a low-entropy password. This paper presents an efficient verifier-based PAKE protocol called VTBSAE (Verifier-based Two-Basis Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) based on the well-known SAE protocol, aiming to provide light, reliable, and scalable security assurances for Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). The primary technological innovation lies in the utilization of two distinct bases over the underlying group to implement the Hash-to-Curve (H2C) function employed in SAE, and transform the symmetric SAE protocol into a verifier-based PAKE protocol, thereby enhancing its resilience against server compromise attacks. Moreover, adopting the widely accepted BPR (Bellare-Pointcheval-Rogaway) security model with further considering the perfect forward secrecy, VTBSAE’s security is formally proved under the standard CDH security assumption. This solid foundation provides VTBSAE with certain security advantages over some existing verifier-based PAKE protocols whose security proofs rely on some stronger security assumptions. VTBSAE can work over both the multiplicative and elliptic curve additive groups. Particularly, in the elliptic curve setting, VTBSAE requires no reliance on the H2C function, which is often challenging to implement securely over the commonly used elliptic curve parameters such as the secp256r1. Therefore, VTBSAE demonstrates significant improvements in ease of secure implementation compared to the existing verifierbased PAKE protocols that rely on the H2C function.
