title: Research on Image Classification Based on ResNeXt and Attention Mechanisms
authors: Zifan Song
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Image classification, as a fundamental task in computer vision, serves as the backbone for operations such as object detection and image segmentation and holds substantial economic and societal significance. However, most existing image classification models face challenges including high computational costs and limited generalization ability. To address these issues, we propose a novel image classification network architecture that integrates dual attention mechanisms into ResNext. Specifically, our model incorporates both channel and spatial attention modules to assign adaptive weights to different positions and channels within feature maps, thereby suppressing irrelevant features while enhancing salient ones. Extensive comparative and ablation experiments conducted on the CIFAR-10 dataset demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed approach.

title: Linear Quadratic Integral Control with Double Integrator for Platooning System: A Numerical Study of String Stability
authors: Carlos Escobar, Luis Severino, Héctor Ramírez, Andrés Peters, Francisco Vargas
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: In this work, we study string stability in vehicle platoons based on linear quadratic integral control (LQI). We focus on platoons modeled as continuous time LTI systems under a predecessor-following configuration with a time headway spacing policy. To ensure zero steady-state tracking error, we employ the LQI technique with double integration. Through numerical simulations, we show the relationship between the time headway constant and the weighting matrices of the LQI cost functional and how these design parameters affect string stab

title: Kalman-Augmented Estimation for Enhancing the Reliability Analysis of Power Electronic Converters via Sensor-Error Mitigation
authors: Esther Omoyiwola, Masoud Davari
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The lifetime and reliability of power electronic converters strongly depend on accurate thermal and electrical monitoring during operation. This paper presents a comprehensive reliability assessment of a buck converter system under sensor-induced uncertainties and introduces an augmented Kalman-based framework for error mitigation. A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$12-\text{kW}$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$300-$</tex> to-125-V buck converter is modeled in Simscape using a halfbridge IGBT-diode structure with detailed loss calculation and a Cauer-type thermal network. The monitoring subsystem records junction temperature, total losses, and load power, along with other measurements, while enforcing a shutdown above 150° C. The study investigates four distinct cases: a baseline converter with ideal sensing, an error-injected model with bias, drift, and gain distortion, a basic Kalman filter for partial correction, and an augmented Kalman filter incorporating sensor fault dynamics. The converter is modeled in MATLAB/Simulink using a coupled electro-thermal structure to evaluate steady-state performance, junction temperature, and lifetime using the Arrhenius degradation model. Results show that sensor errors can elevate the mean junction temperature by over 16° C and artificially extend lifetime prediction by more than 160%. The augmented Kalman filter successfully restores temperature and reliability estimates to within 4% of the baseline, confirming its ability to compensate for both random and systematic sensor deviations. The proposed estimation approach demonstrates a physics-consistent pathway for improving converter health monitoring and reliability prediction in systems affected by sensor degradation.

title: A Mathematical Framework for Wise AI
authors: Santiago Grijalva, Nicholas Grijalva
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: As artificial intelligence systems become increasingly autonomous and consequential, ensuring they make not merely accurate but genuinely wise decisions has emerged as a critical challenge. This paper presents a framework for Wise AI—systems that integrate short-term and long-term utility optimization with ethical reasoning, safety constraints, and long-term value preservation. Unlike conventional AI that maximizes narrow performance metrics, our framework formalizes stochastic multi-objective optimization with hard ethical constraints, domain-specific operational constraints, epistemic uncertainty quantification, and human-in-the-loop mechanisms. We introduce the wisdom function to balance these objectives subject to inviolable safety constraints, problem-specific resource constraints, and uncertainty thresholds. We propose methods for estimating ethical alignment and long-term value using both domain-specific metrics and reinforcement learning. Through simulation across six diverse scenarios—hospital resource allocation, autonomous vehicle collision avoidance, financial trading, social media content moderation, medical diagnosis, and hiring algorithms—we illustrate how Wise AI can achieve higher wisdom scores while maintaining perfect safety compliance. This work provides a framework for developing AI systems aligned with human values and societal welfare.

title: Strategic Location and Generation Optimization in Radial Power Systems Using Voltage Stability Indices and Fuzzy Logic Optimization
authors: Hernan Ramirez Pereira, Rodney Fariña Martinez, David Franco Leon
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of strategic location and generation optimization in radial power systems to enhance voltage stability. A methodology is proposed, combining voltage stability indices to identify critical system nodes, and fuzzy logic optimization to determine the optimal location and capacity of distributed generation. Fuzzy logic allows for consideration of multiple criteria and constraints. Results demonstrate that the proposed methodology effectively improves voltage stability by increasing the load margin in 42 %, while also reducing active power losses in radial distribution systems in 94.9 %.

title: Cavity-Enhanced Gas Raman Spectroscopy With Passively Locked Blue Laser Diodes
authors: Yuji Ichikawa, Ippei Asahi, Tatsuo Shiina, Shigeru Yamaguchi
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: Cavity enhanced Raman spectroscopy (CERS) is an emerging technique for real time analysis of gas mixtures. In this paper, the authors employed a passive self-injection locking technique utilizing an anti-reflection (AR) coated blue laser diode and a high finesse external power build-up cavity (PBC) for trace gas analysis by CERS. With a 20 to 30-mW laser diode passively injection locked to resonant frequencies of the PBC by optical feedback, a power build-up factor greater than 5000 and internal power storage of over 100 W were achieved. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of a power build-up cavity pumped by a passively frequency locked AR coated laser diode in the violet-blue wavelength region. In an implementation of this method, limit of detection (LOD) of 0.7 ppm for hydrogen gas at atmospheric pressure was successfully demonstrated in a 1-minute measurement. This system is simple and robust without any electronic controls, suitable for compact field applications.

title: Effect of Interpupillary Distance Mismatch on Distance and Orientation Perception in Action Space across HMDs
authors: Hunter C. Finney, Maggie K. McCracken, Eric Zhuo, Sarah H. Creem-Regehr, Jeanine K. Stefanucci
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Historically, distances are underestimated in virtual environments, and a mismatch between a participant's inter-pupillary distance (IPD) and the inter-axial distance (IAD) of the head-mounted display (HMD) could contribute to underestimation, but prior work has not examined how this interacts with lens type or target location in the visual field. Prior work testing the effects of IPD mismatch on distance perception in immersive virtual environments (IVEs) shows mixed results for distances within 2-10 meters. One limitation is that this work did not consider the variety of optical lenses employed in different HMDs. The lens type could affect distance perception differently depending on IPD and could affect it even more for targets appearing on the sides of the display rather than in front of the user. We examined whether the HMD type and location of targets in the virtual visual field affect perception when IPD is mismatched and matched. Using a blind walking task, participants judged the distance to targets that were either in front or to the side of the participant. One group viewed targets in the Varjo XR-3 and the other viewed them in the HTC Vive Pro. We found that distances were judged to be farther in the periphery than in front of users for both devices. Only in the HTC Vive Pro did an increase in IPD mismatch result in increased distance estimates for targets presented directly in front of the participant. Directional angular error (error observed in orienting to the target) increased as the target angle increased for both devices. However, in the Varjo XR-3, a higher IPD mismatch resulted in greater angular error when viewing targets to the side. Overall, the findings show that a mismatched IPD can affect depth and orientation judgments depending on target position in the display. However, there are differences in both FOVandresolution that could influence distance and orientation perception. A second experiment was conducted to examine whether these factors contributed to perception. To test for this, we down-sampled the Varjo XR-3 to match the horizontal field of view and resolution of the HTC Vive Pro. We found that orientation error was mostly affected by resolution and FOV as the adjusted Varjo XR-3 yielded similar orientation perception as the HTC Vive Pro. With this knowledge, researchers should consider HMDs, IPD, and viewing area in the display as possible influences on distance and orientation perception in order to make VR accessible and accurate to all users.

title: DOA Estimation Exploiting Moving Sparse Uniform Linear Array With Coprime Frequencies
authors: Long Zhang, Sheng Wang, Chengcheng Liu, Guan Gui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: This paper presents an innovative aperture synthesis strategy for moving sparse arrays with coprime frequencies, effectively integrating array motion and frequency diversity to significantly enhance the virtual array aperture. The proposed synthesis method achieves a substantial increase in virtual array elements compared to existing sparse array aperture synthesis techniques. A closed-form expression for the distributions of the synthetic virtual array is derived to characterize its structural properties. Building on this foundation, a direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation method is developed within a group sparse framework, effectively leveraging the phase differences induced by coprime frequencies. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing aperture synthesis techniques in both DOA estimation accuracy and achievable degrees of freedom (DOFs), highlighting its superiority for high-resolution array signal processing.

title: Two-Stage Framework for Efficient UAV-Based Wildfire Video Analysis with Adaptive Compression and Fire Source Detection
authors: Yanbing Bai, Rui-Yang Ju, Lemeng Zhao, Junjie Hu, Jianchao Bi, Erick Mas, Shunichi Koshimura
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have become increasingly important in disaster emergency response by facilitating aerial video analysis. Due to the limited computational resources available on UAVs, large models cannot be run efficiently for on-board analysis. To overcome this challenge, we propose a lightweight and efficient two-stage framework for wildfire monitoring and fire source detection on UAV platforms. Specifically, in Stage 1, we utilize a policy network to identify and discard redundant video clips, thereby reducing computational costs. We also introduce a station point mechanism that incorporates future frame information within the sequential policy network to improve prediction accuracy. This mechanism allows Stage 1 to operate in a near-real-time manner. In Stage 2, for frames classified as containing fire, we apply an improved YOLOv8 model to accurately localize the fire source in real-time on selected frames. We evaluate Stage 1 using the FLAME and HMDB51 datasets, and Stage 2 using the Fire & Smoke Detection Dataset. Experimental results show that our method significantly reduces computational costs while maintaining classification accuracy in Stage 1, and achieves high detection accuracy with real-time inference in Stage 2.

title: Statistical Horizontal Squeezing Transform and Its Application in Reflection Interface Identification of Deep Carbonate Reservoirs
authors: Yunli Fei, Hui Chen, Ying Hu, Xuping Chen, Yutao Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Carbonate reservoirs exhibit complex internal structures and strong heterogeneity. Their seismic responses commonly show multicomponent superposition and pronounced nonstationary characteristics, which readily induce inter-component interference in time-frequency (TF) representations and pose challenges to the characterization of reflection interfaces. To address this issue, we propose a statistical horizontal squeezing transform (SHST) that reformulates group delay (GD) estimation from a pointwise derivative operation to a statistical estimation problem within local TF neighborhoods. Specifically, a statistical group delay estimator (SGDE) is constructed by introducing a weighted short-time Fourier transform (STFT) spectrum, allowing GD to be inferred through local TF statistical consistency rather than single-point gradients. This strategy enhances robustness against component interference and stabilizes energy reassignment. Experiments on synthetic signals and simulated geological models demonstrate that SHST achieves superior TF concentration and robustness under conditions of closely spaced multicomponent signals and noise. Applications to real deep carbonate seismic data further verify the effectiveness of the proposed method in characterizing the time-depth distribution of reflection interfaces, providing a reliable TF analysis tool for seismic interpretation of complex carbonate reservoirs.

title: Software-Defined Reconfigurable Underwater Wireless Optical Communication System with Laser Array Modulation and Deep Learning Demodulation
authors: Tianchen Tong, Guanjun Gao, Jialiang Zhang, Ziqi Ma, Yonggang Guo, Shanguo Huang
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC) systems face an important trade-off between transmission rate and link robustness, which is further exacerbated by channel attenuation and the nonlinearity of opto-electronic conversion. To address these challenges, we propose a software-defined reconfigurable UWOC (SDR-UWOC) system based on laser diode (LD) arrays for optical superimposition without complex electrical digital-to-analog converters (DACs). The system employs a unified hardware platform that supports dynamic modulation and data rate switching between power-superimposed on-off keying (OOK) for robust communication and signal-superimposed pulse amplitude modulation with four levels (PAM4) for high-speed transmission. To mitigate optical device nonlinearities and channel impairments, we develop a hybrid convolutional neural network and long short-term memory (CNN-LSTM) demodulation scheme that combines spatial feature extraction with temporal sequence modeling. Experimental validation over a 1-meter water channel with controlled attenuation demonstrates that OOK superimposition improves attenuation tolerance by 3.1 dB compared to single-LD operation. In PAM4 mode, the optical combination of three LDs extends the transmitter's linear operating region by approximately 2.4-fold and the CNN-LSTM demodulation algorithm achieves a 0.8 dB improvement in attenuation tolerance compared to fixed-threshold decisions. Through software-defined reconfiguration between each modulation mode, the system achieves a 6.9 dB improvement in attenuation tolerance and extends the usable transmission rate range seven-fold (100-700 Mbps) compared to fixed-modulation baselines. This work provides a feasible solution for dynamically balancing data rate and link robustness in complex underwater optical communication networks, showing promising improvements in both link performance and transmission rate flexibility.

title: Automated Chili Leaf Disease Classification Using a ResNet50-Based Convolutional Neural Network for Sustainable Food Production
authors: Afnan Afnan, Cheng Chen, Putri Nazwa Safira, Maulisa Oktiana, Melinda Melinda
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Sustainable food production, as emphasized in the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal 2 (Zero Hunger), requires timely and accurate detection of plant diseases to reduce crop losses and improve agricultural productivity. This paper presents an automated chili leaf disease classification system based on a ResNet50 convolutional neural network to support early disease identification in chili cultivation. Manual inspection of chili leaves is labor-intensive and prone to error due to the small size and visual similarity of leaf symptoms across different diseases. To address this challenge, a novel dataset consisting of 1,500 chili leaf images (after augmentation) was constructed, covering five leaf-condition classes: curly leaf, spotted leaf, whitefly-infected leaf, yellow virus leaf, and healthy leaf. The ResNet50 model was trained and evaluated under varying learning rates (10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> and 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup>) and training epochs ranging from 20 to 100 to assess its classification performance. Experimental results indicate that the ResNet50 configuration with a learning rate of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> and 100 training epochs achieves the best performance, yielding an accuracy of 99.3%, precision of 99.4%, recall of 99.4%, and F1-score of 99.2% on the constructed dataset. These findings confirm the effectiveness of deep learning–based image classification for automated agricultural inspection and highlight its potential contribution to sustainable food production through enhanced crop health monitoring and early disease detection.

title: Quadruped Robot Prototype Based on Artificial Intelligence Using Deep Reinforcement Learning Through the ROS Framework
authors: Juan Soto, Gerson La Rosa Lama, Jesús Izquierdo Arcaya
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a quadruped robot prototype controlled through deep reinforcement learning, implemented using the Robot Operating System (ROS). The system is structured into modular nodes for sensor acquisition, control, and actuation. Control models based on Q–Table and Deep Q–Network (DQN) were developed and trained in Gym environments for tasks such as posture adjustment and translation. Experimental results demonstrate that DQN offers better adaptability and performance in complex scenarios. The integration of ROS, reinforcement learning, and visual perception provides a scalable solution for autonomous robots in unstructured environments.

title: A Mathematical Review and Improvement of the Multifractal Analysis
authors: Fawaz Hjouj, Mohamed Soufiane Jouini
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a focused mathematical study of multifractal analysis, building on previous applications to reservoir rock characterization. Here, we revisit the theoretical foundations by summarizing key multifractal measures and classification descriptors that quantify the irregularity and scaling behavior of heterogeneous structures. A logistic-based parametric model for the generalized dimension function is then introduced, providing a smooth, analytic representation that complements traditional estimation methods. This compact model regularizes discrete numerical outputs, enables closed-form derivation of related multifractal functions, and supports comparative interpretation of different systems. To validate its utility, we design controlled two-dimensional test measures using classical multinomial cascades. The results demonstrate the model’s ability to capture known multifractal properties and key descriptors such as spectrum width, asymmetry, and skewness. Overall, this study offers a practical analytic framework that enhances the interpretability and classification of multifractal spectra in a fully general mathematical setting, providing a solid basis for future applications to porous media, digital rock physics, and other complex heterogeneous systems where robust multifractal characterization is needed.

title: Variable Stiffness Spring Leg With Human-Driven Stiffness Adaptation
authors: Tiange Zhang, David J. Braun
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: The bicycle transmission enables effortless gear shifting to amplify the force produced by human limbs, but it cannot amplify limb power. Conventional variable stiffness springs, placed in series or parallel with human limbs, can amplify both force and power by increasing stiffness. However, amplifying power requires energy proportional to the amount of energy stored in the spring. Here, we present a human-driven robot leg that functions as an energetically conservative variable stiffness spring. Using a pedaling mechanism, the leg stores energy supplied by the human and allows stiffness to be increased before the stored energy is released. This approach amplifies both force and power, analogous to shifting gears while pedaling a bicycle. The prototype confirms force amplification, power amplification via stiffness modulation, and low-cost stiffness adjustment, demonstrating the potential for human-driven robot exoskeletons that employ a pedaling mechanism to enable human energy input and exceed biological force and power limits during energy release.

title: AgriGS-SLAM: Orchard Mapping Across Seasons Via Multi-View Gaussian Splatting SLAM
authors: Mirko Usuelli, David Rapado-Rincon, Gert Kootstra, Matteo Matteucci
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Autonomous robots in orchards require real-time 3D scene understanding despite repetitive row geometry, seasonal appearance changes, and wind-driven foliage motion. We present AgriGS-SLAM, a Visual–LiDAR SLAM framework that couples direct LiDAR odometry and loop closures with multi-camera 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) rendering. Batch rasterization across complementary viewpoints recovers orchard structure under occlusions, while a unified gradient-driven map lifecycle executed between keyframes preserves fine details and bounds memory. Pose refinement is guided by a probabilistic LiDAR-based depth consistency term, back-propagated through the camera projection to tighten geometry-appearance coupling. We validate the system on a field platform in apple and pear orchards across dormancy, flowering, and harvesting, using a standardized trajectory protocol that evaluates both training-view and novel-view synthesis to reduce 3DGS overfitting in evaluation. Across seasons and sites, AgriGS-SLAM delivers sharper, more stable reconstructions and steadier trajectories than recent state-of-the-art 3DGS-based SLAM methods while maintaining real-time performance on-tractor. While demonstrated in orchard monitoring, the approach can be applied to other outdoor domains requiring robust multimodal perception.

title: Distributed Direct Position Determination of Moving Sources in Asynchronous Sensor Networks
authors: Bowen Ding, Dan Song, Weijie Wang, Wei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Existing direct position determination (DPD) methods for moving radiation sources based on time difference of arrival (TDOA) and frequency difference of arrival (FDOA) typically assume synchronous sensor networks, rendering them unsuitable for asynchronous networks affected by clock and frequency biases. To address this limitation and extend applicability to large-scale sensor networks, this paper proposes a distributed DPD algorithm for moving sources in asynchronous sensor networks based on calibration sources. To achieve distributed global optimization of parameters including clock and frequency offsets between neighboring sensors as well as the position and velocity of the source, this study introduces a distributed multiple particle filtering (MPF) framework. Utilizing the coarse estimates provided by the distributed MPF, the algorithm then applies a diffusion-based gradient method to obtain the refined estimates. Throughout the entire process, the radiation source is regarded as an increasingly reliable calibration source. The algorithm jointly utilizes information from the radiation source and the calibration sources to estimate clock and frequency biases. Meanwhile, information exchange in the algorithm is restricted to neighboring sensors, eliminating the need for multi-hop transmission. Experimental results demonstrate consistent convergence of the estimated parameters across sensors and validate the superior performance of the proposed algorithm, even under challenging conditions of low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and limited observation duration.

title: Word-Line-Aware Garbage Collector for QLC-based NAND Flash Memories
authors: Cassiano Silva de Campes, Rodrigo Marques de Figueiredo, Sandro José Rigo
publish: IEEE Latin America Transactions
abstract: The Garbage Collector (GC) on NAND Flash memories is one of the most expensive operations in modern Solid-State Drives (SSD). However, it is essential to claim more free pages on SSDs. Various researches attempt to reduce the penalty of GC operations at different levels of the Input/Output (IO) path. Nevertheless, within the reviewed related works in Host-side, Open-Channel SSDs, and Device-side areas, we did not observe overlap with our research scope in how the logical pages are scattered in the NAND Word-Lines (WL) at the physical level, and how they impact performance degradation during the Garbage Collection operation. To reduce this bridge gap, we propose a new GC policy: Word-Line Aware Garbage Collector (WLA-GC). The WLA-GC focuses on reducing the Garbage Collector overhead by observing the physical distribution of valid logical pages within the WLs of a NAND Flash block to best select the victim blocks to be erased. Based on these criteria, the GC decisions are made by considering the latency the victim block will take to be fully erased. Analytical modeling shows that the proposed method outperforms the Vanilla GC in all cases, with an average performance of 25.4%. In comparison, the experimental results present a performance improvement of 55% in best-case scenarios.

title: From Pixels to Touch: Direct Tactile Servoing with Learned Photometric Normalization
authors: Lluis Prior Sancho, Tommaso Belvedere, Marco Tognon
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Vision-Based Tactile Sensors (VBTSs) offer highresolution contact information essential for robust robotic contactrich manipulation under occlusions and variable lighting. Direct Visual Servoing (DVS) is an interesting alternative to classical Position-Based Visual Servoing (PBVS) as it operates directly on raw pixel intensities, without requiring feature extraction and simplifying the control pipeline. Applying DVS to VBTSs is a challenge because of the sensors' strong position-dependent lighting effects that violate the brightness-constancy assumption that DVS requires. In this work, we introduce a Direct Tactile Servoing (DTS) framework that adapts the principles of DVS to VBTSs. Our approach integrates a deep convolutional UNet that maps raw RGB tactile images to spatially normalized grayscale representations, restoring photometric consistency while preserving fine contact features. We further conduct targeted ablation studies to identify training strategies that balance generalization performance and data efficiency. This learned normalization enables the direct use of DVS control laws without explicit pose or force estimation. Experiments on a robotic manipulator equipped with a GelSight Mini sensor validate robust and reactive closed-loop tactile servoing across diverse contact conditions.

title: Impedimetric immunosensor for detection of PBP2a, a biomarker of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
authors: Natália Q. F Mota, Sara C. Santos, Elenice Deffune, Marli L. De Moraes
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Staphylococcus aureus is a lethal bacterium and the causative agent of infections. Rapid detection of methicillin‑resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is essential to ensure appropriate treatment, but current methods, although sensitive, are often expensive or slow. Biosensors emerge as low-cost technologies for fast and selective MRSA detection. This paper presents a proof-of-concept comparative study for an impedimetric immunosensor to detect penicillin-binding protein 2a (PBP2a), a biomarker of MRSA, using different polyelectrolytes for antibody immobilization, polyethyleneimine (PEI), chitosan (Chi) or silk fibroin (SF). Using Layer-by-Layer technique, one polyelectrolyte layer was deposited over interdigitated gold electrodes, followed by a monoclonal antibody layer. PBP2a was employed as MRSA antigens and Escherichia coli as the selectivity control. Atomic Force Microscopy was applied for surface characterization and Electrical Impedance Spectroscopy evaluated immunosensor performance. Nyquist plot analysis showed significant impedance changes in the positive control of the SF-based device compared to the negative control and selectivity group. Principal Component Analysis showed stronger group segregation for the SF system with 99.2% of data variability captured by two principal components. The SF-functionalized immunosensor presented higher detection ability and selectivity against a non-target Gram-negative bacterium when compared to Chi and PEI and detected PBP2a in 15 minutes.

title: Resilience Evaluation and Recovery Strategies for IoT-Enabled Unmanned System-of-Systems under GNSS Spoofing Attacks
authors: Dingchen She, Zhiwei Chen, Min Chang, Bo Feng, Junqiang Bai
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT)-enabled unmanned system-of-systems (IUSoS) is increasingly threatened by Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) spoofing attacks, which cause positional deviations in unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and compromise system security. Thus, it is urgent to develop effective countermeasures against GNSS spoofing attacks. However, existing research lacks a comprehensive framework for quantifying IUSoS’s resilience and enabling performance recovery under GNSS spoofing attacks. To address these gaps, this paper incorporates a resilience mechanism into the anti-GNSS spoofing scenario of the IUSoS and investigates its resilience evaluation and recovery strategies. Firstly, a multi-layer IUSoS architecture under GNSS spoofing attacks is proposed to comprehensively describe the system components and their closed-loop operation responses. Subsequently, a three-phase resilience framework designed for the anti-GNSS spoofing scenario is developed, consisting of the resistance, adaptation, and recovery phases. Resilience metrics based on UAV position deviations are then established to assess the IUSoS’s capability against GNSS spoofing. Moreover, a hierarchical recovery strategy integrating neighbor-assisted multilateration and position prediction is presented to enhance the IUSoS’s performance recovery effectiveness under GNSS spoofing attacks. Finally, case studies involving multiple UAV swarms validate the efficacy of the proposed resilience evaluation and recovery strategies. The results provide a reference for ensuring the secure operation and resilience design of the IUSoS under GNSS spoofing attacks.

title: A Topology-aware Quantum Inspired Genetic Algorithm for Secure Quantum Communication
authors: Saumya Priyadarshini, Chandrashekar Jatoth, Rajesh Doriya, Rajkumar Buyya
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Quantum communication ensures that data remains secure from unauthorized access. Despite these advantages, this technology faces several challenges. For instance, photons can be lost when they go through channels, and there is a chance of eavesdropping, that make the system not as trustworthy, safe, and efficient. To make quantum communication systems function effectively in the real world, we need to combine advanced technologies like quantum error correction mechanisms and quantum repeaters. This work suggests a unified Quantum Inspired Genetic Algorithm (QIGA) framework that utilizes collaborative quantum repeaters to conquer the limitations of network links over distance. This study introduces a hybrid optimization topology (OT) that integrates mesh, star, and ring configurations for measurement device-independent quantum key distribution (MDI-QKD) networks. This approach allows for the efficient use of resources for scalable quantum communication networks. The proposed network architecture integrates 12 user nodes, a trusted node, and a single shared quantum repeater within a hybrid topology. The repeater deployment has been designed to support links that are longer than 52 km. This design accommodates deployments on various scales, small, medium, and large, while optimizing resource usage and infrastructure costs. We evaluated the performance and security trade offs in quantum communication networks by optimizing three topologies with a QIGA: ring, ring-star, and ring-star-mesh hybrid (QIGA−OT). The optimization methodology aims to reconcile essential performance metrics, such as the Secure Key Rate (SKR), Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), channel loss, and Merit of Quantum Efficiency (MQE). MQE functions as a metric for evaluating trades concerning security, efficiency, and cost-effectiveness within the network. This work presents a novel approach for utilizing quantum-inspired in the effective design and implementation of scalable network topologies across numerous fields of application.

title: Deep Learning–Enabled Multisource Partial Discharge Diagnostics for Online Monitoring of High Voltage Rotating Machine Insulation
authors: Aryan Raj, Shobhan Barua, J.C. Pandey, Arup Kumar Das, Sivaji Chakravorti
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: In this paper, a deep learning–based approach is proposed to identify single and multiple source partial discharge (PD) activities in high-voltage rotating machine insulation employing high-frequency current transformer (HFCT) measurements. To this end, HFCT-acquired PD signals are converted to phase-resolved partial discharge (PRPD) patterns. A comprehensive PRPD database is developed through controlled laboratory experiments involving different insulation materials and discharge mechanisms. Thereafter, a lightweight custom ConvNeXt model is designed for automated feature extraction and classification of the PRPD pattern. The proposed custom ConvNeXt model achieves an overall classification accuracy of 98.3%. Comparative studies with other deep learning models further validate the effectiveness and computational efficiency of the proposed approach. Hence, the proposed method is potentially worthy for online condition monitoring of high-voltage rotating machines.

title: An Affordable Robotic Manipulation Platform with Flexible Gripper and Vision for AI-Driven Control
authors: Ismael Lopez-Juarez, Kieran I Lopez-Valadez, Roman Osorio-Comparan, Luis A. Castillo-Barrientos, Daniel Arreguin-Jasso
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a low-cost, vision-guided robotic system for automated object recognition and manipulation. The platform integrates a MyCobot 280 Pi robotic arm with a custom 3D-printed, stepperactuated gripper featuring compliant TPU fingers. A fixed overhead camera provides real-time visual feedback, enabling HSV-based color segmentation and homographic transformation for accurate object localization within a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$400 \text{mm} \times 400 \text{mm}$</tex> workspace. Experimental results validate the system's ability to reliably detect, grasp, and relocate colored objects. Designed for flexibility and extensibility, the prototype supports research in AI-enhanced robotics, including one-shot learning with large language models (lLMs), tactile sensing, and closed-loop control strategies. Potential application areas include educational robotics, lab automation, light manufacturing, and AI-driven manipulation research.

title: HemoCell-Det: An Efficient Deep Learning-Based Algorithm for Blood Cell Detection
authors: Han Shu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Red blood cells are essential in hematological research and clinical diagnosis, yet automated detection remains challenging due to diverse morphologies, complex backgrounds, and frequent overlap, especially for small or partially occluded cells. To address these issues, we propose HemoCell-Det, an efficient YOLOv12-based red blood cell detection framework that integrates three enhancements: a Multi-Scale Hemocyte Detection (MSHD) module for robust multi-level feature fusion, a Dynamic Cell State Selection Mechanism (DCSSM) to suppress background noise via adaptive attention, and a Small-Target Hemocyte Detection enhancement (SGHD) module to improve recognition of small, blurred, and overlapping cells. Experiments on a red blood cell image dataset show that HemoCell-Det achieves 96.7% mAP, outperforming mainstream detectors and demonstrating strong accuracy and robustness for complex blood image analysis.

title: Direct Model Predictive Control in a Double-Input Single-Output DC-DC Converter in PV Applications
authors: Diego Rojas, Duberney Murillo-Yarce, Freddy Flores-Bahamonde
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Single-inductor (SI) multiport DC-DC converters have gained importance due to the reduction in the number of inductors required in distributed DC-DC converter applications. However, because of their multivariable nature, the issue of cross-coupling arises, complicating the design of the control strategy. This paper presents a direct model predictive control (MPC) for regulating the voltages of the photovoltaic modules connected to each input port and another MPC for regulating the voltage of one input port and the output port. The results demonstrate a fast dynamic response, with a settling time under 1 ms, in the voltage of each photovoltaic module, achieved without cross-coupling. This enables rapid Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) under varying environmental conditions. It also demonstrates smooth transitions between different control loop changes, proving the effectiveness of the direct predictive control strategy.

title: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers in Brazilian News Classification of a Public Security Agency
authors: Thiago R. Lobo, Karla S. Silva, Claudia A. Martins, Raphael S. R. Gomes, Anderson C. S. Oliveira
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: As part of the institution's 2024-2027 strategic plan, which includes the objective of understanding how the media portrays the organization to strengthen its public image, this work aims to investigate the application of a combination of Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) models, pre-trained in Portuguese, to classify and evaluate news headlines about a public security institution. This classification took place in three positions: neutral, negative, and positive. This study seeks to collaborate with the organization's strategic objectives by presenting the model with the highest metrics during the analysis. In this way, BERT-Large stands out, with an F1 Score of 91.62 % in the model's general metrics and an equally high result compared to the others during the classification of headlines into classes.

title: Adaptive Access Control for Preventing Credential-Based Data Exfiltration in Cloud Analytics Platforms
authors: Abhilash Guntupalli, Tirumala Bala Koteswara Rao Kamisetty
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Credential compromise has become the primary attack vector for breaching cloud analytics systems, with 83 % of cloud breaches in 2024 involving valid credentials. Traditional access control treats authentication as a one-time decision, granting static trust for the entire session duration. This creates a fundamental blind spot: attackers with stolen credentials are indistinguishable from legitimate users. We present the Adaptive Access Control Model (AACM) to address this gap. AACM continuously evaluates trust at every data access request through three mechanisms: ongoing identity assurance scoring, policy enforcement with cumulative volume limits, and trust adaptation based on behavioral feedback. Our formal security analysis, under explicit architectural assumptions, demonstrates that data exposure is bounded by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$E \leq \min \left(C, \alpha \cdot T \cdot R^{-\beta}\right)$</tex>, where <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C$</tex> is a policy-configured cumulative cap providing a hard ceiling on extraction. Compared against five existing frameworks, AACM uniquely provides both credential compromise resilience and bounded worst-case exposure while keeping the system usable for legitimate users.

title: SIGNASS 2026: List of papers
authors: N/A

title: Gmw-Yolo: Enhanced Vehicle and Pedestrian Detection in Foggy Conditions
authors: Jian Wang, Guanghai Zheng
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: To address the challenges of object detection in foggy environments-such as difficult target perception, severe occlusion, and frequent false and missed detections caused by low visibility-we propose GMW-YOLO, a fog-oriented object detection algorithm based on YOLOv11. Firstly, we design a Global Edge Information Aggregation (GEIA) module that integrates edge cues into multi-scale feature representations, enhancing the network's attention to object boundaries. Secondly, aiming at the limitation of fixed receptive fields in conventional convolution, which impairs the simultaneous extraction of small-object fine details and global information, we propose a MultiScale Receptive Field Sharing Convolution (MSRFConv) module. It achieves efficient and consistent information aggregation spanning local details to global receptive fields in single-scale features, and effectively strengthens the feature representation of small objects.Finally, Wise-IoU v3 is adopted as the regression loss, where its dynamic weight adjustment and boundary-sensitive optimization mechanism effectively mitigate boundary misalignment and reduce regression errors for small targets. Experimental results on the RTTS dataset demonstrate that, compared with YOLOv11, GMW-YOLO achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0. 7 \%}$</tex> mAP@50 and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 6. 7 \%}$</tex> mAP@50:95, improving by 3.0 and 3.3 percentage points, respectively, thereby validating the enhanced adaptability and effectiveness of the proposed method in foggy scenarios.

title: Non-Linear Droop Control for Islanded Grid-Forming Inverters Supplying Residential Air Conditioners
authors: Andres Salazar
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: During the startup phase of standard residential air-conditioning (RAC) units, a significant inrush current can compromise the stability of islanded grid-forming (GFm) inverter systems. Failure to support these high-power demands may result in system collapse. This paper introduces a time-varying phasor model for a GFm inverter employing a nonlinear voltage droop control strategy, specifically designed to facilitate reliable RAC startups while achieving superior voltage regulation compared to conventional linear droop methods. The proposed model serves as an effective tool for optimizing droop coefficient selection and analyzing system transient responses. Simulations are presented to validate the model and demonstrate the enhanced performance and robustness of the nonlinear droop control approach.

title: From Concept to Creation: Harnessing AI for Rapid Innovation and MVP Delivery
authors: Aparna Krishna Penumatcha
publish: 2025 IEEE Women in Engineering (WIE) Forum USA East
abstract: Light speed development and Minimum Viable Product (MVP)-driven innovation have become the focus of modern product and software engineering as organizations seek quicker time-to-market and swift market confirmation. In that regard, artificial intelligence (AI) has become one of the primary facilitators of improving concept-to-creation pipelines through the automated ideation process, requirement engineering, design, development, testing, deployment, and evaluation. The review is a synthesis of recent literature that aims to explore how AI changes the traditional innovation life-cycles to data-driven, adaptive, and continuous processes that help deliver MVP's quickly. The results show that AI can dramatically enhance the pace of development, its cost-efficiency, and its accuracy in decisions, as well as allow accelerated iteration and validation of evidence-based ones. Nonetheless, it is also stated in the review that there are persistent knowledge gaps in the area of explainability, governance, long-term quality, and human-AI collaboration in MVP scenarios. On these gaps, future research recommendations have been put across, such as the creation of integrated AI-based innovation models, standardized assessment tools, and responsible AI implementation. Holistically, the review offers a unified outlook of AI-based rapid innovation and identifies its strategic opportunities in developing sustainable MVP's.

title: Research and Design of Electronic Data Evidence Preservation System for Materials Supply Chain Oriented to Multiple Trusted Sources
authors: Ke Yang, Yafei Liu, Junsheng Wang, Qinglei Guo, Hufang Ye, Xiukui Pan
publish: 2025 2nd World Conference on Computer and Information Security (WCCIS)
abstract: At present, the storage and verification methods of electronic data in the material supply chain mostly rely on a single trusted source, making it difficult to ensure the credibility of electronic data in application scenarios such as authentication, auditing, and traceability for multiple internal and external entities of enterprises. Based on this, this paper proposes a multitrusted source-oriented electronic data evidence preservation method and system for material supply chains. Firstly, conduct a comprehensive analysis of the electronic data evidence preservation requirements throughout the entire material supply chain process from aspects such as data types and internal and external application scenarios. Secondly, a quantitative assessment method for the trust strength authentication of electronic data based on multi-dimensional feature modeling and an electronic data-trusted source matching mechanism based on quantitative grading are proposed, providing a basis for the on-demand evidence storage of data. Finally, for the enterprise material supply chain management business, this paper designs the business architecture and functional architecture of the electronic data evidence storage system. This paper has established a new model for electronic data management and authentication in the material supply chain, which holds significant reference value for effectively addressing new legal disputes in the information age and promoting the intelligent and green development of the material supply chain.

title: Reliable mmWave LoS/NLoS Classification via Large Wireless Model Embeddings
authors: Quazi Rian Hasnaine, Eslam Hasan, Mohamed I. Ismail, Rhana Elsayed, Zubair Md Fadlullah, Muhammad Ismail, Mostafa M. Fouda
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The evolution toward 6 G networks necessitates ultra-reliable operation in millimeter-wave (mmWave) and subterahertz bands, where propagation is fundamentally constrained by high path loss and susceptibility to blockage. Consequently, accurate prediction of Line-of-Sight (LoS) and Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) is critical for effective beam management and link adaptation. This paper proposes a real-world LoS/NLoS prediction framework using the DeepSense 6 G dataset and investigates the benefits of transformer-based channel embeddings generated by the large wireless model (LWM). We evaluate a broad set of machine learning (ML) algorithms-including K-nearest neighbors (KNN), support vector machine (SVM), random forest (RF), eXtreme gradient boosting (XGBoost), categorical boosting (CatBoost), light gradient boosting machine (LightGBM), and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) using LWM-based classification embeddings. Experimental results show that LWM embeddings substantially enhance separability between LoS and NLoS links, enabling high-capacity models to capture complex multipath characteristics that raw features fail to represent. The MLP achieves the best performance, with 98.3% accuracy and an area under the curve (AUC) above 0.996, outperforming other machine learning (ML) models by capturing the non-linearity in dynamic 6G networks. Our proposed LWM-based prediction model outperforms state-of-the-art models by more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$9 \%, 15 \%$</tex>, and 12% in LoS, NLoS, and average scenarios, respectively, in terms of accuracy. These results demonstrate the importance of foundation-model-based representations and real-world data for reliable link-state prediction in emerging 6G networks.

title: A Leakage Current-Suppressed K/Ka-Band Bidirectional Amplifier for Satellite Communication Applications
authors: Chenxi Zhao, Shaoping Zhang, Qingfeng Zhang, Huihua Liu, Yiming Yu, Yunqiu Wu, Kai Kang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: After analyzing the causes and propagation paths of current leakage in conventional bidirectional amplifiers, this article proposes a novel bidirectional amplifier architecture to address this critical leakage issue. As the core anti-leakage design, cross-coupled capacitors are integrated to precisely suppress power leakage between the two differential paths. Furthermore, the collaborative design of the amplification unit’s optimal noise source impedance and load impedance significantly enhances the amplifier’s output efficiency and noise performance. To validate the proposed architecture, a prototype is fabricated using a commercial 55-nm SOI CMOS process for K/Ka-band satellite communication (SATCOM) applications, with a core area of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.22~\text {mm}^{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Experimental results confirm that the amplifier achieves superior performance in both LNA and PA modes, fully verifying the feasibility and superiority of the proposed architecture. In LNA mode, it delivers a peak gain of 19.2 dB (18.5–20.5 GHz, 2.0-GHz 3-dB gain bandwidth), a minimum noise figure (NF) of 3.5 dB, and an input 1-dB compression point (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$IP_{1dB}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) ranging from −11.5 to −9.5 dBm. In PA mode, continuous-wave (CW) measurements demonstrate a peak gain of 18.4 dB (23.9–28.4 GHz, 4.5-GHz 3-dB gain bandwidth), a satu-rated output power (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$P_{sat}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) of 17.0 dBm, and a peak power-added efficiency (PAE) of 33.1% at 28 GHz. For 28-GHz modulated signals (100 MSym/s 64-QAM, 7.1-dB peak-to-average power ratio), it achieves an average output power (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$P_{avg}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) of 9.31 dBm, an adjacent channel leakage ratio (ACLR) of −31.4 dBc, and an error vector magnitude (EVM) of −26.4 dB.

title: Impact of Nonlinear Power Amplifier on Massive MIMO: Machine Learning Prediction Under Realistic Radio Channel
authors: Marcin Hoffmann, Pawel Kryszkiewicz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Massive Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (M-MIMO) is one of the crucial technologies for increasing spectral and energy efficiency of wireless networks. Most of the current works assume that M-MIMO arrays are equipped with a linear front end. However, ongoing efforts to make wireless networks more energy-efficient push the hardware to the limits, where its nonlinear behavior appears. This is especially a common problem for the multicarrier systems, e.g., Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM) used in 4 G, 5 G, and possibly also in 6 G, which is characterized by a high Peak-to-Average Power Ratio (PAPR). While the impact of a nonlinear Power Amplifier (PA) on an OFDM signal is well characterized, it is a relatively new topic for the M-MIMO OFDM systems. Most of the recent works either neglect nonlinear effects or utilize simplified models proper for Rayleigh or Line of Sight (LoS) radio channel models. In this paper, we first theoretically characterize the nonlinear distortion in the M-MIMO system under commonly used radio channel models. Then, utilizing 3D-Ray Tracing (3D-RT) software, we demonstrate that these models are not very accurate. Instead, we propose two models: a statistical one and a Machine Learning (ML)-based one using 3D-RT results. The proposed statistical model utilizes the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution to model Signal to Distortion Ratio (SDR) for victim users, receiving nonlinear distortion, e.g., as interference from neighboring cells. The proposed ML model aims to predict SDR for a scheduled user (receiving nonlinear distortion along with the desired signal), based on the spatial characteristics of the radio channel and the operation point of each PA feeding at the M-MIMO antenna array. The predicted SDR can then be used to perform PA-aware per-user power allocation. The results show about 12% median gain in user throughput achieved by the proposed ML-based power allocation scheme over the state-of-the-art, fixed operating point scheme.

title: IEEE Embedded Systems Letters Publication Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Embedded Systems Letters

title: SR-Net: A Heterogeneous Point Clouds Registration Method Based on Deep Learning
authors: Tongtong Lu, Xiaoyong Qiang, Jian Wang, Binfu Ge, Hao Guan, Chaoyang Bian, Fang Huang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: The task of registering heterogeneous point clouds of urban scenes is particularly challenging due to differences in the imaging mechanisms of sensors and the limitations imposed by the conditions under which these images are acquired. The relevant challenges include a large number of outliers, differences in the densities of point clouds of several orders of magnitude, low rates of overlap between them, data scarcity, and large rotational angles that yield severe mismatches in the data distribution. These issues create significant performance-related bottlenecks for traditional methods of point cloud registration as well as those based on feature extraction through deep learning. In response to these issues, this study proposes a framework for registering heterogeneous point clouds called the super-point registration network (SR-Net). SR-Net consists of a pyramid feature extraction module based on KPConv, a super-point feature extraction and matching module, a local feature extraction module, and a local-to-global registration method. To enhance the capability of the model to extract features from heterogeneous point clouds with different resolutions, the authors propose an adaptive Top-K method for point cloud padding. The model incorporates self-attention and cross-attention mechanisms to capture the geometric structure within point clouds and the geometric consistency between them. The geometric information contained in the super-points is encoded through geometric structure embedding. Finally, to compensate for the scarcity of annotated multimodal data, we pre-trained the model on large-scale homologous benchmarks. This enabled it to learn the underlying invariant geometric feature priors across domains that guided it to stably converge on a dataset collected by the authors that involved major differences in the distribution of the features. We comprehensively validated the proposed model through a progressive experimental system (EXP-I∼VII) that covered basic evaluation, ablation, cross-domain generalization, and stress testing for efficiency. SR-Net achieved a relative rotational error (RRE) of 0.24 ° and a registration recall (RR) of 99.8% on the KITTI benchmark. Moreover, it achieved an RR of 96.97%, an RRE of 0.56 °, and a relative translational error (RTE) of 0.92 m on a challenging dataset of heterogeneous point clouds that was collected by the authors, with an average overlap rate of only 7.62% that reflected optimal stability. Our model also yielded an RR of 92.6% and a RTE of 0.31 m in experiments on the Cross3DReg heterologous datasets, significantly surpassing mainstream methods. The final evaluation confirmed that it achieved a high RR of 91.2% on 3DMatch, while optimizing the inferential delay to 85.96 ms. This verifies the high robustness of SR-Net when handling complex tasks of multimodal point cloud registration.

title: A 5G-Based Autonomous Aerial System for Real-Time Anomaly Prevention and Response in Smart Urban Environments
authors: Julio Lucero, Matias Muñoz Catalán, Christian Fernández-Campusano, Jonathan Pereira Mendoza
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This study presents the design, implementation, and experimental validation of an autonomous unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) system for anomaly detection and rapid response in smart cities. The proposed platform integrates a modular opensource hardware/software architecture, real-time embedded artificial intelligence, and hybrid 5G + mesh network connectivity to enable robust and low-latency urban surveillance. The system is composed of a quadcopter with a Pixhawk flight controller, a Raspberry Pi 4 for edge computing, and a Quectel RM520N-GL 5G module, all of which are managed via a cloud-based backend and a responsive web interface. Experimental evaluations-conducted through software-in-the-loop simulations and realworld test flights-demonstrate significant improvements in control responsiveness (5G latency: 49.11 ms vs. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \mathrm{G}: 273.38 \text{ms}$</tex>), flight stability (attitude RMS error <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<5^{\circ}$</tex>), navigation accuracy (cross-track error <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 0.15 \mathrm{m}$</tex>), and energy efficiency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25 \% \text{im}$</tex> provement over WiFi configurations). The integration of lowlatency 5G communication enables real-time control loops within the critical 100 ms threshold, thereby enhancing operational safety and system reactivity. These results validate the proposed system as a cost-effective, scalable, and sovereign technological solution for critical surveillance in complex urban environments.

title: A Hailo-Accelerated System for Precision Agriculture: Real-Time on-Board YOLO Inference on a Drone Platform
authors: Bárbara Vergara, Christian Fernández-Campusano, Hector Kaschel
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Precision agriculture demands real-time solutions for detecting pests and nutritional deficiencies, a task where cloud-based systems falter due to latency and connectivity issues in rural settings. This paper introduces an intelligent agriculture system based on edge computing, designed for on-board deployment on unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). The methodology involved building a platform with a Raspberry Pi 5 and a Hailo-8L AI accelerator. On this hardware, we implemented and benchmarked two models, YOLOv8s and YOLOv10s, which were trained on a custom dataset of agricultural anomalies and then compiled for edge deployment. Their performance was evaluated in two scenarios: operating exclusively on the Raspberry Pi's CPU and utilizing the Hailo-8L hardware accelerator. Key findings reveal that hardware acceleration yields a significant performance increase. Specifically, the system achieves a throughput of up to 27.3 frames per second (FPS)-a 13fold improvement-while reducing inference latency by 91.4 % (from 403.7 ms to 34.7 ms) compared to CPU-only processing. This optimization also reduces the main processor's load from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 99 \%$</tex> to less than 18 %, ensuring thermal stability. The main contribution of this work is providing a robust, quantitative benchmark that validates the viability of this low-power, highperformance architecture, offering a scalable and autonomous solution for real-time agricultural monitoring directly in the field.

title: Is Adversarial Vulnerability a Real Trade-Off in LoRa Fine-Tuned Models?
authors: Ajinkya More, Jonathan Rusert
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods such as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) offer an efficient alternative to full fine-tuning (FFT) for adapting large language models, but their adversarial robustness remains underexplored. We evaluate the robustness of LoRA-tuned models versus FFT models on text classification tasks using several transformer architectures (LLaMA, GPT-Neo, BERT, DistilBERT, ALBERT, RoBERTa) across three benchmark datasets (SST-2, AG-News, IMDB) and three strong textual adversarial attacks (TextFooler, TextBugger, BAE). We also analyze cross-model transfer attacks where attacker and target models differ. Our results show no consistent robustness advantage for FFT over LoRA; in some cases (e.g., TextFooler) accuracy drops to near zero for all models. These findings indicate that LoRA's efficiency gains do not inherently compromise adversarial robustness.

title: Design and Characterization of a Graphene-Based Wideband Fractal Slotted Antenna With Pattern Reconfigurability
authors: Rajesh Yadav, Harshmani Yadav
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: The aim of this article is to design and analyze a graphene-based fractal slotted antenna (GFSA) for high-performance, real-time, pattern-reconfigurable applications in precision agriculture. Multiple layers were utilized for the geometry of the GFSA, including perfect electric conductor (PEC), silicon dioxide (SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>) substrate, silver feedline, and graphene radiating elements. The silver nanostrip feedline is modeled using the Drude approach, vias for connectivity, and fractal-shape graphene radiating arrays which will allow for the tunable multiband operation. The proposed GFSA achieves dynamic pattern reconfigurability through the inherent tunable conductive properties of graphene by selectively enabling or disabling individual radiating arrays. The GFSA operates using seven resonance frequency bands, 0.93, 1.20, 2.00, 2.35, 2.59, 2.72, and 3.15 terahertz (THz) with a total overall operating range of 0.93–3.15 THz with a maximum directivity of 8.02 dBi, a wide fractional bandwidth of 124.75%, maximum gain of 7.64 dB, and a radiation efficiency of 95.3%. Finally, a comprehensive modal analysis was conducted to evaluate the electromagnetic characteristics of the proposed GFSA. The resonance RFs and their respective field cycle patterns were identified through modal analysis along the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$X$</tex-math> </inline-formula>- and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Y$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-axis as <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${M}_{24},{TM}_{46}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${TM}_{6,10}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${TM}_{12,14}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${TM}_{14,16}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${TM}_{16,18}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${TM}_{20,22}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> at 0.93, 1.2, 2.0, 2.35, 2.59, 2.72, and 3.14 THz, respectively. It shows continuous excitation of higher order modes with increasing frequency. This demonstrates that the fractal slot properties can be successfully utilized for excitation of multiple modes, as well as providing additional means for enhancing wideband and multimode performance. The reconfigurable graphene biasing that allows for quick adaptive pattern reconfigurability without using any kind of mechanical device. Thus, making the antenna extremely versatile for adaptive dynamic coverage in agriculture.

title: Design, Fabrication, and Performance of a Multi-Layer Thermal Plant with Digital Control for Precision Timing Research and Development
authors: Neil Furman, Bryan Owings, Todd J. Freeborn
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Active hydrogen MASERs are highly stable atomic clocks that rely on precise temperature control of the instrument and physics package to generate a stable frequency output. Active research efforts are exploring temperature control strategies to improve the long-term frequency stability of these instruments. To test these strategies a thermal plant that realizes the 5 -layers of thermal control of a commercially available active hydrogen MASER has been designed and built. This work presents the design of this thermal plant and demonstrates its functionality with a simple PID control system. Using this control scheme the plant achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 2 ~\mathrm{m}^{\circ} C$</tex> temperature stability in all five temperature controlled layers. Further features of the digital control system to emulate ambient temperature variations for testing/validation are also demonstrated.

title: Advanced Image Encryption with 1-D Group Cellular Automata Techniques
authors: Biswarup Yogi, Ajoy Kumar Khan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: This study presents an image encryption method that uses one-dimensional Group Cellular Automata (1-D GCA). The proposed method combines Cellular Automata with symmetric-key cryptography to protect digital images from unauthorised access. The 1-D GCA generates pseudorandom sequences that change pixel positions and values, providing strong diffusion and confusion. The proposed method is evaluated on the Cameraman and Baboon images. The results show NPCR above 99.6%, UACI near 50%, very low correlation, and entropy close to 8. These values indicate high randomness and strong resistance to statistical attacks. The findings confirm that GCA is effective for image encryption and suitable for future improvements.

title: YOLOv10-DFP: An Improved Model for Small Target Detection in Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Images
authors: Kefeng Li, Shixian Zhao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Small object detection in drone imagery poses a challenge in the field of computer vision due to their tiny size, complex backgrounds, and dense distribution. Although YOLOv10 excels in general object detection, there remains room for improvement in small object detection from drone perspectives. To address this, this paper proposes an enhanced model, YOLOv10-DFP, which significantly improves small object detection capabilities by integrating a dual attention mechanism with a bidirectional feature pyramid network. The backbone network incorporates lightweight convolutional attention modules and fast contextual attention modules to enhance feature representation and contextual awareness. The neck network employs a bidirectional feature fusion strategy to optimize multiscale information flow, while an additional detection head specifically designed for extremely small objects is introduced to improve detection accuracy at high-resolution features. Experiments on the VisDrone dataset demonstrate that the proposed method achieves approximately a 5.7% improvement in mAP@50 compared to the original YOLOv10, outperforming existing mainstream approaches and providing an efficient and reliable solution for small object detection in drone imagery.

title: Fault-Tolerant Predictive Current Control in Nine-Phase Generation System Driven by a Multi-Modular Matrix Converter
authors: Carlos Piris, Bruno Sanabria, Edgar Maqueda, Sergio Toledo, Osvaldo González, Marco Rivera, Gustavo Ojeda, Matias Aguilar, Gianyacomo Zucchini
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper proposes a finite control set model predictive current control (FCS-MPC) strategy for a ninephase generation system driven by a multi-modular matrix converter topology. The studied architecture consists of parallelconnected matrix converter modules, facilitating direct AC-AC power conversion and offering inherent fault-tolerance capabilities by dynamically redistributing load among active modules during contingencies. Comprehensive simulations performed in MATLAB/Simulink validate the proposed method, demonstrating accurate tracking of current references, robust dynamic performance under step changes in load demands, and stable operation during module failure scenarios. The results confirm the suitability and computational effectiveness of the proposed strategy, highlighting its potential application in distributed generation, electric mobility, and aerospace power systems where continuous and reliable operation is critical.

title: Implementing Shor's 9-Qubit Code: Encoding, Decoding, and Correction on IBM and IONQ
authors: Federico Fuidio, Mateo Stipanicic, Marco Zunino, Laura Gatti, Rafael Sotelo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Quantum Error Correction (QEC) is an essential field for the development of Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computation. A foundational approach for QEC is Shor's 9-Qubit Code, which remains a solid starting point for studying quantum circuits' error behavior. In this article we evaluate Shor code's detection and correction procedures applied to encoded logical states, given that this code incorporates a decoding circuit component with self-error-correcting properties. We tested the encoded, decoding, and error-correcting procedures for the Shor's 9-Qubit Code on IBM and IONQ quantum devices. The experiments were carried out in two instances: with and without an intentionally added unitary error. We present an analysis of the resulting error syndromes and decoded states. Results show a better performance by IONQ, probably due to larger decoherence times than IBM.

title: Distributed Robust Route Planning for Liquefied Natural Gas Carriers
authors: Wenhao Yang, Dapeng Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Liquefied natural gas ships sail under unpredictable meteorological conditions and often encounter challenges in vapor management. We have found a robust and low-carbon route planning method. It uses conditional variation auto-encoders to measure Weather forecast errors and build a data-driven uncertainty set. Considering the dynamic balance of vapor, we have constructed a two-stage optimization model to minimize expected costs under worst-case scenarios. The simulation results of the Tianjin-Gladstone route confirmed the effectiveness of this method. It not only ensures navigation safety, but also improves the economy and robustness of the route plan, especially in uncertain environments.

title: Can LLMs Find Bugs in Code? An Evaluation from Beginner Errors to Security Vulnerabilities in Python and C++
authors: Akshay Mhatre, Noujoud Nader, Patrick Diehl, Deepti Gupta
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT-4, Claude 3, and LLaMA 4 are increasingly embedded in software/application development, supporting tasks from code generation to debugging. Yet, their real-world effectiveness in detecting diverse software bugs, particularly complex, security-relevant vulnerabilities, remains underexplored. This study presents a systematic, empirical evaluation of these three leading LLMs using a benchmark of foundational programming errors, classic security flaws, and advanced, production-grade bugs in C++ and Python. The dataset integrates real code from SEED Labs, OpenSSL (via the Suresoft GLaDOS database), and PyBugHive, validated through local compilation and testing pipelines. A novel multi-stage, context-aware prompting protocol simulates realistic debugging scenarios, while a graded rubric measures detection accuracy, reasoning depth, and remediation quality. Our results show that all models excel at identifying syntactic and semantic issues in well-scoped code, making them promising for educational use and as first-pass reviewers in automated code auditing. Performance diminishes in scenarios involving complex security vulnerabilities and large-scale production code, with ChatGPT-4 and Claude 3 generally providing more nuanced contextual analyses than LLaMA 4. This highlights both the promise and the present constraints of LLMs in serving as reliable code analysis tools.

title: Analytically Estimated Cut-Off Frequency as a Decision Criterion Between Hairpin and Conventional Windings
authors: Carlos Gálvez-Araya, Carlos Madariaga-Cifuentes, Cesar Gallardo, Juan A. Tapia
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents an analytical method to estimate alternating current (AC) losses in hairpin windings applied to induction motors operating at high frequencies. Due to their rectangular cross-section and high slot fill factor, hairpin windings enable superior thermal performance and higher current density compared to conventional random windings. However, they are also more susceptible to skin and proximity effects, resulting in higher AC losses at elevated frequencies. Therefore, an analytical model is developed to predict the cut-off frequency, defined as the operating point beyond which hairpin windings exhibit lower AC losses than their conventional counterparts. The model accounts for conductor geometry, slot dimensions, and layer configurations. Finite Element Analysis (FEA) is used to validate the analytical predictions, showing strong agreement across the frequency spectrum. The model exhibits a maximum relative percentage error of 6.5% for a 6-layer winding configuration and 5.7% for an 8-layer configuration, demonstrating its suitability as a good alternative to conventional computational methods.

title: AI-Enhanced Disaster Risk Prediction with Explainable SHAP Analysis: A Multi-Class Classification Approach Using XGBoost
authors: Qiannan Shen, Jing Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Natural disasters pose significant threats to global communities, necessitating advanced predictive frameworks for effective risk assessment and management. This study presents an AI-driven disaster risk prediction system integrating XGBoost machine learning with SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) interpretability analysis. Using the World Risk Index dataset spanning 11 years across 181 countries, we developed multi-class classification models for four key risk indicators: World Risk Index (WRI), Exposure, Vulnerability, and Susceptibility. The XGBoost classifier achieved test accuracies exceeding 0.85 across all categories, with macro-averaged AUC scores ranging from 0.92 to 0.96.
