SHAP analysis revealed critical driving factors influencing disaster susceptibility, demonstrating the interpretability of AIpowered predictions. Our explainable AI framework provides transparent, actionable insights for policymakers and disaster management authorities, bridging the gap between predictive accuracy and decision-making transparency in global risk assessment.

title: Operationalizing Multi-Agent Interoperability via Contract-Driven Model Context Protocols
authors: Aswathnarayan Muthukrishnan Kirubakaran, Vinoth Punniyamoorthy, Adithya Parthasarathy, Akash Kumar Agarwal, Shiva Kumar Reddy Carimireddy, Nachiappan Chockalingam, Akshay Deshpande
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Multi-agent systems powered by large language models are increasingly used to automate complex workflows involving data processing, infrastructure operations, and service orchestration. However, agent-tool interoperability in such systems is often implemented through informal schemas and prompt-embedded conventions, leading to fragile integrations, limited observability, and high recovery costs under interface evolution. This paper presents a contract-driven approach to operationalizing multi-agent interoperability using the Model Context Protocol (MCP). We propose an MCP-mediated architecture that formalizes agent-tool interactions through explicit capability descriptors, schema validation, and semantic version negotiation, while providing structured telemetry and failure isolation. The design decouples agent reasoning from tool integration, enabling independent evolution of agents and tools without breaking compatibility. We evaluate a prototype implementation using representative multi-agent automation workflows across multiple datasets with controlled schema drift. Experimental results demonstrate a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7. 4 \%}$</tex> improvement in integration success rate, a 60.6% reduction in mean time to recovery, and a 4.4 times increase in schema mismatch detection precision, with a P95 mediation latency overhead of 18.4 ms. These results show that contract-driven MCP mediation provides a practical and scalable foundation for building reliable, evolvable, and observable multi-agent systems in production environments.

title: Techno-Economic Analysis for the Implementation of a Photovoltaic System in Goat Farming Facilities in the Coquimbo Region
authors: Daniel Castro, Gonzalo Toledo, Luis Gonzalez, Héctor Chávez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The aim of this paper is to assess the technical and economic feasibility of increasing the support pillar height of a photovoltaic (PV) system for installation on goat farms in Chile's Coquimbo Region. This strategic integration of PV and agriculture results in agrivoltaic systems. The findings indicate that this height increase does not compromise project viability. This research presents a comprehensive review of existing agrivoltaic structural modifications, goat behavioral characteristics, and typical PV configurations. The technical evaluation applied fundamental principles of structural mechanics. The validation was carry on MATLAB and ANSYS of the modified structure performance under the change of the wind pressure. Concurrently, the economic assessment was developed through a cash flow model of the project.

title: Research and Development of a Differential Equation Modeling and Trend Prediction Platform for Large-Scale Data Streams
authors: Xuesong Tang, Jinhong Huang, Yan Liang, Zhiyan Wang, Feifei Wang, Kaiyan Sun
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper tackles the challenges of dynamic system modeling and trend prediction in large-scale data stream environments, and proposes a novel real-time analysis platform based on differential equations called StreamDiff. The platform aims to overcome the bottlenecks faced by traditional static models in dealing with data streams characterized by high throughput, infinite duration, and prone to concept drift, such as low computational efficiency and poor adaptability. The paper elaborates on the layered architecture design of the StreamDiff platform and designs an efficient online parameter estimation algorithm. The core of the platform utilizes a neural differential equation paradigm, realizing the ability to automatically learn unknown kinetic laws from raw data streams. This study demonstrates the feasibility and superiority of deeply integrating differential equation modeling with modern streaming computing technologies, providing a new theoretical perspective and technical approach for building the next generation of intelligent prediction systems.

title: Detecting Injection Attacks in REST APIs Using DNIARS
authors: Khavar Facey, Tian McFarlane, Shaquille Ford, Katoni Shields, Ian McGowan
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: While NoSQL databases continue to enjoy an ever-growing popularity, NoSQL security is yet to receive the attention from the industry or academia it deserves according to recent studies. Injection attacks on NoSQL databases pose a significant threat to web applications and data security, often leading to unauthorized access, data breaches, and service disruption. This project focuses on the development and evaluation of an attack detection mechanism based on the Dynamic Non-Interference with Automated Reasoning (DNIARS) technique. DNIARS enables real-time analysis of application behavior, identifying anomalies and potential injection attempts. We will demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in detecting and mitigating various NoSQL injection attack vectors, contributing to improved web application security and data protection. Although extensive research has explored machine learning–based detection techniques, the feasibility of many current methods in real-world deployments is limited by how much they depend on huge, labeled datasets, high processing costs, and lack of transparency. This study uses a new pairedquery dataset to assess the functional detection accuracy of DNIARS in order to overcome these limitations. According to experimental results, DNIARS achieves a high precision of 97.57%, an overall accuracy of 85.65%, and a detection rate of 86.06%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of scalable, lightweight, and transparent detection techniques like DNIARS, especially for deployed systems and environments with limited resources. However, the findings also highlight shortcomings such as false negatives and the need for greater pattern coverage.

title: Coherence Analysis of Output Spectra from Multilongitudinal Mode FP Lasers
authors: Xiaojuan Li, Yali Wu, Yachao Wang, Lixin Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This article aims to deeply study the coherence characteristics of multi-mode Fabry-Pérot(FP) lasers and analyze the influence of their coherence length on the sensing distance and spatial resolution of Rayleigh and Brillouin scattering selfheterodyne detection systems. A coherent theoretical model for multi longitudinal mode lasers was established, and the relationship between the visibility of interference fringes and the optical path difference was analyzed by deriving the interference field equations of Rayleigh and Brillouin scattering light. The decisive role of longitudinal modulus and laser resonant cavity length on coherence length was discussed in detail. Theoretical analysis and numerical simulations indicate that the coherence length <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$l$</tex> is inversely proportional to the longitudinal modulus <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$M$</tex> and directly proportional to the resonant cavity length <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$L$</tex>. Increasing the longitudinal modulus of the fiber or reducing the cavity length can effectively shorten the coherence length, thereby improving the system spatial resolution. Meanwhile, the maximum sensing distance is fundamentally constrained by the fiber dispersion coefficient and Brillouin spectrum width. The simulation results show that using large effective area fiber can achieve a balance between suppressing four wave mixing and alleviating dispersion limitation, making it the preferred solution for achieving long-distance and high-performance sensing.

title: Interval Mover's Distance: Melodic Stylistic Comparison using Interval Distributions
authors: Dmitriy Sazonov
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Quantifying similarity of melodic features has been a challenge not only in Music Information Retrieval (MIR), but music theory in general. Despite advantages such as resource efficiency and interpretability, music theoretical models are often underutilized in music informatics. This paper proposes the application of Earth Mover's Distance onto a novel representation of the interval content of a song that exploits topological properties of the circle of fifths, dubbing the resulting distance metric Interval Mover's Distance. Interval Mover's Distance may be applied to various MIR tasks concerning stylistic similarity, such as music recommendation algorithms, and for novel tasks such as manifold learning. Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) is then applied in conjunction with Interval Mover's Distance onto a large corpus of MIDI data, creating a low dimensional embedding of the dataset based on melodic features. This graph and dataset, dubbed “Every Interval at Once”, visualizes the spectrum of melodic content in contemporary music, demonstrating viability of advanced music theoretical concepts in music information retrieval pipelines. In addition, the metadata and interval content collected for this project, spanning 23,059 songs across 75 unique genres, is publicly available.

title: GPT for Botnet Detection
authors: Yi Heng Chai, Wei Xiang Eddie Tan, Ray Shiang Nicholas Sng, Syafeen Khan Bin Irzan Khan, Zheng Hao Jonathan Chiu, Khairul Nizam Bin Abdul Rashid, Huaqun Guo
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: As the internet continues to expand, home networks have become increasingly vulnerable to botnet attacks. This growing threat underscores the need for more effective detection methods. This paper proposes a hybrid approach that leverages artificial intelligence (AI) for the chatbot feature and machine learning (ML) models to identify botnet activity and recommend future steps. Specifically, it introduces a Gemini-powered chatbot system that integrates a knowledge-based Large Language Model (LLM), utilizing the Gemini API, and a LightGBM ML model. The chatbot enables users to inquire about botnet-related topics, providing comprehensive knowledge and support, while the ML model analyzes user-provided PCAP files to detect potential botnet activity. This dual approach combines the strengths of LLMs for interactive assistance with the analytical power of ML models for network security, offering a robust solution for botnet detection and prevention.

title: A Self-Aware Data Platform with Predictive Elasticity: Closed-Loop Autonomy for Elastic, Reliable, and Governed Analytics at Scale
authors: John Benito Jesudasan Peter
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Modern data platforms operate under volatile workloads, multi-tenant contention, evolving schemas, and strict governance constraints. Despite advances in separation of compute and storage, orchestration, and observability, most platforms remain reactive: they expose telemetry but lack the ability to reason over platform state, anticipate demand, and verify whether corrective actions actually improve user-facing outcomes. This paper introduces a Self-Aware Data Platform (SADP) architecture that implements governed closed-loop autonomy using an event-graph world model, SLO and error-budget-driven control logic, and verification-first execution. SADP continuously senses platform state, maintains a causal representation of workloads, resources, policies, and actions, and plans bounded interventions under explicit safety and governance constraints. We further propose predictive elasticity as a first-class control capability, combining ML-driven demand forecasting with adaptive warm-pool management across Kubernetes pods and virtual machines. Rather than claiming end-to-end predictive performance, we present a controlled baseline evaluation of reactive autoscaling under burst load on a Kubernetes testbed. The results quantify delayed mitigation, elevated tail latency, and governance-policy violations inherent to reactive elasticity, motivating the need for predictive, policy-aware, and verificationdriven control. The paper contributes a unified architectural blueprint, formal control framing, and evaluation methodology that together establish a foundation for governed autonomous data platforms. SADP shifts platform operations from telemetry-driven reaction toward evidence-based, self-aware decisioning with bounded risk.

title: AI Augmented Big Data Framework for Edge Driven Threat Intelligence in Software Defined Networks
authors: Het Mehta
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The increasing complexity of cyber-attacks on communication infrastructures has highlighted the imperative demand for intelligent, scalable, and adaptive security measures in Software-Defined Networks (SDN). SecureEdge-SDN, a new AI-enhanced security architecture proposed in this paper, uses big data analytics and edge-sourced threat intelligence to secure SDN-based communications networks. The central element of SecureEdge-SDN is an Edge Intelligence Module with Federated Deep Learning (FDL) via LSTM-Autoencoders for distributed anomaly detection from edge SDN switches. This solution provides privacy-preserving, real-time learning without aggregated data collection. At the same time, the system incorporates contextaware risk scoring, processing flow metadata, temporal behavior, and network topology using Apache Spark for stream processing of big data. A reinforcement learning adaptive policy enforcer dynamically updates flow rules considering threat gravity and trust values. Experimental performance on benchmark SDN datasets proves that SecureEdge-SDN attains high accuracy (96.8%), lower false positives, and low latency, indicating the potential of this solution for next-generation secure communication infrastructures.

title: When Do Tools and Planning Help Large Language Models Think? A Cost- and Latency-Aware Benchmark
authors: Subha Ghoshal, Ali Al-Bustami
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Modern large language models (LLMs) increasingly rely on inference-time planning and external tools to improve reasoning. We benchmark this behavior on two real-world settings: event-centric question answering over graph-structured knowledge (Event-QA) and persuasive response generation in Reddit ChangeMyView (CMV). Using LangChain and LangGraph, we compare a one-shot baseline against a plan-execute-replan agent equipped with task-specific tools (DBpedia SPARQL Protocol and RDF Query Language (SPARQL)/lookup/schema exploration, Wikipedia-focused retrieval, and topical web search). We evaluate on 60 examples each from Event-QA and CMV (3 splits of 20), and report both mean end-to-end latency and per-example token costs. We evaluate GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini under identical workflows and report accuracy and end-to-end latency. On EventQA, the best tool-augmented configuration improves accuracy (e.g., <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$47.5 \% \rightarrow 67.5 {\%}$</tex> for GPT-4o) while increasing latency by orders of magnitude <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\sim 8 \mathrm{s} \rightarrow \sim 317 \mathrm{s}$</tex> per example). On CMV, one-shot prompting is strongest (e.g., GPT-4o-mini achieves 75% accuracy at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 6 \mathrm{s}$</tex>), and planning+search increases latency substantially without consistent gains. However, complex multitool orchestration exposes failure modes where the smaller model degrades. Overall, the findings highlight the need for taskspecific, cost-aware choices of both model size and agent/tooling complexity.

title: ABSortQA: An Evaluation of Chemical Reasoning in Large Language Models Through Pairwise Comparison of Acid and Base Strength
authors: Kien Bui, Tien Nguyen
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to scientific discovery, yet distinguishing between rote memorization and genuine reasoning remains a challenge. In this paper, we introduce Acid-Base Sorting QA (ABSortQA), a novel pairwise benchmark focused on acidity (pKa) and basicity (pKaH) prediction to evaluate the chemical reasoning capabilities of LLMs. Unlike existing benchmarks that rely on retrieval, our task requires deriving relative strength from molecular structure using chemical heuristics such as resonance and induction. We evaluate six open-weights models (ranging from 7 B to 20 B parameters), including Llama 3.1, Gemma 2, ChemLLM, and GPT-OSS, on a stratified dataset of 8,161 molecular pairs. Our results indicate that generalist reasoning models often outperform domain-specific models, particularly in tasks requiring logical negation. This work provides a rigorous framework for assessing the logical robustness of AI in chemistry.

title: Angle-Free Mobile Robot Position Control
authors: Douglas R. Isenberg, Nick Maloziec
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This paper describes both a kinematic differential equation and a position control for wheeled nonholonomic mobile robots that uses a Cartesian point on a unit-circle as the rotational parameterization rather than a single angle. It is shown that this choice of rotational parameters results in a drastic speed increase when computing the kinematic differential equation, and it leads to an intuitive control law that allows for simplified decisions regarding forward and reverse motion to be made. Both the kinematic differential equation and the kinematic control are derived, and empirical results are used to justify the claims. Computational speed is examined across several embedded platforms and compared to the speed of computation of both the conventional and quaternion-based mobile robot kinematic differential equations. The control viability is examined empirically via implementation on a differential-drive robot whose motion is measured via a motion capture system.

title: Complex Query Answering for Neural Graph Databases with Dynamic Gradient Recurrence
authors: Qichao Sun, Xizhou Yu, Xiaojun Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Neural Graph Databases (NGDBs) utilize neural symbolic computation to solve complex first-order logic (FOL) queries on large-scale graphs. However, existing inference kernels are limited by inference-static parameterization and computational bottlenecks that scale quadratically with logical depth, thus restricting their adaptability to complex and evolving inference paths. We propose the Dynamic Gradient Encoder (DyGEn), a dynamic sequence inference framework with linear complexity. DyGEn reframes logical inference as an on-the-fly optimization process through a dynamic gradient recursion mechanism. During forward propagation, the model dynamically updates its hidden states using an inner-loop gradient rule based on the input context, thereby adaptively capturing logical dependencies. By integrating a dual-form architecture, DyGEn avoids explicit matrix materialization, achieving linear-time sequence modeling efficiency. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that DyGEn exhibits superior structural generalization capabilities compared to static baselines, establishing its position as a powerful and efficient inference engine for next-generation NGDBs.

title: Research on Vehicle Detection and Tracking Under High-Speed Motion and Dynamic Illumination Using Event Cameras
authors: Zhiyuan Tao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Traditional frame-based cameras are prone to motion blur in high-speed scenarios and exposure anomalies in dynamic lighting. To address these limitations, which often lead to perception failures, this paper proposes a method for vehicle detection and tracking under high-speed motion and dynamic illumination based on event cameras. First, leveraging the high temporal resolution of event streams, a Polarity-aware Spatiotemporal Voxel Grid is constructed as the input representation to preserve sub-millisecond motion information. Second, an Embedded Spatiotemporal Voxel Attention Network (ST-VAN) is designed, which effectively suppresses background noise and enhances the extraction of vehicle edge features from sparse event streams by introducing a spatial attention mechanism into the feature extraction layers. In the tracking stage, an Adaptive Asynchronous Association Strategy based on Event Density is proposed to resolve the challenge of matching targets with large displacements caused by high-speed motion. Experimental results on the public datasets DSEC and MVSEC demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 83.1 % and 80.9 % in extreme lighting and high-speed scenarios, respectively. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the attention mechanism in noise suppression, and the model's parameter size and computational load meet the real-time requirements for vehicular applications.

title: A Lightweight Detection Model Based on Improved Yolov11 for Aero-Engine Surface Defect Inspection
authors: Xiaoxiao Xu, Yafeng Wu, Guocan Lu, Zijun Cheng
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: To address the challenges in aero-engine preoperation visual inspection, such as varying defect scales, strong background interference, and limited deployment resources, this paper proposes a lightweight intelligent detection model based on an improved YOLOv11. The model enhances feature extraction efficiency by replacing the backbone with Rep_HGNetV2. In the Neck module, the SPDConv lossless downsampling module and a self-developed EMSC (Efficient Multi-Scale Convolution) multiscale feature fusion module are introduced. The Focaler-MPDIoU loss function is employed to optimize bounding box regression accuracy. Furthermore, a combined strategy of Group Taylor structured pruning and BCKD knowledge distillation is utilized for model lightweighting. Experiments on the AeBAD blade defect dataset and a self-made assembly fault dataset demonstrate that the model achieves an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{mAP} {@} 0.5$</tex> of 0.924. Compared to the original YOLOv11, the parameters and GFLOPs are reduced by 48.6 % and 50.8 %, respectively. The model exhibits excellent accuracy and real-time performance in detecting both structural defects and non-structural assembly faults, meeting the requirements for edge deployment in aero-engine pre-operation inspection.

title: Intelligent Facades: An IIoT and Digital Twin Framework for Occupancy-Aware Kinetic Envelope Control
authors: Diana Salamaga, David Guerra-Zubiaga, Jade Yang, Razvan Cristian Voicu
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Kinetic facades can improve building performance by regulating daylight and incident solar gains through controllable geometry. In practice, many facade prototypes remain isolated subsystems with limited sensing, limited coordination with lighting and HVAC, and control policies that do not transfer across seasons and occupancy patterns. This paper presents an Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) and digital twin framework that couples multi-sensor monitoring, edge autonomy, and cloud analytics to support repeatable deployment. A functional taxonomy is introduced to map facade intent to measurable objectives and to make sensor selection and evaluation metrics explicit. Building on this foundation, we propose an occupancy-aware, multi-objective controller for louver-based facades that prioritizes visual comfort when spaces are occupied and shifts toward solar heat mitigation and operational efficiency when spaces are unoccupied. The control design integrates a digital-twinseeded surrogate model for fast prediction and includes online calibration to reduce sensitivity to site-specific effects such as sensor placement and interior reflectance. A deployment-oriented algorithm is provided with safety constraints, manual override, and telemetry for continuous improvement. The framework is demonstrated using a louver prototype and a digital twin irradiation analysis. The prototype exhibits actuation response on the order of seconds and illuminance regulation near a 300 lux target during occupied operation.

title: Improved SAC Algorithm for Solving the Interactive Hybrid Disassembly Line Balancing Problem Considering Multiskilled Workers
authors: QingBo Meng, XiWang Guo, ZhiWei Zhang, JiaCun Wang, ShuJin Qin, GuiPeng Xi, Liang Qi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: With the advancement of manufacturing and the progression towards intelligent production, more and more production enterprises are focusing on the hybrid disassembly line balancing problem to enhance efficiency and reduce costs. This work presents an interactive hybrid disassembly line balancing problem considering multiskilled workers and its corresponding mathematical model. Additionally, we incorporated the concept of carbon emissions into the problem to ensure the green sustainability of the production process. We employ an improved soft actor-critic (SAC) algorithm for solving the problem to achieve the goals of maximizing profit, minimizing idle time, and minimizing carbon emissions. The experimental results demonstrate that the improved SAC algorithm performs well on six different experimental scale cases. Specifically, multiobjective soft actor-critic (MO-SAC) produces solutions that are closer to the Pareto front than those obtained by deep deterministic policy gradient and nondominated sorting genetic algorithm II. This advantage is particularly pronounced in medium and large-scale problem instances. Statistical comparisons using nondominated solution metrics (spread, epsilon, and IGD+), Pareto front distributions, and computational time show that MO-SAC achieves better convergence and maintains comparable computational efficiency, highlighting its performance benefits in complex scenarios. The results verify the effectiveness of our proposed approach, highlighting the superiority of the improved SAC algorithm in addressing the multiobjective interactive hybrid disassembly line balancing problem.

title: A Frequency Dithering Technique to Attenuate Conducted Electromagnetic Emission of SiC MOSFET Based DC-DC Converter
authors: Vibhav Pandey, Kousik Ghosh, Kamalesh Hatua
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Fast-switching wide-bandgap (WBG) devices in power electronic converters introduce substantial electromagnetic interference (EMI), resulting in supply-side disturbances. Conventional EMI mitigation predominantly relies on passive filters, which negatively affect converter power density. While existing frequency-dithering techniques are effective in reducing EMI and facilitating filter size reduction, they generally neglect the influence of the 9 kHz resolution bandwidth (RBW) specified by CISPR standards. This paper demonstrates that higher RBW values limit the achievable EMI attenuation and therefore must be explicitly considered in dithering design. A frequency-dithering methodology that incorporates the RBW effect and the converter noise-floor characteristics is proposed and evaluated for three nominal switching frequencies of 100 kHz, 300 kHz, and 500 kHz. The proposed algorithm systematically selects the switching and dithering frequencies to enhance EMI attenuation by reducing the number of spectral components within the RBW and by positioning low-amplitude sidebands near the converter noise floor or frequency-response peaks. Experimental validation using a 1kW SiC MOSFET-based DC-DC converter demonstrates up to 11 dB reduction in conducted EMI measured at 9 kHz RBW and enables EMI filter reductions of up to 63%.

title: Commerce User Review Sentiment Analysis Model Based on BERT and Transformer
authors: Tuli Chen
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Digital Media Technology and Interaction Design
abstract: None

title: Intelligent Design Research on Ceramic Patterns Based on Generative Adversarial Networks
authors: Weijun Wu
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Digital Media Technology and Interaction Design
abstract: None

title: Empirical Study on the Effectiveness of Multimodal Reinforcement Learning-Driven Intelligent Sandbox System and its Career Exploration Function
authors: Yan Lin, Ke Li, Jiawei Xu
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 2nd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Digital Media Technology and Interaction Design
abstract: None

title: Development of Design fuzzy logic hierarchy structure by Using Decision Tree Algorithm
authors: Mais A. Al-sharqi, Huda D. Najeeb, Reem R. Abdulhussein, Hamsa E. Mahmood, Asma D. Najeeb
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Conference on Computing and Emerging Sciences
abstract: None

title: A Python-Based Workflow for Producing Soil Electromagnetic Properties
authors: Todd R. Brownbridge, Nicholas Russo
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This paper presents a Python-based workflow to model the complex permittivity of soil utilizing Geographic Information Systems datasets. The workflow incorporates soil texture, moisture, organic matter, bulk density, and temperature to estimate volumetric water content via Campbell's retention curve and applies the recommendation ITU-R P.527-6 model to compute soil permittivity across a frequency range. Results demonstrate the workflow's utility for electromagnetic modeling, with potential applications in wireless communication and remote sensing.

title: Hybrid Rule-Based and Deep Learning System using GNNs to Detect Credit Card Fraud
authors: Sriharsha Anand Pushkala
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Financial fraud, particularly credit card fraud, poses a growing threat to global financial systems, causing billions in annual losses. As digital transactions rise, fraudsters evolve rapidly, often outpacing traditional rulebased detection systems, which, while interpretable, lack adaptability to novel patterns. Deep learning models, especially recurrent neural networks (RNNs), have shown strong performance in detecting complex fraud patterns, but are criticized for limited transparency. To bridge this gap, we propose a hybrid framework that combines the interpretability of rule-based systems with the predictive power of Bidirectional LSTM (BiLSTM) networks. As a novel enhancement, we incorporate Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for feature engineering by constructing a transaction graph based on statistical and temporal similarity. This allows us to uncover hidden relationships between transactions and enrich each sample with graph-based embeddings before feeding them into the BiLSTM model. The rule engine acts as a first-layer filter, identifying known fraud patterns, while the BiLSTM analyzes the GNN-enhanced sequences for more subtle, sequential anomalies. We further enhance transparency using explainability tools like SHAP. Empirical results on Kaggle Credit Card dataset show that this hybrid approach outperforms standalone models in accuracy, precision, recall, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex>-score, and false positive rates, making it a robust, interpretable solution for real-world fintech and banking applications.

title: Time-Stepped Cyber-Physical Simulation of DoS, DoD, and FDI Attacks on the IEEE 14-Bus System
authors: Manuella Christelle Tossa, Fernando Madrigal, Ryan Blosser, Asma Jodeiri Akbarfam
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Reliable grid operation depends on accurate and timely telemetry, making modern power systems vulnerable to communication-layer cyberattacks. This paper evaluates how Denial-of-Service (DoS), Denial-of-Data (DoD), and False Data Injection (FDI) attacks disrupt the IEEE 14-bus system using a MATLAB-only, time-stepped simulation framework built on MATPOWER. The framework emulates a 24 -hour operating cycle with sinusoidal load variation, introduces attack-specific manipulation of load and voltage data, and performs full AC powerflow solves with reactive-limit enforcement (PV-PQ switching). At each timestep, the system logs true and measured voltages, generator P/Q output, system losses, and voltage-limit violations to capture transient cyber-physical effects. Results show that DoD causes the largest physical distortions and reactive-power stress, DoS masks natural variability and degrades situational awareness, and FDI creates significant discrepancies between true and perceived voltages. The study provides a compact, reproducible benchmark for analyzing cyber-induced instability and informing future defense strategies.

title: High-Precision Tracking Control With Optimal Switching Frequency for a Digital Hydraulic System
authors: Qi Zhong, Enguang Xu, Min Pan, Xuejian Yan, Dongsheng Shan, Liujie Wu, Huayong Yang
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: The switching frequency of high-speed on/off valves (HSVs) is critical for determining the tracking precision of digital hydraulic systems (DHS). While a higher frequency enhances fluid resolution, it can also amplify the duty cycle bias caused by the time error between the control signal and the actual drive signal, thereby increasing the output flow error. To address this issue, an optimal strategy for selecting the switching frequency is proposed in this study. An accurate co-simulation model is developed to reveal the propagation mechanism of pressure waves within the discrete fluid, confirming that microscopic pressure oscillations intensify with the duty cycle bias at high frequencies. Experimental results indicate that as the switching frequency increases from 10 Hz to 125 Hz, the tracking error under different loads initially decreases before subsequently increasing. At the optimal frequency of 75 Hz, the minimum average tracking error and standard deviation of the DHS are 0.24 mm and 0.12 mm, respectively. This selection effectively balances the benefits of enhanced fluid resolution with the limitations of output flow error. The proposed strategy provides constructive guidance in digital hydraulics and possesses significant engineering application value.

title: Real-Time Assessment of Dynamic Event-Triggered Controllers for Islanded Microgrids via High-fidelity Cyber-Physical Platform
authors: Amedeo Andreotti, Bianca Caiazzo, Sara Leccese, Alberto Petrillo, Stefania Santini, Alfredo Vaccaro
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: The stable and efficient operation of inverter-based islanded microgrids remains a significant challenge, particularly if communication constraints, such as time delays and cyber threats, are considered. This paper addresses these issues by high lighting the experimental validation of two different distributed digital control strategies recently introduced in the technical literature. Their common aim is to reduce the communication workload in the controller-to-actuator channel via a dynamic event-triggered mechanism able to achieve a significant reduction of the control updates, while guaranteeing an ease implementation in digital control platform thanks to their sampled-data structure. They differ from each other in terms of large actuation delays compensation capability, which is guaranteed by the second control law by means of the exploitation of the classical model reduction approach reformulated in a distributed way. The experimental validation campaign is conducted by using a home-made high-fidelity, real-time cyber-physical simulation platform developed in MATLAB/Simscape Electrical environment and deployed on a Speedgoat real-time target machine. Results confirm the effectiveness of both controllers in balancing communication resource savings and resilience w.r.t. non-ideal microgrid conditions−both physical and cyber ones−, thus of fering a promising path toward the practical deployment of two different turnkey solutions.

title: Enhanced Lyapunov-Based Model Predictive Control for Wheeled Mobile Robot with Improved Tunnel Performance Constraints
authors: Di Wu, Zengyang Yan, Peng Cheng, Baozhu Du, Yushuai Li, Yibo Zhang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This study addresses the maneuvering control problem of a wheeled mobile robot (WMR) operating in dynamic and uncertain environments, where smooth error convergence and robustness against external disturbances are critical challenges. An enhanced Lyapunov-based model predictive control (ELMPC) method is developed, in which an improved tunnel performance function is incorporated into an auxiliary controller to impose stricter contraction constraints on the WMR system, enabling smoother and faster error convergence. In addition, an improved tunnel performance constraint (ITPC) scheme is introduced to eliminate the initial-condition dependence of conventional prescribed performance control. By employing a performance transition function, the ITPC ensures that performance bounds are satisfied under arbitrary initial errors while avoiding excessive transient responses. To compensate for unknown and time-varying disturbances, a neural network–based disturbance predictor is integrated into the control architecture. The predictor estimates disturbances online and transforms tracking errors into prediction errors, thereby accelerating convergence and enhancing robustness. The proposed control strategy is implemented within a Lyapunov-based model predictive control framework with explicit consideration of actuator constraints, where contraction constraints guarantee closed-loop stability and recursive feasibility. Experimental results demonstrate improved tracking performance, robustness, and constraint satisfaction under complex operating conditions.

title: Knowledge-Enhanced Intent-Driven Flow Scheduling for LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Zhenzi Wang, Chungang Yang, Song Mao, Yao Wang, Ying Ouyang, Zhu Han
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks are characterized by dynamic network topologies and on-demand service requirements from Internet-of-Things (IoT) applications, which make efficient and intelligent flow scheduling challenging. Conventional schemes rely on static configurations or manual rules, thus making it difficult to capture and respond to diverse service demands. Moreover, they often fail to model task–resource relationships effectively, hindering the generation of real-time, executable scheduling policies. To address these challenges, we propose a knowledge-enhanced, intent-driven flow scheduling (KIFS) framework. Specifically, we design a unified pipeline that first translates user intents into precise Quality of Service (QoS) requirements. It incorporates a network state awareness module to estimate per-link bandwidth and utilization, and constructs a task–resource knowledge graph (KG) to enhance the Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent via state augmentation, action pruning, and reward shaping. Finally, the framework translates the resulting policies into standards-compliant SRv6 configurations for real-time deployment. In simulations, the proposed KIFS framework demonstrates superior performance compared to standard baselines in terms of flow success rate and QoS satisfaction.

title: Wideband Active Frequency Selective Surface (AFSS) for Wireless and 5G Mobile Communications
authors: Hsi-Hsir Chou, Chang-Li Tso, Yin-Cheng Wu
publish: IEEE Letters on Electromagnetic Compatibility Practice and Applications
abstract: This paper presents a wideband PIN diode-based active frequency-selective surface (AFSS) for the 5G n78, 5G private network, and WiFi 5.8 GHz bands. The proposed AFSS employs two stacked PCBs and four PIN diodes per unit cell to realize a simple structure with switchable passband/stopband behavior. In the reverse-bias (OFF) state, it provides a passband from 2.9 to 5.9 GHz for both TE and TM incidences. In the forward-bias (ON) state, it exhibits a wide shielding band from 0 to 10 GHz based on the S21<–10 dB criterion. The proposed design also maintains good angular stability for both TE and TM incidences from 0˚ to greater than 60˚. Although some deviations between simulation and measurement are observed at 65˚, the proposed AFSS offers a favorable combination of wideband switching performance, compact implementation, and angular stability, making it promising for electromagnetic interference mitigation in modern 5G and WiFi communication environments.

title: Joint Trajectory and Beamforming Optimization for UAV-ISAC Secure Communications
authors: Zhaolong Ning, Yuzhen Zhang, Xiaojie Wang, Lei Guo, Dusit Niyato, Yan Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) can assist Uncrewed Aerial Vehicle (UAV) secure communications by acquiring information about eavesdroppers. However, existing studies have not systematically investigated ISAC beamforming for simultaneously sensing the channel information of ground eavesdroppers, jamming eavesdropping links, and communicating with users, which poses significant challenges in ensuring both sensing accuracy and communication confidentiality. To address this issue, we propose a UAV-ISAC secure communication algorithm to maximize average secrecy rate by jointly optimizing communication and sensing beamforming, user scheduling, sensing time allocation, and UAV trajectory. We address the formulated NP-hard problem by decomposed it into three subproblems. We first relax binary user scheduling and sensing time allocation by a penalty-based successive convex approximation approach. The UAV trajectory is then iteratively optimized while beamforming is designed using semidefinite relaxation, with matrix lifting applied to handle the rank-one constraint. A triple-layer iterative algorithm is constructed by integrating these steps to achieve a suboptimal solution. Numerical experiment results and theoretical analysis validate the superiority of the proposed algorithm in terms of average secrecy rate, convergence and computational complexity.

title: Dynamic Local Range-Doppler Map and Controlled Feature Fusion for Continuous Human Activity Recognition on Variable Multi-node Radars
authors: Shengze Wang, Mondher Bouazizi, Tomoaki Ohtsuki
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: This work studies subject-independent continuous human activity recognition (HAR) using a modulation-independent multi-radar network. Our method is designed as a practical solution for real-world deployment, with a strong emphasis on robust generalization to unseen subjects, while also preventing abrupt performance drops and ensuring stable operation under varying radar availability. We introduce a Dynamic Local Range–Doppler Map (DL-RDM) that automatically tracks the line-of-sight (LoS) component range to generate subject-centered, short-time range–Doppler patches. These features are fused with micro-Doppler spectrograms via a learned gate, and multiple radar streams are combined using radar-wise attention. To reduce subject dependence, a triplet-loss pretraining stage precedes fine-tuning for frame-wise classification. Evaluations on public 5-node and 3-node HAR datasets show that gated fusion consistently outperforms single-stream models. On the 5-node dataset, fusion raises accuracy from the best single stream’s 85.3% to 87.2%, and to 87.5% with triplet pretraining, while the macro-F1 score increases from 77.2% to 81.2%. On the 3-node dataset, the MD spectrogram baseline reaches 71.9% accuracy; our proposed DL–RDM improves to 85.3%, gated feature fusion achieves 89.3%, and the pretrained gated fusion attains 89.4%. Our method achieves lower inter-subject performance variation, enhancing generalization ability on unseen users. Moreover, performance remains stable as active sensors are removed, preventing abrupt drops and demonstrating robustness under varying radar availability, making it well-suited for practical, real-world deployment.

title: Novel and Efficient Video Compressive Sensing Reconstruction Approach for IoVT Applications
authors: Davoud Fani, Aliasghar Beheshti-Shirazi, Mohammad Ghanbari
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Despite the promising performance of video compressive sensing (CS) in compressing volumetric visual data from resource-constrained sensors in emerging internet of video things (IoVT) applications, achieving high-quality video CS reconstruction remains challenging. The most representative video CS reconstruction approaches include multi-hypothesis (MH) prediction and deep neural network-based schemes. However, in almost all existing methods, motion-induced irregular deformations are neglected, leading to suboptimal reconstruction quality. In this paper, these irregular deformations are addressed by devising irregular-shaped hypotheses into the geometrical hypothesis set. Relying on the geometrical hypothesis set, a novel and efficient geometrical MH prediction approach is proposed for video CS reconstruction. The proposed approach simultaneously considers both the computational cost and the optimality of the hypothesis set. Extensive experiments and comparisons with recent state-of-theart methods on various video contents and resolutions demonstrate that the proposed approach not only achieves remarkable average reconstruction quality—with overall average improvements of 2.95 dB, 6.91 dB, and 7.56 dB in PSNR and 0.125, 0.051, and 0.155 in SSIM over standard CIF-sized test video sequences, real-world capsule endoscopy videos, and realworld surveillance videos, respectively—but also effectively suppresses severe and undesirable fluctuations in the quality of reconstructed key and non-key frames. Furthermore, the approach preserves the structural integrity and texture details of video frames while maintaining reasonable computational complexity and runtime. These high-quality reconstructed frames with well-preserved structures and textures contribute to more reliable diagnosis and decision-making, thereby enabling optimal intelligent services in various IoVT applications.

title: Characterization of Electrical Crosstalk in FDM Readout for a CMB Experiment
authors: Eugenia Di Giorgi, Vishal Anvekar, Marcel Bruijn, Luciano Gottardi, Kevin Ravensberg, Emanuele Taralli, Andrea Tartari, Jan van der Kuur, Sven Visser, Martin de Wit, Giovanni Signorelli, Davide Vaccaro
publish: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
abstract: The new generation of Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) experiments exploits the properties of low-temperature detectors, particularly superconducting Transition Edge Sensor (TES) bolometers, whose characteristics make them ideal for present and future CMB measurements. Frequency Domain Multiplexing (FDM) configuration is performed by connecting an LC filter in series with each TES. The presence of the LC filters and the SQUID input coil and wiring in series with all the LC channels cause systematic electrical crosstalk.In this work we present the tests and characterization performed on an LC chip produced by SRON (Space Research Organization Netherlands) for the experiment SWIPE. These LC chips are composed of lithographically fabricated inductors and by parallel plate capacitors. The measurements were focused on retrieving the resonance frequencies to be compared to the expected design values, the quality factor and the parasitic resistance. The results were consistent with previous measurements performed on similar setups, showing Q-factors above <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf {10^{4}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and parasitic resistance on the order of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf {10^{-2}\;\Omega }$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The dependence of the properties of the LC filters and the SQUID on the temperature and the bias settings was studied. Measurements to define the level and the major contributors to electrical crosstalk were performed. Both modeling and experimental results indicated an electrical crosstalk level on the order of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-4</sup>.

title: CogniScript: Hybrid Learning with XAI for Handwriting-Based Personality Analysis with Big Five Traits and Honesty-Humility
authors: Dipankar Basu, Abhrendu Bhattacharya, Suman Bhattacharya, Mahamuda Sultana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Latent neuromotor and cognitive hints are present in handwriting and are tightly correlated with personality traits. The current paper proposes a hybrid machine learning model to predict personality based on a set of handwriting features that incorporate the different projections of dimensions using a principal component analysis technique with ensemble-based models, XGBoost, and LightGBM. Explainability tools such as LIME and SHAP were used to increase interpretability, trying to find contributions on the feature level. The model was tested on curated handwriting databases, with changes aimed at improving prediction compared to single-model designs. The conclusions of the findings showed high R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> values and lower mean squared errors in expanded tests on each of the eight models. Notably, SHAP-based insights indicated that the dynamics of curvature and the speed at which a person writes were among the most significant predictors, aligning with neurocognitive research on motor control. Recent research suggests that we can determine someone's Big Five personality traits and Honesty-Humility by analyzing their handwriting. By blending smart technology with clear explanations, we've built a reliable system that psychologists, investigators, and researchers can trust for important decisions.

title: GWSoD: A Conditional Diffusion Model for Cross-Band Galaxy Image Generation from WISE to SDSS
authors: Shaohua Yang, Cuixiang Liu, Bo Qiu, Hui Xing
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Multi-band astronomical observations provide critical information for investigating galaxy morphology, stellar pop-ulations, and dust properties. However, substantial discrepancies in wavelength coverage, spatial resolution, and noise characteristics across different surveys pose significant challenges for data fusion and joint analysis. This paper presents GWSoD(Galaxy Image Conversion from WISE to SDSS based on DDPM), a conditional denoising diffusion probabilis-tic model designed to generate high-quality SDSS optical galaxy images from WISE infrared observations. In the pro-posed framework, WISE images are incorporated as conditional inputs via channel-wise concatenation throughout the denoising process, enabling the model to learn complex cross-band mappings. An improved UNet(NewUNet) architecture equipped with attention gates and explicit time-step embeddings is introduced to enhance multi-scale structural feature modeling. The training objective combines a noise prediction loss with a perceptual loss to jointly optimize pixel-level accuracy and visual consistency. Experiments conducted on paired WISE-SDSS galaxy image datasets demon-strate that GWSoD consistently outperforms several state-of-the-art image translation and cross-modal generation methods, achieving 29.74 dB in PSNR and 0.8057 in SSIM. Qualitative evaluations further confirm that the proposed approach more faithfully preserves galaxy morphology, brightness distributions, and structural details. These results indicate that GWSoD provides an effective and robust solution for cross-band astronomical image generation and mul-ti-wavelength data fusion.

title: Research on Urban Street Perception Prediction and Mechanism Interpretation Based on Large Language Models
authors: Tianyi Ren, Fei Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper examines urban street perception prediction and intelligent design using LLMs and street-view imagery, with Xueyuan Road Subdistrict (Haidian, Beijing) as the case. Street-view images are semantically segmented with Mask2Former to extract pixel-level streetscape elements. GPT-5.1 then rates Safety, Aesthetic, and Vitality; compared with human ratings, it shows significant positive correlations at the 1 % level (Safety <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{r}=0.097, \mathrm{p}=0.009$</tex>; Aesthetic <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{r}=0.260, \mathrm{p}<0.001$</tex>; Vitality <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{r}=0.191, \mathrm{p}<0.001$</tex>), supporting its use as a proxy for large-scale perception mapping. Random forest regression further captures nonlinear element-perception relationships, identifying dimension-specific drivers: Safety is most influenced by sky, people, and sidewalk; Aesthetic by vegetation and sky; and Vitality by facade, road, and people, with clear nonlinear response patterns.

title: Bifunctional Metasurface for Simultaneous Absorption and Polarization Conversion
authors: Thayana Mayrink Lessa de Sousa, Maurício Weber Benjó da Silva, Pedro Vladimir Gonzalez Castellanos, Antônio Luiz P. de Siqueira Campos, Francisco Diego Martins Nobre, Raimison Bezerra de Almeida
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This article presents a thin, single-layer metasurface capable of simultaneously achieving dual-band absorption (5 G and WLAN), and polarization conversion in the X-band. The structure is composed of sub-resonators formed by spirals and a square patch with a diagonal cut in the shape of a fishbone, printed on a substrate, placed over a solid ground plane, and covered by a superstrate. The unit cell exhibits absorption peaks of 94.18 % and 97.15 % at 3.58 and 5.40 GHz and a polarization conversion ratio (PCR) above <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 0 \%}$</tex> in the range from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8. 1 5 ~ G H z}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9. 4 6 ~ G H z}$</tex>. No equivalent single-layer structures have been reported in previous studies.

title: Implementation of an Agricultural Monitoring System Based on Vegetation Indices
authors: Renato Meléndez Meléndez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents the implementation of an agricultural monitoring system in San Clemente, Maule Region, Chile, using unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) equipped with multispectral sensors for maize cultivation. A flight at 100 m altitude generated orthomosaics and vegetation indices (NDVI, GNDVI, NDWI) with a spatial resolution of 6 cm pixel and positional accuracy of 1.4 cm after PPK correction. Critical areas were detected: 21 % of the crop showed low vigor (NDVI <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<0.4$</tex>), 14 % presented potential water stress (NDWI <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<0.2$</tex>), and 18 % indicated nutritional deficiencies (GNDVI <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<0.45$</tex>). Compared to traditional monitoring, UAV-based analysis reduced response time from 7 days to less than 48 hours, while estimating savings of 18 % in water and 12 % in fertilizers. The results demonstrate that integrating UAVs, GNSS and GIS significantly improves spatial precision, decision-making efficiency, and sustainability in maize cultivation, confirming the relevance of precision agriculture in rural Chile.

title: Comparative Analysis of VSI Vs. NPC Inverter Topologies in Grid-Connected PV Systems Under Rapid Irradiance Transitions
authors: Adolfo Gonzalez, Gianyacomo Zucchini, Osvaldo González, Raúl Gregor, Julio Pacher, Eduardo Espinosa
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This article presents a detailed comparison between two-level Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) and three-level Neutral Point Clamped (NPC) topologies in a two-stage grid-connected photovoltaic system (GCPVS), composed of solar panels, a boost converter, a VSI or NPC inverter, and an LCL filter. Key performance metrics such as root mean square error (RMSE), total harmonic distortion (THD), settling time, system efficiency, and power factor (PF) are evaluated under variable irradiance conditions. A synchronous <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d q 0$</tex> reference frame control strategy is implemented to ensure high-quality power injection. The inverter models are designed, simulated, and validated in MATLAB/Simulink. Unlike prior studies limited to static conditions, this work introduces a dynamic benchmark involving rapid irradiance transitions. Simulation results show that the VSI topology achieves faster transient response and slightly improved PF tracking, while the NPC achieves lower THD, enhanced voltage stability, and reduced RMSE. These findings support topology selection based on whether fast adaptation or power quality is prioritized in GCPVS design.

title: Structuring Ontologies for Bayesian Reasoning in LLM Contexts
authors: David Araya, Catalina Valle, Carla Taramasco
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have proven to be effective tools for generating coherent text and supporting decision making across domains. However, in complex settings such as healthcare, decision making requires reasoning under uncertainty, causal inference, and the ability to quantify risks or probabilities related to specific events. These capabilities are not fully supported by LLMs, in part due to their lack of an explicit, structured knowledge domain. An emerging line of research seeks to address this limitation by equipping LLMs with symbolic knowledge through ontologies. Some approaches even leverage LLMs to automatically generate ontologies from domain-specific documents by identifying classes, relationships, and properties. However, these automatically gener-ated ontologies often exhibit high structural variability, making it difficult to quantify decisions or compute probabilistic outcomes, especially in formal reasoning tasks like those found in medicine. This work proposes an architecture that constrains the generative freedom of the LLM through structured prompts which define expected entity types and semantic relations. This controlled output enables the automatic transformation of the resulting ontology into a Bayesian network, establishing conditional dependencies with associated probabilities. The resulting system supports both symbolic and probabilistic inference. The implementation uses LangChain and ChatGPT via API for ontology extraction, OWLready2 for formal ontology representation, and pgmpy for Bayesian network construction. The system was evaluated on a corpus of eight clinical documents related to oral cancer, successfully generating an interpretable ontology and a functional Bayesian network. Simulated responses demonstrated plausible risk estimates based on combinations of symptoms and observed factors, highlighting the potential of this integration to build decision support tools that not only explain their outputs but also quantify them in a transparent and auditable way.

title: From Language to Action: Small Language Model—Driven Humanoid Control with Visual Grounding Under the Model Context Protocol
authors: Rhimesh Lwagun, John Yare, Justin Lukose, Olasubomi Rufai, Justin Thomas, Yan Fang
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Humanoid robots operating in human-centered environments require seamless integration of perception, reasoning, and action while remaining accessible to non-expert users. Recent advances in Small Language Models (SLMs) and VisionLanguage Models (VLMs) offer new opportunities for languagedriven robot control, yet deploying these models in embodied humanoid systems remains challenging due to resource constraints and execution reliability. This paper presents a modular humanoid robot control framework that integrates SLM-based task planning and VLM-based visual grounding through the Model Context Protocol (MCP). MCP serves as a structured orchestration layer that enables language models to interact with perception modules and predefined robot action primitives in a controlled, tool-oriented manner. High-level natural language commands are decomposed by an SLM into executable action sequences, while VLM-based perception grounds linguistic references in open-world visual observations. Experimental evaluations demonstrate that the proposed system can execute multistep humanoid tasks, including object-directed navigation and manipulation, using natural language instructions without end-to-end policy learning. A comparative study across multiple SLM planners further shows that planning reliability depends strongly on interface-level compliance rather than syntactic correctness alone, with higher-compliance models achieving substantially improved task success rates. These results indicate that MCP-based modular integration of SLMs and VLMs provides a practical and scalable foundation for language-guided humanoid robot control, enabling reliable embodied execution while maintaining flexibility for future model and capability extensions.

title: A Computational Economic Complexity Model for Regional Economic Integration: Analysis of the EU, MERCOSUR, URUPABOL, and the AndeanCommunity
authors: Cristhian Marchuk, Luis Ríos, Arturo González, Sanny González, Gabriel Pereira, Christian von Lücken
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Regional Economic Integration is a process by which countries seek mutual benefits through the reduction of trade, social, and political barriers. This paper introduces a computational mathematical model grounded in Economic Complexity Theory to analyze economic blocs as unified entities. Four case studies are examined: the European Union, MERCOSUR, URUPABOL, and the Andean Community. Using real export data and complexity metrics, we identify the combined productive capacities of member countries. Results reveal that integration enhances product diversity and increases the ubiquity of exports within the bloc. The study demonstrates that regional integration boosts development and strengthens competitiveness in the global economy. The proposed methodological approach provides a novel tool for regional analysis and serves as a foundation for future strategies in economic cooperation and productive planning. This research contributes to understanding how collective capabilities can generate synergies that exceed individual national potentials, particularly in the context of Latin American regional development.

title: Study of Demagnetization in a Coreless Axial-Flux Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator for Small Hybrid Vertical-Axis Wind Turbines
authors: Alvaro Hoffer, Felipe Alarcón, Matís Jiménez, Diego Obando, Boris Pavez, Héctor Young, Javier Coloma, Jonathan Mac-kay, Felipe Ruiz, José Rodríguez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a study on the demagnetization of a coreless axial flux permanent magnet synchronous generator (CAFPMSG) for a small hybrid vertical-axis wind turbine (VAWT). Two harsh operating scenarios that could compromise the functionality of the generator are analyzed: an overload caused by high wind speeds, and a short-circuit fault in the generator connection line. The study involves a comprehensive system characterization including the hybrid VAWT, load controller, and generator, followed by numerical simulations to analyze the behavior of the PMs under the harsh operating scenarios. The results indicate that even under rated operating conditions, partial demagnetization of the permanent magnets (PMs) may occur. Under overload conditions, the demagnetization effects become more pronounced, resulting in a torque reduction of approximately 15% compared to the ideal condition. Furthermore, short-circuit analysis reveals that around 13% of the PM material becomes fully demagnetized, compromising the integrity of the machine.

title: Hybrid CNN-LSTM Framework for Automated Arrhythmia Detection from ECG Signals
authors: Kazi Redwan, Arnob Aich Anurag, Mustakim Ahmed, Sajedul Islam, Abu Shufian
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Arrhythmia detection from electrocardiogram (ECG) signals is a difficult task. The problem arises from variations in heartbeat patterns and the imbalance across different arrhythmia classes. This research presents a hybrid deep learning framework that combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks for automated arrhythmia classification. The CNN extracts spatial features such as waveform patterns, while the LSTM captures temporal dependencies across beats. The model was trained and evaluated using four benchmark ECG datasets with five arrhythmia classes: normal, supraventricular ectopic beat, ventricular ectopic beat, fusion beat, and unknown beat. The proposed CNN-LSTM model achieved a training accuracy of 96.6% and a validation accuracy of 97.1%, with validation loss converging to 0.094. Performance evaluation through confusion matrix, ROC curves, and precision-recall analysis showed excellent results for the normal class with precision and recall above 0.98. However, the minority classes, such as fusion and supraventricular ectopic beat, were less well classified because of imbalance. Overall, the hybrid CNN-LSTM model performed strongly and shows potential for reliable arrhythmia detection. These findings suggest that deep learning models can support clinicians in early and accurate diagnosis of cardiac conditions.

title: Quantum Resource Management in the NISQ Era: Challenges, Vision, and a Runtime Framework
authors: Marcos Lammers, Federico Hernán Holik, Alejandro Fernandez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Quantum computers represent a radical technological advancement in the way information is processed by using the principles of quantum mechanics to solve very complex problems that exceed the capabilities of classical systems. However, in the current NISQ era (Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum devices), the available hardware presents several limitations, such as a limited number of qubits, high error rates, and reduced coherence times. Efficient management of quantum resources, both physical (qubits, error rates, connectivity) and logical (quantum gates, algorithms, error correction), becomes particularly relevant in the design and deployment of quantum algorithms. In this work, we analyze the role of resources in the various uses of NISQ devices today, identifying their relevance and implications for software engineering focused on the use of quantum computers. We propose a vision for runtime-aware quantum software development, identifying key challenges to its realization, such as limited introspection capabilities and temporal constraints in current platforms. As a proof of concept, we introduce Qonscious, a prototype framework that enables conditional execution of quantum programs based on dynamic resource evaluation. With this contribution, we aim to strengthen the field of Quantum Resource Estimation (QRE) and move towards the development of scalable, reliable, and resource-aware quantum software.

title: Design of DSSS Signal Receiver for Real-Time Decision Adjustment
authors: Ying Tian, Wei Chen
publish: 2025 2nd World Conference on Computer and Information Security (WCCIS)
abstract: To enhance the anti-interference performance of wireless communication systems in Gaussian white noise environments, the study designs and implements a direct sequence spread spectrum (DSSS) signal receiver based on FPGA. Design with pseudo-random code synchronization as the core, using linear feedback shift register (LFSR) to generate msequences as spread codes, implementing signal carrier shift through demodulation, and combining with relevant integrators to complete the spread decision. In response to dynamic channel noise, the receiver introduces an adaptive threshold control module, which adjusts the decision threshold in real time through a sliding average algorithm to suppress the impact of sudden noise on the integration results. Hardware testing has verified that the receiver has complete functionality, with a demodulation synchronization error of less than 0.5 symbols, and can operate stably in low signal-to-noise ratio scenarios. This design provides a highly reliable solution for wireless communication devices in noisy environments.

title: Effect of Shift-Left Security Testing on Early Vulnerability Detection in CI/CD Pipelines
authors: Yogesh S. Thanvi, Karthik Pappu, Ankit Parashar
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This study synthesizes evidence from academic research, industry reports, and regulatory frameworks to evaluate the technical, operational, and organizational impacts of shiftleft adoption in cloud-native CI/CD pipelines. The results indicate that early security integration improves the mean time to detect (MTTD), reduces the severity and frequency of latestage vulnerabilities, and increases pipeline reliability by solving problems before deployment. Beyond technical results, shiftleft practices support more predictable workflows, strengthen developer engagement, and distribute security responsibilities across engineering teams. The study also identifies limitations that affect the effectiveness of shift-left, including tool noise, false positives, limited exploitability context, and the inability of static tools to detect runtime-specific vulnerabilities. Differences in automation maturity, security culture, and workflow integration further shape adoption outcomes. When supported by calibrated tooling, governance alignment, and complementary runtime defenses, shift-left practices provide a practical and scalable pathway to strengthen security posture and operational resilience in cloudnative CI/CD ecosystems.

authors: N/A
