Because chirality is bred by frustration, the spin system will be in a frustrated state due to chirality caused by twisting strain into the torsion-connected loop. In a current study, for every of the separate string-open DNA particles, we projected their melting trends and the repercussions are in exceptional synchronization through the analysis. It is discovered that the free_energy/length agrees well with the value determined by other writers.

title: A Source of Income for Elderly People: Opportunities in Oral Storytelling
authors: Lucio Cañete Arratia, Pablo Adasme, Enrique San Juan Urrutia
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This article presents a ranking of state actions promoting private demand for oral storytelling services performed by older adults in Chile. Considering the cultural richness of oral storytelling and the current and projected economic hardship of many older adults, the research explores mechanisms that allow this performing art to be positioned as a legitimate source of income, regardless of subsidies. The methodology includes a literature review, artificial intelligence support, expert interviews, a preferential ranking technique using the Analytic Hierarchy Process, and impact simulation using Monte Carlo. The result is an inventory of state actions according to their projected performance in the art market, in the following order: NonTransferable Incentives, Training, Advertising, PublicPrivate Partnerships, and Promotion of Cultural Projects. It is hoped that this article on Industrial Cybernetics will contribute to alleviating the hardship of older adults and improving state management.

title: A Comparative Study of Api Deployment on On-Prem, EC2, and AWS Lambda
authors: Sowmya Chintakindi
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Choosing the most suitable environment is always challenging for organizations. They often face trade-offs among performance, latency, maintenance, and operational costs. This study conducts a holistic comparative analysis of three deployment models: On-Prem Windows machines, cloud-based AWS EC2 windows instances, and serverless architectures using AWS Lambda with DynamoDB. An identical RESTful API workload was implemented across each environment and tested under varying loads to measure latency, scalability, operational overhead, and cost. The study indicates that the On-Prem setup delivers the most stable performance in terms of lower response times, low latency, and minimal cold start issues. The Serverless setup excels in scalability, reliability, and reduced maintenance, despite occasional cold start delays. The Cloud environment provides a middle ground, offering moderate stability and performance while balancing ease of management and scalability.

title: A Medical Insurance Question Answering System Based on Multi-Strategy Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Haolin Yan, Huawei Song
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: With the growing demand for medical insurance question answering (QA) systems, providing accurate and reliable services amidst complex insurance policies, terminological synonyms, and cross-entity relationships has become a significant challenge. Existing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) methods, while effectively combining information retrieval with generation models, often face issues such as unstable performance across different scenarios and increased generation noise due to their simple fusion methods. To address the aforementioned issues, this paper proposes the Tri-Router, a multi-strategy retrievalaugmented generation framework. It combines three complementary strategies: sparse retrieval (BM25), dense retrieval (DPR), and knowledge graph retrieval (Graph-RAG), and dynamically predicts optimal modal weights via a Transformer attention router to achieve fine-grained retrieval dispatching. Through this mechanism, this paper significantly enhances the retrieval accuracy and generation quality of the QA system in complex scenarios. Experimental results show that the method based on this framework surpasses traditional methods on multiple evaluation metrics, and ablation studies demonstrate the effectiveness of the dynamic routing mechanism and the tri-modal complementary structure.

title: BiFPN-Enhanced Multi-Scale Detection for Laser-Guided Fungal Remediation in Plant Tissue Culture
authors: Xinyi Gao, Xin Qi, Liwen Hou, Yongming Fu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Plant tissue culture success rates are critically hampered by fungal contamination, causing seedling loss and substantial resource waste. This paper presents an enhanced object detection framework for real-time identification of seedlings and contaminating fungi, enabling precise laser-based treatment. We adopt YOLO11n as baseline and integrate Bidirectional Feature Pyramid Network (BiFPN) with learnable weighted fusion to address extreme scale variations between large seedlings and small fungal colonies. Experiments on a custom dataset of 3,000 tissue culture images demonstrate our method achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$83.2 \% \operatorname{mAP} {@} 0.5$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$60.8 \% \operatorname{mAP} {@} 0.5: 0.95$</tex>, outperforming baseline by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{+ 3. 6 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{+ 4. 5 \%}$</tex> respectively, while maintaining 171 FPS inference speed suitable for embedded deployment. Ablation studies confirm each component's contribution, validating the design for practical laser treatment systems in plant biotechnology applications.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Real-Time IoMT Monitoring with LSTM-Based Predictive Anomaly Detection
authors: Shreya Swar, Suman Bhattacharya, Promita Chakraborty, Ananjan Maiti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: The Internet of Medical Things has driven a boom in real-time patient monitoring. However, it also poses critical challenges related to parameters such as latency, reliability, and data integrity. The next generation of IoMT predictive systems is strengthened through blockchain integration. The physiological signals (heart rate, oxygen saturation, body temperature) collected during exercise from wearables will be processed in real time by a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network deployed at the edge, with sub-second response time. Anomaly detection will occur before an emergency arises. Every detected anomaly will be stored immutably on a permissioned blockchain, ensuring cryptographic integrity. As a result, this ensures auditability while preserving patient privacy. The experiments conducted on the TAD dataset at scale show that the detection accuracy is 98.2 % with only 35 ms end-to-end latency, which is better than the cloud-only solutions. Security analysis shows that the blockchain's consensus mechanism detects and rejects malicious data injections. Further, the data's provenance is maintained under the pressure of an adversary. Main contributions are (i) an edge-based LSTM technology for ultra-low-latency predictive anomaly detection, (ii) a tamper-resistant anomaly logging and blockchain-enforced integrity mechanism, and (iii) significant resilience against sophisticated attacks in telemedicine scenarios. A scalable, secure, and patient-friendly system for remote health monitoring can be achieved through a two-layer design that combines edge intelligence and decentralised trust to enable telemedicine platforms.

title: Adaptive Intrusion Detection in Edge-Cloud Environments Using Reinforcement Learning
authors: A K M Nuruzzaman Laskar, Khandaker Abu Raihan, Khandakar Rabbi Ahmed, Gohar Masood Khan, Md Kauser Miah, Sunil Kumar Goyal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Traditional, static intrusion detection models are rendered ineffective by the constant exposure of modern edge cloud networks to dynamic, complex cyber threats. Nevertheless, many of these systems have high false-negative rates in real-time settings because they are unable to adjust to changing attack behaviors. In order to close this gap, this work suggests an adaptive Reinforcement Learning-based intrusion detection system, or RL-IDS, that optimizes decisions using a reward function intended to reduce false negatives and formulates network flow classification as a Markov Decision Process. Then, robust scaling, duplicate record removal, and binary attack labeling are used to preprocess the CIC-IDS2017 flow-based dataset. CNN, LSTM, XGBoost, and Logistic Regression are the baselines against which the RL-IDS is trained and evaluated. According to experimental results, the RL-IDS significantly outperforms all baseline models, achieving 99.26% accuracy, 98.07% precision, 97.04% recall, and a 97.55% F1-score with an AUC of 0.9972. Additionally, it shows excellent class-wise performance and ensures the best detection of uncommon and covert attacks like Heartbleed and Infiltration. Our main contribution is to demonstrate that, far more effectively than any traditional supervised approach to IDS, reinforcement learning enables continuous, iterative policy refinement to improve detection performance and robustness in dynamic edge-cloud settings.

title: A Transmitter Architecture for Digital-Pre-Distortion of Power Amplifiers for Systems with Insufficient Sampling-Rate
authors: Walid K. M. Ahmed, Laurence Mailaender
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: With the ever-increasing demand for data rates and low-power wireless, mobile and IoT devices that operate off of limited power supply such as battery or solar-cells, more efficient, hence non-linear, power-amplifiers (PA's) for wireless transmitters continue to be in demand and the need for wideband digital pre-distortion (DPD) operation is increasing. Such demand has become specially the case with the advent of high-speed RF system-on-chips (RFSoCs) FPGA/ASIC commercial products nowadays, since it is now possible to aggregate (super-impose) multiple sparsely-separated carrier signals on the same transmitter antenna path. Thus, increasing the instantaneous bandwidth (IBW) required for the path by much more than the individual bandwidths of the component carriers. In this paper, we propose a “true” beyond-Nyquist-rate DPD architecture that does not require ADC/DAC rates more than what's available and Nyquist-suitable for the transmit signal BW, but still achieves full capture of the entire wideband spectrally-regrown PA output. We demonstrate that our approach is capable of correcting the nonlinearity all the way out to the band edge as accurately as using the much higher Nyquist sampling-rate. We demonstrate the performance of our novel architecture using both least-square (LS) and machine-learning (ML) calculation approaches of the PA memory-polynomial transfer function, and we also compare to sub-optimal architectures we have attempted to exploit the multi-band sparsity of the component carriers.

title: sCNN: An Improved Convolutional Neural Network for Early Detection of Alzheimer's Disease
authors: Subhabrata Roy, Gyanendra Kumar Maurya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Diagnosing neurodegenerative diseases has improved significantly with better medical imaging, a crucial development for managing progressive conditions like Alzheimer's disease (AD). Since undetected dementia can evolve into AD, identifying the associated structural brain changes is essential. To address this, we developed a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that differentiates AD from healthy brain scans. We modified the architecture by using the Swish activation function rather than the conventional ReLU. Our testing confirmed that this modification improved the model's performance, demonstrating that a Swish-enhanced CNN is a robust solution for early diagnosis.

title: A Hybrid Geometric-Learning Framework for Dynamic Object Determination in RGB-D SLAM
authors: Yu Bian, Mingwu Ren
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Dynamic objects severely degrade the robustness of visual SLAM by introducing unstable features and inconsistent geometry. To address this challenge, we propose a hybrid geometric-learning approach for reliable dynamic object determination. Our method first models depth measurement noise and evaluates global motion consistency using an uncertainty-aware Mahalanobis metric. To capture local non-rigid motion, we introduce a Robust Percentile Hausdorff measure that detects subtle geometric inconsistencies while suppressing outliers. In addition, a lightweight PointNet module predicts point-level dynamic probabilities from local geometric features. The three cues are jointly fused to generate a refined dynamic mask, which filters dynamic regions before tracking and optimization. We evaluated the proposed approach by integrating it into classical SLAM front-ends using representative dynamic sequences. Experimental results demonstrate that it improves pose accuracy and robustness in dynamic scenes.

title: Zero-Shot Recommendation via LLM-Generated Synthetic Interaction Data
authors: Yike Peng, Lu Chang, Yuan Tang, Jiaxuan Li, Tian Zhang, Qin Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Zero-shot recommendation is one of the most challenging issues in personalized information retrieval, as new users and items lack historical interaction records to support collaborative filtering. This paper proposes a new zero-shot recommendation model that leverages large language models (LLMs) to generate synthetic interaction data that simulates authentic user-item behavioral data. The model does not rely on heuristic cold-start procedures; instead, it constructs pseudo-profiles based on controlled prompt engineering, domain-conditioned conditioning, and refined preferences. These synthetic interactions are then introduced into the recommendation pipeline as a hybrid architecture that combines embedding initialization, cross-modal alignment, and optimization for downstream recommenders. The three benchmark datasets obtained via experiments demonstrate substantial improvements in synthetic data generated by the LLM over state-of-the-art zero-shot baselines. The findings suggest that the large-capacity generative models could serve as latent preference simulators to enable scalable cold-start solutions without any annotations or even exposure to personal user data. This work's contributions include an end-to-end operational pipeline, a conceptual formulation of interaction synthesis based on LLMs, and a formal analysis demonstrating the functionality and effectiveness of the LLCM-enhanced zero-shot recommendation proposal.

title: Escaping Local Minima: A Finite-Time Markov Chain Analysis of Constant-Temperature Simulated Annealing
authors: Hansini Ramachandran, Bhaskar Krishnamachari
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Simulated Annealing (SA) is a widely used stochastic optimization algorithm, yet much of its theoretical understanding is limited to asymptotic convergence guarantees or general spectral bounds. In this paper, we develop a finite-time analytical framework for constant-temperature SA by studying a piecewise linear cost function that permits exact characterization. We model SA as a discrete-state Markov chain and first derive a closed-form expression for the expected time to escape a single linear basin in a one-dimensional landscape. We show that this expression also accurately predicts the behavior of continuous-state search up to a constant scaling factor, which we analyze empirically and explain via variance matching, demonstrating convergence to a factor of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sqrt{3}$</tex> in certain regimes. We then extend the analysis to a two-basin landscape containing a local and a global optimum, obtaining exact expressions for the expected time to reach the global optimum starting from the local optimum, as a function of basin geometry, neighborhood radius, and temperature. Finally, we demonstrate how the predicted basin escape time can be used to guide the design of a simple two-temperature switching strategy.

title: Patch Based Diffusion for Rib Fracture Synthesis in Chest Radiographs
authors: Shang-Lin Chung, Yu-Ling Chen, Chi-Tung Cheng, Chien-Hung Liao, I-Fang Chung
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Rib fracture detection in chest X-ray (CXR) images suffers from a scarcity of large-scale annotated data, leading to suboptimal model sensitivity. Since fractures occupy a small, localized region, augmenting the entire image often fails to specifically target the pathology. In this study, we propose a patch-based augmentation strategy utilizing specific rib regions to effectively increase the available fracture images. Our method integrates three models: a Rib Region Segmentation Network (RibSegNet) to segment the rib cage, a Fracture-Augmenting Diffusion Model (FracDiff) to generate fracture patches, and a Fracture Classification Network (FracNet) for final classification. After incorporating the augmented images, the model shows consistent improvements across sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, F1-score, and AUC. These results indicate that the synthetic patches help the model better capture fracture-related patterns and support the utility of the proposed augmentation strategy.

title: ALMA-IA: A Real-Time System for Automatic Analog Gauge Reading Using Edge Devices and Deep Learning
authors: Micaela Jara Ten Kathen, Maximiliano Bonnin, Ariel Guerrero, Mario Arzamendia
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents ALMA-IA, a real-time system based on edge devices and deep learning for the automated reading of analog gauges. The main objective is to support the digital transformation of industrial environments by offering a low-cost solution that does not require replacing existing equipment. Manual gauge reading remains common but is prone to human error, longer operational times, and increased costs. ALMA-IA is proposed as an alternative, combining computer vision and deep learning techniques to automatically extract gauge readings. To validate the system, an edge device equipped with an integrated camera was calibrated and used to capture images of analog gauges. These images were transmitted via WiFi to a local server, where they were processed using deep learning models. The results confirm the technical feasibility of the proposed system, demonstrating its ability to operate in real-time with high accuracy. Tests conducted in both simulated and real-world environments showed a mean absolute error below 0.01, a mean absolute percentage error under 7%, and a coefficient of determination above 0.98. Limitations observed during implementation include a 7% frame-level failure rate, primarily due to missed detections of the needle tip and visual degradation introduced by grayscale conversion.

title: Multivariate Fields of Experts for Convergent Image Reconstruction
authors: Stanislas Ducotterd, Michael Unser
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Imaging
abstract: We introduce the multivariate fields of experts, a new framework for the learning of image priors. Our model generalizes existing fields of experts methods by incorporating multivariate potential functions constructed via Moreau envelopes of the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\ell _\infty$</tex-math></inline-formula>-norm. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposal across a range of inverse problems that include image denoising, deblurring, compressed-sensing magnetic-resonance imaging, and computed tomography. The proposed approach outperforms comparable univariate models and achieves performance close to that of deep-learning-based regularizers while being significantly faster, requiring fewer parameters, and being trained on substantially fewer data. In addition, our model retains a high level of interpretability due to its structured design. It is supported by theoretical convergence guarantees which ensure reliability in sensitive reconstruction tasks.

title: RadioMapMotion: A Dataset and Benchmark for Proactive Spatio-Temporal Radio Environment Prediction
authors: Nan Cheng, Honggang Jia, Xiucheng Wang, Haixia Peng, Ruijin Sun, Conghao Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Radio maps (RMs), which provide location-dependent pathloss estimations, serve as a foundational enabler for proactive and environment-aware communications in 6G networks. However, most existing deep learning-based RM construction methods treat dynamic environments as a series of independent static snapshots, thereby omitting the temporal continuity inherent in signal propagation variations induced by the movement of dynamic entities. To overcome this limitation, we investigate the task of spatio-temporal RM prediction, which entails forecasting a sequence of future maps based on historical observations. A major challenge in this predictive paradigm lies in the absence of datasets that capture the continuous evolution of environmental dynamics. To fill this gap, we introduce RadioMapMotion, the first large-scale public dataset comprising continuous RM sequences generated from physically consistent vehicular trajectories. As a benchmark for this task, we further propose RadioMotionNet, a UNet architecture incorporating Convolutional Long Short-Term Memory (ConvLSTM) modules, tailored for multi-step sequence forecasting. Experimental evaluations show that RadioMotionNet achieves superior prediction accuracy and structural fidelity compared to representative baselines, while maintaining low inference latency, indicating its potential for real-time network deployment. Our project will be publicly released at https://github.com/UNIC-Lab/RadioMapMotion upon paper acceptance.

title: Pesaj’s Code: We Choose the Road! Building identity through creative programming
authors: Erika Victoria Sztyrle
publish: IEEE Revista Iberoamericana de Tecnologias del Aprendizaje
abstract: In April 2025, third-grade students at Arlene Fern Community School engaged in Pesaj’s Code: We Choose the Road!, an interdisciplinary project developed around Pesaj (Passover), the Jewish holiday commemorating the Israelites’ liberation from slavery in ancient Egypt. The initiative invited children to explore Jewish history, values, and identity through digital storytelling. Using ScratchJr, students collaborated in small groups to animate key moments of the Exodus narrative, integrating Spanish, Hebrew, and coding as a triple-literacy creative experience. The project followed the Coding as Another Language (CAL) framework developed by Prof. Marina Umaschi Bers, which positions programming as a medium for meaning-making, expression, communication, and ethical reflection. Students storyboarded scenes, selected characters and actions, incorporated Hebrew vocabulary, and translated their ideas into code while collaboratively shaping interpretations of the Exodus narrative. Educators’ reflections highlight how coding functioned not only as a technical skill supporting computational thinking but also as a language for cultural storytelling, enabling students to connect with Jewish traditions while expressing values such as freedom, resilience, and empowerment. This paper describes the design, implementation, and outcomes of the project, illustrating how CAL principles can support social-emotional learning and values-based, cross-curricular experiences in early elementary education.

title: Self-Reconfiguration Planning for Deformable Quadrilateral Modular Robots
authors: Jie Gu, Hongrun Gao, Zhihao Xia, Yirun Sun, Chunxu Tian, Dan Zhang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: While deformable modular self reconfigurable robots offer enhanced reconfiguration flexibility, strict kinematic constraints present complex self reconfiguration planning challenges. This letter presents a novel self-reconfiguration planning algorithm for deformable quadrilateral MSRRs. The method first constructs feasible connect/disconnect actions using a virtual graph representation, and then organizes these actions into a valid execution sequence through a Dependence-based Reverse Tree (DRTree) that resolves interdependencies. We also prove that reconfiguration sequences satisfying motion characteristics exist for any pair of configurations with seven or more modules (excluding linear topologies). Finally, comparisons with a modified BiRRT algorithm highlight the superior efficiency and stability of our approach, while deployment on a physical robotic platform confirms its practical feasibility.

title: Calibration of Error Distributions in Robot Kinematics for Increased Precision in Manipulation Tasks
authors: Tim Gerstewitz, Peter Lehner, Lukas Burkhard, Natalija Topalovic, Alin Albu-Schäffer, Daniel Leidner, Máximo A. Roa
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: The accuracy of robotic forward kinematics is commonly improved by calibration. However, most calibration methods only take deterministic errors, such as inaccurate geometry and unknown stiffnesses, into account and neglect errors with stochastic characteristics, including joint friction, gear backlash and component wear. In order to incorporate these effects, this paper presents a calibration procedure for a probabilistic forward kinematics model which identifies both systematic errors and error sources whose combined effects are modeled as distributions within a kinematic chain. Additionally, we present a method for using the identified distributions to enhance task-relevant accuracy by minimizing the end-effector uncertainty resulting from such distributions in a task-specific way. Finally, this idea is demonstrated in an experiment with a research rover tasked with picking up a payload box from a lander. Here, we show that end-effector error in task-relevant directions can be reduced by 40% by choosing low-uncertainty over high-uncertainty configurations.

title: Concentration and Diversity in Collaboration Networks in Electrical Engineering: Evidence from Brazilian Researchers
authors: Fernando Almeida do Carmo, Gustavo S. Silva, Raimundo C. S. Freire, Antonio Fernando Lavareda Jacob Junior, Fábio Manoel Franca Lobato
publish: IEEE Latin America Transactions
abstract: Scientific collaboration networks shape research dynamics in fields such as Electrical Engineering, where Brazils CNPq productivity grants play a pivotal role. Expanding upon previous analyzes focused on Microelectronics, this study broadens the scope to encompass the major field of Electrical Engineering, covering seven subareas: Electrical Materials; Electrical, Magnetic, and Electronic Measurements; Instrumentation; Electrical, Magnetic, and Electronic Circuits; Power Systems; Industrial Electronics, Systems, and Electronic Controls; and Telecommunications. The analysis considers collaboration networks among 419 CNPq productivity grant holders based on 18,386 publications. The results reveal a hierarchical structure in which top-tier researchers form a highly influential core, while lower-level researchers remain peripheral. The network exhibits a pronounced geographic concentration in Brazils Southeast and South regions, accompanied by notable gender disparities. Over time, the average author influence has increased, and collaboration clusters have become more compact, suggesting a trend toward smaller, more centralized groups. Despite its thematic diversity, the network exhibits structural inequality and limited cross-level integration. Future research could further investigate temporal shifts, inter-institutional collaboration patterns, and the co-evolution of network structures and research focuses.

title: An Enhanced Fire Perception Framework for Firefighting Robots: ECA-BiFPN Boosted YOLO-EB with Multi-Modal Fusion
authors: Botao Ni, Lei Huang, Ying Xiang, Yan Zhu, Lin Li, Yunfei Zhou, Jingjing Yang, Hao Tang
publish: IEEE Latin America Transactions
abstract: To address the challenges posed by complex fire environments to flame perception and situational assessment in firefighting robots, this paper proposes a multimodal perception method integrating flame segmentation, spatial localization, and situational awareness. Based on an enhanced YOLO-EB segmentation network, this approach combines stereo vision and spectral information to achieve precise flame detection and localization. The YOLO-EB network incorporates an Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) mechanism and a Bidirectional Feature Pyramid Network (BiFPN) to enhance its representational capabilities, effectively balancing accuracy and real-time performance. Ablation experiments demonstrate that the synergistic effect of these modules significantly improves model performance: compared to the YOLOv8-seg baseline, the proposed model achieves a flame recall of 0.680 and a mean average precision (mAP) of 0.821. Leveraging high-quality segmentation masks, a stereo-spectral fusion perception framework is constructed, achieving precise flame localization at medium distances with an average error of 23%. Through dynamic fusion of spectral and visible-light features, the system attains an average situational awareness accuracy of 92.45%, significantly outperforming single-modal methods. Experimental results confirm that this integrated approach provides firefighting robots with stable and reliable capabilities for early fire detection, accurate localization, and dynamic assessment, demonstrating strong potential for practical deployment.

title: Joint Device Clustering, Secure Collaborative Service Caching, and Resource Allocation in Ultra-Dense MEC Networks
authors: Tianqing Zhou, Guangqiang Dai, Fei Tang, Xuan Li, Chunguo Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: When computational tasks are offloaded to mobile edge computing (MEC) servers, service caching can reduce both real-time latency and bandwidth costs associated with retrieving and setting up service applications. This paper investigates the problem of secure collaborative service caching and task offloading to minimize the total network energy consumption. Specifically, we jointly optimize mobile device (MD) association, auxiliary base station (BS) selection, task execution decisions, security service assignment, caching decisions, MD clustering, and task allocation, subject to constraints such as task deadlines, security costs, and the maximum BS storage capacity. The goal is to minimize energy consumption across the entire network. Given the nonlinear and mixed-integer nature of the optimization problem, we employ an improved immune algorithm (IIA) to solve it, and also analyze the algorithm's convergence and computational complexity. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed IIA significantly reduces total network energy consumption compared to benchmark algorithms.

title: Random Latent Exploration-Based Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Multi-UAV Path Planning
authors: Jinchao Chen, Yuwei Li, Qing Zhou, Zhaohui Liu, Qinwei Zhang, Weihua Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have become increasingly popular in civilian and military applications due to their low cost and high maneuverability. Multi-UAV path planning is a highly challenging problem with an NP-hard complexity, and frequently solved by machine learning algorithms, especially in dynamic and open environments. However, the sparse-reward problem in traditional machine learning algorithms often makes solutions trapped in local optima and results in unsatisfactory f light paths, seriously affecting the coordination effect of UAVs. In this paper, we propose an enhanced random latent exploration based multi-agent reinforcement learning framework to provide a reasonable flight path for each UAV and efficiently achieve the group missions. First, we analyse the constraints and objectives of the multi-UAV path planning problem and abstract it as a multi-constraint combinatorial optimization one. Then, inspired by exploration bonus and randomized value function mechanisms, we propose a random latent exploration-based multi-agent reinforcement learning framework to enable UAVs to acquire more diverse rewards in path planning. Simulation experiments in a multi-agent particle environment are conducted to verify the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed approach. Compared with baselines, our approach shows significant advantages and achieves a 71.2% increase in average reward, a 46.5% improvement in success rate, a 26% reduction in flight time, and a 33.6% reduction in path length.

title: Observer-Based Adaptive Fuzzy Assistance Steering Control for Vehicle Path Tracking Considering Driver Manipulate Error
authors: Wenfeng Li, Zheng Lian, Junru Jia, Pak Kin Wong, Zhengchao Xie, Jing Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Due to the abnormal conditions such as fatigue, distraction, and emotional instability, the driver is inevitably to make manipulate errors to reduce the driving safety. Thus, this paper investigates the observer-based adaptive fuzzy assistance steering control problem for vehicle path tracking taking the driver manipulate errors into account. Firstly, considering the abnormal steering behaviors, the driver manipulate errors are described and integrated into the global driver-vehicle-road system. The interval type-2 fuzzy method is introduced to handle the parameter uncertainties of both the driver steering behavior and vehicle dynamics. Secondly, to obtain the information of driver manipulate errors, a fast adaptive observer is proposed to estimate the driver steering torque. This observer can effectively improve the accuracy of torque estimation by taking into account the time-varying properties of the driver torque. Thirdly, to compensate the driver manipulate errors, an adaptive fuzzy assistance steering control method is proposed to enhance the vehicle path tracking performance and dynamic stability. Moreover, an event-based mechanism is incorporated into the control loop to minimize communication overhead. Finally, comparative experiments are conducted to assess the controller, and the results indicate that the proposed control method can significantly enhance the safety and stability of vehicle path tracking.

title: Message from the Chairs: SMC-IT 2025
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Space Mission Challenges for Information Technology (SMC-IT)
abstract: SMC-IT was launched in 2003 (Pasadena) to create a single venue where the space-mission community and the information-technology community could interact across organizational and national boundaries. Over the years, the conference has drawn participants from a deliberately broad mix of backgrounds, including U.S. and non-U.S. civil space agencies, U.S. national laboratories, U.S. defense organizations, academia in the U.S. and abroad, and the international space industry. The 11th SMC-IT took place July 28 — August 1, 2025, at the California Science Center in Los Angeles, California, and was co-located with the 16th International Conference on Space Computing (SCC). Co-location strengthened the joint mission of both meetings: giving system designers, engineers, scientists, practitioners, and space explorers a shared forum for technical interchange on the hardware and software that enable IT applications for space missions.

title: Dstformer: Leveraging Dynamic Spatial-Temporal Graphs and Transformer Architecture for Short-Term Wind Speed Forecasting
authors: Haonan Cui, Jiayi Cao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Short-term wind speed forecasting is critical for integrating renewable energy into power grids and ensuring operational reliability of wind farms. However, existing methods face three major limitations: (1) they rely on static spatial graphs that fail to capture time-varying inter-station correlations driven by dynamic atmospheric processes, (2) they struggle to model abrupt wind speed fluctuations caused by weather regime transitions, and (3) they neglect the multi-scale temporal dependencies inherent in wind dynamics spanning turbulent micro-scales to synoptic macro-scales. We propose DSTFormer, a Dynamic Spatial-Temporal Transformer framework that addresses these challenges through three key innovations: (1) a Dynamic Similarity Distance Matrix (DSDM) mechanism that adaptively constructs graph topologies based on instantaneous wind flow patterns, enabling the model to capture evolving spatial correlations, (2) a DSDM-guided spatial attention module that aggregates information from meteorologically coupled stations with time-varying weights, and (3) a multi-scale temporal encoder using dilated causal convolutions to simultaneously extract shortterm turbulent features and long-range synoptic patterns. Extensive experiments on three real-world hourly wind datasets demonstrate that DSTFormer achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5. 2 - 3 0 \%}$</tex> improvement in MAE over state-of-the-art baselines including Transformer and graph neural network architectures, with consistent performance gains across different geographic locations and forecast horizons.

title: Trend-Aware Tinyml Co-Design on Microcontrollers: Quantization, Structured Sparsity, and On-Device Adaptation Under Tight Memory-Energy Budgets
authors: Naga Sujitha Vummaneni, Adarsh Mittal, Ishan Kumar, Srilakshmi Bharadwaj, Himani Varshney
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Tiny machine learning (TinyML) is evolving from “small-model inference” into a full-stack systems discipline that couples model design, compiler/runtime choices, kernel libraries, and (increasingly) on-device adaptation. This paper presents a quantitative, simulation-driven research study of modern TinyML trends on microcontroller-class platforms, focusing on the accuracy—latency—energy—memory trade-offs induced by (i) post-training INT8 quantization, (ii) structured pruning/sparsity that is compatible with embedded kernels, and (iii) lightweight on-device adaptation via partial fine-tuning or small adapters. We propose a trend-aware methodology that unifies these techniques through a common cost model, and we provide a budgetaware selection rule that makes the technique choices explicit under hard flash/SRAM limits. Using four representative workloads (keyword spotting, visual wake words, human activity recognition, and a lightweight vision-transformer proxy) and a cycle/energy model calibrated to typical Cortex-M deployments, our results show that INT8 inference delivers a consistent <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.8 2.2 \times$</tex> latency reduction over FP32 baselines while shrinking weight storage by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \times$</tex>. Structured sparsity yields a further 1.4 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.9 \times$</tex> speedup when kernel support and memory layouts exploit the structure; otherwise, gains are limited by packing overhead and activation SRAM. We also identify a recurring “knee region” where peak activations dominate SRAM and small architectural changes cause large deployability differences. Finally, we quantify multi-device scaling (sensor hub + main MCU) under bursty arrivals and show that splitting inference across <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 - 4}$</tex> MCUs can reduce deadline miss rate by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3}-\mathbf{6} \times$</tex> at high offered load.

title: Copyright Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 14th International Conference on Power Science and Engineering (ICPSE)

title: Quantifying Cyber-Risk in Commercial Egg Production: A Stochastic Simulation Approach
authors: Gilbert Munoz Cornejo, Margaret Barth, Sydeena Isaacs, Kathryn Hoy, John Arrowood
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The U.S. food-and-agriculture sector runs on Operational Technology (OT) and cyber attackers target it. We modeled an in-line egg-powder processing line as a stochastic discrete-event simulation in SimPy and quantified cyber-induced production loss. The route spans washing, grading, candling, breaking, pasteurization, drying, storage, and packing. We inject OT attacks that halt conveyance, blind inspection, and corrupt thermal setpoints, including a bundled ransomware “kill-chain.” Over 100 replications, availability attacks reduce packed output by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 33 \%$</tex> and drive backlog accumulation, while dryer tampering shifts output into <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 12 \mathrm{k}$</tex> eggs/day of quality discard <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(16-21 \%)$</tex>. Global sensitivity analysis shows pasteurization temperature/hold time creates a sharp where small degradations cross the minimum-equivalent threshold, shifting output from compliant to noncompliant/held product. Results are reported for a reference configuration using synthetic public-source parameters to support relative risk ranking, not plant-specific prediction.

title: Customer Financial Fraud Detection and Risk Profiling Using a Cloud-Native Swarm of AI Agents on AWS
authors: Vimal Pradeep Venugopal
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Financial institutions increasingly require real-time fraud detection and risk profiling systems that can operate at scale while satisfying regulatory requirements for explainability, auditability, and fault isolation. Traditional rule-based systems and monolithic machine learning pipelines struggle to adapt to rapidly changing market conditions and heterogeneous data sources. This paper proposes a cloud-native, agentic architecture for customer financial fraud detection and risk profiling using a swarm of autonomous AI agents orchestrated on AWS. The proposed system decomposes risk assessment into specialized agents responsible for customer profiling, transaction analysis, repayment behavior evaluation, market condition analysis, and decision summarization. These agents collaborate through an event-driven orchestration layer with shared contextual memory, enabling parallel execution, resilience, and adaptive reasoning. The architecture leverages AWS Strands Agents, Amazon Bedrock for foundation model inference, and managed cloud services such as Amazon S3, DynamoDB, and Simple Notification Service (SNS). Experimental results from a prototype implementation demonstrate improved modularity, scalability, and interpretability compared to centralized risk analysis approaches. The proposed design illustrates how agentic AI systems support regulated financial workflows while maintaining transparency and operational robustness.

title: Understanding Stability of Choices: Toward Robust Choice-Based Authentication in Cybersecurity
authors: Sayanton Dibbo, Sudip Vhaduri, Samuel Gomez, Andrea Gajic
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Traditional security questions (e.g., mother's maiden name or first school) are easy to implement and use, but vulnerable to adversarial guessing or research, due to the increasing availability of personal information online, which reduces their effectiveness in cybersecurity authentication. Choice-based security questions offer a promising alternative by leveraging stable personal preferences that are harder for adversaries to predict. Prior studies have not comprehensively examined the temporal stability and consistency of users' responses to a wide range of choice-based questions and their potential to improve the authentication. In this work, we study and analyze responses from 276 participants to nine choice-based questions (e.g., favorite color, number, season, day, animal, sport, month, food, and artist), collected three times with one-month intervals. Results show that questions on color, number, season, day, and animal exhibit high stability over a 2 -month period, while maintaining high uncertainty against adversarial guessing, making them reliable candidates for robust authentication. In contrast, sport, month, food, and artist questions demonstrate lower stability. These findings highlight the importance of balancing stability (in terms of user memorability and recall consistency) with uncertainty (in terms of resistance in adversarial prediction) when designing choice-based questions, enabling more secure, user-friendly authentication mechanisms in cybersecurity systems.

title: Always-On vs Gated External Signals for Demand Forecasting on the M4 Dataset
authors: Krishna Kumar Neelakanta Pillai Santha Kumari Amma
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Forecasting studies often incorporate external signals as always-on exogenous features, even though web signals like search interest and marketplace activity are intermittently informative and can be noisy. We propose a gated external-signal approach that modulates external influence over time, relying on demand history during normal periods and activating external inputs during attention-driven regimes. We evaluate the method on monthly M4 demand series (2011-2016) augmented with Google Trends and Amazon-derived features, and compare against seasonal naive, classical statistical baselines and always-on fusion. Results show that gated integration is more reliable than unconditional fusion, reducing over-reaction to external fluctuations and improving performance around demand surges, supporting the guideline that external signals are most effective when used selectively rather than continuously.

title: A Probabilistic Multi-Signal Framework for Directional Movement Forecasting in Financial Time Series: A Statistical Learning Perspective
authors: Manan Agrawal, Arindam Mukherjee
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Financial time series exhibit complex temporal dependencies, regime shifts, and low signal-to-noise ratios that challenge traditional models. This paper presents a probabilistic multi-signal framework for predicting directional price movements using gradient-boosted decision trees as conditional probability estimators. The framework integrates 97 features spanning technical indicators, candlestick encodings, fundamental ratios, and sentiment proxies while avoiding look-ahead bias through strict temporal ordering. We model <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$P(Y=1 \vert X)$</tex> via XGBoost, evaluating outputs under a proper scoring rule framework (Brier decomposition, ECE) and a decision-theoretic threshold formulation. Out-of-sample results on U.S. equity data demonstrate well-calibrated probabilities <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{ECE}=0.043)$</tex>, meaningful discrimination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{AUC}=0.682$</tex>), and improved risk-adjusted returns versus buy-and-hold (Sharpe 0.92 vs. 0.71), with interpretable feature attributions via SHAP values.

title: Real-Time Multimodal Sensing and Spatiotemporal Event Logging on Mobile Devices
authors: Sharad Sharma, Katikala Johannas Meela, Pretom Roy Ovi
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a real-time mobile-based system for anomaly detection, emotion detection, and time series analysis using location-based spatial data. Building upon a previously developed Unity application that enabled GPS-based pinning and memory capture, the enhanced system introduces a multi-modal detection pipeline capable of identifying objects, human poses, facial identities, emotions, and critical audio cues from the live camera feed. Using lightweight models such as YOLOv11 for object and pose detection, ArcFace for face recognition, FERPlus for emotion analysis, and Hugging Face's speech recognition for audio alerts, the system logs spatiotemporal data including location, timestamp, and recognized entities. All detected events are stored locally with user context via a multi-user authentication module, enabling user-specific data tracking.

title: Design and Preliminary Evaluation of a Compliant Rolling-Contact Bipedal Robot Guided by Human Motion Data
authors: Regina Martinez Moctezuma, Rob Montgomery, Connor Talley, Deniz Kerimoglu, Ayse Tekes
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Bipedal robot locomotion remains challenging due to the strong coupling between mechanical design, sensing, and control. This paper presents the design and preliminary evaluation of a compliant rolling contact bipedal robot guided by human motion data. The proposed system features a single-piece leg design that integrates a compliant rolling contact knee joint and a compliant foot, enabling load support and controlled deformation inspired by human lower limb mechanics. Rather than relying on explicitly programmed gait trajectories, the robot motion is guided directly by human gait data acquired using inertial measurement units mounted on both the human subject and the robot. To support early-stage design evaluation, a MATLAB Simscape model of the bipedal robot is developed and driven using the same human motion data, allowing the mechanical behavior and motion response to be examined before finalizing the physical prototype. Preliminary experiments are conducted using a suspended test setup that enables free leg motion without ground contact, allowing focused evaluation of the compliant knee and foot behavior. The results demonstrate the feasibility of combining compliant joint and foot design with human motion-guided control as a foundation for future bipedal locomotion systems.

title: A Hybrid Model for Missile Detection Using YOLOv8 and Machine Learning Enhancement
authors: Zainab A. Mezher, Kadim K. Mohsen
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Conference on Computing and Emerging Sciences
abstract: None

title: Improving the Circuit Realization of Grover's Quantum Search Algorithm by Replacing Hadamard with √X Gates
authors: Ali Al-Bayaty, Ali Al-Shuwaili, Abbas AlZubaidi, Marek Perkowski
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Conference on Computing and Emerging Sciences
abstract: None

title: Responsible AI in Cloud Security: A Human-Centric Framework for Transparent Threat Detection and Identity Governance
authors: Meletius Mgbeodichimma Igbokwe, Divine Uzodinma, Ugochukwu Ibeawuchi, Uchenna Victor Moses
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Cloud identity management systems process millions of authentication decisions daily through AI automation, yet this creates a trust paradox: users must depend on systems they cannot observe or control. When AI-driven conditional access policies deny authentication requests, users receive minimal explanation beyond generic errors, undermining both security effectiveness and user confidence. We address this through a human-centric framework integrating automated threat detection with explainable AI (XAI) interfaces and human oversight mechanisms. Security assessments of Azure Active Directory combined with user surveys <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(n=62)$</tex> support three design principles for responsible AI in enterprise security applications: graduated automation based on risk levels (achieving 87-100% detection accuracy for reconnaissance and credential attacks in controlled conditions), mandatory XAI interfaces exposing decision logic, and preserved user agency through override capabilities. Survey findings reveal 94% of users prioritize security infrastructure visibility over cost <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\chi^{2}= 14.3, p<0.001$</tex>), yet 71% expect providers to manage security—demonstrating demand for transparent automation. Organizations receiving clear AI explanations implemented 68% of security recommendations versus 42% without explanations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\chi^{2}=8.7, p<0.01$</tex>). Our framework aligns with NIST AI RMF 1.0 and IEEE P7001 transparency standards, demonstrating that responsible AI deployment in cybersecurity systems requires deliberate architectural choices balancing automation efficiency with human-centric transparency.

title: Mining User Forums to Evaluate Quality-in-Use of Environmental Software
authors: Yvette D. Hastings, Jeffrey C. Carver, A. Redempta Manzi Muneza, Robert A. Payn, Stephanie A. Ewing, Stephan Warnat, Ann Marie Reinhold
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Environmental research software applications are foundational tools for modeling and predicting Earth system dynamics. Among these, reactive transport models (RTMs) are particularly important because they simulate critical system processes that affect contaminant transport and water quality. Many RTMs are developed by domain scientists rather than software engineers, and have quality-in-use (QIU) issues that frustrate end users. Addressing these frustrations requires a clear understanding of the problems and challenges that users experience. We characterize the QIU frustrations most concerning to RTM users using a systematic approach. Specifically, we analyze RTM user forums and codify user-reported challenges by operationalizing the QIU characteristics and subcharacteristics defined in the ISO/IEC 25019:2023 standard. We extracted 3,941 forum threads from four widely used RTM user forums. Terms and phrases related to QIU barriers were identified using natural language processing (NLP) and mapped to QIU subcharacteristics to calculate their frequencies. The QIU characteristic of Beneficialness challenged users most frequently. Within Beneficialness, Usability caused the most frustration across the four forums, suggesting users struggle to achieve their goals effectively, efficiently, and with satisfaction. These findings highlight the importance of providing clear, accurate user resources, such as documentation and tutorials, to support research goals. By operationalizing the full scope of the QIU standard, we offer a transferable method for identifying end-user challenges across domains. By treating QIU as a first-class citizen, developers can improve user satisfaction and develop user-centric, trustworthy software systems across software domains.

title: Hybrid LLM Architectures for On-Device and Cloud-Based Intelligence in Regulated Industries
authors: Sivarama Krishna Akhil Koduri
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) in regulated sectors such as healthcare, finance, and legal services is currently hindered by a rigid dichotomy: the privacy risks of centralized cloud APIs (e.g., Azure OpenAI, AWS Bedrock) versus the computational limitations of local devices. This paper proposes a Hybrid LLM Architecture that bridges this gap by utilizing commoditized on-device runtimes like Ollama on consumer silicon (Apple M-Series, NVIDIA GeForce) as a “sovereign gatekeeper” for hyperscale cloud intelligence. We analyze the hardware physics of Unified Memory Architectures (UMA) versus discrete GPU VRAM, formally define privacy leakage using Fisher Information Loss (FIL), and present a mathematical framework for dynamic workload offloading. Benchmarks suggest that local Llama 3 70B models may exhibit hallucination rates competitive with GPT-4 (e.g., 4.1 % vs. 9.6 % in specific tasks), supporting the potential of “sovereign reasoning” for sensitive data processing.

title: Attack Detection in Internet of Medical Things Using Machine Learning
authors: Anand Choubey
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The growing proliferation of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) devices offers the potential for significantly improved patient care, but also creates new risks for cyberattacks that can compromise the privacy and even safety of patients. To prevent such attacks from occurring, intrusion detection systems can be utilized to detect any attempted attacks and take action accordingly. In this paper, we explore the use of machine learning models, which offer greater adaptability and ease of deployment compared to traditional approaches, for intrusion detection in the context of Bluetooth, which, despite its widespread use in IoMT devices, remains largely unexplored in terms of intrusion detection compared to other protocol stacks. Our results indicate that the four models tested are all promising candidates for securing IoMT systems, with logistic regression achieving a precision of 0.9875, a recall of 1, an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F_{1}$</tex> score of 0.9934, and an AUC score of 0.999.

title: Preserving Discrete Morse–Smale Complexes in Error-Bounded Lossy Compression
authors: Yuxiao Li, Mingze Xia, Xin Liang, Bei Wang, Hanqi Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Scientific applications are generating unprecedented volumes of data that overwhelm storage and transmission systems, posing significant challenges for the design of data management tools and scientific databases. Lossy compression has emerged as a promising strategy to address this problem, but most existing compressors fail to preserve the topology of scientific data, leading to inaccuracies in downstream analyses and potentially erroneous scientific conclusions. In this work, we present a methodology for fully preserving the topology, specifically, Morse-Smale complexes (MSCs), in lossy-compressed 2D and 3D scalar field data from scientific simulations. We generalize the edit-based strategy introduced in MSz [1] (a previous method that preserves only segmentations and cannot preserve saddles or separatrices) by extending the framework to the full MSCs, including all critical points and separatrices. Our approach corrects the MSCs in the decompressed output of any error-bounded lossy compressor (e.g., SZ3 or ZFP), referred to as the base compressor, using an iterative editing strategy that preserves all critical points and their connectivity via separatrices. During compression, we generate a sequence of quantized edits that are applied to the decompressed output, ensuring accurate preservation of topological features while maintaining the error within prescribed bounds. The strategy iteratively fixes critical points and separatrices in alternating steps until convergence is achieved in a finite number of iterations. To meet diverse application needs, our method offers flexible options (e.g., whether to preserve the geometry of separatrices) that balance compression efficiency with feature preservation. To reduce computation time, we leverage GPU parallelism to accelerate each component of the workflow. Experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that our method achieves 100% preservation of Morse-Smale complexes.

title: M-GBT: Multi-region Short-term Electricity Load Forecasting Framework based on Graph Bayesian Transformer Network
authors: Shiqi Zhou, Zhekang Dong, Junfan Wang, Xiaoyue Ji, Yujie Yuan, Chun Sing Lai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Accurate forecasting of short-term electricity load is key to achieving efficient household energy management, optimizing resource allocation, and ensuring reliable grid operation. However, existing methods still suffer from inherent constraints in data distributional assumptions and inter-regional correlation modelling, which jointly compromise the forecasting accuracy and practical applicability. Based on this, a multi-region short-term electricity load forecasting framework that explicitly captures dynamic data distributions and inter-regional dependencies is developed. Two key components constitute the forecasting framework: a graph neural network and a Bayesian Transformer. The former is designed to explicitly construct graph structural features across multiple regions, facilitating inter-regional correlation modelling. The latter integrates a hybrid attention mechanism, allowing it to better cope with evolving load data profiles and deliver robust probabilistic forecasting. Correspondingly, an adaptive learning strategy with the ability to capture multi-region dependencies is designed. Furthermore, experimental validation on two real-world multi-region datasets (New England dataset, London household dataset) shows that the proposed forecasting framework surpasses established benchmarks, with average improvements of 21.2%, 25.6%, and 24.8% in RMSE, pinball loss, and CRPS, respectively.

title:
Prediction and Optimization of S
                    <sub>11</sub>
                    (Reflection Coefficient) in Log-Periodic Toothed Antenna on Flexible Kapton Substrate Using Machine Learning
authors: Md Abdur Rashid, Anandi Dutta, Yihong Maggie Chen
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Designing ultra-wideband log-periodic toothed antennas (LPTAs) on flexible substrates usually relies on fullwave electromagnetic simulations, which are accurate but computationally expensive for large design spaces and iterative optimization. Machine learning (ML) offers a practical way to speed up this process by replacing many of these simulations with fast surrogate models. This paper applies Random Forest and Gradient Boosting regressors to predict and optimize the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}$</tex> reflection coefficient of LPTAs on flexible Kapton substrates. A large HFSS-generated dataset is used, in which element spacing, scaling factors, and operating frequency serve as input features and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}$</tex> is the target. The Random Forest model achieves a test <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> of 0.996 and a test mean squared error of 0.185, clearly outperforming Gradient Boosting. Comparison of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}$</tex> versus frequency shows that the Random Forest predictions closely follow the HFSS curves for a representative LPTA design, indicating that the ML model can reproduce the reflection response very effectively across the operating band. Feature-importance analysis identifies frequency as the dominant factor, with the scaling parameters Tau <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\tau)$</tex> and Sigma <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\sigma)$</tex> also having a strong influence on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}$</tex>, while residual analysis confirms that the prediction errors are small and largely unbiased. Overall, the results show that ML can significantly reduce the number of required electromagnetic simulations while maintaining high accuracy, helping to accelerate the design workflow for nextgeneration flexible and ultra-wideband antenna systems.

title: MHDash: An Online Platform for Benchmarking Mental Health–Aware AI Assistants
authors: Yihe Zhang, Finn Mohawk, Kaiying Han, Vijay Srinivas Tida, Manyu Li, Xiali Hei
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in mental health support systems, where reliable recognition of high-risk states such as suicidal ideation and self-harm is safety-critical. However, existing evaluations primarily rely on aggregate performance metrics, which often obscure risk-specific failure modes and provide limited insight into model behavior in realistic, multi-turn interactions. We present MHDash, an opensource platform designed to support the development, evaluation, and auditing of AI systems for mental health applications. MHDash integrates data collection, structured annotation, multiturn dialogue generation, and baseline evaluation into a unified pipeline. The platform supports annotations across multiple dimensions, including Concern Type, Risk Level, and Dialogue Intent, enabling fine-grained and risk-aware analysis. Our results reveal several key findings: (i) simple baselines and advanced LLM APIs exhibit comparable overall accuracy yet diverge significantly on high-risk cases; (ii) some LLMs maintain consistent ordinal severity ranking while failing absolute risk classification, whereas others achieve reasonable aggregate scores but suffer from high false negative rates on severe categories; and (iii) performance gaps are amplified in multi-turn dialogues, where risk signals emerge gradually. These observations demonstrate that conventional benchmarks are insufficient for safety-critical mental health settings. By releasing MHDash as an open platform, we aim to promote reproducible research, transparent evaluation, and safety-aligned development of AI systems for mental health support.

title: Unifying Variables in Neural Scaling for General Audio Representations via Embedding Effective Rank
authors: Xuyao Deng, Yanjie Sun, Yong Dou, Kele Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: In neural scaling, scaling laws have profoundly shaped our understanding of model performance across easily scalable and expressible parameters. However, when scaling involves multiple parameters that present complex expressions or are difficult to express, it becomes challenging to formulate scaling functions that encompass such parameters. In this work, We found that in the audio domain, the embedding effective rank (RankMe) can serve as a unifying metric that enables label-free, information-theoretic quantification of audio embeddings, encapsulates the impact of diverse variables including masking rate, model size, data volume, computational budget, and architectural configurations on representation quality and demonstrates more significant effectiveness compared to traditional scaling laws. Our empirical findings reveal a consistent power-law relationship between RankMe and representation quality, suggesting that embedding effective rank serves as a reliable proxy for assessing model performance in audio representation learning. Based on RankMe as a label-free proxy metric, our work demonstrates its role as an early-stage monitoring tool. Furthermore, we provide an empirically validated framework for early screening of optimal models from multiple complex hyperparameter settings during pre-training, which saves computational resources.

title: CLAPC: A Hybrid CNN-LSTM Architecture for Automated Passenger Counting from Video Streams in Public Transport
authors: Sambu Seo, Klaus Obermayer
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Information on passenger occupancy in public transport is crucial for service providers to enable accurate revenue sharing within transportation networks. This requires reliable methods to count boarding and alighting passengers and to differentiate between passenger categories, such as those requiring a valid ticket. In previous work, a recurrent Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network (NAPC) was applied to depth videos captured by time-of-flight sensors mounted above train doors to perform passenger counting and height-based differentiation. In this work, we investigate whether this approach can be transferred to 2D gray-scale video data recorded from the same overhead perspective, where explicit depth information is unavailable. To this end, we extend the LSTM architecture by integrating a convolutional neural network, resulting in the CLAPC model, which improves the extraction of image features. Additionally, region-of-interest cropping and spatial down-sampling are explored to better address privacy concerns, and the training procedure is refined to reduce undercounting. Applying CLAPC to depth data improves counting accuracy compared to NAPC. Moreover, high accuracies are achieved on 2D video data despite the absence of explicit depth information. The highest performance is obtained using down-sampled door-path crops, yielding mean accuracies of 99.73% for boarding and 99.87% for alighting passengers. On depth data, accuracies of 99.79% and 99.97% are achieved for boarding and alighting, respectively. These results meet the ± 1% accuracy requirement of VDV Guideline 457. The 95% confidence intervals from an equivalence test lie within the ± 1% bounds, which confirms that CLAPC is statistically robust.

title: Ultrathin Dual Circularly Polarized Transmitarray Antennas Based on Polarization Conversion Huygens' Metasurface
authors: Haibo Wang, Chunhua Xue, Rui Wang, Teng Li, Xi Gao, Zhi Ning Chen
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This paper presents a Huygens' metasurface integrated with polarization conversion for designing dual circularly polarized (DCP) transmitarray antenna (TAA). With the proposed double-resonant unit cells, the metasurface provides full transmission phase coverage for two linear polarizations (LPs) and maintains a stable 90° phase difference across a broad bandwidth, enabling the conversion of ±45°-LP waves into DCP waves with precise phase control. When applied to a TAA, it supports DCP high-gain beams. Simulations and measurements validate the design. For left-handed CP (LHCP), aperture efficiencies of 32.28% is achieved, with 3-dB gain bandwidths of 17.5% and 3-dB axial ratio (AR) bandwidths of 16.8%. For right-handed CP (RHCP), aperture efficiencies of 32.43% is achieved, with 3-dB gain bandwidths of 17.1% and 3-dB AR bandwidths of 17.9%. These results offer valuable insights for future millimeter-wave communications.

title: POTR: Post-Training 3DGS Compression
authors: Bert Ramlot, Martijn Courteaux, Peter Lambert, Glenn Van Wallendael
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has recently emerged as a promising contender to Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) in 3D scene reconstruction and real-time novel view synthesis. 3DGS outperforms NeRF in training and inference speed but has substantially higher storage requirements. To remedy this downside, we propose POTR, a post-training 3DGS codec built on two novel techniques. First, POTR introduces a novel pruning approach that uses a modified 3DGS rasterizer to efficiently calculate every splat’s individual removal effect simultaneously. This technique results in 2-4× fewer splats than other post-training pruning techniques and as a result also significantly accelerates inference with experiments demonstrating 1.5-2× faster inference than other compressed models. Second, we propose a novel method to recompute lighting coefficients, significantly reducing their entropy without using any form of training. Our fast and highly parallel approach especially increases AC lighting coefficient sparsity, with experiments demonstrating increases from 70% to 97%, with minimal loss in quality. Finally, we extend POTR with a simple fine-tuning scheme to further enhance pruning, inference, and rate-distortion performance. Experiments demonstrate that POTR, even without fine-tuning, consistently outperforms all other post-training compression techniques in both rate-distortion performance and inference speed.

title: An application of the mean motion problem to time-optimal control
authors: Omri Dalin, Alexander Ovseevich, Michael Margaliot
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: We consider time-optimal controls of a controllable linear system. It is well-known (see, e.g., [1, Theorem 6-8]) that if all the eigenvalues of the matrix describing the linear system dynamics are real then any time-optimal control has a bounded number of switching points, where the bound does not depend on the length of the time interval. Here, we consider the case where the governing matrix has purely imaginary eigenvalues, and show that then, in the generic case, the number of switching points is bounded from below by a linear function of the length of the time interval. The proof is based on relating the switching function in the optimal control problem to the mean motion problem that dates back to Lagrange and was solved by Herman Weyl.

title: Automatic detection of suicidal ideation on social media using a hybrid CNNBiLSTM architecture
authors: Sadala Nagib Salame Neto, Kaique da Silva Pinto, Silvio Tadeu Teles da Silva, Miércio Cardoso de Alcântara Neto
publish: IEEE Latin America Transactions
abstract: Suicide is a serious public health problem, responsible for more than 700,000 deaths annually worldwide, according to the World Health Organization. Early identification of signs of suicidal ideation can support prevention initiatives and reduce fatal outcomes. This study investigated the application of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and deep learning techniques for the automatic detection of suicidal ideation in social media posts. A hybrid architecture based on Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM), combined with convolutional layers and regularization strategies, was proposed. The evaluation was conducted on a Reddit dataset processed in two versions: with and without stopword removal. The results showed that the proposed architecture achieved high performance in both versions, reaching an accuracy of 95.77% and an F1-score of 95.79% on the test set without stopword removal. Despite the higher computational cost, this approach outperformed previous studies reported in the literature. In summary, the proposed model demonstrates potential to support early monitoring strategies for suicidal ideation in digital environments, offering a relevant contribution to public health and suicide prevention.

title: Selective Active Zero-State Pulse-Width Modulation (AZSPWM) for Reduction of Common-Mode Voltage (CMV) of Three-Phase Inverter Considering Saturation of Filter Inductor
authors: Jaekeun Lee, Shanshan Zhao, Chaeyoung Jeong, Kiwoo Park, Shenghui Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Conventional active zero-state PWM (AZSPWM) techniques are effective in limiting common-mode voltage (CMV) but typically assume linear and balanced three-phase filter inductances. In practice, however, filter inductors are designed for high-power density and inevitably operate near magnetic saturation (e.g., using powder-core materials). This results in periodic unbalanced dynamic inductances among the three phases and thereby degrading the effectiveness of the well-established pulsewidth modulation (PWM) techniques for CMV reduction. This paper reveals the failure of existing PWM techniques on CMV reduction under such conditions and proposes a saturation-aware selective AZSPWM method that adaptively selects the optimal pair of opposite active vectors based on predicted CMV. A common-mode equivalent circuit incorporating unbalanced dynamic inductances is derived, and an online CMV estimation framework is developed using an approximated <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$B-H$</tex-math></inline-formula> curve of the inductor to enable optimal AZSPWM selection. Experimental results on a 220 kVA grid-connected industrial inverter demonstrate that the proposed method significantly reduces CMV compared with conventional AZSPWM method, particularly under the high load and high power factor conditions.

title: From Dataset to Deployment: A Full-Stack Autoencoder-Based Anomaly Detection Pipeline for Gas Turbine BLDC Fuel Pumps
authors: Viet Linh Nguyen, Duy Lanh Chu, van Huy Nguyen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Reliable health monitoring of fuel pumps is essential for maintaining the stability and safety of gas turbine engines. Brushless DC (BLDC) fuel pumps operate under tightly coupled electrical–mechanical–hydraulic dynamics, where fault progression is often subtle and real fault data are scarce. This article presents a full-stack anomaly detection pipeline spanning dataset construction, feature engineering, model development, evaluation, and embedded deployment, built on an unsupervised autoencoder trained exclusively on normal operating data. A real-world dataset was collected from BLDC fuel pumps integrated into gas turbine testbeds over multiple engines and years; however, the available corpus remains modest, comprising 18 log files and nearly three hours of cumulative operation. A 48-dimensional handcrafted feature set was designed to capture statistical, energetic, derivative, and autocorrelation characteristics of pump behavior. The autoencoder learns a compact representation of healthy operation, while anomalies are identified through reconstruction error using a fixed threshold and a persistence-based decision rule. In offline analysis, the proposed autoencoder achieves competitive discrimination relative to strong unsupervised baselines, while its primary practical advantage lies in supporting a deterministic end-to-end TinyML deployment pipeline rather than maximizing offline discriminative performance alone. Real-time on-device experiments on an STM32 platform demonstrate successful detection for the evaluated dry-run and aeration scenarios, while no alarms are produced under normal steady and transient tests after persistence filtering. Under the current feature set and calibration, the evaluated partial-blockage and gradual-restriction scenarios did not produce persistence-triggered alarms, indicating incomplete fault-mode coverage and motivating future work on broader fault characterization and adaptive calibration. These results support the practical feasibility of real-time, on-device anomaly detection for gas-turbine fuel-pump monitoring in a resource-constrained TinyML setting.

title: FedDiDy: Federated Class-Incremental Fault Diagnosis for Industrial IoT Rotating Machinery Under Dynamic Edge Participation
authors: Yanxin Hu, Yan Huang, Zhenzhen Xie, Junjie Pang, Chao Cheng, Zhipeng Cai
