abstract: The rapid evolution of cybersecurity threats has led to an increased focus on advanced cyberattacks, such as fileless malware. This research aims to explore how fileless malware, which operates in memory, poses new challenges for traditional file-based detection techniques. The primary objective of this research is to simulate real-world fileless attacks and understand how threat actors can exploit system weaknesses through this approach, providing new insights to enhance cybersecurity defenses. We developed offensive proof-of-concept fileless malware orchestration scripts and executed them in simulated attacks within a controlled, virtualized environment to demonstrate fileless malware techniques, identify exploitable vulnerabilities, and assess the effectiveness of conventional defensive techniques. The results of our research suggested several gaps in existing cybersecurity defenses and highlighted vulnerabilities that could be exploited by malicious actors. As a result, our research concludes with suggestions for enhancing traditional defenses against fileless threats.

title: Intelligent Information Systems for Precision Livestock Farming in Dairy and Poultry
authors: Mateus de Castro Duarte Cardoso, Julio Cesar Schendroski, Andre Sirikaku Miyashiro, Iran José Oliveira da Silva, Thiago Luís Lopes Siqueira
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Creating a world free of hunger requires decreasing food insecurity by means of developing food production systems that improve both sustainability and yield. However, animal diseases threaten production, economy, livelihood and health. Therefore, interdisciplinary research and development involving animal sciences and information technology play a key role to prevent diseases and mitigate their effects. The development of intelligent information systems for these purposes demands composing diversified teams, studying and assessing existing solutions, creating prototypes and evaluating their feasibility, and finally searching for collaborations with non-academic stakeholders to transfer technology and increase the societal impact. In this paper, we focus on precision livestock farming in dairy and poultry. First, we reevaluate solutions for diagnosing cow mastitis. Second, we describe a prototype for computer vision that detects heifers in images. Third, we detail a prototype for computer vision that detects objects inside a poultry barn. Fourth, we describe an application that maps and searches for farms, since farmers are potential partners. We also discuss our results considering: interdisciplinarity, data as the center of intelligent information systems, and technological debt and divide in rural areas.

title: WTN: A Wavelet Transform Network for High-Resolution Meteorological Downscaling
authors: Zhibo Kong, Li Wu, Jianlong Zhang, Jia Yu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: The demand for higher spatial-resolution meteorological information has been steadily increasing in both operational and research meteorology, where capturing fine-scale structures and sharp gradients is crucial for applications such as wind and temperature analysis, urban meteorological studies, and weather-related disaster risk management. However, acquiring long-term and stable high-resolution meteorological fields remains challenging due to limited observation density, the high cost of data assimilation, and the substantial computational burden of high-resolution numerical simulations. As an efficient alternative, deep learning-based statistical downscaling has attracted growing attention in recent years, yet existing methods still struggle with multi-scale representation and the recovery of local details, often exhibiting over-smoothing, blurred boundaries, and insufficient characterization of extreme signals. To address these challenges, we propose a Wavelet Transform Network (WTN) for meteorological downscaling. WTN formulates downscaling as a super-resolution reconstruction task and introduces Wavelet Convolutions in the feature domain to explicitly model large-scale background patterns and fine-scale details through wavelet decomposition and fusion. In addition, we design a lightweight Gated Feature Aggregation Unit (GFAU) to enhance local information interaction and improve the optimization stability of deep networks. The overall framework adopts a group-residual iterative refinement strategy to progressively compensate high-frequency structures, thereby improving detail fidelity while preserving large-scale consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that WTN consistently achieves better quantitative performance across different upscaling factors and regional scenarios, and produces more realistic fine-scale structures in representative case studies, validating its effectiveness and applicability for meteorological downscaling.

title: Comparative Study of Fuel Cell Battery Hybrid Powertrains for Heavy Duty Vehicle Applications
authors: Gui-Jia Su
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This paper presents a comparative study of fuel cell-battery hybrid powertrains (FCBHPs) for a Class-8 heavyduty vehicle. System-level efficiency is evaluated over a representative driving cycle for an FCBHP incorporating a bidirectional DC/DC converter (BDC) between the battery and the traction inverter. The BDC enables dynamic adjustment of the inverter DC-bus voltage to reduce inverter and motor losses. Under selected operating conditions, such as regenerative braking, BDC switching is disabled to eliminate switching losses. For benchmarking, an alternative FCBHP configuration without a battery-side BDC is also analyzed. The results quantify the efficiency tradeoffs associated with variable DC-bus voltage control and converter operating strategies in heavy-duty fuel-cell electric drivetrains.

title: Pedagogical Experience in AI Teaching for Technical High Schools: a Regional Approach From UNLP
authors: Isabel Miyuki Kimura, Claudia Alejandra Queiruga, Francisco Javier Díaz
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This article presents an experience of teaching Artificial Intelligence in the technological training of technical high school students specializing in “Professional and Personal Computing” and “Programming” in the province of Buenos Aires, Argentina, developed in a university extension project at the National University of La Plata. The conceptual framework of this work draws on the ideas and practices of the university extension programs of Argentina's national universities, the scientific and technological training provided in technical high education, and approaches to teaching computer science in schools, specifically the contributions of “computational thinking 2.0 ” and current revisions of its concepts and practices in relation to emerging technologies, including machine learning. Workshops were held that combined content on machine learning, deep learning, and ethical aspects, using accessible and free tools such as Google Teachable Machine and MIT App Inventor. Students built and trained supervised learning models that they integrated into mobile applications. The evaluation of the workshops showed that students recognize AI in everyday applications and demonstrate a high level of interest in practical activities, particularly those involving real data. The importance of producing contextualized data to enrich teaching and strengthen technical skills and crosscutting abilities such as creativity and critical thinking is highlighted. Initial results support the pedagogical relevance of integrating AI knowledge into technical high school from a local and regional perspective, promoting critical, ethical, and socially engaged literacy.

title: Evaluating the Impacts of Realistic Communications on a Microgrid Central Controller Through Real Time Simulation
authors: Cristóbal Undurraga, Patricio A. Mendoza-Araya
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This work studies the performance of a microgrid when affected by problems in its communications loop. The microgrid is modeled in the Typhoon HIL 604 real-time simulator. Through a Controller Hardware in the Loop setup, a centralized microgrid control is implemented externally on a Raspberry Pi device that connects to the simulation on the Typhoon HIL device via Ethernet and uses the Modbus TCP communication protocol. Ten scenarios of interest were chosen to investigate the performance of the microgrid when subjected to the inclusion of delay in the communication link between the digital simulation and the externally implemented microgrid control system. The experimental results show that, in certain scenarios, an increase in the delay of the communications link causes a progressive decrease in the performance of the microgrid, becoming unstable in one case. This reinforces the idea that communications design for future microgrids must be thoroughly explored in order to maintain stable operation that meets security and quality of service requirements.

title: Event-Based Vision for High-Dynamic Observation and Physical Parameter Extraction of Discharge Channel Evolution
authors: Sijia Liu, Xirui Zhang, Yuman Nie, Yong Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Discharge phenomena exhibit highly dynamic branching morphologies that are crucial for understanding atmospheric breakdown mechanisms and enhancing lightning prediction. However, conventional imaging techniques face inherent limitations: standard cameras lack the frame rate to resolve rapid evolution, while high-speed cameras are constrained by limited dynamic range, hindering the capture of fine structural details. Event-based vision sensors (EVS), with their neuromorphic design, offer microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range, enabling clear observation of discharge processes across a broad intensity spectrum.In this work, we propose an EVS-driven method for robust discharge and lightning observation.The proposed approach integrates multi-scale smoothing, morphological reconstruction, iterative skeletonization, and temporal voting. Through adaptive baseline modeling and spatiotemporal consistency enhancement, it achieves robust detection across a wide range of discharge intensities.From the reconstructed skeletons, we derive key parameters including discharge duration, propagation speed, channel length, and energy release. Experiments on Tesla coil discharge sequences demonstrate the method's effectiveness and scalability, highlighting the potential of eventsignal processing for quantitative analysis of sparse, high-speed physical phenomena.

title: Graph Spectra of Technical Documentation
authors: Rocker D'Antonio
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Technical documentation serves as a critical interface between the design of technological systems and the users who implement, maintain, and operate them. As documentation is increasingly consumed not only by humans but also by automated tools, understanding its structural properties has become more important. While documentation corpora encode rich structural information through explicit cross-references, this structure is rarely examined quantitatively at the level of global link organization. In this work, we model technical documentation corpora as page-level hyperlink graphs derived from explicit inline links and analyze their structure using spectral graph methods. Directed graphs are constructed from multiple real-world documentation sources using a standardized, quality-assured extraction pipeline, and Laplacian spectral properties are computed for both the full graphs and their largest weakly connected components. The resulting measurements reveal substantial variation in graph size, connectivity, and fragmentation across corpora constructed under identical rules, illustrating observable differences in explicit cross-referencing structure.

title: GPT Model for Incident Response
authors: Thiam Aik Goh, Joo Ting Chiam, Zoie Chew, Claudia Xin Lin Chee, Peishan Li, Huaqun Guo
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This study focuses on the fine-tuning of a pre-trained GPT-2 language model to address a critical domain-specific challenge: enhancing automated assistance for cybersecurity incident response. Incident response is a structured process organizations use to manage and mitigate the effects of cybersecurity events, such as data breaches, malware attacks, or unauthorized access. Due to the complexity and dynamic nature of cyber threats, creating an intelligent system that provides accurate, context-aware guidance is essential for improving response efficiency and minimizing potential damage.

title: Counting Characters: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Character-Level Understanding in Large Language Models
authors: Yuyang Zeng, Yuqi Yi
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit systematic failures in character-level tasks despite strong performance on complex reasoning. The “strawberry problem” (incorrectly counting the letter ‘ <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r$</tex> ’ in “strawberry”) exemplifies this limitation. We present a 5,000 -sample benchmark covering five task types to evaluate character counting as a fundamental proxy for character-level understanding. We assess eight open-weight 79-B parameter models deployed locally on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU across four prompting strategies. Our findings reveal: (1) word length is the primary difficulty factor (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=-0.521, p<0.001$</tex>), with token count showing a small independent effect after controlling for word length (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r_{\text{partial}}=-0.085, p<0.001$</tex>), (2) chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting yields the largest average improvement (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$+13.7 {\%}$</tex>), with Qwen2.5 gaining +36.8%, while character decomposition shows model-dependent effects, and (3) qualitative analysis of reasoning traces reveals that structured character enumeration is the key mechanism behind CoT effectiveness. These results provide a quantitative baseline for future character-level research.

title: Soft-Q: Softwarized Quantum Network for ERNET Topology Using a Multi-Layer Graph Decomposition
authors: Bhumika Adhya, Soumyapriya Goswami, Deborsi Basu, Samriddha Sanyal, Uttam Ghosh
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The advent of quantum networking, with softwarization has fundamentally reshaped communication infrastructures, especially within university and research backbones. Yet adapting quantum networks to established topologies, like ERNET, still poses an optimization challenge due to the co-existence of both SDN (Software Defined Network) and non-SDN networks or traditional networks. This work presents Soft-Q, a softwarized quantum networking framework that applies multi-layer graph decomposition to achieve quantum resource allocation across the ERNET topology. Unlike strategies that hinge exclusively on network design, our method taps into quantum-classical hybrid architectures to wrestle with the computational complexity of rolling out quantum networks within existing infrastructures. The proposed solution shows pronounced gains in distribution efficiency, entanglement rates and network scalability when measured against typical quantum networking approaches.

title: Title Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)

title: Autonomous Ground Vehicle for Precision Weed Detection and Targeted Pesticide Spraying Using Deep Learning
authors: Justin Finn, Thomas Paxton, Jesse Warren, Arden Stanley, Barbara Darroch, Saman Sargolzaei
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Efficient and targeted weed management remains a significant challenge in modern agriculture, as conventional blanket pesticide application increases chemical consumption, operational cost, and environmental impact. This paper presents an autonomous ground vehicle for precision weed control that integrates perception, navigation, and actuation within a single platform. The system employs a Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) sensor for autonomous navigation across uneven agricultural terrain. Simultaneously, a real-time weed detection and classification pipeline is implemented using the YOLO (You Only Look Once) deep learning framework integrated with a camera-based vision system. The proposed framework continuously captures field imagery along an autonomously generated navigation path and performs real-time object detection to identify and classify weed species. Detected weeds are spatially localized with high inference speed and evaluated using standard performance metrics, including mean Average Precision (mAP) and Intersection over Union (IoU). Based on the classification output, control commands are generated to actuate a localized pesticide spraying mechanism, ensuring chemical application is restricted exclusively to detected weed regions. Experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of combining deep learningbased object detection with autonomous agricultural vehicles for accurate and scalable precision spraying. The proposed system reduces unnecessary pesticide usage and off-target spraying, contributing to more sustainable and environmentally responsible weed management practices.

title: Network Intrusion Detection Model Based on VMA-Attention and CNN
authors: NanZhen Pan
publish: 2025 2nd World Conference on Computer and Information Security (WCCIS)
abstract: Traditional Convolutional Neural Network have achieved certain success in network intrusion detection. However, they still lack the ability to effectively extract temporal features from network traffic data. Therefore, this paper proposes and designs a Variational Mask Additive Attention mechanism. While using the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\beta}$</tex>-Variational Autoencoder to generate new data to handle the imbalance in network traffic datasets, this mechanism replaces the matrix multiplication in traditional Attention mechanism with element-wise multiplication and implements a masking mechanism throughout the process. This not only effectively extracts temporal features from network traffic data but also addresses the issues of traditional Recurrent Neural Network, Long Short-Term Memory and Gated Recurrent Unit being unable to handle long-term dependencies in sequences and the high complexity and information leakage problems of traditional Attention mechanism. Experiments have proven that the proposed model demonstrates good performance in both binary and multi-class classification tasks across multiple datasets.

title: CircuChain: Disentangling Competence and Compliance in LLM Circuit Analysis
authors: Mayank Ravishankara
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) advance toward expert-level performance in engineering domains, reliable reasoning under user-specified constraints becomes critical. In circuit analysis, for example, a numerically correct solution is insufficient if it violates established methodological conventions such as mesh directionality or polarity assignments—errors that can propagate in safety-critical systems. Yet it remains unclear whether frontier models truly apply first-principles reasoning or rely on entrenched training priors that conflict with explicit instructions. We introduce CircuChain, a diagnostic benchmark designed to disentangle instruction compliance from physical reasoning competence in electrical circuit analysis. CircuChain consists of counterbalanced Control/Trap problem pairs across five canonical circuit topologies, augmented with systematic variations in sign conventions, current orientations, and polarity definitions. A multi-stage verification pipeline—combining symbolic solvers, SPICE simulation, and an LLM-based error taxonomy—enables fine-grained attribution of failures to convention errors, physics errors, arithmetic mistakes, or hallucinations. Across 100 tasks per model, we observe a consistent Com-pliance-Competence Divergence. The strongest model evaluated exhibits near-perfect physical reasoning but a high rate of convention violations when Trap conditions deliberately invert natural sign patterns. Conversely, weaker models display lower physical fidelity yet superior adherence to explicit instructions. These results suggest that increased model capability does not guarantee improved constraint alignment and highlight the need for new evaluation frameworks that stress instruction-following under mathematically rigid domains. CircuChain provides one such framework and offers actionable insights for both engineering education and AI alignment research.

title: Organizing Committee
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Space Mission Challenges for Information Technology (SMC-IT)

title: Technical Architecture and Empirical Research on Personalized Teaching of Higher Vocational Music Based on Multimodal Learning Analysis
authors: Qiong Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: To address key pain points in education (inefficient multimodal fusion, lagging personalized teaching), this paper proposes a multimodal learning analysis architecture for higher vocational music teaching. Based on computer vision, speech processing, and machine learning, it builds a full-link system covering data collection, preprocessing, fusion, state diagnosis, and strategy generation. Key designs include an improved CNN-LSTM fusion model and edge computing nodes for real-time processing, enabling efficient handling of audio-visual-behavioral data (single-sample delay <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\leqslant} \mathbf{5 0 0 m s}$</tex>, fusion accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\geqslant} \mathbf{9 2. 3 \%}$</tex>). A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 6}$</tex>-week empirical test in higher vocational music teaching shows 100% data collection coverage, 3.7% learning state diagnosis error rate, and 68.5% faster personalized strategy response. Core contributions lie in the proposed lightweight processing scheme and optimized cross-modal fusion algorithm, providing technical references for computer technology application in education.

title: The Variational Quantum Classifier from Qiskit Machine Learning Applied to the Classification of Pulsar Stars
authors: Anna B. M. Souza, Clebson Cruz, Marcelo A. Moret, Otto M. Pires
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Quantum Machine Learning is a new computational tool that combines the quantum properties from quantum computing with the pattern recognition from machine learning. In this paper, we apply the Variational Quantum Classifier algorithm to the problem of pulsar classification of candidates from the High Time Resolution Universe 2 dataset. We use Qiskit Machine Learning circuits to compare the performance of the model using different feature selection methods, various number of features and training data size. Comparisons on the model from changing the data encoding and ansatz options are also reported.

title: Improved Hopping Control on Slopes for Small Robots Using Spring Mass Modeling
authors: Heston Roberts, Pronoy Sarker, Sm Ashikul Islam, Min Gyu Kim
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Hopping robots often lose balance on slopes because the tilted ground creates unwanted rotation at landing. This work analyzes that effect using a simple spring-mass model and identifies how slope-induced impulses destabilize the robot. To address this, we introduce two straightforward fixes: adjusting the body's touchdown angle based on the slope and applying a small corrective torque before takeoff. Together, these steps effectively cancel the unwanted rotation caused by inclined terrain, allowing the robot to land smoothly and maintain stable hopping even on steep slopes. Moreover, the proposed method remains simple enough to implement on low-cost robotic platforms without requiring complex sensing or computation. By combining this analytical model with minimal control actions, this approach provides a practical path toward reliable hopping on uneven terrain. The results from simulation confirm that even small slope-aware adjustments can dramatically improve landing stability, making the technique suitable for future autonomous field robots that must navigate natural environments such as hills, rubble, and irregular outdoor landscapes.

title: GenAITD Score: A Quantitative Framework for Measuring Enterprise Technical Debt and Generative AI Readiness
authors: Pankaj Kumar Jain, Jahnavi Thirumalasetty
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The rapid adoption of Generative Artificial Intelligence (Gen-AI) in enterprise environments has outpaced the readiness of existing technical and governance infrastructures, exposing latent forms of technical debt across data pipelines, legacy workflows, and machine learning operations (MLOps) practices. While organizations are under increasing pressure to operationalize Gen-AI capabilities, existing maturity models lack the granularity required to identify the specific architectural and operational constraints that hinder reliable deployment. This paper introduces the Gen-AI Technical Debt (GenAITD) Score, a quantitative framework designed to measure enterprise readiness and Gen-AI-specific technical debt. The framework constructs a composite index on a normalized <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0-500$</tex> scale and evaluates enterprise readiness across five weighted dimensions: Data Debt & Governance, Process & Automation, AI Platform Architecture, Organizational Readiness, and Risk & Compliance. A total of 45 objectively defined Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are aggregated to produce both a numerical score and a corresponding letter-grade classification (A+ to D), enabling a standardized, audit-able mechanism for stakeholders to benchmark maturity.

title: The Spheres Dataset: Multitrack Orchestral Recordings for Music Source Separation and Information Retrieval
authors: Jaime García-Martínez, David Diaz-Guerra, John Anderson, Ricardo Falcón-Pérez, Pablo Cabañas-Molero, Tuomas Virtanen, Julio J. Carabias-Orti, Pedro Vera-Candeas
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: This paper introduces The Spheres dataset, multitrack orchestral recordings designed to advance machine learning research in music source separation and related MIR tasks within the classical music domain. The dataset is composed of over one hour recordings of musical pieces performed by the Colibrì Ensemble at The Spheres recording studio, capturing two canonical works-Tchaikovsky's Romeo and Juliet and Mozart's Symphony No. 40-along with chromatic scales and solo excerpts for each instrument. The recording setup employed 23 microphones, including close spot, main, and ambient microphones, enabling the creation of realistic stereo mixes with controlled bleeding and providing isolated stems for supervised training of source separation models. In addition, room impulse responses were estimated for each instrument position, offering valuable acoustic characterization of the recording space. We present the dataset structure, acoustic analysis, and different experiments for orchestral family separation and microphone debleeding. Results highlight both the potential and the challenges of source separation in complex orchestral scenarios, underscoring the dataset's value for benchmarking and for exploring new approaches to separation, localization, dereverberation, and immersive rendering of classical music.

title: Descriptor: Energy Demand and Renewable Supply Dataset for Pacific Island Countries (EDRS-PIC)
authors: Akash Jyoti Handique, Rebecca A.M. Peer, Gabriel Lopez, Mai ElSayed, Maxi-Ann Campbell, Christian Breyer, Jannik Haas
publish: IEEE Data Descriptions
abstract: Current and future energy demand and supply datasets of countries become crucial in analysing and quantifying future energy transition pathways to mitigate global temperature rise. To address the energy data scarcity for 14 Pacific Island countries, we developed a comprehensive dataset that includes their existing electricity generation capacity, current and future load projections for power, heat, and transport sectors, as well as the offshore renewable energy potential of wave energy and offshore wind energy. The dataset also includes biomass resources potential based on crops, woods, and biogas. We further compared our energy demand and supply dataset with reports and energy statistics from reliable sources for validation. Our final database is the most comprehensive dataset to date for the Pacific Islands and is useful for understanding the energy landscape and potentially facilitating the energy transitions for these often-overlooked island nations.

title: 3-Dimensional Gas Source Localization via Chemotaxis Using Uncrewed Aircraft Systems
authors: Jiahao Li, Lingxiao Wang
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Gas source localization (GSL) enables robotic agents to trace gas plumes and identify gas source locations. This paper presents a three-dimensional (3D) GSL navigation algorithm using an uncrewed aircraft system (UAS) to autonomously locate an active gas source. Two search modes, SCAN and SEARCH, are designed to guide the UAS motions. In the SCAN mode, the UAS performs a multi-layer lawn-mower trajectory to explore the environment and to detect initial plume signals. Once high gas concentrations are detected, the system switches to the SEARCH mode, in which the UAS follows local concentration gradients to approach the gas source location. The proposed approach is evaluated using a 3D Gaussian plume model and a quadrotor UAS simulator. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed GSL navigation algorithm can reliably guide the UAS to the gas source location with varying gas source and initial UAS locations.

title: Analysis and Realization of Fractional-Order Tow-Thomas Biquad Using a Cole-Impedance Model
authors: Garrett Casey, Norbert Herencsar, Jaroslav Koton, Todd J. Freeborn
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This work presents an analysis of the Tow-Thomas biquadratic filter when realized using a Cole-impedance as a replacement for a traditional capacitor. This replacement yields a (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1+\alpha$</tex>) order response that alters the magnitude characteristics when compared to the traditional lowpass and bandpass functions. The proposed circuit theory is validated in simulation and experimentally when apples are used as the Cole-model impedance in the topology with measurements collected from 100 Hz to 500 kHz.

title: SecureLogX: A Java–Native, AI–Augmented Logging Framework for Privacy–Preserving, Parallel Log Processing in Enterprise Systems
authors: Syed Danish Ali
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Enterprise logging systems must meet increasing demands for real-time privacy compliance under regulations such as HIPAA and GDPR while sustaining high throughput. This paper presents SecureLogX, an AI-powered Java logging framework for inline, privacy-preserving observability. SecureLogX integrates ONNX-based transformer inference with parallel and asynchronous log processing to enable adaptive masking of sensitive entities across JSON, XML, and text logs. The hybrid NER engine dynamically identifies personal identifiers without reliance on static regex rules. Experimental evaluation demonstrates low-latency performance suitable for distributed microservice environments.

title: An Enhanced Artificial Bee Colony Algorithm for Optimizing Lexicon-Augmented BERT in Named Entity Recognition for Minority Ethnic Narratives
authors: Xiuqin Pan, Biao Wu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Named Entity Recognition (NER) in minority ethnic narratives remains challenging due to data sparsity, linguistic complexity, and culturally embedded semantics. This paper proposes an enhanced Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm to optimize Lexicon-Enhanced BERT for low-resource NER on a Dai ethnic storytelling dataset. The proposed framework integrates chaotic initialization, adaptive neighborhood search, domain knowledge constraints, elitist, perturbation, and multi-population collaboration to improve optimization efficiency and convergence stability in high-dimensional non-convex spaces. Experiments show that the optimized model achieves an F1 score of 91.1%, outperforming default LEBERT and BERT-CRF by 5.3% and 7.3%, respectively. Ablation and comparative results further confirm the robustness and effectiveness of the proposed approach for domain-specific NER.

title: Research on Traffic Small Object Detection Based on an Improved YOLO11 Framework
authors: Yaming Tong, Haiyi Bian
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Small object detection in road scenes remains challenging due to limited pixel size, frequent occlusions, and complex backgrounds. To address these issues, this paper proposes an improved YOLOv11-based detection framework. An adaptive multi-scale LoG stem is introduced to enhance shallow structural feature extraction, and a content-guided attention based fusion module is employed to refine multi-scale feature aggregation. In addition, a scale-aware SIoU loss is designed to improve small object localization accuracy. Experiments on the UA-DETRAC dataset show that the proposed method improves precision, recall, F1-score, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{mAP} {@} 0.5$</tex> by 3.7 %, 3.8 %, 6.9 %, and 5.3 %, respectively, compared with the baseline YOLOv11. Although additional computational cost is introduced, the improved detection accuracy makes the proposed method suitable for accuracy-oriented traffic monitoring applications.

title: Application of Complex Variable Function Theory in Denoising Analysis of Complex Data
authors: Xuesong Tang, Jinhong Huang, Qiuye Yu, Haiying Zhang, Qiushui Yu, Kexin Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: The paper delves into the potential applications of complex function theory in denoising of complex data. Initially, it proposes a signal reconstruction algorithm based on contour integration in the complex plane, grounded in the principles of analytic functions and the Cauchy integral formula. This approach capitalizes on the uniqueness theorem for analytic functions, effectively filtering out high-frequency noise that is non-analytic. Furthermore, the paper introduces a strategy for manifold dimensionality reduction and denoising, which relies on conformal mapping. This strategy involves mapping data with intricate topological structures onto a standard complex plane domain, thereby achieving a geometric dissociation of signal from noise. Experimental findings indicate that, compared to conventional methods like wavelet threshold denoising and empirical mode decomposition, the complex function theory-based method exhibits superior performance in enhancing the signal-to-noise ratio and reducing the mean squared error, particularly when dealing with phase-sensitive data.

title: LF Sketch: A Sketch-Based Algorithm for Long-Term Frequent Itemsets Mining
authors: Zelin Wei, Niuniu Zhang, Shangsen Li, Lailong Luo
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: In large-scale data stream environments, traditional frequent itemsets mining methods struggle to efficiently detect long-term frequent itemsets, particularly when data undergoes dynamic changes over long time spans. Long-term frequent itemsets are itemsets that persist over extended periods, exhibiting stability and intermittent occurrences. Traditional methods often fail to capture the evolution of these patterns. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a Sketch-based algorithm for long-term frequent itemsets mining (LF-Sketch). The algorithm compresses data streams and constructs efficient summary structures, ensuring storage efficiency while rapidly capturing the evolving trends of long-term frequent itemsets, especially those that occur intermittently but persist over time. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the LF-Sketch algorithm significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of time efficiency, space overhead, and mining accuracy.

title: Occlusion-Proof Vehicle Counting Using Computer Vision and Artificial Intelligence
authors: Diego Palacios, Derlis Gregor, Mario Arzamendia, Fabian Palacios, Kevin Galeano, Sergio Toral Marin
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: In this work, a vehicle counting algorithm is developed and implemented in an urban monitoring environment, through artificial vision. Counting is done through unique identifiers of objects detected in a video stream and doesn't need to predefine a region of interest (RoI). To achieve this, it implements tracking that is robust even in occlusion contexts to avoid errors. The algorithm uses two models based on artificial neural networks in a single implementation: an object detection model in video streams based on convolutional neural networks, and a trajectory prediction model based on MLP (Multi Layer Perceptron). The concept of Transfer Learning is used for the detection model implementing YOLOv8 (You Only Look Once), and the concept of Continuous Learning for the MLP trajectory prediction model. The MLP Trajectory Predictor starts operating without prior training, and constantly acquires knowledge of the observed context, learning the motion patterns of detected objects through continuous real-time training during application. The Trajectory Predictor allows to predict the positions and motion of hidden objects to maintain tracking and identification in occlusion conditions. A trajectory prediction model was obtained with an average error of 0.68 % in a steady state, that is, when the model observed and automatically learned all the movement patterns of the objects on the screen. The counting capacity by unique identifier across the entire screen of the proposed algorithm improved with the Trajectory Predictor was compared versus the state of the art tracking method DeepSORT and YOLOv8 with its Kalman filter-based tracking, obtaining an improvement of 87.3 % on average, in favor of the algorithm proposed in this work.

title: An Efficient Non-Convex Optimization Algorithm Fusing Dynamic Adjustment and Curvature Awareness
authors: Bing Liu, Jingcong Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper proposes the Adaptive Momentum-Curvature Optimizer (AMCO), which addresses the non-convex optimization challenges in deep learning and reinforcement learning through three core mechanisms: dynamic momentum, adaptive learning rate, and curvature awareness. By approximating curvature via gradient difference, AMCO maintains the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{O}(\mathbf{n})$</tex> complexity of first-order algorithms while efficiently utilizing second-order information. Experiments on standard test functions, image classification, and natural language processing show that compared with Adam and SGD, it improves convergence speed by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5 \% - 3 0 \%}$</tex> and enhances learning rate robustness by 2 orders of magnitude, providing a new solution for complex non-convex optimization.

title: Semantic Resonance: Distributed Backdoor Attacks via Semantic Trigger Families in Federated NLP
authors: Jian Wang, Hong Shen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Federated learning (FL) is widely used in privacysensitive natural language processing applications, but its distributed nature also introduces serious security vulnerabilities, particularly backdoor attacks. Existing federated NLP backdoor methods typically rely on fixed token triggers, which suffer from gradient discordance under severe non-IID data and are easily exposed by robust aggregation defenses. In this paper, we propose Semantic Resonance, a novel federated NLP backdoor attack framework that integrates distributed attack strategies with semantic-level triggers. By instantiating an abstract semantic concept as a family of natural and diverse trigger variants and distributing them across malicious clients, the proposed method induces consistent gradient directions in a shared semantic subspace during federated aggregation, enabling robust backdoor implantation in the global model. Experiments on the IMDB dataset show that our method achieves a 92.8% attack success rate on BERT-base with only a 0.8% drop in clean accuracy and a malicious update detection rate of 12.5%, significantly outperforming four baselines. Under extreme non-IID settings, the attack success rate remains at 88.6%, and clear advantages are preserved under robust aggregation defenses such as Krum.

title: Data-Driven Predictive Modeling of Stress and Sleep Issues in Young Adults from Device Usage
authors: Nabeel M Sheikh, Amit Kumar, Vansh Agarwal
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Young adults have increasingly engaged in the overuse of electronic gadgets, raising questions about their mental health and sleep quality. This research examines the associations between daily technology use–such as screen time, social media engagement, gaming, and pre-sleep device usage–and selfreported stress levels and sleep duration using a publicly available dataset of young adults in the United States [17]. The study employs chi-square testing, multinomial logistic regression, and non-parametric bootstrapping. Results indicate that individuals who used electronic devices for over six hours daily were more likely to report higher stress levels, while those who used devices within two hours before bedtime tended to report shorter sleep duration and elevated stress. Social media usage did not show a statistically significant association with stress levels (p=0.2388). Model diagnostics and bootstrapped confidence intervals further confirmed that social media effects were inconclusive, warranting cautious interpretation. Overall, excessive screen time, particularly at night, is associated with elevated stress and reduced sleep duration among young adults. These findings suggest that technology moderation and mindfulness practices may support the mental and physical well-being of young adults. The crosssectional design of this study limits causal inference; longitudinal research is needed to establish directionality.

title: Bin-Based R: Resource-Efficient RF Modulation Classification Using Envelope Statistics
authors: Srinivas Rahul Sapireddy, G Surekha, Hemanth Bandi
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Lightweight and accurate RF modulation classification is critical for real-time processing on edge and embedded platforms. This paper presents a rule-based modulation classification framework that leverages bin-based <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}$</tex>-values, a statistical measure derived from the ratio of variance to squared mean of segmented signal envelopes [1]. Each received signal is partitioned into fixed-length time bins, and the most informative <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}$</tex>-values are selected to form a compact feature representation. Classification is performed using statistically defined decision thresholds, enabling training-free and low-latency operation. Two envelope extraction methods are evaluated: the Hilbert transform and the short-time Fourier transform (STFT). The proposed approach achieves high classification accuracy, with the Hilbert-based implementation yielding 98.4% (AM), 99.5% (DSB), and 98.9% (SSB), and the STFT-based implementation achieving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$98.8 \%, 99.2 {\%}$</tex>, and 98.4%, respectively. In addition to accuracy, computational efficiency is assessed in terms of memory usage, where the proposed framework achieves up to a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$14 \times$</tex> reduction in storage for the Hilbert-based implementation and a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.8 \times$</tex> reduction for the STFT-based implementation compared to prior approaches. These results demonstrate that the proposed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}$</tex>-value framework provides an effective and resource-efficient solution for modulation classification in IoT, software-defined radio, and edge AI systems.

title: Sparse Spectral Modeling of SVPWM Harmonics for Efficient Electromagnetic Loss Mapping in EV Traction Drives
authors: Zihao Wang, Tenghui Dong, Jiapu Sun, Haibo Long, Chong Zhu, Xi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: SVPWM harmonics cause significant electromagnetic losses in electric vehicle traction drives, yet conventional time-domain co-simulation is computationally prohibitive due to timescale mismatches. This paper proposes a unified and analytically transparent surrogate framework based on the spectral manifold of SVPWM. The methodology employs a hierarchical sparsity mechanism incorporating both Hard Sparsity and Soft Sparsity to establish a deterministic mapping from modulation physics to a compact Dominant Excitation Subspace. Furthermore, Analytical Sensitivity Mapping is utilized to resolve non-ideal boundary perturbations induced by dead-time effects without exhaustive re-integration. Experimental results on a 55kW traction platform show fundamental and carrier sideband errors below 0.3% and 0.44%, respectively. Integrated with 3D finite-element analysis, the framework maintains a rotor loss error of 1.03% while reducing normalized computation time to only 0.29 p.u. at high speeds. This approach provides a high-fidelity and efficient alternative for system-level electric drive optimization.

title: FoV Prediction-Based Adaptive Streaming Mechanism for 6DoF Volumetric MR Applications in Multi-Base-Station Networks
authors: Li-Chin Siang, Wen-Hsing Kuo, Pei-Chieh Lin, Chih-Wei Huang, De-Nian Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: The emergence of mixed reality (MR) as a significant application in mobile networks has garnered significant attention. Wireless headsets enable unrestricted user movement within femtocell networks comprising numerous small base stations, offering a promising solution for MR applications. However, the complexity of these systems poses challenges in optimizing resource allocation across base stations. This paper proposes a novel resource allocation method for volumetric MR streaming in multi-base-station environments. The method consists of two phases. Firstly, the method uses neural networks to model and forecast users’ viewing directions. Leveraging these predictions, their confidence levels, and layer characteristics, the algorithm adjusts video quality for each user and allocates transmission resources across base stations to optimize overall performance. Through comprehensive analysis, we prove that this novel problem is NP-hard and show that our approach achieves a performance within a bounded gap from the optimal solution. Simulation results reveal that our proposed algorithm outperforms existing techniques, enhancing aggregate performance across diverse scenarios.

title: Energy-Efficient Sequential Estimation Via Sensor Ordering
authors: Chen Quan, Geethu Joseph, Nitin Jonathan Myers
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: Estimation problems in wireless sensor networks (WSNs) typically involve collecting and processing data from distributed sensors at the fusion center to infer the state of an environment. However, not all measurements contribute equally to estimation accuracy. In this work, we incorporate the concept of ordered transmission into sequential estimation to select the most informative measurements from different sensors, while ensuring the desired estimation quality. We analyze a general estimation problem with different estimator choices and derive stopping rules for collecting measurements. Then, we derive the expected number of transmissions required for our ordered transmission-based sequential estimation scheme and compare it with that of a conventional sequential estimation scheme with unordered transmissions. To validate the proposed protocol, we apply it to a radar-based WSN for target localization and velocity estimation, designing an ordered transmission strategy and a sequential stopping rule. Simulation results show that our protocol requires fewer transmissions compared to conventional sequential estimation while maintaining similar estimation accuracy in general WSNs. In a radar-based WSN, the proposed protocol achieves reliable estimation with reduced communication overhead and improved response time.

title: Learning Where to Look: Differentiable Slice Selection and Efficient Channel Attention for FCD-II MRI Classification
authors: Himanshu Kumar Pathak, Shruti Priya, Unnati Chaurasia, Koushlendra Kumar Singh, Marios Antonakakis, Michalis Zervakis
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Focal Cortical Dysplasia (FCD) is a major cause of drug-resistant epilepsy both in children and adults. In most such cases, surgery is the most effective treatment unless other treatments, such as rehabilitation, are the most effective intervention; hence, it is important to have the correct identification of FCD in order to make a clinical decision. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a common technique of imaging FCD because it is painless and offers good resolution images. Nevertheless, MRI is still a big challenge in detecting the lesions of FCD. The lesions are diverse in size and shape, and their location andimagingfeatures are usually subtle and atypical. Therefore, the process of manual identification is not only time consuming but also very much relies on the knowledge of the epileptologist, which brings inconsistency in diagnosis. To overcome these difficulties, this research is de voted to the automated detection of FCD lesions (i.e., FCD type-II) with the help of the state-of-the-art deep-learning methods. An automatic slice selection architecture based on Gumbel-softmax hard thresholding is proposed, which selects the top k important slices in a 3D MRI volume. The selected slices are then passed to Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) enhanced pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks(CNNs) of DenseNet201, VGG16 and VGG19. The proposed method can detect the changes in healthy brain tissue, FCD-II lesions and T1w features by comparing FCD II lesions and T1w features with healthy brain tissue us ing FCD-II, T1-weighted (T1w) and FLAIR MRI sequences. Amongthese models, ECA-DenseNet201 demonstrated the best performance in classification, achieving high accuracy (96.7% for FLAIR and 96.8% for T1w), precision (0.972 for FLAIR and 0.957 for T1W), and F1-score (0.953 for FLAIR and 0.967 for T1W) in distinguishing FCD-II slices from healthy brain slices.

title: A Statistical Physics Framework for Intermittent Neural Control of Human Balance
authors: Yongkun Zhao, Kaichen Yin, Emanuele Abbagnano, Dario Farina
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: Human quiet stance appears to be a simple postural task, yet it relies on complex neural control mechanisms to maintain balance. Although quiet stance control has been studied extensively, the underlying stabilization strategy remains debated. Intermittent neural feedback control is one of the few plausible mechanisms that can simultaneously stabilize upright posture and account for the characteristic sway observed during standing. However, intermittent control during quiet stance constitutes a distinctive class of nonlinear control characterized by state-dependent switching and manifold-triggered activation. These properties complicate the application of conventional nonlinear control frameworks to rigorous stability analysis. Consequently, much of the existing literature has relied predominantly on numerical simulations rather than analytical results. In this study, we systematically investigated the stability of quiet stance under manifold-triggered intermittent neural feedback control, with particular emphasis on marginal stability. We found that the control manifold destabilized the system in some activation regions but enhanced stability in others. To address the theoretical challenges posed by delays and stochastic noise, we further developed a statistical-physics-based approach for stability characterization that complements Lyapunov-based analyses and is applicable to noise-perturbed delayed dynamics. Using this framework, we delineated the stable parameter regions for intermittent neural control and quantified how key system parameters shape the stability boundaries through both theoretical analysis and numerical validation. Together, these results provide a principled perspective for studying noise-perturbed delayed nonlinear dynamics in intermittent control models of quiet stance, offering insights into neural balance regulation and informing potential applications such as fall-risk mitigation and the control design of lower-limb exoskeletons.

title: Energy-Efficient Quantum Federated Learning over Low-Altitude UAV Networks with Theoretical Guarantees
authors: Shaba Shaon, Atit Pokharel, Dinh C. Nguyen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: This paper studies a novel energy-efficient quantum federated learning (QFL) framework designed for low-altitude quantum-enabled unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) networks. We develop a novel energy minimization problem by jointly considering UAV’s quantum computing power, computational frequency, transmit power, number of quantum noise measurements, and trajectory. The energy minimization problem we formulate is inherently non-convex and cannot be efficiently solved using conventional convex optimization methods in its original form. To overcome this challenge, we develop an iterative optimization approach that integrates block coordinate descent (BCD) with successive convex approximation (SCA) techniques to effectively find optimal solutions for QFL’s energy optimization. Moreover, we conduct a holistic convergence analysis of federated quantum model training, which reveals the important trade-off between the convergence rate and number of quantum noise measurements. Evaluations under different data settings demonstrate that our theoretical results are consistent with QFL model training simulations, thereby facilitating future QFL algorithm design. Extensive simulation results also show that our optimization methodology significantly improves energy efficiency over baseline methods.

title: Hybrid intelligent multi-objective dynamic optimization based on MOEPSO-GA
authors: Jun Fu, Linqing Du, Huan Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: In this paper, a hybrid intelligent optimization method based on multi-objective enhanced particle swarm optimization-genetic algorithm (MOEPSO-GA) is proposed for solving multi-objective dynamic optimization problem. This method innovatively combines the global search capability of heuristic algorithms with the high precision and guaranteed optimality of deterministic algorithms, ensuring an evenly distributed Pareto front. Firstly, a global dual optimal selection mechanism is introduced, utilizing a genetic algorithm to balance the diversity and convergence of non-dominated solutions. Secondly, an adaptive flight parameter adjustment mechanism is designed to assist particles in adaptively selecting learning modes, thereby balancing the global exploration and local development capabilities. Thirdly, the optimal replacement mechanism significantly enhances population diversity, enabling MOEPSO-GA to rapidly explore non-dominated regions. Fourthly, the adaptive ε-constraint method is combined with sequential quadratic programming to optimize the non-dominated set explored by MOEPSO-GA, promoting rapid convergence to the true Pareto front and thereby reducing computational costs. Finally, the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed algorithm are validated through numerical simulations and comparisons with state-of-the-art algorithms.

title: ASBench: Image Anomalies Synthesis Benchmark for Anomaly Detection
authors: Qunyi Zhang, Songan Zhang, Jiaqi Liu, Jinbao Wang, Xiaoning Lei, Guoyang Xie, Guannan Jiang, Zhichao Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Anomaly detection plays a pivotal role in manufacturing quality control, yet its application is constrained by limited abnormal samples and high manual annotation costs. While anomaly synthesis offers a promising solution, existing studies predominantly treat anomaly synthesis as an auxiliary component within anomaly detection frameworks, lacking systematic evaluation of anomaly synthesis algorithms. Current research also overlooks crucial factors specific to anomaly synthesis, such as decoupling its impact from detection, quantitative analysis of synthetic data and adaptability across different scenarios. To address these limitations, we propose ASBench, the first comprehensive benchmarking framework dedicated to evaluating anomaly synthesis methods. Our framework introduces four critical evaluation dimensions: (i) the generalization performance across different datasets and pipelines (ii) the ratio of synthetic to real data (iii) the correlation between intrinsic metrics of synthesis images and anomaly detection performance metrics, and (iv) strategies for hybrid anomaly synthesis methods. Through extensive experiments, ASBench not only reveals limitations in current anomaly synthesis methods but also provides actionable insights for future research directions in anomaly synthesis. Code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/M-3LAB/ASBench</uri>.

title: Comparative Electric Field Stress Analysis in Power Transformer Insulation Under Variable Moisture, Paper Types, and Dielectric Fluids
authors: Ismael Antolin, Carlos H. Alonso, Ramazan Altay, Felix Ortiz, Alfredo Ortiz, Francisco De Leon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: This work presents a comparative analysis of the electric field in power transformer insulation systems. The study considers three types of insulating papers (Kraft, Thermally Upgraded Kraft, and Diamond Printed Enhanced) and three dielectric liquids (mineral oil, natural ester, and synthetic ester) under controlled moisture contents (ranging from 1% to 5%). A two-dimensional Finite Element Method model was used to evaluate the electric stress distribution in a 220 MVA power transformer under steady state and impulse test conditions. The results confirm that all configurations satisfy insulation limits and withstand impulse stress requirements. The study demonstrates that moisture content influences the electric field behavior and that any ester-impregnated insulation becomes advantageous at medium moisture content levels. For each type of ester, we propose a moisture content value which can be used by transformer owners as an optimal threshold to conduct a retrofilling procedure.

title: BinEnhance-Pro: Enhancing Binary Code Search by Distinguishing Similar but Non-Homologous Functions
authors: Yongpan Wang, Qi Liu, Zhen Lei, Siyuan Li, Xiaojie Zhu, Zhihui Zhao, Xiaodong Gu, Yongle Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Binary code search is essential in applications such as software reuse detection and vulnerability identification. Existing methods rely on intra-function semantics or their combination with call graphs, which struggle to address real-world complexities like function inlining (missing callees) and highly similar calls. Our previous work However, its effectiveness is limited by low-quality random training data, which fails to adequately distinguish Similar but Non-homologous Functions (SNFs), resulting in a high false positive rate. In this paper, we propose <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BinEnhance-Pro</small>, a general framework that integrates a hard negative mining strategy into the training process. Specifically, we select SNFs with high similarity in internal embeddings as hard negatives, and introduce an adaptive balancing mechanism that determines their optimal ratio to random samples based on the learning capability of the internal semantic model. This strategy forces the model to learn finer-grained decision boundaries, effectively distinguishing true semantic equivalence from superficial structural similarity. We conduct experiments across six different tasks, and the results demonstrate both the performance and robustness of <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BinEnhance-Pro</small> . When applied to existing methods such as Diemph, HermesSim on a public dataset, <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BinEnhance-Pro</small> demonstrates a notable improvement in Mean Average Precision (MAP), increasing from 54.2% to 70.3%, and outperforming the enhancements brought by <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">BinEnhance</small>

title: Indoor Fingerprint Collection under Environment Changes by Vehicular Crowdsensing: A Bayesian Reinforcement Learning Approach_supp1-3678457.pdf
authors: Guozheng Li
publish: N/A
abstract: Indoor localization is crucial for applications such as navigation, asset tracking, and emergency response. Fingerprint-based methods that use RSSI are widely adopted; however, they fail under large environmental changes. Unmanned Vehicles (UVs) equipped with high precision sensors are able to collect fingerprints, serving as a promising way by forming a Vehicular Crowdsensing (VCS) campaign. In this paper, we propose “BRAVE”, a Bayesian RL Approach for VCS under Environment changing, while introducing a new metric “Calibration Benefit” to explicitly quantify how effectively a learned trajectory updates those regions of the fingerprint database that have changed and matter most for localization. Specifically, we propose a spatial-temporal Bayesian Network(BN) for change detection, a region rearrangement method for fewer restarts, and an optimistic strategy to balance the exploration and exploitation trade-offs in optimizing calibration benefit. Extensive results on two real-world datasets from SML Center (Shanghai) and Haopu Fashion City (Shanghai) demonstrate that BRAVE outperforms eight baselines and the derived dataset has better localization accuracy compared with the original dataset.

title: Autonomous Navigation in Unstructured Environments: A Probabilistic Approach for Generating Local Guidance With Limited Prior Information
authors: Yafeng Bu, Zhenping Sun, Binhan Du, Yunfei Xie, Xiaohui Li, Hui Shen
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Generating reliable local guidance information is crucial for autonomous navigation in unstructured environments with limited prior information. Conventional approaches often fuse navigation cues with different physical semantics into a single scalar objective through manually weighted cost terms, making the contribution of each cue difficult to interpret and often requiring scenario-specific parameter tuning. To address this issue, we propose a probabilistic local reference path generation method that uniformly models environmental traversability, goal direction, kinematic preference, and temporal consistency as angular distributions in a polar coordinate system. Their agreement with environmental evidence is quantified by the Bhattacharyya Coefficient to derive adaptive fusion weights. On top of the fused posterior, anchor inference is formulated as a dynamic-programming-based sequence optimization over selected radial layers, enabling real-time generation of smooth local reference paths in continuous space. We conducted simulation experiments on the BARN dataset, where the proposed method significantly outperforms baseline methods in terms of task completion rate, while maintaining competitive performance in metrics such as path length, smoothness, and safety. Furthermore, we conducted real-world vehicle experiments, including autonomous driving trials that covered 3.2 km in real off-road scenarios, which further validate the effectiveness and practicality of the method under limited prior information.

title: SceneGlue: Scene-Aware Transformer for Feature Matching without Scene-Level Annotation_supp1-3684799.pdf
authors: Songlin Du
publish: N/A
abstract: Local feature matching plays a critical role in understanding the correspondence between cross-view images. However, traditional methods are constrained by the inherent local nature of feature descriptors, limiting their ability to capture non-local scene information that is essential for accurate cross-view correspondence. In this paper, we introduce SceneGlue, a scene-aware feature matching framework designed to overcome these limitations. SceneGlue leverages a hybridizable matching paradigm that integrates implicit parallel attention and explicit cross-view visibility estimation. The parallel attention mechanism simultaneously exchanges information among local descriptors within and across images, enhancing the scene’s global context. To further enrich the scene awareness, we propose the Visibility Transformer, which explicitly categorizes features into visible and invisible regions, providing an understanding of cross-view scene visibility. By combining explicit and implicit scene-level awareness, SceneGlue effectively compensates for the local descriptor constraints. Notably, SceneGlue is trained using only local feature matches, without requiring scene-level groundtruth annotations. This scene-aware approach not only improves accuracy and robustness but also enhances interpretability compared to traditional methods. Extensive experiments on applications such as homography estimation, pose estimation, image matching, and visual localization validate SceneGlues superior performance. The source code is available at https://github.com/songlindu/ SceneGlue.

title: Frequency-Tunable Thermoacoustic Signal Generation and Simulation via Changing Microwave Pulse Properties
authors: Muran Li, Zhiyuan Zhang, Yingjian Cao, Weijie Wang, Wei Jiang, Yelei Yao, Jianxun Wang, Lin Huang, Guo Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Electromagnetics, RF and Microwaves in Medicine and Biology
abstract: This work performs a systematic parametric study on the correlation between microwave pulse parameters (including frequency, pulse width, and rise/fall times) and the spectral characteristics of thermoacoustic (TA) signals, while conducting rigorous experimental validation of the underlying relationship —— thereby providing the physical basis for both microwave-induced thermoacoustic imaging (MTAI) and the microwave auditory effect (MAE). A multiphysics simulation model describing this relationship was experimentally validated using a soy sauce tube, demonstrating excellent agreement with COMSOL simulations, with spectral peak deviations below 30 kHz. The systematic analysis of pulse parameters yields quantitative TA frequency-tuning laws, which are lacking in previous fragmented studies. Analysis of pulse parameters enables the derivation of TA frequency-tuning laws. Based on these relationships, optimized microwave waveforms were designed to mitigate the trade-off between spatial resolution and penetration depth in MTAI. Furthermore, the optimized parameters allow the generation of controllable acoustic waveforms, enabling targeted auditory stimulation in MAE. These findings enhance the theoretical understanding of TA mechanisms and provide guidance for high-resolution deep-tissue imaging and precise acoustic signal synthesis. Additionally, this study lays the groundwork for the use of ultrashort electromagnetic pulses in generating high-frequency ultrasound signals.

title: Adaptive Automatic Generation Control in Multi-area Power Systems: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach for Model-based Controller Tuning
authors: Mkhutazi Mditshwa, Komla A. Folly, David T.O. Oyedokun
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The increasing penetration of renewable energy sources (RES) reduces power system inertia, causing severe frequency excursions following disturbances. Conventional Automatic Generation Control (AGC) using fixed-gain controllers struggles to adapt to these rapidly changing dynamics. While deep reinforcement learning (DRL) shows promise for adaptive AGC, most studies lack validation on industrial-grade platforms. This paper addresses this gap by developing a comprehensive DRL framework for optimal AGC tuning via a novel PowerFactory-Python co-simulation interface. Four DRL algorithms such as TD3, SAC, PPO, and DDPG, are benchmarked for the real-time optimization of PI, PID, and Fractional-Order PID (FOPID) controllers. Extensive simulations on a modified IEEE 14-bus system with 46% renewable penetration demonstrate that TD3 provides the most stable frequency response, preventing dangerous oscillations during critical transitions like generator restoration. The results establish a clear performance hierarchy: TD3 outperforms SAC, followed by DDPG, with PPO being the worst performing. Among the controllers, FOPID achieves the best overall damping; quantitative evaluation shows that TD3-FOPID reduces the Integral of Absolute Error (IAE) by 55.9% compared to conventionally-tuned baselines. Finally, configuration training times fall within a 24-hour window, proving compatibility with day-ahead economic dispatch scheduling for overnight retraining. This high-fidelity co-simulation framework provides a practical pathway for the industrial adoption of intelligent AGC.

title: A Review of Automotive AR-HUD Interfaces Across Driver Roles and Vehicle Automation Levels
authors: Leonardo Vezzani, Francesco Strada, Andrea Bottino
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Augmented Reality Head-Up Displays (AR-HUDs) have emerged as a transformative technology in the automotive sector, significantly enhancing driver awareness and safety by seamlessly integrating critical information into the driver’s direct line of sight. However, implementing effective AR-HUD systems presents several challenges, including designing intuitive yet minimally distracting user interfaces, ensuring accurate spatial registration, and adapting visualizations to different contexts and levels of vehicle automation. This article provides a comprehensive overview of the current literature on automotive AR-HUDs and offers a structured taxonomy that classifies existing studies along two main dimensions: the specific features of the AR-HUD interface, which capture the relationship between display-related and interaction-related characteristics across tasks and driving contexts, and the human-automation roles, which frame the evolving shift of control, attention, and responsibility between the human and the automated system as vehicle autonomy increases. Additionally, we critically analyze existing testing methodologies and identify significant gaps, such as the overreliance on testing in virtual environments and the lack of standardized frameworks for the progressive evaluation of AR-HUD interfaces, from conceptual designs to immersive virtual simulations and real-world assessments. We conclude by discussing key open research questions and future research directions needed to overcome current limitations and realize the full potential of AR-HUD technology.

title: OGC-SSD: Overlap-Guided Calibration and Progressive Regression for 3D Object Detection
authors: Xuanqiang Yu, Jianwei Li, Sixi Chen, Li Jianyu
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: Reliable ranking of detection hypotheses remains challenging in single-stage 3D object detection due to the misalignment between classification confidence and localization quality. From a signal processing perspective, localization quality can be viewed as a latent and noisy signal that is difficult to estimate under sparse and occluded observations. In this letter, we propose OGC-SSD, a lightweight and detector-agnostic framework that improves ranking reliability and regression stability by modeling localization quality uncertainty and overlap-dependent optimization behavior. First, we formulate 3D IoU estimation as a discrete probabilistic signal modeling problem, where localization quality is predicted as a categorical distribution and its expectation is used for confidence calibration and ranking refinement. Second, we introduce an overlap-guided regression strategy that shifts optimization from coarse alignment to fine-grained geometric refinement according to the overlap stage between predicted and ground-truth boxes. Experiments on the KITTI benchmark show that the proposed method consistently improves ranking consistency and localization accuracy with negligible inference overhead, especially for small and hard-to-detect objects. The proposed modules are plug-and-play and readily applicable to existing single-stage detectors.

title: ICT-META: In-Context Aware Few-Shot Learner for Encrypted Traffic Classification
authors: Chengxiang Chang, Ying Li, Suranga Seneviratne, Xingquan Cai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Machine Learning in Communications and Networking
abstract: Encrypted traffic classification is a fundamental task in cybersecurity and network traffic management. With the popularity of deep learning, many studies have used it to achieve high accuracy in this field. However, these models rely on large amounts of labeled data and require retraining or fine-tuning when faced with new tasks, which is time-consuming and resource-intensive. Due to this limitation, few-shot traffic classification methods have emerged to reduce dependence on large labeled datasets by using only a few samples. However, these models often still require pre-training and fine-tuning to perform well. Meanwhile, Large Language Models (LLMs), such as ChatGPT, have attracted attention for their capacity to learn new concepts during inference without fine-tuning. Inspired by this, we propose In-Context Encrypted Traffic, a novel few-shot network traffic classification method allowing for few-shot classification without the need for fine-tuning. We use two existing encrypted traffic datasets to demonstrate the effectiveness of our method when facing new samples. We evaluate our method on a realistic 5-way few-shot classification task over encrypted traffic, and it achieves up to 5.7% higher accuracy than representative meta-learning baselines, without requiring fine-tuning.
