title: SGFAM: Semantic and Geometric Features Aggregation for Dense Shape Matching in Generalizable Robotic Manipulation
authors: Paolo Sebeto, Christian Hartl-Nesic, Jean-Baptiste Weibel, Daniel Zimmer, Andreas Holzinger, Markus Vincze
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: To automate processes like polishing or cleaning at scale, robots must be able to adapt learned skills to new object instances without manual reprogramming. Applications requiring tool-surface interactions face a significant challenge in transferring manipulation strategies to novel objects due to substantial shape and appearance variations. Robust, generalized dense shape correspondence is essential for solving this problem. We present SGFAM, a zero-shot pipeline integrating pre-trained vision foundation models and geometric encoders via functional maps. Unlike prior works that rely on simple averaging, we introduce 1) an alignment-based feature aggregation to prioritize optimal viewing angles, and 2) Kernel PCA fusion to preserve non-linear descriptor manifolds. Our evaluations demonstrate that this approach not only outperforms state-of-the-art baselines but also enables lightweight vision backbones to achieve matching precision comparable to larger models. We validate SGFAM experimentally by successfully transferring continuous surface paths in real-world industrial and household robotic scenarios without requiring any finetuning.

title: IDHNet: A CNN-Transformer Hybrid Network with Feature Interaction for Robust Automatic Modulation Classification
authors: Wentao Li, Zhuoran Cai, Yi Lou, Yun Lin, Guan Gui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Automatic Modulation Classification (AMC) is a critical enabling technology in modern wireless communication systems, essential for spectrum resource management, signal detection, and adaptive transmission optimization. Recent efforts have focused on hybrid neural architectures to improve AMC performance. However, existing CNN–Transformer designs still exhibit inherent limitations in feature extraction, which hinder effective representation learning of complex I/Q signals and consequently restrict classification accuracy and robustness under challenging wireless environments. To address these issues, this paper proposes a high-performance CNN–Transformer hybrid architecture, termed IDHNet. The network consists of two dedicated modules. First, an Inter-branch Fusion Convolution Module (IFCM) is introduced in the Convolutional Stem to extract multi-scale waveform features, where different receptive fields interact during feature construction to form more expressive local representations. Second, HDSFormer is introduced to model non-restricted temporal–channel signal features. This module combines a lightweight attention mechanism with a convolutional front-end to collaboratively capture global context and fine-grained temporal details. Based on this architecture, IDHNet establishes an integrated local–global representation learning pipeline, enabling complementary collaboration between convolution and attention while preserving detailed I/Q waveform information. The experimental results on the RadioML2016.10a, RadioML2016.10b, and RML22 datasets show that IDHNet achieves classification accuracies of 63.81%, 65.43%, and 70.92%, respectively, surpassing the state-of-the-art baselines. Furthermore, the proposed model exhibits strong robustness in low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions and in scenarios involving highly confusable modulation types.

title: Multi-objective Model Predictive Control for Virtually Coupled Train Set Under Heterogeneous Slopes and Uncertain Dynamics
authors: Zhengwei Luo, Jidong Lv, Wanli Lu, Hongjie Liu, Ming Chai, Tao Tang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: IoT-based virtual coupling systems enable reduced headways for high-speed trains, yet most Virtually Coupled Train Set (VCTS) studies still rely on a leader-follower paradigm where followers simply match the leader’s speed. Under position-dependent slopes/speed limits, asynchronous departures, and uncertain train dynamics, it remains difficult to jointly achieve traffic efficiency, cooperative tracking accuracy, and ride comfort. This paper proposes a hierarchical multi-objective Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework for follower trains in VCTS with a multi-step decision structure consisting of an offline tightening design and an online two-step regulation procedure. Offline, we solve an optimization-based tightening design that jointly determines tightened constraint sets and disturbance-affine feedback gains under Automatic Train Protection (ATP) supervision, improving traffic-efficiency potential by reducing conservatism that can otherwise trigger unnecessary braking actions and degrade throughput. Online, a two-step regulation is executed under uncertainty: first, a tracking-prioritized robust MPC enforces ATP-compliant operation; second, using the resulting optimal and feasible tracking-cost information consistent with the offline-designed feedback gains, we construct a Lyapunov-based constraint that yields an explicit tracking-cost tolerance bound, within which a comfort-oriented MPC improves ride comfort. A comfort-aware adaptive mechanism then tunes the tolerance online for more consistent tracking-comfort behaviour. The proposed distributed framework is proved to be recursively feasible and input-to-state stable. Simulations on the Beijing-Tianjin high-speed railway line with CR400BF parameters demonstrate that, compared with representative weighted-sum and lexicographic baselines, the proposed method achieves a more reliable tracking-comfort trade-off with less conservative traffic-efficiency performance.

title: Wavelet-Guided Water-Level Estimation for ISAC
authors: Ayoob Salari, Kai Wu, Khawaja Fahad Masood, Y. Jay Guo, J. Andrew Zhang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Real-time water-level monitoring across many locations is vital for flood response, infrastructure management, and environmental forecasting. Yet many sensingmethods rely on fixed instruments—acoustic, radar, camera, or pressure probes—that are costly to install and maintain and are vulnerable during extreme events. We propose a passive, low-cost water-level tracking scheme that uses only LTE downlink power metrics reported by commodity receivers. The method extracts per-antenna RSRP, RSSI, and RSRQ, applies a continuous wavelet transform (CWT) to the RSRP to isolate the semidiurnal tide component, and forms a summed-coefficient signature that simultaneously marks high/low tide (tide-turn times) and tracks the tide-rate (flow speed) over time. These wavelet features guide a lightweight neural network that learns water-level changes over time from a short training segment. Beyond a single serving base station, we also show a multi-base-station cooperative mode: independent CWTs are computed per carrier and fused by a robust median to produce one tide-band feature that improves stability and resilience to local disturbances. Experiments over a 420 m river path under line-of-sight conditions achieve root-mean-square and mean-absolute errors of 0.8 cm and 0.5 cm, respectively. Under a non-line-of-sight setting with vegetation and vessel traffic, the same model transfers successfully after brief fine-tuning, reaching 1.7 cm RMSE and 0.8 cm MAE. Unlike CSI-based methods, the approach needs no array calibration and runs on standard hardware, making wide deployment practical. When signals from multiple base stations are available, fusion further improves robustness.

title: Adaptive Reinforcement Learning Optimal Time-Varying Formation Control for Nonlinear Multiagent Systems via a Dynamic Event-Triggered Mechanism
authors: Zhenghua Li, Shuo Ding, Ben Niu, Ning Zhao, Xudong Zhao
publish: IEEE Systems Journal
abstract: This article investigates an event-triggered optimal time-varying formation (TVF) control problem for nonlinear multiagent systems with unmeasurable states and unknown nonlinear dynamics. A neural network-based state observer is first designed to reconstruct unmeasurable system states. Meanwhile, neural networks are employed to approximate unknown nonlinear functions. Subsequently, an adaptive reinforcement learning algorithm is proposed to address the optimal control issue within an identifier–critic–actor framework. To save system communication resources, this article designs a dynamic event-triggered mechanism in sensor-controller channels and develops an adaptive state event-triggered control scheme. Unfortunately, state-triggered control may cause nondifferentiability in virtual control laws, posing significant challenges for the design of an optimal TVF controller. To overcome this obstacle, we use observer output signals to construct virtual control laws. Then, an event-triggered controller is constructed by replacing estimated states with intermittent estimates. The results show that formation tracking errors can converge to a small neighborhood near the origin, and all closed-loop signals are semi-globally uniformly bounded. Finally, the effectiveness of the control scheme is verified by an example simulation.

title: Adaptive Optimal Control of Complex Active Vehicle Suspension Systems: State-Safety-Aware Regulation Mechanism and Experimental Validation
authors: Ning Liu, Xiangpeng Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper develops an adaptive optimal control with a state-safety-aware regulation mechanism for uncertain nonlinear active quarter-vehicle suspension systems (AQVSS), in which safety influence is adaptively regulated according to operating conditions to ensure constraint satisfaction without excessive conservatism in optimal performance. Within this control framework, an unscented Kalman filter (UKF)-based adaptive dynamic programming (ADP) approach is developed to combine accurate state estimation with optimal policy learning under unknown disturbances, while satisfying multiple safety constraints including dynamic deflection, wheel load, and actuator force limits. The optimization incorporates safety constraints through control barrier function (CBF)-based penalty terms with a state-safety-aware regulation mechanism. This enables automatic strengthening of safety influence during aggressive operation or large state deviations, and avoids undue conservatism near steady state. The actor-estimator-critic structure is employed for efficient policy learning. Theoretical analysis establishes the convergence properties of the proposed method. Simulation results and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) experiments demonstrate improved ride comfort, effective constraint enforcement, and reduced control energy consumption, while maintaining robust safety performance.

title: GQKD: Greedy Query Knowledge Distillation for Efficient 3D Instance Segmentation
authors: Taehyun Jeong, Hyeon-Cheol Moon, Suhyeon Lee, Jinwoo Jeong
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: 3D instance segmentation is a fundamental component of various 3D scene understanding applications. Recent transformer-based approaches have achieved strong performance through query-driven decoding. however, their repeated multi-layer decoder architectures impose substantial computational overhead, motivating the need for model compression. In this work, we identify two key phenomena across decoder layers: object disappearance, where valid object queries vanish as decoding proceeds, and object degradation, where intermediate-layer predictions can be more reliable than final-layer outputs. Based on these observations, we propose Greedy Query Knowledge Distillation (GQKD), an effective distillation framework for lightweight 3D instance segmentation. GQKD constructs a pseudo-GT query set to supervise the student model by selecting the best true-positive query for each instance across multiple decoder layers, along with low-confidence negative teacher queries. This Pseudo-GT recovers high-quality object hypotheses lost during decoding and provides balanced supervision by supplementing recovered positives with low-confidence negatives. Experimental results on ScanNetV2 demonstrate that GQKD enables a student model with approximately 50% reduced decoder depth to achieve performance close to the teacher when used alone, and further improves results when combined with existing distillation strategies.

title: Preserving Runtime Control Flow Integrity Transparently Through Hardware-based Stack Confidentiality and Integrity on RISC-V IoT Devices
authors: Weike Wang, Bingzheng Li, Xiaolei Wang, Rikang Zhao, Senyang Li, Xiang Wang, Juin Jei Liou
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: RISC-V devices are widely used in diverse IoT applications, where security is critical. Control flow hijacking attacks can circumvent existing security mechanisms to transfer the program control flow to harmful gadget chains arranged by adversaries, which are primarily initiated by tampering with the data frame and return address within the stack. In this work, we present a microarchitecture-level, hardware-assisted, lightweight scheme for encrypting and verifying stack integrity, thereby transparently enforcing runtime control-flow integrity. The encrypted stack hinders attackers’ analysis and increases the difficulty of assembling malicious gadgets. Stack integrity verification enables the system to detect malicious actions that tamper with return addresses and crucial registers at runtime. We implement the system on an Xilinx XC7K325T FPGA with the T-head Xuantie E906 RISC-V core. The experimental results show that, with a 16KB D-Cache across the ten selected benchmark sets, this work incurs an average performance overhead of 4.89%. At the same time, the CoreMark score decreases by merely 0.10%. The area introduced by the proposed hardware is 10.01% of the total SoC under SAED 32nm Technology.

title: Less is More: Latent Diffusion for Efficient IoT Side-Channel Analysis
authors: Zekai Zhang, Donglong Chen, Wangchen Dai, Jinfa Hong, Yu Hin Chan, Çetin Kaya Koç, Patrick S.Y. Hung, Ray C.C. Cheung
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The proliferation of cryptographic primitives in resource-constrained Internet of Things (IoT) devices has made them prime targets for Side-Channel Analysis (SCA). However, designing effective defenses against these attacks has become increasingly complex, as traditional deep learning approaches rely heavily on extensive profiling datasets that are difficult to obtain in the context of widely distributed and physically restricted IoT environments. This challenge is further exacerbated by countermeasures such as clock jitter and random delays. To overcome this limitation, this paper introduces a novel and data-efficient three-stage framework for generating high-fidelity synthetic side-channel traces. First, we employ a Supervised Variational Autoencoder (S-VAE) to map noisy, high-dimensional raw traces into a compact and denoised latent space, effectively creating an information-rich manifold. Second, a conditional Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM), powered by an advanced attention-augmented U-Net, is trained exclusively on this computationally tractable latent space to learn the complex conditional data distribution. Finally, we empirically validate our framework on the public ASCAD benchmark and ChipWhisperer CW308T UFO platform. The results are compelling: an attack model trained solely on our synthetic data successfully recovers the secret key in the most challenging ASCAD_desync100 scenario using only 4107 traces and using only 2560 traces, 97.7% accuracy can be achieved on the Chipwhisphere platform. This work provides a practical and efficient pathway for the robust security evaluation of cryptographic implementations in data-scarce IoT environments, significantly lowering the barrier for thorough side-channel vulnerability analysis.

title: DRL With Dual-Actor and Risk-Critic Coupling for Safe Navigation of USVs: Sim-to-Field Validation
authors: Mengmeng Lou, Xiaofei Yang, Zhengrong Xiang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Autonomous collision avoidance is essential for unmanned surface vehicles (USVs) in Maritime Internet of Things (MIoT) systems. Existing deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods often suffer from poor exploration-exploitation balance and weak safety integration, which may lead to local optima and unsafe actions. To address these issues, a risk-aware role-differentiated dual-actor deep deterministic policy gradient framework (RADA-DDPG) is proposed. Abandoning homogeneous structures, a heterogeneous dual-actor design mitigates local optima by assigning distinct exploration and exploitation roles, dynamically balanced via a Q-value-based switching strategy. Additionally, an embedded risk-critic network directly shapes actions during end-to-end policy optimization, rather than serving merely as an external constraint. Extensive simulations demonstrate that RADA-DDPG outperforms baselines in learning efficiency, safety, and robustness. Furthermore, real-world field experiments with dynamic obstacles validate its feasibility.

title: Fluid-Antenna-aided AAV Secure Communications in Eavesdropper Uncertain Location
authors: Yingjie Wu, Junshan Luo, Weiyu Chen, Shilian Wang, Fanggang Wang, Haiyang Ding
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: For autonomous aerial vehicle (AAV) secure communications, traditional designs based on fixed position antenna (FPA) lack sufficient spatial degrees of freedom (DoF), which leaves the line-of-sight-dominated AAV links vulnerable to eavesdropping. To overcome this problem, this paper proposes a framework that effectively incorporates the fluid antenna (FA) and the artificial noise (AN) techniques. Specifically, the minimum secrecy rate (MSR) among multiple eavesdroppers is maximized by jointly optimizing AAV deployment, signal precoder, AN precoder, and FA positions. In particular, the worst-case MSR is considered by taking the channel uncertainties due to the uncertainty about eavesdropping locations into account. To tackle the highly coupled optimization variables and the channel uncertainties in the formulated problem, an efficient and robust algorithm is proposed. Particularly, the uncertain regions of eavesdroppers, whose shapes can be arbitrary, are disposed by constructing convex hull. In addition, two movement modes of FAs are considered, namely, free movement mode and zonal movement mode, for which different optimization techniques are applied, respectively. Also, both single-user and multi-user scenarios are considered. Numerical results show that, the proposed FA schemes boost security by exploiting additional spatial DoF rather than transmit power, while AN provides remarkable gains under high transmit power. The synergy between FA and AN results in a secure advantage that exceeds the sum of their individual contributions, achieving a balance between security and reliability under limited resources.

title: 2-T Embedded Flash Memory using Standard 28 nm Logic CMOS Technology
authors: Jonghyun Ko, Jeseung Jeong, Jiseong Im, Kyung Min Lee, Kangwook Choi, Gyuweon Jung, Jong-Ho Lee
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: A 2-transistor (2-T) embedded flash (eflash) memory fully implemented using a 28 nm logic CMOS technology (Samsung 28 nm LPP) is proposed. The proposed 2-T eflash requires no additional process steps. Compared with prior eflash memory employing three or more transistors and requiring program/erase voltages above 6.8 V, the proposed 2-T eflash reduces the device count and lowers the operating voltage to 4.5 V, substantially simplifying the peripheral circuit configuration. Owing to the low-voltage tunneling-based operation, the eflash exhibits high endurance exceeding 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup> cycles and retention characteristics are maintained for up to 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup> s at an elevated temperature of 80°C. While preserving high-performance retention, the proposed eflash achieves endurance exceeding 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">7</sup> cycles, showing nearly a two orders of magnitude improvement, compared to conventional flash memory endurance. In addition, selective program and erase operations are demonstrated, enabling precise threshold-voltage tuning in a memory array. These results indicate that the proposed 2-T eflash is a promising candidate for scalable compute-in-memory applications.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning and Statistical Forecasting for Short Term Electric Load
authors: Sevan Brodjian, Jason Liao, Abhishek Goswami, Prasenjit Shil
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of various statistical and deep learning frameworks for short-term electric load forecasting, with an emphasis on hybrid modeling approaches. Utilizing extensive real-world data from electric utilities across two Midwest states, we evaluate these frameworks over a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 4}$</tex>-hour forecasting horizon. The study highlights the development of hybrid models that combine statistical methods with deep learning techniques, notably sequence-to-sequence (S2S) models with and without attention mechanisms, as well as the LightGBM and Prophet models. Key achievements include a lightweight model architecture capable of training within one hour on standard CPUs and achieving high prediction accuracy, with the best performing S2S hybrid model achieving a Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) of 1.85%. The research demonstrates the feasibility of sub-daily model retraining while maintaining high accuracy, addressing a crucial need in the dynamic environment of electric load forecasting.

title: Dual-Charging Portable Power Bank with Solar and Grid Using PSO-Based MPPT
authors: Muntasir Elhaj, Adam Bottenfield, Isaac Warren, Eduard Muljadi, R. M. Nelms
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This paper discusses the design and simulation of a portable power bank with dual charging capability, utilizing both solar energy and grid electricity. The system operates on grid power at night, when sunlight is unavailable, and on solar photovoltaic (PV) energy during the day when sunlight is accessible. The objective is to enhance power accessibility in both urban and off-grid areas. To maximize efficiency, the system incorporates Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT), which allows the PV system to make precise adjustments and operate at its highest efficiency, delivering optimal electrical output. This power bank is designed to serve as a reliable and sustainable energy source for charging mobile devices and small electronics, particularly in regions with unstable electricity supply or during outdoor and emergency situations. To ensure optimal power flow, the system is modeled and simulated using MATLAB.

title: Enhancing Software Quality Engineering in Enterprise Systems Using Predictive and Generative AI
authors: Srikanth Singireddy, Abinaya Mettupatti Sivagnanam, Gayathri Surianarayanan
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Software quality engineering is considered as a ma jor challenge for emergent enterprise systems, which is attributed to the complexity of systems and the demand for fast releases driven by dynamic distributed architecture scaling. Conventional quality assurance techniques are heavily dependent on static rulebased testing and human work in a way that is unsuitable for the changes in software behaviors and defects. Recent developments in artificial intelligence provide new ways to improve software quality processes with data-based insights and smart automation. We propose an enterprise-centric, software quality engineering improvement framework built on top of predictive analytics and generative artificial intelligence methods. This strategy uses predictive models to pinpoint at risk components and defective regions early in the design lifecycle, while generative AIs are used to automate testing case creation and validation processes. By integrating these functions into CI/CD pipeline the framework enables scalable, adaptive quality engineering for large enterprise systems. They experimentally evaluate the detection accuracy of defective code, improvement in test coverage, and efficiency of the validation by using enterprise application datasets and realworld testing situations. We show that the proposed framework increases defect prediction accuracy, automated test coverage and decreases regression testing effort as compared to traditional quality engineering methodologies. Furthermore, responsible AI concerns regarding validation checks and hallucination alleviation are taken into account to ensure dependable deployment in enterprise scenario. The results demonstrate the potential of predictive and generative AI to improve quality engineering outcomes, in large enterprise systems, with reliability, scalability, and governance removed/rewrite.

title: Estimation of Heart Rate and Respiratory Rate from NIRS Signals in Athletes using Empirical Mode Decomposition and Continuous Wavelet Transform
authors: Selim Echeverría, Raúl Caulier-Cisterna, Jorge Vergara-Quezada, Felipe Contreras-Briceño, Patricio Fuentealba
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This study explores the estimation of heart rate (HR) and respiratory rate (RR) from near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) signals in elite athletes during incremental exercise. Signals from the prefrontal cortex and m. Intercostales regions were preprocessed using empirical mode decomposition (EMD) to isolate intrinsic mode functions (IMFs) associated with cardiac and respiratory oscillations. These IMFs were analyzed using continuous wavelet transform (CWT) to obtain time-resolved frequency estimates of HR and RR. Performance was evaluated per subject using mean, median, standard deviation (STD), and interquartile range (IQR) of the absolute error, along with Pearson's correlation. For HR, the mean absolute error was 7.71 cycles/min, with a median of 5.35 cycles/min, STD of 9.05 cycles/min, and IQR of 2.72 cycles/min. For RR, the mean absolute error was 3.38 cycles/min, with a median of 2.97 cycles/min, STD of 2.25 cycles/min, and IQR of 1.71 cycles/min. Considering typical physiological ranges during exercise (HR: 120–180 bpm; RR: 30–60 breaths/min), these errors are within acceptable limits for practical applications. Correlation values exceeded 0.8 in 91.5% of subjects for HR but only 51.7% for RR, indicating that while RR errors were numerically smaller due to its narrower physiological range, its consistency across subjects was lower. Despite these limitations (particularly regarding respiratory estimation, motion artifacts, and sampling frequency), the results support the feasibility of EMD+CWT as a non-invasive and adaptive framework for physiological monitoring in sports settings.

title: A Multi-Physics PINN Framework for Interpretable Wind Power Ramp Forecasting
authors: Vinod Kumar Maddineni, Nihar Panchal, Rahul Reddy Devarapally, M.M. Harussani, NagaBabu Koganti
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Wind power ramp events pose significant challenges to grid stability. While data-driven models like eXtreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) achieve high predictive accuracy, they lack interpretability in terms of physical mechanisms. This study introduces a novel multi-physics physics-informed neural network (MP-PINN) framework specifically designed for wind power ramp forecasting, integrating wake propagation, terrain-induced turbulence, and dynamic power curve adaptation supported by rigorous ablation studies isolating individual physics contributions. Using Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) data from five turbines, this study demonstrates that while (XGBoost) attains a marginally higher <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 9 9 9}$</tex>, the proposed MP-PINN achieves the highest ramp detection F1-score of 0.881 and exhibits superior physical consistency and cross-turbine generalization with an average <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> of 0.997. The framework enables physics-based explainability through constraint contribution analysis and advances forecasting toward causal attribution of ramp events for reliable grid integration.

title: Proactive Handoff Mechanisms in 6G Mobile Networks: AI-Driven Innovations for Seamless Mobility
authors: Sambit S. Mondal, Biplab Kumar Mondal, Khushi Banerjee, Bikas Mondal, Sujata Kundu, Sudip Das, Anirban Kundu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: The transformation in mobility management by shifting from traditional reactive handoff approaches to intelligent proactive strategies, the upcoming Sixth Generation (6G) wireless network will play a major role. These proactive systems using Advanced Artificial Intelligence & machine learning, will support mobility decision which are predictive, dynamic optimization and network control parameters will be adopted automatically. Hyun-Seo Park et al. (2024) and Abbas Ibrahim Mbulwa et al. (2024) suggested that, handover failures will be almost zero, with AI Driven prediction, if required in diverse terrestrial and non-terrestrial network setting also. this article tries to find Artificial intelligence powered proactive handoff unified framework which will incorporate time series prediction, deep reinforcement learning and tuning parameters automatically. Findings of this article include, performance comparisons, and simulated quality-of-service (QoS) which confirms that proactive handoff mechanisms will provide significant improvements over conventional reactive methods in terms of latency, reliability, and efficient resource utilization.

title: TeadiagnosticX: A Deep Residual CNN Framework for Tea Leaf Disease Classification with Multilingual Intelligent Dashboard Support
authors: Krishna Kumar Jha, Ishita Das, Ismat Sayeed, Arshi Parveen, Priyanka Mishra, Bobita Majhi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: TeaDiagnosticX is a deep learning solution that automates the detection and classification of tea-leaf diseases. TeaDiagnosticX leverages a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) framework employing 7×7 convolutional filters, residual blocks, and global average pooling layers to enhance feature extraction and minimise overfitting, ensuring consistent results across a wide range of image datasets. TeaDigonostix was trained on a localised dataset of more than 4,000 images from Sylhet, Bangladesh, successfully categorising eight common tea plant diseases. Given its high-performance metrics, this tool offers significant potential for advancing precision agriculture in the tea industry. The experimental results demonstrated the classification accuracy, assuring the automation and efficiency of the solution, thus making TeaDiagnostix a promising tool for automated teaplant disease detection and precision agriculture.

title: Comparative Analysis of mmWave Microstrip Antennas for 5G and Optimization Proposal Using Machine Learning
authors: Tomás Bavestrello, Héctor Kaschel, Sergio Cordero
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative analysis by electromagnetic simulation of square patch microstrip antennas with slot structures designed for three millimeter-wave (mmWave) frequency bands: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$22 \text{GHz}, 28 \text{GHz}$</tex> and 32 GHz. Specifically, the antennas are evaluated based on key performance metrics, such as gains of 2.17 dBi (at 22 GHz), 2.32 dBi (at 28 GHz) and 3.21 dBi (at 32 GHz); return loss (S11) values of -12.42 dB, -13.36 dB and -16.97 dB, respectively; voltage standing wave ratio (VSWR) of 1.62, 1.54 and 1.33, together with radiation patterns and impedance characteristics. The results obtained from these simulations are analyzed to identify frequency-dependent trends and design trade-offs inherent to mmWave antenna structures. Furthermore, the study describes a proposal that leverages machine learning (ML) techniques, specifically Gaussian Process Regression (GPR), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) and Support Vector Machines (SVM), to improve and streamline the antenna design process. This ML approach aims to minimize iterative electromagnetic simulation efforts, thereby optimizing future antenna designs by predicting key performance metrics.

title: Semi-Supervised Multimodal Medical Image Segmentation Based on Bidirectional Gated Attention Fusion and Multi-Level Consistency Alignment
authors: Hongxing Li, Libin Lan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Multimodal learning leverages complementary information from different modalities to enhance the performance of medical image segmentation. However, current mainstream multimodal learning methods face challenges in medical image segmentation tasks, such as scarcity of annotated data and anatomical misalignments between modalities. To address these issues, this paper proposes a semi-supervised learning framework based on Bidirectional Gated Attention Fusion (BGAF) and multi-level consistency alignment. In terms of fusion mechanism, a bidirectional gated attention fusion module is designed. It extracts modality-invariant knowledge inherent to each modality through bidirectional attention interaction and adaptive gated weight learning across modalities. The extracted information is then integrated into a unified feature fusion layer for deep feature fusion. For consistency constraints, a multi-level constraint system is constructed, which includes feature-level channel semantic alignment and prediction-level contrastive anatomical similarity alignment. By using a channel semantic alignment loss, the framework ensures the alignment of modality-invariant information at the feature level, thereby improving its robustness to misalignments in multimodal scenarios. Additionally, the anatomical structure is regularized by a contrastive anatomical similarity loss, enabling anatomical prediction alignment on unlabeled data in semi-supervised segmentation tasks. Experiments on multimodal medical image datasets show that the proposed method achieves competitive performance compared to other multimodal approaches. Moreover, the method demonstrates outstanding robustness in scenarios involving scarce annotated data and modality misalignments.

title: Hybrid Transformer as an Active Filter
authors: Samuel Rebolledo Isla, José Espinoza C., Pablo Epul Santana
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This study presents a Hybrid Transformer (HT) that performs as an Active Filter addressing challenges in modern power systems from renewable integration and non-linear loads. One one side, conventional transformers are limited, while SolidState Transformers are costly and less efficient. HT offer a flexible and controllable solution, combining both to provide voltage regulation, reactive power compensation, and harmonic filtering with improved efficiency and lower costs. The research focuses on a shunt-series distribution HT to ensure nominal load voltage and unity power factor at the PCC. It employs static decouplers and PI control loops for sag/swell compensation and harmonic filtering, modeling the system with a rotating transformation. Simulation results demonstrate effective mitigation of voltage sag/swell and current harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th). This achieves a significant THD reduction at the PCC, maintaining desired load voltage and unity power factor.

title: Estimation of NaCl Concentration in Aqueous Solution Using Partial Least Square Regression and Raman Spectroscopy
authors: Edgardo Pérez Chambi, Daniel Sbárbaro Chambi, Rodrigo Fuentes, Fernando Castillo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The objective of this study is to quantify the concentration of NaCl in aqueous solutions using Raman spectroscopy and the Partial Least Square (PLS) model. Five NaCl solutions with concentrations of 1-5 g/100 mL were prepared at different temperatures (10-50 °C), and Raman spectra were recorded. Spectral analysis was performed using a multi-Gaussian fit of the O-H band, generating nine physical descriptors per spectrum. Two PLS approaches were compared: one with the full spectrum and one using only the physically informed descriptors. The results obtained show that the model with physical descriptors outperforms the model based on the full spectrum, with an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.9939 and a significantly l o wer R M SECV. The proposed methodology provides an accurate and robust tool for the quantification o f N a Cl i n a q ueous solutions.

title: Adaptive Machine Learning for Intrusion Detection in IoT Environments Under Concept Drift: A Comparative Study Using IoT-23
authors: Jorge Andrés González-Quintana, Juan Ignacio Iturbe-Araya, Helena Rifà-Pous
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The performance of machine learning-based intrusion detection systems (IDS) in smart home environments deteriorates over time due to concept drift, where the statistical properties of network traffic evolve. This paper presents a comparative evaluation of three drift adaptation techniques (ADWIN, HDDMW, DWM) applied to four classical classifiers (Random Forest, Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression) using the IoT-23 dataset. The results indicate that adaptation significantly improves performance in key metrics, particularly those robust to class imbalance like the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) and balanced accuracy. Among the techniques, HDDMW yields the most stable results, while ADWIN is more efficient in computational terms. The Random Forest model combined with HDDMW achieves the highest average accuracy (99.55 %) and MCC (0.98), confirming the benefit of adaptive strategies in maintaining detection capabilities over time.

title: Research on an Automatic Scoring Model for Chinese–English Translation
authors: Weiwei Zhao, Lanke Su, Jiayin Gao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Traditional automatic translation scoring often relies on surface matching and thus may fail to provide a sufficiently comprehensive and reliable assessment. To address this issue, this paper proposes an automatic scoring model for Chinese–English translation based on a hybrid semantic space. The model integrates (i) grammatical error detection by combining rule-based patterns and a statistical language model, and (ii) semantic relevance estimation by measuring similarity between student translations and reference translations using retrofitted embeddings in the hybrid space. The final score is produced by a weighted fusion strategy. To mitigate semantic drift in retrofitting, we specify a constrained procedure for selecting near-synonym pairs and assigning edge weights, so that the semantic space adapts to student lexical choices while preserving the global semantic structure. In particular, the grammar and semantic modules output normalized scores in [0, 1], which are fused into an exam-scale score, enabling a balanced assessment of linguistic correctness and meaning preservation. Experiments on 800 Chinese-English translation samples from the A/B-level English exam show that the proposed method achieves an average absolute scoring error of of 1.13 points (on a 15-point scale) and a Pearson correlation of 0.88 with human scores, indicating strong agreement with manual evaluation.

title: Analysis of Characteristics of Incoming Students to Informatics Programs at the Facultad PolitéCnica of the Universidad Nacional De AsuncióN
authors: Ellen L. Mendez Xavier, Rita Cantero, Christian von Lücken
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Choosing a university degree is always a great challenge for young people. In these times, Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) fields are playing an important role as fundamental pillars for economic and social progress globally, and Paraguay is no exception. This paper presents an analysis of the characteristics in different dimensions of incoming students in the Computer Engineering and Computer Science programs at the Facultad Politécnica of the Universidad Nacional de Asunción in 2024. To carry out this study, a survey was conducted, revealing that the majority of incoming students are male, that they enter the university between the ages of 18 and 20 after completing a high school diploma generally related to the field of computer science, that most come from the capital city and nearby cities, and that their areas of interest focus on software development, cybersecurity, artificial intelligence, and machine learning. This study presents the first systematic characterization of students entering computer science programs at FPUNA, providing a baseline for future research and empirical information on academic, socioeconomic, and motivational variables of these students, contributing to documenting the situation in Paraguay and to the literature on the characteristics of students who choose a career in computer science.

title: The Drone Tree Network: A High-Assurance Distributed Framework for BVLOS-Compliant UAV Logistics
authors: Praveen Manimangalam
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The commercialization of distributed Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) logistics, particularly Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS) operations, requires addressing regulatory compliance, autonomous scalability, and system assurance. This paper presents the Drone Tree Network (DTN), a hierarchical cyberphysical framework designed to support performance-based BVLOS operations aligned with emerging Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Part 108 requirements. The DTN integrates a cognitive decision stack combining a Weighted Sum Model for mission prioritization with an enforced emergency dispatch threshold and a hybrid Lazy Theta and D Lite path-planning architecture for real-time rerouting. Payload release safety is ensured through a dual-authorization protocol requiring both cryptographic validation using a FIPS 140-3-compliant hardware module and physical ground verification via a convolutional neural network and PointNet-based sensor fusion model, generating a ground-stability confidence score. Command-andcontrol resilience is strengthened through frequency-hopping spread spectrum and Unscented Kalman Filter-based positioning integrity. Simulation-based validation demonstrates measurable improvements in path efficiency, command-and-control robustness under jamming conditions, and fleet scalability, providing empirical evidence toward scalable BVLOS-compliant UAV logistics.

title: A Lightweight DMA Controller for Edge AI in RISC-V Based SoC
authors: Zhenyou Kang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: With the progressive integration of the Internet of Things (IoT) and artificial intelligence (AI), IoT edge devices are placing higher demands on real-time performance, energy efficiency, and data processing capabilities. Traditional CPU-based data transfer methods struggle to meet the transmission requirements of large-scale matrix data in edge AI tasks. To address this, this paper designs and implements a specialized Direct Memory Access (DMA) controller for edge AI SoCs. It supports the AXI bus protocol and features automatic address increment, multi-mode transmission, and byte alignment capabilities, effectively enhancing data access efficiency and system energy efficiency. This paper also develops a RISC-V CPU-based SoC platform to functionally verify the DMA controller and implements a prototype system on a Xilinx Virtex FPGA. The implementation result shows that the DMA Controller occupies 11.36% of the LUTs and 11.02% of the Registers, demonstrating that the DMA controller exhibits favorable lightweight characteristics in resource utilization, providing an efficient and low-cost data transfer solution for edge AI devices.

title: Improved Prompt Generation Framework Using Gemma-3-Based Context-Aware Language Models
authors: Komal Paul, Ariyan Basu, Tamal Ghosh, Sajal Saha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Language models are powerful tools for generating text, yet they often falter when faced with vague or unstructured prompts common in real-world use. This paper proposes an automated framework to transform such messy inputs into clear, structured prompts through a systematic decomposition process based on elements such as what, how, when, and where. A compact model, Gemma-3 4B, trained on test datasets, performs this reframing and outputs results in JSON format. Comparative analysis with Llama 3.2, Phi 2, and Queen 2.5 demonstrates its superior ability to enhance response accuracy and contextual relevance in domain-specific applications. The model's modular, scalable architecture also supports adaptation across domains such as healthcare, education, and manufacturing. Overall, this approach advances the usability of language models by teaching them to interpret human intent more effectively.

title: A Survey on Fuzz Testing for Quantum Computing
authors: Fernando Betanzo S´nnchez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: As quantum computing transitions from theoretical concepts to commercially available quantum processing units, ensuring the quality, reliability, and correctness of quantum software systems has become paramount. The unique challenges of quantum software testing have necessitated the adaptation of classical software engineering techniques to the quantum domain. One such technique is fuzz testing, or fuzzing, an automated software testing technique that generates large volumes of inputs to identify bugs and unexpected behaviors. In this work we provide a comprehensive examination of how fuzz testing has been successfully applied to quantum computing. We analyze the application of fuzzing across the quantum ecosystem, from testing quantum programs to verifying quantum software stacks, quantum compilers, and quantum simulators. This survey establishes the foundation for understanding how fuzz testing can ensure the reliability and correctness of quantum software systems and outlines promising future research directions.

title: Closed Loop V/F Control Fed Induction Motor Under IGBT Open Circuit Fault
authors: Noureddine Horri, Jakson Bonaldo, Ahmed Hamida Boudinar, Marco Rivera, Patrick Wheeler, Sergio Toledo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Three phase induction motors fed by Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) have been widely used in industries, to increase efficiency and flexibility, especially with closed loop control relying on speed sensors, such as closed loop V/F control technique. However, several types of faults may occur in the drive systems affecting the overall performances. Therefore, predictive maintenance techniques have been developed to avoid production perturbation and expensive repair costs. A common issue on VSI-driven induction motors is open circuit switch's fault. To identify the presence of this type of fault and its impact on the performance of the drive system, this work presents a new fault detection method based on Power Spectral Density (PSD) to diagnosis of electrical open circuit faults. A simulation model is built in MATLAB/SIMULINK considering the scalar speed control (V/F) based on Space Vector Pulse Width Modulation applied to a three-phase induction motor in different speeds. The model is tested considering healthy and faulty inverter conditions in order to validate the proposed open circuit fault detection technique.

title: Classification of Streak Contamination in Hubble Space Telescope Images via ResNet-Inception Transfer Learning
authors: Murtuza Lemonwala, Kayla D. Taylor, Naqiya Rangwala
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The burgeoning commercial space industry and the proliferation of satellite mega-constellations are introducing unprecedented challenges to astronomy research by adding obtrusive light and noise pollution to astronomical images. Manually inspecting images for evidence of satellite streaks has become too labor-intensive to be sustained without data-driven modeling and computational approaches. To address this challenge, we introduce a dual-backbone ResNet-Inception ensemble that fuses the edge-preserving representations of ResNet-50 with the multi-scale feature sensitivity of Inception-v3 through logit-level averaging. The experiments on a 5-fold split of a labeled Hubble Space Telescope (HST) image repository (114,607 images) used the first fold for model development and evaluation (1,600 training images and 400 validation). Results show that ResNet-50 alone performs near chance level (~ 0.50 accuracy), while Inception-v3 and the ensemble achieve precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy above 0.875 at the default threshold of 0.50. A threshold-tuned ensemble (0.32) achieves performance metrics above 0.90. Collectively, these contributions provide a practical solution for automated streak/no-streak classification in astronomical imaging.

title: Improving Deep Reinforcement Learning Efficiency in PMSM Drive Control Using Transfer Learning
authors: Luciano Radrigan, Anibal S. Morales
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSMs) require advanced control strategies to achieve precise torque tracking and energy-efficient operation. Model Predictive Control (MPC) offers optimal performance under constraints but depends on accurate system modeling and high computational resources. Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) methods, such as Deep Q-Network (DQN), provide model-free alternative that learn control policies directly from data. This study investigates the control performance and sample efficiency of DQN vs. MPC within a standardized gym-electric-motor simulation framework. Key metrics—torque tracking error, current consumption, and the percentage of steps in which physical limits were violated, either due to excess current or voltage—are used for evaluation. Furthermore, transfer learning from MPC-generated trajectories is applied to accelerate DRL training. Results show that DQN approach can achieve comparable or superior control performance with reduced dependence on system modeling. The proposed fine-tuning transfer learning scheme significantly improves DRL sample efficiency, highlighting its potential for data-driven PMSM control in real-world applications.

title: Joint Grid Reservation and ADMM-Based PAPR Reduction Method for OTFS Systems
authors: Yuxu Guan, Kaiming Liu, Yuan'an Liu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Orthogonal Time Frequency Space (OTFS) modulation is regarded as a key technology for 6G communications due to its robustness in high-mobility scenarios. However, it faces the severe challenge of high Peak-to-Average Power Ratio (PAPR), which limits the power efficiency of transmitters and leads to non-linear distortion. To address this issue, this paper proposes a joint PAPR reduction scheme based on Grid Reservation (GR) and the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM). Unlike traditional signal pre-distortion methods or strictly distortion-free grid reservation techniques, this paper introduces a “Limited Distortion” strategy. This approach allows for controllable distortion on the data grids while utilizing reserved Delay-Doppler grids to transmit peak-canceling signals, thereby effectively expanding the solution space of the optimization problem. We formulate this problem as a constrained convex optimization problem and employ the ADMM framework to decompose it into tractable sub-problems for efficient iterative solution. Simulation results demonstrate that, the proposed scheme achieves significantly superior PAPR reduction performance while maintaining less Bit Error Rate (BER) degradation and good out-of-band radiation performance.

title: Video-Text Retrieval with Good Bias Exploitation
authors: Hua Lan, Chaohui Lv
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: As a core task in multimodality, video-text retrieval faces two major limitations: text bias in data annotations and spurious correlations learned due to video-text information asymmetry. Existing debiasing methods regard all biases as harmful information, overlooking the positive role of “good biases” (i.e., commonsense-based semantic extensions). To address this issue, this paper proposes a text-guided selective debiasing framework, which takes text semantics as the anchor to filter redundant frames and enhance the representation of key frames associated with “ good biases”. Subsequently, it constructs accurate semantic associations through cross-modal alignment optimization. Comprehensive experiments are conducted on the MSR-VTT and LSMDC datasets, comparing the proposed framework with state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods such as X-CLIP and DFLB. The results demonstrate that the framework can effectively leverage “good biases” and improve retrieval accuracy.

title: A Digital Twin-Driven Improved MADDPG Multi-Robotic-Arm Cooperative Control Method
authors: Yi Liu, Peng Wang, Jie Huang, Chunxia Jia
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: To address the issues of low obstacle avoidance efficiency and slow convergence speed in multi-manipulator cooperative control in dynamic industrial environments, based on the background, a control strategy that combines Digital Twin (DT) technology with an improved version of the Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG) algorithm is put forward. Moreover, with the use of MADDPG, an attention mechanism along with prioritized experience replay is adopted. Additionally, based on the improved MADDPG algorithm, a hierarchical collision penalty function together with an enhanced oscillation constraint mechanism is designed to improve its sensitivity to important environmental information as well as the efficiency of experience replay. The experiment results show that the improved MADDPG algorithm has strong robustness because, under the conditions of gradients with low, medium, and high densities of obstacles, the task completion ratio throughout the convergence process remains above 91.3 %.

title: Unified Multi-Domain Framework With Outlier Exposure and Adversarial Learning for First-Shot Unsupervised Anomalous Sound Detection
authors: Shouzhi Liu, Hui Zhang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Unsupervised anomalous sound detection for industrial machinery faces critical challenges including anomalous sample scarcity and domain shift across acoustic environments, particularly in first-shot scenarios requiring generalization to unseen machine types without hyperparameter tuning. This paper proposes a unified multi-domain framework that synergistically integrates Outlier Exposure, domain-adversarial learning, and self-supervised regularization. We introduce a novel strategy treating supplemental normal sounds as pseudo-outliers with margin-based deviation loss, hybrid domain adaptation combining gradient reversal with Maximum Mean Discrepancy constraints, and adaptive multi-task learning with rotation prediction for robust knowledge transfer. Extensive experiments on DCASE 2025 Task 2 development dataset demonstrate superior performance with average improvements of 9.1 % in source AUC, 15.2 % in target AUC, and 8.0 % in pAUC across seven machine types, validating the effectiveness of our approach for industrial predictive maintenance applications.

title: Model Predictive Control with PWM for Reactive Power Compensation in Three-Level NPC Converters
authors: Fernando Antúnez, Adolfo Gonzalez, Matías Aguilar, Jairo De Oliveira, Juan Insfran, Raúl Gregor, Leonardo Comparatore, Osvaldo González
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a three-phase Shunt Active Power Filter (SAPF) based on a three-level Neutral-PointClamped (NPC) inverter, controlled through a finite-control-set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) strategy and operated using sinusoidal pulse-width modulation (PWM). Reference currents are generated based on the Instantaneous Reactive Power Theory (IRPT). A proportional-integral (PI) controller is employed to balance and stabilize the voltages across the NPC capacitors. Simulation results in MATLAB/Simulink confirm that the system achieves a total harmonic distortion (THD) of 3.89 % in grid currents, remaining below the 5 % limit established by the IEEE 519 standard. The proposed hybrid strategy enables effective reactive power compensation and significant power quality improvement by combining control accuracy, hardware efficiency, and industrial compatibility, making it a robust and high-impact solution for grid-connected systems.

title: Autonomous Walking Trajectory Control of a Pin Replenishment Robot Considering Obstacles in the Surrounding Environment
authors: Liu Cao, Yuchen Zhu, Jiang Guo, Jianmin Hu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Path planning based solely on preset simple rules can only achieve mechanical obstacle avoidance in a fixed pattern, resulting in poor trajectory control stability. Therefore, this study investigates the autonomous walking trajectory control of a pin replenishment robot considering surrounding environmental obstacles. Constructing a dynamic point cloud map of the robot's surrounding environment, an improved <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A^{*}$</tex> algorithm is introduced to incorporate a dynamic obstacle avoidance cost function for global path planning, and a dynamic cost function is designed to generate the robot's optimal safe path. A PID controller is used to adjust the robot's linear velocity and angular velocity, and acceleration and angular velocity constraints are introduced to achieve autonomous walking trajectory control for the pin replenishment robot. Experiments show that the proposed method maintains linear velocity control deviations mostly within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.1 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$</tex> and angular velocity control deviations mostly within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.2 \text{rad} / \mathrm{s}$</tex>. Compared to the comparative method, it can plan a smoother trajectory that is closer to the ideal path, achieving an obstacle avoidance success rate of 95.8 % with 50 obstacles, thus improving the stability of trajectory control.

title: Influence of Dielectric Properties and Thickness on Inset-Feed Patch Antenna Characteristics
authors: Puja Paul, Arpita Santra, Moupali Roy, Swati Barui, Anurima Majumdar, Antara Ghosal, Palasri Dhar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: The performance of a microstrip patch antenna can change drastically because of the material beneath it and design of the antenna. In this research work an inset-feed rectangular microstrip patch antenna is designed and simulated in High Frequency Structure Simulator (HFSS) to show how substrate material and thickness affect the antennas behavior. Using one fixed layout, two contrasting materials FR4_epoxy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{r}}=4.4$</tex>) and Rogers RT/duroid <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5880\left(\mathcal {E}_{\mathrm{r}}=2.2\right)$</tex> are modeled at three thickness: 0.5 mm, 1.6 mm and 3 mm. with the standard design equation the FR4 based antenna had a calculated patch width of 19.0 mm and a length 14.37 mm while the lower permittivity Rogers substrate resulted in a much larger patch size of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$W \approx 30.8 \text{mm}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$L \approx 19.0 \text{mm}$</tex>. This structured variation shows how dielectric properties and substrate height influence resonant frequency, return loss, bandwidth and radiation patterns. The study identifies the best setup for improved radiation and stable operation. It offers helpful insights for engineers to improve the performance of the microstrip antenna beyond typical limits.

title: Performance Analysis and Enhancement of DC-DC Pseudo Partial Power Converter Fast EV Chargers Using FCS-MPC
authors: Daniel Pesantez, Mokhtar Aly, Hugues Renaudineau, Samir Kouro, Jose Rodriguez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Partial power converters (PPCs) have gained significant interest in various DC-DC applications due to their high efficiency, reduced ratings, and compact size. For fast electric vehicle (EV) charging applications, the pseudo-PPC demonstrated high performance over a wide operating range compared to other presented PPC topologies and configurations. The pseudo-PPC processes a fraction of the system's rated power by utilizing a switched capacitor cell, thereby eliminating the need for high-frequency transformers. However, proper control algorithms are essential for this topology to optimize the converter response, efficiency, and partiality. This paper presents an analysis and enhancement of pseudo-PPC using the finite control set model predictive control (FCS-MPC) method. In this paper, a sequential FCS-MPC is proposed for controlling the output current and switched capacitor voltages separately, without the use of weighting factors, which is a common problem in designing FCS-MPC algorithms. The obtained results prove the feasibility of FCS-MPC on PPC with multiple objectives control and without weighting factors. Additionally, better efficiency and partiality are achieved using the proposed FCS-MPC method.

title: PATS: Physics-Aware Transformer Scheduler for MPQUIC in LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Zhanglei Wu, Yidian Liu, Zhanjun Liu, Chengchao Liang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper introduces PATS, a novel MPQUIC scheduler designed to address the high Bandwidth-Delay Product and dynamic link conditions of LEO satellite networks. To overcome the limitations of existing bandwidth estimation, PATS integrates a physics-guided hybrid estimator with Deep Reinforcement Learning (DDPG) and Deficit Round Robin (DRR) scheduling. Key innovations include a satellite-aware adaptive filter, a Fluid Congestion Index, and a Transformer-based temporal feature extractor. NS-3 simulations demonstrate that PATS significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, offering superior throughput stability and reducing flow completion time by approximately 40%.

title: HCAS:HOP-Count-Aware Adaptive Selection Strategy for NoC
authors: Yamin Li, Chaoqun Xu, Maoju Tan, Kang Su, Changhong Wang, Tuo Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: With the rapid development of multi-core digital chips, there is an increasing demand for efficient on-chip communication. As a critical technology for multi-core on-chip communication, Network-on-Chip (NoC) has attracted extensive attention. Routing algorithms and selection strategies are key factors that determine NoC performance. This article proposes an adaptive selection strategy based on the remaining hop count, which incorporates a weight for the “packet remaining hop count” into the selection process. The remaining hop count is weighted and combined with the buffer state. The fewer the remaining hops (i.e., the closer the packet is to its destination) and the more idle the network status, the higher the priority for obtaining path resources. The Noxim simulator was used to model and simulate the HCAS selection strategy. Simulation results indicate that the average network latency is reduced by 26.69% compared to the Random selection strategy and by 8.76% compared to the Buffer_Level strategy. The improvement is especially significant under high load conditions.

title: Facilitating Open Data Discovery and Exploration: A Case Study on Uruguay's Open Data Catalog
authors: Nicolás Buero, Raúl Maglione, Agustina Simoncelli, Lorena Etcheverry, Adriana Marotta
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper examines the challenges of data discovery and exploration within open data repositories, with a focus on the Open Data Catalog of Uruguay as a case study. This catalog, which is built on the CKAN platform, allows users to search using filters or keywords. However, it lacks features for discovering related datasets or identifying datasets relevant to specific questions. To address these limitations, our approach combines advanced search methods, navigation, and semantic annotation. We utilize technologies such as D3L, Aurum, TableMiner+, language models (LLMs), and RDF graphs, along with an automatic metadata enrichment system. Our findings indicate that inferring relationships between columns, leveraging semantic annotations, and executing natural language queries on a generated RDF graph significantly enhance data exploration and retrieval.

title: An Approach to Variable Clustering: K-Means in Transposed Data and its Relationship with Principal Component Analysis
authors: Victor Saquicela, Kenneth Palacio, Mario Chifla
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and K-means constitute fundamental techniques in multivariate analysis. Although they are frequently applied independently or sequentially to cluster observations, the relationship between them, especially when K-means is used to cluster variables rather than observations, has been scarcely explored. This study seeks to address this gap by proposing an innovative method that analyzes the relationship between clusters of variables obtained by applying K-means on transposed data and the principal components of PCA. Our approach involves applying PCA to the original data and K-means to the transposed data set, where the original variables are converted into observations. The contribution of each variable cluster to each principal component is then quantified using measures based on variable loadings. This process provides a tool to explore and understand the clustering of variables and how such clusters contribute to the principal dimensions of variation identified by PCA. We analyze multiple data sets with varying variability structures (USAr-rests, Iris, Decathlon2) to show that the correspondence between clusters of variables and principal components depends on the data's inherent structure. For cases of simple variability, such as USAr-rests, the clusters group variables with high loadings into specific components, facilitating a clear interpretation. In more complex structures, such as Iris and Decathlon2, the relationship is a bit fuzzy, since even though K-means clustering of variables on the transposed data still provides useful complementary information on the joint behavior of the variables. The method not only enriches the interpretation of PCA by linking principal components to meaningful groups of variables but also, provides a reproducible methodological framework for exploring and understanding variable clustering in multivariate analysis. The proposed method itself becomes a valuable tool for exploratory data analysis and applications with high-dimensional data, facilitating pattern identification, variable selection and feature engineering, contributing to a deeper understanding of complex data sets.

title: Role Play as a First Approach to the Professional Role in Students of Electrical Engineering
authors: Felipe Alarcón, Matías Pavez, Alvaro Hoffer, Constanza Lazcano, Camilo Maury
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The simulation of real-life cases in the initial training of electrical engineers remains a rarely used strategy, despite the benefits reported in previous research. This article analyzes the strengths and challenges experienced by electrical engineering students when confronted with their first real-world professional situation, through the Role-Playing methodology. The experience involved 14 students, and 2 external expert evaluators who employed a pre-designed rubric to assess the students' performance across the various tasks proposed in the simulation. In addition, a perception survey and a focus group interview were conducted to perform a data crossing analysis and evaluate the consistency of the findings. The results indicate that simulating real-world scenarios through Role-Playing, supports the application of theoretical knowledge acquired throughout the course, promotes its integration with content from other courses, strengthens collaborative work and fosters the development of engineering professional identity. Furthermore, it significantly enhances students' intrinsic motivation when addressing problems related to their field of development.

title: Proximity Effect Correction in Electron Beam Lithography Using CUDA to Increase Efficiency
authors: Cherwa Vang, Olivia Yee, Nishchay Kulkarni, Shadi Alawneh
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: The purpose of this paper is to understand and implement a highly parallelized Proximity Effect Correction algorithm for Electron Beam (e-beam) lithography using the NVIDIA CUDA platform. E-beam lithography is critical for high-resolution pattern generation in micro-fabrication as well as nano-fabrication processes. However, the “Proximity effect”, caused by scattering of electrons within the substrate and resist, leads to the pattern containing some distortions. This is where correction methods are required. The approach of this paper focuses on the core computation burden of the Proximity Effect Correction process. These calculations are highly parallel in nature. Each “pixel” of the IC circuit is independent of its neighbors. By taking advantage of the parallel architecture of GPUs, this paper aims to prove that the Proximity Effect Correction algorithm will run more efficiently on a GPU.

title: Integrated $\text{CO}_{2}$ Compression, Heat Recovery, and ORC System for Energy-Efficient Carbon Capture and Liquefaction
authors: Yitong Wu, Chairunnisa, Kyaw Thu, Takahiko Miyazaki
publish: 2025 14th International Conference on Power Science and Engineering (ICPSE)
abstract: CO2 capture is currently one of the main methods to mitigate global warming. The energy loss caused by the insufficient utilization of thermal energy during the storage or utilization of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> after capture is a key barrier to large-scale deployment. To solve this problem, this study proposes an integrated system that compresses the captured <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> into a liquid state, recovers the heat released during the multi-stage compression through the inter-stage cooling of the heat recovery unit, and uses it for power generation in the Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC). Meanwhile, LNG from the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> capture system is combined as the cold source to support ORC condensation. The thermodynamic efficiency of carbon capture and storage (CCS) systems enhanced by coupling compression, heat recovery and power generation into a unified framework.

title: Proceedings
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Space Mission Challenges for Information Technology (SMC-IT)

title: Space It Education: a CubeSat Approach
authors: Daniel J. Freburg, Will Lewis, Charles Edamala, Michael Lembeck, Jacob Oakley
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Space Mission Challenges for Information Technology (SMC-IT)
abstract: CubeSats have served as an excellent educational tool in the aerospace field and have helped facilitate STEM learning activities across various educational institutions. CubeSats have also become a valuable vehicle for space-related science missions and have created their own Low Earth Orbit small satellite industry. As the space industry continues expanding, the demand for spacerelated IT skills increases. Yet, IT disciplines have not been heavily involved in teaching and research in this area. This paper describes the need for, and the value of, a Space IT educational CubeSat, which will be used to introduce and teach information technology, computer science, and cybersecurity aspects of space tech to IT students.

title: Toward Smart LoRa Network Management Through System Identification and Digital Twins: Mérida Case Study
authors: Yumil Rueda Flores, Julio Heredia Lozano, Renan Quijano-Cetina, Alejandro Castillo-Atoche, Johan Estrada-López, Javier Vázquez-Castillo, Ismael López-Juárez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Smart LoRa networks revolutionize urban planning by enabling cost-efficient, scalable, and real-time insights into data-rich ecosystems that design resilient, sustainable, and liveable urban environments as global blueprints. In this context, managing large-scale deployments in dynamic cities, faces inherent complexity due to battery-powered, bandwidth-limited devices requiring dynamic management to optimally adapt to real-time changes and diverse application needs (e.g., urban noise, temperature, and relative humidity sensing). This study aims to overcome these limitations by integrating System Identification (SI) and Digital Twins (DTs) into the management framework, using Mérida city, Mexico, as a practical case study to demonstrate the approach. SI transforms noisy and hysteresis-affected signals into reliable data sources through mathematical modelling, converting raw sensor streams into high-fidelity insights for urban decision-making, while DTs leverage these models to create realtime virtual replicas enabling predictive simulations of urban temperature-humidity hotspots and noise patterns. Controlled experiments with the EnergyTrace tool of Texas Instruments MSP430FR show up to 40% of battery-life extension for a baseline <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex>-minute fixed-duty-cycle deployment.

title: Agentic Manufacturing: Enabling Autonomous, AI-Powered Industrial Robotics
authors: Ismael Lopez-Juarez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper traces the evolution of learning-based autonomy in manufacturing robotics, from early neural-network driven assembly learning and procedural control strategies, through multimodal perception and incremental knowledge acquisition, to the emergence of Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for adaptive skill formation and Sim2Real deployment. Building on this historical and experimental trajectory, we analyze the ongoing transition toward hybrid DRL-LLM embodied reasoning for agentic manufacturing systems, particularly in industrial activities such as manipulation, welding, and assembly. The work integrates our prior research on neural-based assembly perception, DRL visual path following, procedural simulation, and robot experimentation, and extends it toward edge-based multimodal autonomy, Sim2Real robustness, and humanoidready agentic architectures for future factory environments.

title: Graphene-based interconnect exploration for FPGA design and optimization towards the end of the roadmap
authors: Zhenlin Pei, Sheng Lu, Liuting Shang, Sungyong Jung, Qilian Liang, Chenyun Pan
