title: Quantum-Inspired Constrained Matching for Large-Scale Banking Reconciliation Under Tolerance and Many-to-Many Breaks
authors: Phaneendra Vayu Kumar Yerra
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Banking reconciliation is a very important operational process through which consistency is ensured in records of transactions in the financial systems. Recent reconciliation is characterized by high volumes of transactions, value differences that are tolerant, and many to many matching relationships, which render time-honored approaches, which emphasize rules, ineffective, and inefficient to scale. In this paper, the author presents a quantum-inspired constrained optimization model of large-scale banking reconciliation with toleration and many-tomany breaks of transactions. This reconciliation problem takes the form of a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) model, in which accuracy, the ability to tolerate irregularities, and operational constraints are combined as a single objective function. A quantum-motivated annealing solver is employed to get quality matching solutions. Synthetic and realworld transaction datasets of different sizes are considered in order to evaluate the proposed approach. Quantitative data indicate a great improvement in the match rate and a massive decrease in residual unmatched classification as opposed to rulebased and greedy matching procedures. The process also shows good solver stability and scalability by using window-based decomposition. Moreover, the volume of exceptions, as well as the effort in manual review, are greatly minimized. The results show that quantum-inspired optimization can have some practical use in banking reconciliation based on standard or hybrid hardware. The contribution provided by this work is that it is a scalable and quantifiable reconciliation framework that acts to reconcile the theory of high-level optimization and the actual operational demands of financial systems.

title: Electromagnetic Transient Simulations Using Hypersim and Matlab Simscape
authors: Brian Muñnoz Cabrera, Héctor Chavez Orãstica, Gonzalo Toledo Ordãñez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper evaluates the capabilities of Simscape, a MATLAB toolbox, for electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulations in power systems by comparing its performance with Hypersim, a specialized software that enables real-time simulation. Two case studies of increasing complexity are analyzed using Simscape and Hypersim, applying identical fault events in each environment. Graphical and quantitative comparison metrics are used to assess voltage, current, and generator speed responses. The results highlight the limitations and advantages of Simscape for EMT studies and provide insight into its potential applicability in real-time simulations for academic and industrial environments.

title: Emoticlass: Harnessing Artificial Intelligence for Emotion Detection to Enhance Learning in the Classroom
authors: Gonzalo Garcia Cañete, Gabriel Gatica Cassanova, Cristian Molina Pedernera
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Cognitive load detection in educational environments remains a critical challenge for adaptive learning systems, with traditional methods being intrusive and disruptive to the learning process. This study addresses this gap by validating a facial emotion recognition (FER) system for detecting potential indicators of cognitive load in controlled educational settings. Using the DeepFace model, the system captured and analyzed 29,834 emotional data points from eight participants in a controlled, simulated environment over a structured learning session. A descriptive analysis revealed a high prevalence of neutral (27 %) and sad (26 %) states, with significant differences between emotion means confirmed by ANOVA <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\mathrm{F}(6,42)=2799.73, \mathrm{p}<.001, \eta \mathrm{p}^{2}=.998\right)$</tex>. Additionally, a Kruskal-Wallis test <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{H}=15569.94, \mathrm{p}<0.001)$</tex> further corroborated these significant differences in emotion distributions. Furthermore, time series analysis, including the autocorrelation function (ACF), showed a consistent ascending trend and significant temporal dependency in sadness probabilities. This study makes three key contributions: (1) proposes and provides preliminary evidence for sadness as a potential proxy for cognitive load in controlled environments (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{r} =-0.33$</tex> with neutrality, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{p}<.001$</tex>), (2) demonstrates temporal persistence of this potential indicator through autocorrelation analysis, and (3) establishes a functional prototype at Technology Readiness Level 3 for future educational applications. The results validate the system as a functional laboratory prototype and underscore a critical challenge for affective computing in education: the need to distinguish between genuine emotional expressions and indicators of cognitive effort. These findings provide a preliminary empirical basis for developing non-intrusive cognitive load monitoring systems in real classroom environments.

title: Autonomous Vertical Recovery System for Hypersonic Vehicles via Adaptive Guidance and Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Shengchen Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: This paper addresses the autonomous vertical recovery problem for hypersonic vehicles returning from near-space flight by developing an integrated guidance and control framework. The proposed methodology decomposes the vertical recovery task into trajectory optimization and terminal landing control phases, combining convex optimization techniques for generating fuel-optimal vertical descent paths with deep reinforcement learning-based adaptive control for robust landing execution. The trajectory planning module formulates the vertical recovery as a constrained optimal control problem, ensuring convergence to the target vertical descent angle of −90° while satisfying aerothermal heating limits, structural load constraints, and precise terminal positioning requirements. The landing control module employs a Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient algorithm to synthesize thrust vectoring policies that maintain vertical orientation and nullify lateral velocities despite atmospheric disturbances and model uncertainties. Simulation experiments validate that the proposed vertical recovery system achieves landing position accuracy within 0.62 meters, maintains 89.5% success rate under combined environmental disturbances, and demonstrates 18.7-21.9% fuel savings compared to SCP/MPC baseline methods under identical evaluation conditions, confirming its effectiveness for enabling reusable hypersonic vehicle operations.

title: A Novel RGB-Guided Thermal Masking Pipeline for Low-Cost Vineyard Canopy Temperature Extraction
authors: Fernando Fuentes-Peñailillo, Ma Luisa del Campo-Hitschfeld, Emmanuel Torres-Quezada, Ricardo Vega, Arturo Bisonó, Francisco Orgaz
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Thermal infrared sensing is a key technology in precision agriculture for monitoring crop water status via canopy temperature analysis. However, low-cost thermal sensors often lack sufficient spatial resolution to reliably distinguish vegetation from background elements, such as soil or sky, resulting in noisy temperature data and less accurate stress indicators. To address this limitation, we propose a reproducible, unsupervised, and modular pipeline for vegetation masking in thermal images guided by synchronized RGB imagery. Our method uses empirical HSV-based color segmentation to generate a binary canopy mask from the RGB image, which is then geometrically aligned and applied to the corresponding thermal frame. Designed for low-power embedded systems, the pipeline operates without prior training or complex calibration. Implemented fully in Python with opensource libraries and organized into verifiable steps within a Jupyter Notebook, preliminary results demonstrate improved canopy temperature consistency by filtering out non-vegetative artifacts. This approach enables the deployment of cost-effective thermal monitoring solutions in resource-constrained vineyard environments, facilitating scalable and energy-efficient sensing for digital agriculture.

title: Machine Vision-PLC Integrated High-Precision Target Positioning System Design
authors: Ehui Lan, Xiaoli Hu
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: To meet the demand for the automated, high-precision positioning of bottle mouths in industrial settings, this study proposes an integrated system combining machine vision with three-axis motion control using a programmable logic controller (PLC). A Hikvision industrial camera (pixel size: 3.45 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu \mathrm{m} \times 3.45 \mu \mathrm{m})$</tex> served as the core vision sensor. A high-angle ring light was employed to optimize illumination and enhance the contrast between the edge and the background. Within the VisionMaster software platform, image preprocessing, nine-point calibration (for pixel-to-world coordinate transformation), and sub-pixel edge detection were performed to locate the center of the circular bottle mouth. Motion control was implemented with an Inovance PLC, which receives real-time world coordinates of the bottle mouth from VisionMaster via a high-speed communication interface. The PLC controls a three-axis motion stage comprising servo motors and ball screws, achieving closed-loop coordinated control for XY-plane positioning and Z-axis height adjustment. Experimental results demonstrate that the system achieves an average positioning error of less than 0.05 mm and a repeatability precision of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.03 \text{mm}$</tex>. The system demonstrates excellent stability and fast response, fully meeting the requirements for high-precision bottle-mouth positioning in industrial inspection applications.

title: LLM-Prefetch: Accelerating Long-Context LLM Inference via Explicit Prefetching
authors: Shu Wang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Serving Large Language Models (LLMs) with low latency is critical for user experience, particularly in multi-round conversations where historical context accumulates significantly and TTFT is often bottlenecked by the prefill phase. While modern inference engines (e.g., vLLM) leverage Prefix Caching to mitigate the overhead of processing these long historical contexts, they typically manage the KV cache using passive, Least Recently Used (LRU) eviction policies. This leads to a fundamental mismatch between eviction algorithm and human interaction patterns: the natural “thinking time” between conversation rounds is often misinterpreted as system inactivity, causing prefix cache eviction. Consequently, the system is forced to recompute the prefix upon the user's return, severely degrading Time to First Token (TTFT). To bridge this gap, this paper proposes LLM-Prefetch, a crosslayer system that shifts KV cache management from passive retention to active, signal-driven scheduling. Inspired by instruction prefetch in memory hierarchies, LLM-Prefetch coordinates the application frontend with the inference backend. Specifically, LLM-Prefetch instruments the frontend chatbot framework to capture the onset of user input in ongoing multi-turn sessions. These events are propagated to the inference backend as explicit hints for Prefix Cache scheduling, triggering the preparation of prefix KV blocks using idle resources. Experimental results demonstrate that LLM-Prefetch significantly improves the hit rate of the in-GPU memory Prefix Cache, leading to a substantial 36% reduction in Time to First Token (TTFT) compared to baseline strategies.

title: Deep Learning for Gas Emission Localization in Open-Air Dump Using Uav-Based Thermal Imaging
authors: Angelica M. Vidal, Mathias P. Solis, Maira Santacruz, Derlis O. Gregor
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Open-air landfills represent a major environmental challenge due to their uncontrolled greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. This paper presents a practical approach for the automatic detection and localization of gas emission zones using Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) and computer vision. We developed and trained a Faster R-CNN model with a ResNet-50 backbone and Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) on a custom dataset of thermal images generated through color-threshold segmentation, which allowed for automated annotation of heat zones. The dataset was split into training (80 %) and validation (20 %) subsets, and the model was trained for 60 epochs. The proposed system achieved a precision of 83.8 %, recall of 84.2 %, and F1-Score of 84 %, demonstrating robust detection performance. Beyond its technical performance, this approach has clear practical applicability as a complementary tool for landfill environmental monitoring, enabling early identification of critical emission areas, supporting risk management, and informing public-health and regulatory decision-making processes related to GHG mitigation.

title: CLIFT: Cross-City Mobility-Derived Lifestyle Pattern Transfer for Improved Multi-City Next Location Prediction
authors: Haru Terashima, Naoki Tamura, Kazuyuki Shoji, Tahera Hossain, Shin Katayama, Kenta Urano, Takuro Yonezawa, Nobuo Kawaguchi
publish: ACM Transactions on Spatial Algorithms and Systems

title: A Sampling Frac-N PLL with Current Steering Inverse-Constant-Slope DTC
authors: Ergen Tao, Guofu Niu, Fa Dai
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: We present a current-steering, inverse-constantslope digitally controlled delay line (DTC) for coarse tuning. We analyze the proposed DTC architecture and its key performance metrics. We then describe a fractional-<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N$</tex> PLL that incorporates the proposed DTC and a CDAC-based sampling phase detector. The PLL is designed using GlobalFoundries' 22 FDX technology. Simulation results are presented.

title: Beyond Content Filtering: A “Circuit Breaker” Architecture for Autonomous Agent Action Safety
authors: Harini Sridharan, Reshma Nair
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve into “Agentic AI,” they are increasingly granted access to external tools and APIs to perform autonomous tasks. While significant research exists on filtering toxic textual output, there is a critical lack of safety mechanisms for functional execution. A hallucinating agent with write-access to a database or financial gateway poses a catastrophic risk that standard content moderation cannot mitigate. This paper introduces the “Action Circuit Breaker” (ACB), a deterministic middleware layer that sits between the LLM agent and external APIs. The ACB evaluates each structured action request against an explicit risk policy before execution. We provide (i) a minimal prototype design of an ACB deployed in front of REST endpoints and (ii) a simulation illustrating blocked vs. allowed actions, alongside a theoretical analysis of latency overhead <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(<100 \text{ms}$</tex> per hop in typical gateway settings) and risk reduction subject to policy correctness and coverage.

title: Blockchain Transaction Anomaly Detection
authors: Xing Ying Chia, Ming Quan Goh, ZhengXu Lin, Shawn Kuik, Kek Peng Tan, Kai Wei Jake Wong, Huaqun Guo
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Blockchain transaction fraud detection presents significant challenges due to the pseudonymous nature of transactions and evolving attack patterns that render rule-based systems inadequate. This paper implements and evaluates three machine learning approaches for detecting illicit transactions in Bitcoin networks: Graph Attention Network (GAT), Graph Convolutional Network (GCN), and Random Forest (RF). Using the Elliptic++ dataset comprising 203,769 transactions and 234,355 edges, we formulate fraud detection as a node classification problem with rigorous temporal validation. A unique rolling (sliding) window method is developed for GAT and GCN training. These findings and method establish graph attention networks as the optimal approach for blockchain security applications requiring comprehensive and reliable fraud detection capabilities.

title: Electric Vehicles in Microgrids: A Review of Control Architectures and Integration Approaches
authors: Ignacia Loyola, Enrique Espina, Karina Barbosa
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: The integration of electric vehicles (EV) into micro-grids (MG) introduces new control challenges due to the vari-ability of charging demand and the need to coordinate multiple distributed energy resources. In response, a variety of control schemes have been proposed in the literature. This work presents a functional classification of control architectures implemented in MG with EV, structured according to hierarchical control levels. The classification e nables a d etailed a nalysis oft he relationship between each control level and the specific s ervices i t provides to the MG. The proposed framework facilitates the comparative evaluation of existing strategies and offers a more structured perspective on current research trends and gaps in the field.

title: MAE-GAN: A Multi-Feature Adaptive Enhancement Approach for Multi-Angle Spaceborne InSAR Complex-Valued Images
authors: Yuanhao Li, Xingzhe Zhao, Zhiyang Chen, Xin Xie, Cheng Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Multi-angle spaceborne interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) offers comprehensive observational capabilities by capturing additional angle-dependent information from multiple observation angles. It enables the retrieval of parameters that can’t be obtained from a single-angle observation, such as three-dimensional deformation. However, variations in observation geometries and platform configurations often lead to noticeable differences in image features, including spatial resolution, texture, and noise level, which present significant challenges for joint processing and consequently degrade the accuracy of parameter retrieval. To address these challenges, a Multi-feature Adaptive Enhancement method based on Generative Adversarial Network (MAE-GAN) is proposed. The network adaptively enhances InSAR complex-valued image while simultaneously preserving additional angle-dependent information such as phase and scattering characteristics. Specifically, a Cross-Domain Cycle(CDC) adversarial learning is employed to align images from low-quality angles with those from the highest-quality angle in the deep feature space, thereby enhancing the quality of low-quality observations. Moreover, a fractal loss is introduced to protect additional angle-dependent information by maintaining self-similarity across scales. Experimental results on both simulated multi-angle datasets and real multi-angle Space-Surface Bistatic SAR (SS-BSAR) measurements demonstrate that MAE-GAN achieves balanced improvements in texture consistency, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), spatial resolution, and phase accuracy. These results confirm the effectiveness of proposed method in enhancing the quality and reliability of multi-angle InSAR complex-valued images.

title: Wide-Area Power System Oscillations from Large-Scale AI Workloads
authors: Min-Seung Ko, Hao Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Systems
abstract: This paper develops a new dynamic power profiling approach for modeling AI-centric datacenter loads and analyzing their impact on grid operations, particularly their potential to induce wide-area grid oscillations. We characterize the periodic stochastic power fluctuations inherent to large-scale AI workloads during both the training and fine-tuning stages, driven by the state-of-the-art graphics processing unit (GPU) computing architecture design. These sustained, large power fluctuations, unlike conventional load ramping, act as persistent forcing inputs capable of interacting with and amplifying local and inter-area oscillation modes. Using the WECC 179-bus system and the NPCC 140-bus system, we have numerically studied the amplitude and variability of oscillatory responses under different factors. These factors include system strength, penetration level, fluctuation frequency range, individual datacenter size, geographical deployment, fluctuation suppression level, and workload ratio. Simulation results show that, notably, narrower fluctuation bands, larger single-site capacities, or dispersed siting can intensify oscillations across multiple modes. Our models and numerical studies provide a quantitative basis for integrating AI-dominant electricity demand into grid oscillation studies and further support the development of new planning and operational measures to power the growth of AI/computing load demands.

title: Phase-Sensitive Dynamic Filters: A Conceptual Model for Energy-Preserving, Instantaneously Tunable, Symbol-Level Signal Processing Using Complex IIR Filters
authors: Sean C. Chen, Kamal D. Bhakta, Ian P. Roberts, Yuanxun Ethan Wang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Filters used for modulated RF signals are often matched bandpass filters that have larger bandwidths than that of the modulation. Therefore, the time constants of the filters must be shorter than the symbol periods. This paper presents an idealized theoretical model for Phase-Sensitive Dynamic Filters (PSDF), a class of emerging filters that can be tuned in real time, as fast as the symbol rate of a signal but with time constants longer than the symbol periods. With the foreknowledge of the frequency and phase shifts of a given signal, such filters are capable of filtering across multiple symbols of a constant envelope and compressing the noise bandwidth to a fraction of the modulated signal bandwidth. The transient response of the filters can be longer than the symbol rate of the signal. This can mathematically be modeled by infinite impulse response (IIR) filters with long time constants yet fast time-varying complex coefficients in the feedback path. Phase-sensitive dynamic filters preserve energy across symbols and also exhibit phase-sensitivity. The proposed models indicate that PSDFs can be employed in applications such as rejection of self-interference in the front ends of full-duplex communications systems. Such systems pave the way for the possibility of higher isolation and robustness compared to other prototypical approaches in dynamically changing systems.

title: AESF-LIO: Adaptive Error-State Fusion LiDAR-Inertial Odometry for Ground Vehicles in Structured Environments
authors: Zhishuai Huang, Chao Sun, Jianghao Leng, Bo Wang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: In LiDAR-based Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) systems for vehicles, the point-to-plane Iterative Closest Point (ICP) method is widely used for scan matching. This approach incorporates all planar points into a single objective function for optimization, yet does not explicitly distinguish the differences in the constraints that ground and non-ground points impose on the pose variables. Ground points primarily constrain the z translation as well as roll and pitch, whereas non-ground points predominantly constrain horizontal translation and yaw. The constraints provided by non-ground points on the z translation, roll, and pitch depend on their spatial distribution and can be weak for z in environments dominated by vertical structures. When one point type dominates in number while providing weak constraints on certain pose variables, the joint optimization may become biased, resulting in insufficient refinement of the weakly constrained variables and degraded odometry accuracy. To address this issue within a point-to-plane ICP framework, this paper proposes a LiDAR-inertial odometry system that separately minimizes point-to-plane residuals for ground and non-ground points and adaptively fuses their corresponding error states to improve pose accuracy. The system employs ground segmentation to divide the point cloud into ground points and non-ground points; it then computes the error state vectors for ground and non-ground points separately and updates the pose using an adaptive weighted fusion strategy. Experiments in simulation, on public datasets, and in real-vehicle experiments demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves accuracy compared with advanced SLAM baselines.

title: Mislabel Identification Using Transfer Learning-Based Ensemble Method
authors: Md Shariful Islam, Min Jun Kim, Prashanta Dutta
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Accurate labeling of training data is essential for reliable supervised machine learning, particularly in sensitive applications such as virus classification, autonomous driving, precision manufacturing, and medical diagnostics. However, the labeling process is labor-intensive and error-prone. Even widely used datasets such as MNIST and ImageNet contain numerous mislabeled samples. To address this challenge, we developed a transfer learning based ensemble method that identifies mislabeled data through majority filtering and consensus filtering using fine-tuned pretrained deep neural networks, including ResNet-50, ResNet-101, VGG-16, EfficientNet, MobileNet, and Inception. Our approach was first validated on the MNIST dataset, where the ensemble detected approximately 751 label inconsistencies, which closely aligns with previously reported estimates of mislabeled samples. Additional experiments with synthetically injected mislabels demonstrated that the method could recover up to 100% of known corrupted labels using majority and consensus voting strategies. The method was then applied to a highly pure adeno-associated virus (AAV) nanopore dataset, where artificial mislabels were introduced for evaluation; the ensemble successfully identified most mislabeled samples and correctly recovered their true labels. Experiments on balanced and unbalanced AAV datasets further showed improved performance on the balanced subset, where all injected mislabels were detected. Compared to classical filtering techniques such as KNN, k-means clustering, and advanced machine learning based mislabel detection (e.g., DivideMix), the proposed ensemble method demonstrated superior accuracy, stability, and true-label recovery, establishing it as a strong general-purpose mislabel detection framework—particularly well-suited for complex, fine-grained datasets such as nanopore signals and other biological measurement data.

title: A Discrete Variable Stiffness Actuator for Robotic Hand
authors: Jiahang Zhu, Ke Shi, Tongshu Chen, Maozeng Zhang, Aiguo Song
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Variable stiffness actuators can dynamically adjust output stiffness to accommodate varying task demands, making them well suited for grasping applications. Nevertheless, conventional variable stiffness actuators often suffer from bulky structures, slow response, and complex control, which hinders their integration into robotic hands. To overcome these limitations, we designed a discrete variable stiffness actuator (DVSA) based on electrostatic clutches (ESCs). The DVSA comprises multiple parallel units, each with a linear spring in series with an ESC. By selectively activating ESCs, the DVSA couples different springs to achieve discrete stiffness modulation. This design provides a compact structure and fast response. Furthermore, a cable-driven robotic hand was developed with the DVSA integrated into the palm. A grasping control strategy was also designed to estimate object stiffness in real time and adjust the stiffness of the DVSA accordingly. The performance of the DVSA and the robotic hand was evaluated experimentally. The results indicate that the robotic hand demonstrates excellent stiffness modulation performance under both static and dynamic grasping conditions.

title: Vehicle Heading Estimation Using Positioning and Inertial Data-Based Adaptive Tandem Kalman Filter
authors: Aleksei Fjodorov, Sander Ulp, Taavi Laadung, Alar Kuusik, Muhammad Mahtab Alam
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles
abstract: This paper proposes an Adaptive Tandem Kalman Filter (ATKF) algorithmic method for accurate, robust, and magnetometer-free heading estimation through the competitive fusion of inertial and positioning data. It aims to mitigate the angular heading drift, typically present in MEMS IMU (Micro-Electromechanical Systems Inertial Measurement Unit) sensors, without the use of environmentally sensitive magnetometers. Due to its adaptive nature, the ATKF algorithm is capable of iterative estimation of the input data significance, based on the tracked vehicle behavior, and performing a corresponding weighted fusion. A novel tandem structure allows to perform a series of consecutive data processing steps within a single Kalman filter iteration, thus minimizing the delay in algorithmic response to the input data. Series of simulated comparison tests with one of the state-of-the-art algorithms have demonstrated the high stability and robustness of the proposed algorithm. It has shown a consistent 40% to 90% improvement in the estimated heading accuracy and precision, depending on the maneuvering intensity and the data quality. Simulation results were experimentally validated during the full-scale test campaign, conducted in the industrial environment using a highly maneuverable forklift. Real-time forklift heading was tracked by the proposed ATKF algorithm with a 1<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula> overall median error and 2.3<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula> median error during the active movement periods. The proposed method has respectively shown 95% and 93% improvement in initial IMU heading accuracy and precision. Experimental performance evaluation of the magnetometer-based heading estimation methods has practically confirmed their unreliability and inconsistency in industrial applications. The proposed ATKF heading estimation algorithm may find a variety of possible applications in the field of robotics and intelligent vehicles. It is expected to be especially useful in magnetometer-denied environments.

title: Method for Evaluating Power Supply and Demand Balance Control
authors: Tatsuya Oyama, Hiromi Yamamoto
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper introduces two novel metrics—the active contribution ratio (ACR) and the reactive contribution ratio (RCR)—for quantitatively evaluating the contributions of multiple control devices to power supply–demand balancing in the frequency domain. Conventional Fourier-based approaches rely solely on signal amplitude, limiting their ability to capture timing characteristics and delays. To overcome this limitation, our proposed metrics incorporate phase information for accurate assessment of control timing and delays. Additionally, we introduce a novel algorithm to enhance the accuracy of Fourier analysis for localized waveform segments. By combining these methods, device contributions can be evaluated across a wide frequency range, from economic dispatch to primary frequency control. We applied these methods to analyze operational data and to improve existing BESS control strategies. This approach successfully reduced BESS charging and discharging losses without increasing frequency deviation. The proposed method was validated on the IEEE 9-bus system for short-term transient analysis, demonstrating its versatility in supply–demand balancing studies. These findings offer a robust framework for evaluating and tuning control strategies using real operational data, thus supporting the development of future power systems with high renewable penetration.

title: Simulation for Non-Contact Radar-Based Respiratory Measurement Using Depth Camera-Derived Human 3D Models
authors: Kimitaka Sumi, Takuya Sakamoto
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: In this study, a simulation framework is proposed that serves as a foundation for improving the accuracy of human vital sensing using millimeter-wave radar. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on simplified models of body geometry or displacement, the proposed method simulates the signals received by frequency-modulated continuous-wave radar during respiratory monitoring using realistic human body shapes and motions acquired via a depth camera. Furthermore, the proposed method enables fast data generation by using time-averaged human body geometry and discrete scattering centers. Experiments were conducted on six participants under varying conditions, including varying target distances, seating orientations, and radar types, with simultaneous acquisition from radar and the depth camera. Relative to conventional model-based methods, the proposed technique achieved improvements of 7.6%, 58.7%, and 3.7% in the correlation coefficients of radar images, displacements, and spectrograms, respectively. This work contributes to the generation of radar-based physiological datasets through simulation and enhances our understanding of factors that affect the accuracy of non-contact sensing.

title: Tradeoff Between Energy Consumption and BER Performance in Bio-inspired Molecular Communications
authors: Dongliang Jing, Linjuan Li, Lin Lin, Andrew W. Eckford
publish: IEEE Transactions on Molecular, Biological, and Multi-Scale Communications
abstract: In bio-inspired molecular communication (MC) systems, energy consumption plays a crucial role in determining bit error rate (BER) performance. This paper investigates the fundamental trade-off between energy consumption and BER in MC systems employing a bio-inspired imperfect transmitter. The transmitter collects two types of molecules and releases them as a mixture, but the presence of undesired molecules reduces signal purity and degrades detection accuracy at a selective-receptor receiver. To mitigate this effect and enhance signal distinguishability, the transmitter transfers one molecule type from the reservoir to the external environment to increase molecular purity, at the cost of additional energy expenditure and reduced operational lifetime. This purification mechanism introduces a fundamental trade-off between energy efficiency and communication reliability. To characterize the resulting energy–reliability interplay, we introduce two complementary tradeoff functions: a weighted-sum formulation that combines normalized energy and BER with adjustable weights, and a ratio-based formulation that directly reflects the energy cost per successfully decoded bit. Grid search and binary search are employed to optimize the weighted-sum and ratio-based metrics, respectively. Analysis and simulation results validate the proposed framework, reveal the sensitivity of BER to molecular purification in low-energy regimes, and provide practical guidelines for energy-efficient MC system design under stringent energy constraints.

title: LGAT: Towards Local-Global Adaptive Temporal Graph Representation Learning
authors: Hongjiang Chen, Pengfei Jiao, Xuan Guo, Mengzhou Gao, Zhidong Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence
abstract: Temporal graph representation learning aims to model evolving networks by embedding nodes with respect to their dynamic interactions and attributes over time. Real-world temporal graphs typically exhibit power-law degree distributions, where a small subset of high-degree nodes dominates interactions while the majority of low-degree nodes maintain sparse connections. This inherent imbalance, characterized by node heterogeneity and edge sparsity, poses fundamental challenges: existing approaches that rely on first-order neighborhood aggregation cannot adequately represent under-connected nodes, and the limited number of observed edges restricts the availability of supervision signals. To address these challenges, we propose Local-Global Adaptive Temporal Graph representation learning (LGAT), which integrates local topological features with global evolutionary semantics. The core insight is that local neighborhood information alone is insufficient for heterogeneous networks, while a purely global perspective risks over-smoothing well-connected nodes. LGAT therefore adaptively balances these two perspectives based on each node’s structural role. Specifically, LGAT employs a hybrid temporal decay mechanism that combines fixed exponential decay with dynamic time decay to model temporal dependencies, quantifies mutual neighbor co-occurrence frequencies to capture fine-grained local topology, and maintains a global evolutionary representation that disproportionately benefits under-represented nodes through inverse-degree weighting. Furthermore, LGAT introduces a contrastive pretext task that enriches supervision signals by maximizing mutual information between interacting node pairs, directly mitigating the edge sparsity problem. Extensive experiments on six real-world datasets demonstrate that LGAT significantly outperforms state-of-theart methods, with particularly pronounced improvements on networks exhibiting strong heterogeneity and sparsity.

title: Federated Anonymous Blocklisting across Service Providers and its Application to Group Messaging
authors: David Soler, Carlos Dafonte, Manuel Fernández-Veiga, Ana Fernández-Vilas, Francisco J. Nóvoa
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Instant messaging has become one of the most used methods of communication online, which has attracted significant attention to its underlying cryptographic protocols and security guarantees. Techniques to increase privacy such as End-to-End Encryption and pseudonyms have been introduced. However, online spaces such as messaging groups still require moderation to prevent misbehaving users from participating in them, particularly in anonymous contexts.. In Anonymous Blocklisting (AB) schemes, users must prove during authentication that none of their previous pseudonyms has been blocked, preventing misbehaving users from creating new pseudonyms. In this work we propose an alternative Federated Anonymous Blocklisting (FAB) in which the centralised Service Provider is replaced by small distributed Realms, each with its own blocklist. Realms can establish trust relationships between each other, such that when users authenticate to a realm, they must prove that they are not blocked in any of its trusted realms. We provide an implementation of our proposed scheme; unlike existing AB constructions, the performance of ours does not depend on the current size of the blocklist nor requires processing new additions to the blocklist. We also demonstrate its applicability to real-world messaging groups by integrating our FAB scheme into the Messaging Layer Security protocol.

title: Bio-MutClassNet: A Context-Aware Hybrid Transformer Framework for Precision Oncology via Attention-Based Genetic Mutation Classification
authors: Sultanul Arifeen Hamim, Md Tafhimul H. Sadi, M. F. Mridha, Md. Jakir Hossen
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: The automatic categorization of genetic mutations from medical narratives is a key application in personalized oncology, but this remains an intricate problem because of both linguistic complexity and a very imbalanced class in medical texts. Conventional deep learning methods, which solely utilize fixed word embedding or non-diversified architectures, are unable to discern semantically subtle differentials in benign and pathogenic variants. In this work, we present Bio-MutClassNet, a context-aware hybrid framework for genetic mutation classification from clinical narratives. The model leverages domain-specific transfer learning through BioBERT embeddings and integrates complementary convolutional and recurrent neural components to capture both local lexical patterns and long-range contextual dependencies. Instead of relying on static feature concatenation, we employ an adaptive branch-level attention fusion strategy that dynamically weights heterogeneous representations according to input characteristics. Evaluated on the widely used MSKCC dataset using stratified cross-validation, Bio-MutClassNet achieves an accuracy of 95.2% and an F1-score of 0.95, demonstrating consistent performance improvements over traditional machine learning approaches and standalone biomedical transformer baselines under comparable experimental settings. In addition, we incorporate explainability analysis to examine whether model decisions align with biologically meaningful terminology, supporting greater transparency in AI-assisted mutation classification.

title: A Low-power Wearable Infrared Sensing System for Intermittent Core Body Temperature Monitoring
authors: Wei Ju, Yixi Zhu, Srinjoy Mitra
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: We propose a wearable, event-triggered infrared sensing system designed for intermittent Core Body Temperature (CBT) monitoring. The device measures 44 mm in diameter and 21 mm in thickness and weighs 23 g. It incorporates an optical-alignment triggering mechanism to enable low-power operation, supporting an estimated operating lifetime of one week without recharging. A pilot study validated the system against a clinical-grade infrared thermometer, showing that most measurement differences remained within the predefined <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula>0.3 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }\mathrm{C}$</tex-math></inline-formula> standardized tolerance. Additional evaluation indicated that loose sensor insertion underestimated CBT, whereas other two deeper insertion conditions remained close to the rectal reference. These findings suggest that the proposed system would support energy-efficient and practical CBT monitoring for routine use.

title: Asymptotically Optimal Search for a Change Point Anomaly under a Composite Hypothesis Model
authors: Liad Lea Didi, Tomer Gafni, Kobi Cohen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing
abstract:
We address the problem of searching for a change point in an anomalous process among a finite set of M processes. Specifically, we address a composite hypothesis model in which each process generates measurements following a common distribution with an unknown parameter (vector). This parameter belongs to either a normal or an abnormal space depending on the current state of the process. Before the change point, all processes, including the anomalous one, are in a normal state; after the change point, the anomalous process transitions to an abnormal state. Our goal is to design a sequential search strategy that minimizes the Bayes risk by balancing sample complexity and detection accuracy.

We propose a deterministic search algorithm that alternates between exploration and exploitation phases to efficiently identify the anomalous process. We rigorously analyze its performance under both the case of known normal parameters, and the case where all parameters are unknown. In both settings, we establish asymptotic optimality of the proposed algorithm in minimizing the Bayes risk as the error probability vanishes. The notion of optimality aligns with standard performance bench-marks in each setting, accounting for differences in available information and the associated fundamental limits. Simulation results are presented to validate the theoretical findings.

title: Circularly Polarized Microstrip Antenna on a Magneto-Dielectric Substrate: Design Considerations
authors: Liebert Lemes da Silva, Leticia Tavore de Lima, Daniel Chagas do Nascimento
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This work presents the design considerations for a miniaturized circularly polarized microstrip antenna printed on a magneto-dielectric substrate. First, the behavior of microstrip lines on this material is analyzed, with emphasis on the fringing effect and the effective propagation constant, both critical to antenna design. Next, the modeling and predesign procedures based on the resonant cavity model are discussed, followed by an investigation of ground-plane dimensions on radiation efficiency. The study is validated through comparisons with full-wave simulations and the experimental results of a rectangular patch, Daniel Chagas do Nascimento equals the ϵrED of an ED substrate, the same miniaturization index is obtained; however, MPA on MD substrates exhibit wider bandwidth and improved radiation efficiency, assuming the same loss tangent for both. operating at 375 MHz with dimensions of 0.103λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub> × 0.097λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub>. The prototype achieves an impedance bandwidth of 20.25 MHz (5.4%) and an axial-ratio bandwidth of 6 MHz (1.6%), yielding competitive results for a miniaturized antenna of area ≈ 0.01λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub><sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: Front Cover
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters

title: Beyond the Black Box: Applying XAI to Supervised Multiclass Intrusion Detection
authors: Jiaming Ding, Peng Shan, Rui Ding, Elly Yijun Zhu, Heng Li, Yue Wu, Tianze Kang, Yuxin Qiao, Chufeng Jiang
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Due to “black box” issues of machine learning (ML) in network intrusion detection, it is difficult to trust system utilizing ML in important circumstances. This paper introduces a multiclass and transparent intrusion detection model, and we show that tree-based methods can achieve high accuracy (94.4%) on the CIC-IDS-2018 dataset while retaining transparency. Our explainable AI (XAI) methodology combines rule extraction, SHAP, and LIME to provide global and local explanations. We also identify three different attack signatures: DDoS/DoS erect signature by timing/packet size; BF probing erect signature by feature distributions and Botnet C&C posture by coordinated communication. XAI also reveals that causes of errors are rooted in confusions resulting from class imbalance and hidden attack attributes. The extracted rules are actionable towards closing the gap between machine learning performance and cybersecurity needs in practice.

title: Fortress: A Case Study in Stabilizing Search Recommendations Via Temporal Data Augmentation and Feature Pruning
authors: Milind Pandurang Jagre, Jia Huang, Dayvid V. R. Oliveira, Zhinan Cheng, Babak Seyed Aghazadeh, Puja Das, Chris Alvino, Jinda Han, Kailash Thiyagarajan
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: In search and recommendation systems, predictive models often suffer from temporal instability when certain input features introduce volatility in output scores. This instability can degrade model reliability and user experience especially in multi-stage systems where consistent predictions are critical for downstream decision making. We introduce Fortress, a general framework for enhancing model stability and accuracy by identifying and pruning features that contribute to inconsistent prediction scores over time. Fortress leverages historical snapshots temporally partitioned datasets capturing score fluctuations for the same entity across periods and follows a four-step process: (1) collect historical snapshots, (2) identify samples with unstable predictions, (3) isolate and remove instability- inducing features, and (4) retrain models using only stable features. While semantic features from LLMs and BERT-based models improve generalization, they often lack full query or entity coverage. Engagement-based features offer strong predictive power but tend to introduce temporal instability. Fortress mitigates this trade-off by suppressing the volatility of engagement signals while retaining their predictive value leading to more stable and accurate models. We validate Fortress on a query-toapp relevance model in a large-scale app marketplace. Offline experiments demonstrate notable improvements in prediction stability (measured by Coefficient of Variation) and classification performance (measured by PR-AUC).

title: SmartTrip: An AI-Powered Travel Planning Platform with Intelligent Agent Support, Voice and Multilingual Capabilities
authors: Dhivya Guru, Senthil Raj Subramaniam
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This paper presents Smart Trip, an AI-enhanced travel planning and booking platform designed to serve both travelers and travel agents through a unified interface. The system incorporates role-based logins, enabling travelers to search, plan, and manage trips while allowing agents to perform bookings and manage client interactions. Smart Trip features intelligent chatbots for both travelers and agents, supporting voice interaction and multilingual capabilities to ensure inclusive and accessible communication. It leverages invoice and expense scanning using OCR(Optical Character Recognition) and AI(Artificial Intelligence), enabling users to manage financial records seamlessly within chat interfaces. Embedded summarization tools provide real-time financial summaries for enhanced decision-making. The backend leverages Google Vertex AI with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and embeddings for contextual responses and personalization. The platform is designed with scalability in mind, and a React/Next.js frontend ensures a modern, userfriendly experience. This paper presents the system architecture, technology stack, testing results, and future directions for Smart Trip, highlighting its potential to revolutionize personalized travel management and agent workflows.

title: Improved Split-Window CNN-LSTM Architecture for Stress and Cardiac Monitoring Heart Rate Variability Analysis
authors: Sudhangshu Sarkar, Sudipta Santra, Anilesh Dey, Aniruddha Chandra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: Heart rate variability (HRV) is a physiological indicator that effectively identifies stress and evaluates cardiac health. This study presents a deep learning architecture for resilient HRV signal processing that integrates Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) units enhanced by a Split-Window Approach (SWA). The SWA improves the model's ability to detect transient patterns associated with cardiac irregularities and stress by effectively segmenting HRV data into temporally consistent slices. The proposed CNN-LSTM-SWA model significantly outperforms conventional HRV analysis techniques, as demonstrated by studies on standard HRV datasets. It attains exceptional precision in both stress identification and cardiac condition classification. These findings indicate that real-time applications in wearable and mobile health monitoring systems could be feasible.

title: Brain MRI Image Segmentation and Neurological Disease Diagnosis Using Deep Learning Model
authors: Sabyasachi Mukherjee, Shatabdi Mondal, Bhaskar Roy, Annesha Dubey, Bikas Mondal, Pallav Dutta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: A comprehensive Deep Learning (DL)-based system for automatically detecting and segmenting brain tumours from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is presented in this work. A pre-processing pipeline that consists of resizing, converting to greyscale, normalising, and diminishing noise makes sure that the input data is the same before it is broken down into groups and classified. To find tumour areas, segmentation is done using classical methods like thresholding and region growing, as well as a deep segmentation stage based on U-Net. A custom five-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is trained on 1,500 MRI images of tumours and 1,500 MRI images of non-tumors. The model is assessed with standard performance metrics: accuracy (82.05%), precision (88.46%), recall/sensitivity (85.19%), F1score (86.79%), and ROC-AUC (0.9915). Results obtained after simulation show that the proposed pipeline differentiates between tumour and non-tumor cases while also making segmentation maps to make the results easily understandable. The system segments and classifies to help make diagnoses faster and more consistent in places where resources are limited. In the future, the framework will be expanded to include multi-class tumour grading and a wider range of neurological disorder detection.

title: A Novel Butterfly Slotted CMPA with Peripheral Ring Slot for 5G Sub-6 and ISM Band Applications
authors: Anurima Majumdar, Antara Ghosal, Suparna Biswas, Moupali Roy, Arpita Santra, Swati Barui
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: In this paper, a new compact, ultra-wideband (UWB) patch antenna is proposed using a Coplanar Waveguide(CPW) fed technique by inserting a butterfly slot on the circular patch. The proposed antenna is designed to operate in Sub-6GHz frequency range of 5G Communication. The proposed antenna is also operating at 2.4 GHz (ISM bands) which makes it suitable for biosensor applications. The proposed antenna shows multiband resonance. The proposed antenna demonstrates a simulated impedance bandwidth(IBW) of 187% ranging from 0.4 GHz to 12.4 GHz covering the whole 5G Sub-6GHz lower band along with ISM bands. The proposed antenna is simulated and optimized using Ansoft HFSS Software tool. The simulated and measured results are in good agreement with each other.

title: A Comparative Study of Machine Learning Models for Multiclass Employee Absenteeism Prediction with Explainability
authors: Rafael Silva Taveira, Helena S. Balbino, Jurandir Silva, Rogerio Salvini, Juliana Felix
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Employee absenteeism poses a significant challenge for organizations, affecting productivity and operational planning. This paper investigates the use of machine learning techniques for multiclass prediction of employee absenteeism severity based on workplace and personal attributes. Seven classification models are evaluated using stratified <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex>-fold cross-validation, with performance assessed using accuracy and the macro-averaged F1-score. Statistical significance is analyzed using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test. The results show that ensemble-based models achieve the best performance, with LightGBM and XGBoost presenting comparable predictive capability. To enhance transparency, SHAP-based explainability is employed to identify class-specific factors influencing absenteeism predictions. The findings demonstrate that combining robust evaluation protocols with explainable machine learning yields reliable, interpretable models that support human resource decision-making.

title: Reliable AI for Predictive Modeling: From Training-Time Accuracy to Operational Forecasting
authors: Dmytro Smirnov
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Predictive modeling is now commonly used for forecasting demand, assessing risk, and guiding operational decisions. However, many promising projects fail to progress beyond initial experiments due to issues such as reduced performance in real-world settings, changing feature pipelines, or stakeholders' inability to interpret model confidence. We present a practical framework for building reliable predictive models that remain effective after deployment. We connect modern learning methods, sequence models, and attention-based architectures with engineering decisions that help maintain model stability. Our workflow integrates leakage checks, evaluation, interpretability, and continuous monitoring at every stage of model development. An experimental study illustrates how different model families behave and compared how various model types manage an imbalanced failure-prediction task and show why relying only on accuracy can be misleading. This work offers a comprehensive framework for teams aiming to achieve reliable, sustainable, and verifiable predictive outcomes.

title: Redesigning Home Energy Management Systems with Energy Harvesting and Integrated Control in Distributed Energy Resources
authors: Eshwara Prasath Ramesh, Maciej A. Noras
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Modern residential energy systems increasingly integrate distributed energy resources (DERs) such as rooftop solar, battery storage, and bidirectional electric vehicle chargers. Conventional Home Energy Management System (HEMS) architectures rely on uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), local controllers, and residential routers to coordinate energy flows during grid outages. This introduces higher costs, complex installation requirements, and reliability risks when backup devices fail. This paper proposes a redesigned architecture that eliminates the need for standalone UPS and controllers by introducing an Energy-Harvesting Signal Unit (EHSU) and utilizing the control functions directly from the DER devices. The EHSU is a low-power module that harvests ambient energy and transmits outage-triggered signals directly to the inverter, which then transitions into grid-forming mode. Cloud connectivity is provided through an integrated cellular communication module, removing dependence on consumer routers. Comparative analysis shows that the proposed design reduces system cost by up to 70%, improves resilience by eliminating single points of failure, and reduces outage response latency from hundreds of milliseconds to less than 100 ms in CAN-based signaling. The result is a simplified, resilient, and future-ready residential energy system architecture that aligns with emerging DER trends and regulatory requirements.

title: Characterization of Solar Radiation Effects on Free Space Optical Communication Using High-Altitude Balloon Measurements
authors: Lucas Marsh, Hemani Kaushal, Nirmalkumar G. Patel
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Free-space optical (FSO) communication offers high bandwidth and secure data transmission but remains vulnerable to environmental impairments. This study experimentally investigated the impact of solar-induced radiation on the operating environment of FSO communication links during a period of increased solar activity. Radiation measurements were collected using a Radiacode detector deployed on a high-altitude balloon flight reaching 28,304m in May 2025. The results show that solar-induced background noise increases the bit error rate (BER), particularly under low transmitter power, larger beam divergence, and higher attenuation conditions.

title: Nomad-AMR: Generative Edge Intelligence and Predictive Modeling for Antimicrobial Stewardship in Data-Scarce Populations
authors: Pooja Walia, Rajat Rawal
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) is a rapidly increasing threat to global health, driven in part by delayed or unavailable diagnostics that force empiric antibiotic prescribing. These challenges are amplified in rural and resource-constrained environments where laboratory access, logistics, and connectivity limitations prevent timely antimicrobial susceptibility insights. We propose Nomad-AMR, a decentralized multi-modal edge-AI (Artificial Intelligence) framework for antimicrobial stewardship in data-scarce populations as witnessed in Region 3. The system combines (i) nanopore signal classification for genomic resistance markers, (ii) computer vision-based phenotypic monitoring via low-cost microscopy imaging, and (iii) volatile organic compound (VOC) time-series analysis using electronic-nose (e-nose) sensing. To address limited rural data availability and rare resistance profiles, Nomad-AMR incorporates generative modeling for synthetic data augmentation, including sequence-to-signal simulation for nanopore voltage traces and diffusion-based augmentation for microscopy variability. The framework employs late-fusion probabilistic estimation to generate a unified resistance risk score and supports offline deployment on low-power edge hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson-class embedded GPUs) using quantized small language models (SLMs) for clinical decision support. Nomad-AMR is designed to improve diagnostic responsiveness, robustness, and deployability in rural clinical and agricultural-adjacent environments.

title: CNN-Powered Brain Tumor Classification and Detection Using MRI Based on Deep Learning Methods
authors: Bingshati Mondal, Ritwika Mukherjee, Sekh Aship, Sanjida Khatun, Mukesh Kumar, Priyanshu Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal Analysis for Smart Systems (SIGNASS)
abstract: It is very essential to use magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to tell the differences between brain tumors, so that doctors could make a quick diagnosis and plan treatment. The study presents an automated model for the detection and classification of brain tumors using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The proposed method prepares MRI images, identifies significant features, and categorizes tumors into four distinct groups. There are a lot of convolutional layers in the CNN architecture, and they use batch normalization and max pooling. Then it has dense layers with dropout regularization. We used a publicly available MRI dataset of brain tumors to train and test the model. The method is much better at classifying tumors than traditional machine learning models, as shown by experimental results.

title: Reliable mmWave Blockage Detection Under Severe Imbalance: RUS/SMOTE with Ensemble Models
authors: Mohamed I. Ismail, Eslam Hasan, Rhana Elsayed, Quazi Rian Hasnaine, Zubair Md Fadlullah, Mostafa M. Fouda
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Millimeter-wave (mmWave) communication systems are highly vulnerable to blockage, leading to abrupt link degradation and service interruptions. Reliable blockage detection is therefore essential for enabling proactive beam management and maintaining link robustness. However, real-world mmWave blockage datasets are inherently imbalanced, leading to biased learning and poor blockage detection performance when conventional machine learning models are applied directly. In this paper, we present a comprehensive performance evaluation of machine learning-based blockage classification across different data-balancing strategies. Decision Tree (DT) and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) classifiers are evaluated using the original unbalanced dataset as well as balanced datasets generated via Random Under-Sampling (RUS) and the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE). Simulation results demonstrate that data imbalance severely degrades blockage recall, resulting in 91 missed blockage events in the unbalanced case. Applying SMOTE reduces false negatives to 69, while RUS further decreases them to 36. Moreover, the MLP model trained with SMOTE achieves the best overall performance, attaining a recall of 0.932 and an ROC-AUC of 0.958, significantly enhancing blockage sensitivity and classification robustness. These findings highlight the importance of combining data-balancing strategies with advanced learning models to enable reliable, proactive blockage detection in mmWave and future 6G wireless systems.

title: Multi-Agent-Based Network Traffic Dataset Quality Evaluation Framework
authors: Linzhe Li, Jun Pan
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Data-driven network traffic analysis has become the mainstream method for network traffic classification. However, many publicly available network traffic datasets still exhibit significant shortcomings in terms of authenticity, accuracy, and other aspects, leading to difficulties in maintaining excellent performance of network traffic classification algorithms under real-world network environments. This paper proposes a multi-agent-based network traffic dataset quality evaluation framework, aiming to assess the deficiencies of datasets in terms of authenticity, accuracy, selection bias, and other factors. By introducing a network traffic data shaping method, designing authenticity, accuracy, and selection bias evaluation agents, and formulating workflows among the agents, the framework enables efficient quality evaluation of network traffic datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that this framework effectively evaluates the multi-dimensional quality of datasets and identifies potential risks associated with the use of mainstream network traffic datasets.

title: Multi-Objective Optimal Scheduling of Smart Microgrid with Uncertainty Analysis of Electric Vehicle
authors: Moslem Dehghani, Seyyed Mohammad Bornapour, Jose Rodriguez
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: To develop the concept of Microgrids (MGs), distributed generation units, Energy Storage Systems (ESSs), and Electric Vehicles (EVs) are being combined, which in turn automate distribution networks and also better integrate renewable energy sources into the MG. The objectives of this study are to simultaneously reduce the daily cost and pollution emission of a small MG system incorporating Photovoltaics (PVs), Wind Turbines (WTs), ESSs, and EVs while considering ESS and EV charging requirements, as well as consumer loads. Besides minimizing operational costs and emissions, the proposed Energy Management System (EMS) takes into account the initial energy level of the ESS and EV, as well as the uncertainties associated with the EV in the proposed EMS. The prices of PV and WT energy are considered, and a time-of-use tariff is applied to the energy bought from the utility. The gray wolf algorithm is employed to solve the optimization problem, and a weighted sum method is applied to address the multiobjective problem alongside the suggested algorithms. In the smart MG, diverse combinations of resources, including WT, PV, ESS, and EV with uncertainties, are considered to demonstrate the effectiveness and performance of the suggested EMS. The results show that by considering the ESS and EV, both goals, such as cost and emission, are reduced. Furthermore, by increasing the participation time of EVs, the cost and emissions are reduced by 5.4 % and 0.14 %, respectively.

title: Engineering Reliable Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Regulatory and Compliance Systems
authors: Thiyagarajan Kalyanasundaram, Shravya Bussari, Dinalan Sundaram, Kapil Kumar Sharma
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Regulatory systems in domains such as healthcare, finance, and legal compliance require more than accurate question answering; they demand reliability, traceability, and stability as policies evolve over time. While Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves factual grounding compared to standalone large language models, many deployments remain fragile in production due to retrieval mismatches, unsupported generation, and silent behavioral regressions following document updates. This paper presents a reliability-engineering approach to RAG for regulatory systems. We integrate hybrid densesparse retrieval with cross-encoder reranking, faithfulness guardrails that enforce evidence-backed generation, and continuous regression testing triggered by policy changes. Together, these mechanisms treat RAG as an evolving software system rather than a static inference pipeline. Experimental evaluation on a validated regulatory question-answer set shows that hybrid retrieval improves Recall@5 by up to 18% over dense-only baselines, while faithfulness validation reduces unsupported statements by 67%. A regression framework detects policy-induced answer changes in controlled update scenarios, supporting audit-ready operation. The proposed framework provides a practical blueprint for engineering reliable and auditable RAG systems in compliance-heavy enterprise environments.

title: A Comparative Study of Grid Search and TPE for Tuning PPO on Atari Games
authors: Enrique Escalona V., Patricio Olivares R.
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Hyperparameter tuning is a key component in optimizing the performance of reinforcement learning (RL) agents. This paper focuses on the effectiveness of Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE), a probabilistic optimization algorithm, for tuning the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) method in Atari game environments. We benchmark TPE against the traditional Grid Search strategy across five representative Atari games (Breakout, Pong, Frogger, Donkey Kong, and Pacman) using Stable-Baselines3 library and the ALE framework. For each method, we analyze the best found hyperparameter configurations in terms of final reward and average reward. Results show that TPE outperforms Grid Search in 3 out of 5 games, notably improving Pong's final score from -12.84 to -8.23, while Grid Search remains competitive in more stable environments such as Breakout and Pacman. These findings highlight the advantages of adaptive, model based optimization approaches like TPE over exhaustive search strategies in complex RL tasks.

title: Trajectory Tracking Control of Omnidirectional Mobile Robots: A Comparison between SMC and Null-Space Controllers
authors: Richard López, Esteban Pérez, William Chamorro, Viviana Moya, Marco Herrera, Alvaro Javier Prado-Romo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Sliding Mode Control (SMC) and Null-Space Controllers (NS) are widely recognized for their robustness and adaptability in handling nonlinear systems and multi-objective control tasks, making them suitable for mobile robot trajectory tracking. This study presents the implementation and comparative analysis of several trajectory tracking strategies for an omnidirectional mobile robot. The evaluated methods include SMC approaches (lyapunov-based and transfer function) and NS controllers (PI, PD, PID, and Lyapunov-based variants). The controllers were assessed on a rectangular and a lemniscate shaped trajectory, with fixed and trajectory-facing orientation modes. All controllers were deployed on a real robotic platform using ROS Noetic. The results were evaluated against a visual ground-truth system, which used a top-down recording rig and a low-error 2D visual estimator for precise trajectory measurement. The results demonstrated that the Lyapunov-based controllers (named SMC-LY and NS-LY) outperformed the other variants, achieving enhanced trajectory and orientation tracking accuracy, improved disturbance rejection, and greater energy efficiency. These findings underscore their robustness and practical applicability for trajectory tracking in autonomous mobile robotics.

title: Fine-Tuning Large Vision Models for Robust Diabetic Retinopathy Detection
authors: Nada Elmenabawy, Eslam Hasan, Mostafa M. Fouda
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) has emerged as a global public health challenge and the leading cause of blindness among the working-age population. While deep learning offers a scalable solution for automated screening, existing models often struggle with consistent performance across diagnostic metrics, particularly recall, or lack open-source availability for real-world deployment. To address these limitations, this study proposes a generalizable Large Vision Model (LVM) based on the ConvNeXT Tiny architecture, fine-tuned on the APTOS 2019 dataset for binary DR detection. The methodology incorporates rigorous preprocessing and augmentation to mitigate class imbalance, ensuring robust feature extraction. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves superior performance, recording an accuracy of 98.09 %, a recall (sensitivity) of 98.09 %, and a specificity of 99.45 %. Furthermore, a Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK) score of 0.9618 indicates near-perfect agreement with ground truth labels. This work benchmarks the LVM against state-of-theart methods and releases the model as an open-source tool to facilitate reproducibility and clinical application.

title: Privacy Without Compromise: Benchmarking Local Open-Weight LLMs for Financial Data Extraction
authors: Yash Sharma
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Financial data extraction has traditionally required a compromise: sacrificing data privacy for the convenience and assumed superior accuracy of cloud-based solutions. We challenge this paradigm by benchmarking local open-weight LLMs on consumer-grade hardware (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 18 \text{GB}$</tex> VRAM). Using a 752 -transactions proprietary dataset, we evaluated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 6}$</tex> model configurations across syntactic compliance, extraction accuracy, and semantic categorization. We distinguish between ‘hallucination’ and ‘filter failure’ in financial contexts, revealing that specific models (e.g., Ministral 3, Qwen 3) can match state-of-the-art accuracy. Our findings demonstrate that high-fidelity financial parsing does not require a trade-off between data sovereignty and extraction precision.

title: A Game-Theoretic Federated Learning Approach with Heterogeneity-Sensitive Multimodal Policy for Dynamic Scheduling in Baseband Processors
authors: Jie Peng, Wei Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Heterogeneous multi-core baseband processors are pivotal for next-generation communication systems, yet they face critical scheduling challenges due to dynamic heterogeneity and task diversity. Traditional methods often fail to adapt to fluctuating workloads or capture complex task-core interactions. This paper proposes a Heterogeneity-Sensitive Multimodal Policy Network (HS-MPN) integrated within a game-theory-driven personalized federated learning (PFL) framework. The HS-MPN utilizes a bidirectional attention mechanism to effectively fuse multimodal features of tasks and cores, enabling adaptive priority adjustment and load balancing. Simultaneously, a three-layer PFL architecture, optimized via a dual-level game mechanism, ensures efficient global convergence while preserving individual node adaptability. Experimental results on an Intel FlexRAN-based platform demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. With a workload of 1,000 tasks, the approach shortens task-completion time by 41.28%, reduces system energy consumption by 30.7%, and lowers coreload variance by 34.1%, while achieving an 86.75% Quality-of-Service (QoS) compliance rate. This study provides a robust, intelligent scheduling solution that balances theoretical innovation with engineering practicality for high-concurrency communication scenarios.

title: Hybrid Architectures with Attention Mechanisms and Evolutionary Hyperparameter Optimization for Robust Pneumonia Detection
authors: Marco Fidel Mayta Quispe, Leonid Alemán Gonzales, Charles Ignacio Mendoza Mollocondo, Juan Carlos Juarez Vargas
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: For medical artificial intelligence, automated X-ray-based pneumonia diagnosis is a major challenge. This paper suggests a thorough and reliable method that combines CNNs with hybrid CNN-Transformer architectures and visual attention mechanisms (Grad-CAM, CBAM). Stratified cross-validation is used to optimize hyperparameters using evolutionary methods (both Bayesian and genetic). Numerous open Kaggle databases are used to evaluate the method, guaranteeing patient demographic variety across imaging modalities and clinical contexts. AUC, sensitivity, and accuracy gains over baseline models are statistically significant, according to the results. The suggested framework aids in the creation of clinical decision support systems that are both explanatory and adaptive, making them appropriate for practical application in a variety of healthcare environments.

title: Heterogeneous Graph Neural Tangent Kernel
authors: Ruiyang Chen
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)

title: Photovoltaic Defect Detection Based on the Improved Yolov8
authors: Baoluo Chai, Ling Tang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: In photovoltaic (PV) panel defect detection, defects are typically small, diverse in shape, and exhibit subtle texture differences from normal regions, which makes detection prone to missed detections and false alarms. To improve detection accuracy and reliability, this paper proposes PV-YOLO, an enhanced detector built upon the YOLOv8 framework. Specifically, a DWR module is introduced to extract richer feature representations, and it is integrated with the DilatedReparamBlock (DRB) from UniRepLKNet <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">[16]</sup> to further strengthen feature extraction and defect discriminability. Moreover, the fused module is incorporated into a redesigned C2f block in YOLOv8 to better adapt to the characteristics of PV defects. Experimental results demonstrate that PV-YOLO improves mean average precision (mAP) from 86.0% for the YOLOv8 baseline to 92.4%, while reducing model complexity from 3,012,018 parameters and 8.2 GFLOPs to 2,859,314 parameters and 8.0 GFLOPs, achieving higher accuracy with efficient computation for practical deployment.

title: Adaptative Serious Game as a Support Tool for Children with Reading and Writing Difficulties
authors: Matias Ricardo Villanueva Paz, Hernan Alejandro Quintana Cruz
