publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Learning disabilities affect fundamental psychological processes related to language comprehension and usage, resulting in significant reading and writing difficulties. Additionally, conventional methods have become outdated and unengaging for many individuals experiencing these challenges. For this reason, the present study assesses the effectiveness of a game designed with Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA) as a supportive tool for children with learning disabilities in their reading and writing processes. A group of children aged 7 to 12 with reading difficulties participated in a two-week intervention. Their reading skills were assessed using the Prolec <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}$</tex> battery before and after the intervention. Results indicated significant improvements in word reading, pseudoword reading, discrimination between identical and different words, and error detection. Additionally, usability results demonstrated the game's ease of use and participants' high level of acceptance. These findings underscore the potential of adaptive serious games as complementary tools in the psycho-pedagogical treatment of learning disabilities.

title: A Multi-Level Intelligent Framework for Unknown Vulnerability Capture and Alert Screening
authors: Jiming Bai, Baojiang Cui, Wenchuan Yang
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Addressing the limitations of current detection methods against complex threats like APTs, we propose a four-layer intelligent framework. It integrates a Base Platform for efficient high-speed traffic filtering; a Vulnerability Discovery System targeting unknown zero-day exploits (e.g., command injection, buffer overflow); an Alert Aggregation System to minimize false positives; and a Deep Analysis System utilizing semantic models for threat interpretation. This architecture effectively resolves challenges in real-time processing and high-dimensional data analysis, significantly enhancing vulnerability capture accuracy.

title: Explainability First: Designing Trustworthy Machine Learning Models for Real-World Deployment
authors: Mounica Achanta, Dharanidhar Vuppu
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Machine learning models are increasingly shaping decisions of vital importance across industries. However, their implementation in real-world systems is often limited by issues of trust, transparency, and governance. Although predictive accuracy is still the main goal in most machine learning workflows, explainability is simply seen as a post hoc diagnostic. This document claims that, for machine learning to be reliable, auditable, and trusted by stakeholders, explainability should be the means trusted throughout the lifecycle. We put forward the idea of an Explainability First framework for structured data systems that helps to bring about feature transparency, model diversity, cross, model explanation consistency, and stability analysis into model development and evaluation. We argue using public datasets and several model families that similar predictive performance of models does not guarantee that they will have similar explainability behavior or retraining stability. Our findings emphasize that explanation stability and cross-model agreement are more reliable deployment readiness indicators than mere accuracy. The suggested framework is independent of the model used, it can be reproduced, and it is applicable to any industry. It provides practical advice to those who are involved in the designing of trustworthy machine learning systems.

title: Convolutional Multi-Scale Feature Enhanced Industrial Anomaly Detection
authors: Dan Li, Xujun Li, Zhaowei Zeng
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Industrial anomaly detection aims to identify samples that deviate from normal patterns and plays a critical role in quality inspection. Although CLIP demonstrates strong generalization ability through vision-language alignment, its Vision Transformer backbone is limited in modeling fine-grained local attributes, which restricts its performance in industrial anomaly detection. To address this issue, we propose a CLIP-based industrial anomaly detection framework with convolutional multi-scale feature enhancement. A multi-scale patch embedding strategy is introduced to capture local information at different resolutions, while a dual-branch architecture combines CNN-based local feature extraction with Transformer-based global modeling. Furthermore, a differential attention mechanism is designed to enhance anomaly-sensitive regions and suppress background interference. Multi-scale and image-level contrastive learning are jointly employed to improve feature discrimination. Experimental results on multiple industrial anomaly detection datasets demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Scalable Multi-Agent DRL for Joint Offloading and Resource Allocation in MEC Networks
authors: Yaoyi Hu, Hua Qu, Jihong Zhao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: A fundamental challenge in Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) systems is minimizing the long-term weighted cost of delay and energy through joint task offloading and resource allocation in dynamic, large-scale, multi-agent environments. While existing multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) methods, such as multi-agent deep deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG), are promising, they often face limitations in global coordination efficiency and training stability. To address these challenges, we propose an advanced MADRL framework. The framework introduces a structured attention mechanism designed to explicitly model resource contention, enabling the critic to dynamically weigh the most critical inter-agent dependencies. Furthermore, a hybrid critic architecture synergistically combines this structured attention pathway with a holistic MLP pathway. This dual-pathway approach captures fine-grained coordination patterns with high precision while maintaining robust global value estimation. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves faster convergence and superior performance in minimizing the total system cost compared to conventional MADRL baselines.

title: Modelling of Structural Vibrations Excitation on RTG Cranes
authors: Nelson Cáceres Venegas, Constanza Ahumada Sanhueza, Doris Saez Hueichapan
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents an integrated structuralelectrical model of an RTG crane to analyze operational vibrations. It combines a finite element beam structure, non-linear pendulum dynamics, and induction motor models for hoisting and trolley movement. The study includes model reduction analysis for efficiency, demonstrating the ideal number of nodes for the crane arm. Results present the vibrations observed in the crane arm structure due to lifting and lowering cargo, along with the machine variables of the hoisting motor. This illustrates the benchmark for structural vibration control in RTG cranes.

title: An Interactive Beam Element Analyzer for Problem-Based Learning and Rapid Assessment in Mechanics of Materials
authors: Stephen Andrew Wilkerson
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: This paper presents an interactive MATLAB Live Script-based Truss Builder developed to support problem-based learning, formative assessment, and conceptual understanding in undergraduate engineering statics courses. The tool enables students and instructors to construct, modify, and analyze planar truss structures through a graphical user interface, allowing joints, members, supports, and loads to be defined interactively and visualized in real time. By coupling immediate graphical feedback with underlying equilibrium-based analysis, the software encourages students to explore “what-if” scenarios and develop intuition about force paths, support reactions, and internal member forces. The Truss Builder is implemented using a modular and welldocumented code structure, enabling straightforward customization by instructors for course-specific needs or extensions. Beyond its role as a visual demonstration aid, the tool functions as an assignment and quiz generator by allowing instructors to rapidly create unique truss configurations, as well as a problem-checking and self-evaluation platform that students can use to verify hand calculations and identify conceptual errors. These features support formative assessment by emphasizing process, exploration, and iterative learning rather than reliance on static, pre-defined textbook problems. The software is distributed freely through the MATLAB File Exchange, accompanied by instructional video tutorials that demonstrate classroom and independent study use cases. The paper situates the tool within the broader literature on problem-based learning and visual learning aids in engineering education and discusses how interactive, student-constructed problem environments can enhance engagement and conceptual understanding in statics. Example truss problems are presented to illustrate the tool's analytical accuracy and instructional value. The results suggest that the Truss Builder provides an effective, accessible platform for reinforcing core statics concepts while supporting modern pedagogical approaches in engineering education.

title: A Method of 3D Path Planning for Multi-Drone Based on Improved Genetic Algorithm
authors: Liuyuan Qiao, Haihui Zhou
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: When multiple UAVs perform missions together in the urban area, they need to consider the energy consumption balance, the shortest three-dimensional flight path, and no collision between each other. To solve this problem, an improved genetic algorithm (IGA) is proposed. The goal of this method is to minimize the total path length of multiple UAVs, balance the task allocation as much as possible, and make the flight path as collision free as possible. Based on the traditional genetic algorithm, the population initialization strategy, fitness function design, crossover and mutation operators, elite retention mechanism, and multi strategy mutation strategy are optimized. The simulation results show that the improved genetic algorithm proposed in this paper can converge to the optimal solution within 100 generations in the simulation scenario of 200 m long * <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 0 0 m}$</tex> wide * <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 m}$</tex> high, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 0}$</tex> must pass targets, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6}$</tex> UAVs, and 8 tall buildings. Compared with the traditional unimproved genetic algorithm, it has certain advantages in the total path length, task allocation balance, and convergence speed.

title: Blending Potential Fields and Hough Transform for Advanced Obstacle Avoidance in Mobile Robotics Systems
authors: Esteban Pérez, Richard López, William Chamorro, Viviana Moya, Marco Herrera, Alvaro Javier Prado-Romo
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: Omnidirectional robots with mecanum wheels offer enhanced maneuverability, making them ideal platforms for realtime obstacle avoidance in static and dynamic environments. In this work, we propose an obstacle avoidance framework that combines 2D LiDAR data with the Hough transform to detect and reconstruct line segments, enabling structured mapping of the environment. These segments are integrated into an Artificial Potential Field (APF) algorithm, generating repulsive and attractive reactive navigation forces. The method is implemented on a Hiwonder omnidirectional robot, utilizing a Jetson Nano and ROS Noetic architecture, which enables the modular integration of different trajectory controllers. We evaluate the performance of the APF algorithm in combination with three controllers, including Sliding Mode Control (SMC) and null space controllers. The experiments conducted in both static and dynamic environments demonstrated that a fast, structured reconstruction enables the handling of obstacles with varying sizes and morphologies, particularly when combined with robust trajectory controllers such as SMC and null space. All implementation code is made available to support reproducibility and future developments.

title:
Combined Impedance Spectroscopy and Physical Characterization Unveils Kinetics of Ag Cations in the SET State of Ag/ZrO
                    <sub>x</sub>
                    :Al/Pt-RRAM with Variable Oxygen Vacancies
authors: Suman Gora, Mandeep Jangra, Deepak Yadav, Jay Singh, Monojit Bag, Arnab Datta
publish: IEEE Transactions on Materials for Electron Devices
abstract: Kinetics and complementary roles of Ag cations and oxygen vacancies to the formation of dual-conduction filament (dual-CF) in the SET state of an Al-doped ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> (ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>:Al)-based (Ag/ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>:Al/Pt) resistive random access memory (RRAM) have been investigated here. Al doping increased oxygen vacancies in ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>, which impacted diffusion and reduction of the injected Ag<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> ions (from top contact) during the SET state of this RRAM. Detail impedance spectroscopy demonstrates faster Ag<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> diffusion in a vacancy richer background, which was eventually tailored by Al doping. Traces of Ag in ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>:Al film was found through separate XPS and EDX characterization. Furthermore, a vacancy migration until the counter electrode (Pt) complemented the said Ag<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> diffusion and reduction, which was corroborated by dielectric relaxation behaviour of Ag/ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>:Al/Pt RRAM for variable Al doped ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub>. In this regard, dual-CF was assessed utilizing XPS, which revealed synergy between the field driven excess vacancies and O<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2-</sup> migration that led to phase transition in ZrO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> (tetragonal to monoclinic) under the applied SET bias

title: A Four-Pillar Framework for Achieving 99.9% Reliability in Early-Stage Distributed Systems
authors: Sandhya Vinjam
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Early-stage (0-to-1) distributed products face unique reliability challenges that existing frameworks do not adequately address. Traditional approaches assume organizational maturity, established processes, and dedicated reliability teams-luxuries unavailable to startups and new product initiatives. We present a systematic four-pillar framework-Detection, Mitigation, Resolution, and Prevention-specifically designed for achieving 99.9% reliability in resource-constrained environments. Through production deployment at a large-scale platform serving millions of daily users, we demonstrate 80% incident reduction, 95% improvement in Mean Time to Detection, and 90% improvement in Mean Time to Mitigation. Theoretical analysis projects that widespread framework adoption could prevent $3.6-7.2B in annual losses across e-commerce, food delivery, fintech, and logistics industries. The framework provides concrete implementation patterns, measurable KPIs, and achieves ROI in 6-12 months with 3-4 month implementation timeline.

title: Dynamic and Fair Allocation of Shared Energy Resources in Energy Communities
authors: Eduardo J. Salazar, Sebastián Salazar-Pérez, Veronica A. Rosero Morillo, Mauro Jurado, Sebastián Vaca
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This paper presents a mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model for the dynamic and fair allocation of shared energy in small-scale energy communities. The model computes hourly coefficients to distribute energy from a shared photovoltaic (PV) plant and a community battery energy storage system (BESS). Its primary goal is to minimize the total electricity bill while enforcing quantitative fairness among members. Fairness is achieved through daily constraints on each member's energy share and a minimax term that limits the maximum cost any single member pays for grid electricity. The model also includes revenues from flexibility services sold to the grid. Simulation results for a four-household community show weekly bill reductions of at least 78 % compared to individual operation. The model guarantees each member at least 25 % of the shared PV and BESS energy, achieving fair allocation in a single optimization step with an equity penalty below 3%.

title: Scaling YOLOv8n for Road Object Detection Under Limited Data: A Comparative Study Using BDD100K Subsets
authors: Jateen Joharapurkar, Sahar Hooshmand, Benyamin Ahmadnia, Ali Jalooli, Reza Roofegari Nejad
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Road object detection is a critical component of intelligent transportation systems, supporting applications ranging from autonomous driving to road infrastructure mapping and asset management. While recent deep learning-based detectors achieve strong performance, they typically require largescale datasets and substantial computational resources, limiting accessibility for researchers and organizations operating under resource constraints. In this work, we present a systematic study of data-efficient road object detection using the YOLOv8 architecture trained on reduced subsets of the BDD100K benchmark. We evaluate YOLOv8n on two dataset configurations consisting of 1,000 and 3,000 training images, respectively, and analyze detection performance using standard metrics including mean Average Precision (mAP) and confidence-based evaluation curves. Our results show that YOLOv8n achieves functional detection performance with limited data, attaining an mAP@0.5 of 0.288 on the mini subset and improving to 0.317 on the medium subset. Further analysis of precision-recall and F1-confidence curves reveals improved calibration and robustness with increased dataset size, while class-wise evaluation highlights the impact of object frequency and class imbalance. These findings demonstrate that practical road object detection systems can be developed with substantially reduced training data. This provides valuable insights for resource-constrained research and infrastructurefocused deployment scenarios.

title: DFLADMM-M: Decentralized Federated Learning via Momentum-Accelerated ADMM
authors: Shaohe Xiao, Jia Yu, Gang Wang, Weikang Liu, Qinquan Hu, Bin Cao
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) has emerged as a viable alternative to traditional federated learning by eliminating the central server and enabling peer-to-peer collaboration. Despite its benefits in scalability and communication efficiency, it suffers from model inconsistency across clients and local overfitting due to data heterogeneity. To address these challenges, we propose DFLADMM-m, a DFL algorithm that integrates the Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) with momentum method. The ADMM enforces consensus among local models, while stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with momentum accelerates updates and alleviates oscillations. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks such as MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate that DFLADMM-m achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art DFL algorithms, offering improved accuracy under heterogeneous data settings and various communication topologies.

title: Balancing Privacy and Model Performance: An Experimental Analysis of Data Anonymization Techniques
authors: Nachiappan Chockalingam, Aswathnarayan Muthukrishnan Kirubakaran, Adithya Parthasarathy, Sumit Saha, Abhirup Mazumder, Mayilsamy Palanigounder, Nitin Saksena, Bikesh Kumar
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Balancing data privacy with model performance remains a central challenge in deploying machine learning systems on sensitive datasets. This paper presents an experimental analysis of three widely used syntactic data anonymization techniques k -anonymity, l -diversity, and t -closeness and evaluates their impact on both privacy protection and downstream machine learning utility. Using the Adult Census and German Credit benchmark datasets, we systematically measure privacy outcomes through re-identification risk and attribute disclosure risk, and utility outcomes through classification accuracy, AUC, and information loss across multiple learning models. The results demonstrate that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{t}$</tex>-closeness provides the strongest privacy guarantees, achieving up to 94.2% reduction in re-identification risk, but incurs the highest degradation in model performance. K -anonymity preserves the greatest utility while exhibiting significant vulnerability to attribute disclosure under skewed sensitive attribute distributions. L-diversity consistently offers a balanced tradeoff, maintaining moderate privacy protection with acceptable utility loss. The study further analyzes the influence of dataset characteristics, model choice, and computational overhead on anonymization effectiveness. Based on empirical findings, we derive practical guidelines to support informed selection of anonymization techniques according to application sensitivity and performance requirements, contributing actionable insights for privacy-aware machine learning practice

title: Intelligent Super-Resolution Fusion Method for Satellite and UAV Data Based on Differential Neural Networks
authors: Guozhu Yang, Chao Gao, Haitao Cheng, Shirui Sun, Huamin Sun, Yuanyuan Li
publish: 2026 5th International Symposium on Computer Applications and Information Technology (ISCAIT)
abstract: Shallow fusion of spectral spatial information from satellite and UAV data results in low super-resolution fusion quality. To address this, a super-resolution intelligent fusion method based on differential neural networks for satellite and UAV data is proposed. Multi-source remote sensing data from different satellites and UAVs are collected according to research needs. Metadata is recorded and uniformly stored in GeoTIFF format. Two-step geometric correction is performed on SAR images, radiometric correction is applied to all images, a reference image is selected for registration, and the data is integrated into a complete dataset. Considering the characteristics of multimodal data, a dynamically learnable differential convolution kernel is designed, a hierarchical channel attention module is proposed, and a combination of cross-modal feature interaction and sub-pixel convolution is used to extract data features based on differential neural networks. A super-resolution reconstruction method based on generative adversarial networks is employed. Utilizing multisource datasets and extracted features, a generator and discriminator with an encoder-decoder structure are alternately optimized and trained to generate high-quality, high-resolution fused images, achieving intelligent fusion. Experiments show that the research method stabilizes the PSNR at a high level during the final convergence phase for both 3 x and 5 x magnification tasks. The SSIM (Signal Simulation Index) fluctuates narrowly across multiple test sets, and in some scenes it approaches the level of native satellite data. The average NIQE (National Information Quality Index) on the multi-scene test set is as low as 2.84, approximately 0.73 lower than the best value of the comparative method, significantly improving the quality of super-resolution fusion.

title: Real-Time Hardware-in-the-Loop Validation of Metaheuristic-Based PID Speed Control for a PMDC Motor
authors: Batool A. Younis, Bayadir A. Issa, Hayder D. Almukhtar
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) motors are commonly used in educational setups, small robotic systems, and many industrial applications because they are simple, reliable, and offer good speed-control performance. Although simulations provide an initial understanding of controller behavior, practical validation is still necessary since real hardware introduces effects that are not perfectly captured in models, such as nonlinearities, quantization limits, sampling issues, and communication delays. In this work, a low-cost Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) platform was built to test the real-time speed control of a PMDC motor using PID controllers tuned by different metaheuristic optimization techniques. The setup connects MATLAB/Simulink with an Arduino Uno, an L298N motor driver, and an incremental encoder to form a closed-loop bidirectional system operating at 115200 bps. To ensure a fair comparison, the PID gains obtained earlier using PSO, FPA, and DE were applied directly on the hardware without any further adjustments. The experimental results revealed noticeable differences among the controllers optimized using PSO, FPA, and DE. PSO yielded the fastest rise time while FPA realized the smoothest and most stable response with almost no visible oscillations. The best overall balance was provided by DE, which reached very low overshoot (0.565%) and the smallest steady-state error (0.0317). These findings show that Differential Evolution maintains strong reliability when applied to real hardware. Furthermore, they show that a low-cost HIL platform can successfully validate the intelligent methods to control an embedded DC motor application.

title: Vision-Based Path Planning for a Small-Scale Autonomous Vehicle with Neural Networks
authors: Rodrigo Báez, Micaela Jara Ten Kathen, Mario Arzamendia
publish: 2025 IEEE CHILEAN Conference on Electrical, Electronics Engineering, Information and Communication Technologies (CHILECON)
abstract: This work presents the design and simulation of a route planner for a scale autonomous vehicle, based on computer vision and convolutional neural networks. The system goal is to keep the vehicle centered on the route while completing a planned path, using semantic segmentation to interpret navigable areas. Two deep learning models were trained and evaluated: Fast-SCNN and DeepLabV3 with MobileNetV2 as backbone. Both were tested in simulated environments to assess their performance in route segmentation and control response. The evaluation focused on multiple metrics, including segmentation accuracy, inference speed (FPS), and the planner behavior in terms of turning precision and velocity adaptation. Fast-SCNN outperformed DeepLabV3 in all aspects, showing lower turning error, better speed adjustment, and higher real-time processing capabilities. However, the planner showed limitations in detecting and reacting to extreme deviations, such as 90° turns, indicating the need for a complementary global path planner and object detection model. The results highlight the potential of lightweight CNN architectures for real-time autonomous navigation on resourceconstrained platforms, while also identifying the necessity of enhancing perception modules for complex scenarios.

title: A Systematic Study of Depth-Aware Text-to-Image Generation for Underwater Object Detection
authors: Lujun Zhai, Enrique Morales Mateo, Suxia Cui
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Underwater object detection remains challenging due to severe visual degradation caused by light absorption, scattering, and depth-dependent color attenuation, as well as the limited availability of large-scale and diverse underwater datasets. Recent advances in large-scale text-to-image generative models offer a scalable alternative for synthesizing underwater imagery across varied environmental conditions. This paper investigates whether depth-aware text-to-image generation can produce synthetic underwater scenes with perceptual characteristics suitable for data augmentation in underwater vision. We generate underwater images across shallow (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0-6 \text{ft}$</tex>), middepth (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6-30 \text{ft}$</tex>), and deep-water (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30+\text{ft}$</tex>) ranges using modern text-to-image models, and systematically compare them with real underwater images. A comprehensive, reference-free evaluation is conducted using both underwater-specific perceptual quality metrics and complementary generic image analytics to assess color fidelity, structural clarity, contrast, and perceptual naturalness. The results reveal consistent depth-dependent visual trends shared by real and synthetic images, along with distinct modelspecific behaviors across depth ranges. In particular, different generative models exhibit clear trade-offs between perceptual enhancement, visual stability, and adherence to natural image statistics. While improved perceptual quality does not necessarily guarantee better detection performance, our findings suggest that depth-aware synthetic imagery can effectively complement real datasets by enriching underrepresented depth conditions and visual characteristics, providing practical insights for data augmentation in underwater object detection.

title: Signal Sensitivity-Aware Parameter Discretization for Efficient Sampling
authors: Fadi Hantouli, David Guerra-Zubiaga, Sumit Chakravarty
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Parameter discretization plays a critical role in model-based signal processing, synthetic data generation, and simulation-driven learning frameworks. Conventional discretization strategies commonly rely on uniform or heuristic grids that neglect parameter relevance, measurement noise, and quantization constraints. Consequently, such approaches frequently oversample weakly influential parameters while introducing parameter increments that induce signal variations below the system's detectability threshold, yielding refinements that are physically insignificant. To address these limitations, this paper introduces a detectability-driven parameter discretization framework that integrates local sensitivity analysis with Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS). By exploiting partial derivatives of parametric signal models, the proposed method quantifies each parameter's contribution to observable signal variations and explicitly incorporates noise variance and quantization limits to determine minimum detectable parameter step sizes. This results in a sampling strategy that allocates resolution preferentially to influential parameters while eliminating imperceptible discretization levels.

title: ResSR: A Computationally Efficient Residual Approach to Super-Resolving Multispectral Images
authors: Haley Duba-Sullivan, Emma J. Reid, Sophie Voisin, Charles A. Bouman, Gregery T. Buzzard
publish: SoutheastCon 2026
abstract: Multispectral imaging (MSI) plays a critical role in material classification, environmental monitoring, and remote sensing. However, MSI sensors typically have wavelength-dependent resolution, which limits downstream analysis. MSI super-resolution (MSI-SR) methods address this limitation by reconstructing all bands at a common high spatial resolution. Existing methods can achieve high reconstruction quality but often rely on spatially-coupled optimization or large learning-based models, leading to significant computational cost and limiting their use in large-scale or time-critical settings. In this paper, we introduce ResSR, a computationally efficient, model-based MSI-SR method that achieves high-quality reconstruction without supervised training or spatially-coupled optimization. Notably, ResSR decouples spectral and spatial processing into two sequential steps. ResSR first computes a spectrally-informed high-resolution estimate of the MSI using singular value decomposition together with a spatially-decoupled approximate forward model. It then applies a residual correction step to restore low-frequency spatial consistency while preserving high-frequency detail recovered by the spectral reconstruction. ResSR achieves comparable or improved reconstruction quality relative to existing MSI-SR methods while being <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 \times$</tex> faster. Code is available at https://github.com/hdsullivan/ResSR.
